bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.locus: Reconstructed Bṛhadāraṇyaka commentary fragments, Hiriyanna 1924, on Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 1.4.7
bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.sanskrit_iast:
tad dha idaṃ tarhy avyākṛtam āsīt ।
tan nāmarūpābhyām eva vyākriyata ॥
bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.english_close: Then this was unmanifest. It was differentiated precisely by name and form.

bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.locus: Reconstructed Bṛhadāraṇyaka commentary fragments, Hiriyanna 1924, on Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 2.3.1 and 2.3.6
bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.sanskrit_iast:
dve vāva brahmaṇo rūpe mūrtaṃ caivāmūrtaṃ ca ।
neti neti ॥
bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.english_close: Brahman has, indeed, two forms: the formed and the formless. 'Not this, not this.'

bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.locus: Reconstructed Bṛhadāraṇyaka commentary fragments, Hiriyanna 1924, on Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 1.4.10
bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.sanskrit_iast:
ahaṃ brahmāsmi ।
atha yo 'nyāṃ devatām upāste 'nyo 'sāv anyo 'ham asmīti na sa veda ॥
bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.english_close: I am Brahman. But he who worships another deity, thinking 'He is one thing and I am another,' does not know.

bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.locus: Reconstructed Bṛhadāraṇyaka commentary fragments, Hiriyanna 1924, on Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 2.4.5 and 1.4.15
bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.sanskrit_iast:
ātmā vā are draṣṭavyaḥ śrotavyo mantavyo nididhyāsitavyaḥ ।
ātmānam eva lokam upāsīta ॥
bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.english_close: The self, dear one, is to be seen, to be heard about, to be reflected upon, and to be meditated upon. One should worship the self alone as the world.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.Āgama-prakar.sanskrit_iast:
anādimāyayā supto yadā jīvaḥ prabudhyate
ajam anidram asvapnam advaitaṃ budhyate tadā //

prapañco yadi vidyeta nivarteta na saṃśayaḥ
māyāmātram idaṃ dvaitam advaitaṃ paramārthataḥ //

vikalpo vinivarteta kalpito yadi kenacit
upadeśād ayaṃ vādo jñāte dvaitaṃ na vidyate //
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.Āgama-prakar.english_close: When the jīva, asleep through beginningless māyā, awakens, then it comes to know the unborn, sleepless, dreamless non-dual. If the manifold world were real, it would cease; there is no doubt. This duality is mere māyā; non-duality is the ultimate reality. Conceptual construction would cease, if it had been projected by anyone; this teaching is by way of instruction. When That is known, duality is not found.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.Vaitathya-pr.sanskrit_iast:
svapnajāgaritasthāne hy ekam āhur manīṣiṇaḥ
bhedānāṃ hi samatvena prasiddhenaiva hetunā //

ādāv ante ca yan nāsti vartamāne 'pi tat tathā
vitathaiḥ sadṛśāḥ santo avitathā iva lakṣitāḥ //
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.Vaitathya-pr.english_close: The wise have said that dream-state and waking-state are one. They say so on the well-known reason: the parity of the differentiations [in the two]. What is not present at the beginning and is not present at the end is likewise not present in the middle either. Being similar to unreal things, they are nevertheless perceived as if they were not unreal.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.Advaita-prak.sanskrit_iast:
ātmā hy ākāśavaj jīvair ghaṭākāśair ivoditaḥ
ghaṭādivac ca saṃghātair jātāv etan nidarśanam //

ghaṭādiṣu pralīneṣu ghaṭākāśādayo yathā
ākāśe saṃpralīyante tadvaj jīvā ihātmani //

yathaikasmin ghaṭākāśe rajodhūmādibhir yute
na sarve saṃprayujyante tadvaj jīvāḥ sukhādibhiḥ //

rūpakāryasamākhyāś ca bhidyante tatra tatra vai
ākāśasya na bhedo 'sti tadvaj jīveṣu nirṇayaḥ //

nākāśasya ghaṭākāśo vikārāvayavau yathā
naivātmanaḥ sadā jīvo vikārāvayavau tathā //
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.Advaita-prak.english_close: The Self, indeed, is here presented as like space, with the jīvas presented as like pot-spaces. And, with respect to origination from aggregates as from pots and the like, this is the illustration [for the jīvas' apparent multiplicity]. Just as, when pots and the like dissolve. Pot-spaces and the rest re-merge into space. So too do the jīvas here re-merge into the Self. Just as, when one particular pot-space is conjoined with dust, smoke, and the like. Not all [pot-spaces] are thereby conjoined. So likewise, jīvas are not all conjoined with one [jīva's] pleasures and pains. Forms, functions, and corresponding names differ from place to place. But of space itself there is no real difference; the conclusion with respect to jīvas is the same. As pot-space is not a modification or a part of space, so likewise the jīva is never a modification or a part of the Self.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.Advaita-prak.sanskrit_iast:
māyayā bhidyate hy etan nānyathājaṃ kathaṃcana
tattvato bhidyamāne hi martyatām amṛtaṃ vrajet //

ajātasyaiva bhāvasya jātim icchanti vādinaḥ
ajāto hy amṛto bhāvo martyatāṃ katham eṣyati //

na bhavaty amṛtaṃ martyaṃ na martyam amṛtaṃ tathā
prakṛter anyathābhāvo na kathaṃcid bhaviṣyati //
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.Advaita-prak.english_close: This unborn reality is differentiated by māyā, indeed, not otherwise in any way; for, if it were being differentiated in reality, the deathless would pass into mortality. The disputants posit birth for a reality that is itself unborn; yet how would an unborn, deathless reality go to mortality? The deathless does not become mortal, nor likewise the mortal deathless; becoming otherwise than one's intrinsic nature will not occur in any way.

kesava-kasmiri/krama-dipika/1.4.sanskrit_iast:
sarveṣu varṇeṣu tathāśrameṣu
nārīṣu nānāhvaya-janmabheṣu |
dātā phalānām abhivāñchitānāṃ
drāg eva gopālaka-mantra eṣaḥ ||
kesava-kasmiri/krama-dipika/1.4.english_close: Among all varṇas, and likewise among the āśramas as well. Among women too. And among those of various names and birth-stars. This Gopālaka-mantra is indeed the swift giver of the fruits desired.

kesava-kasmiri/krama-dipika/1.5.sanskrit_iast:
nūnam acyuta-kaṭākṣa-pātena
kāraṇaṃ bhavati bhaktir añjasā |
tac-catuṣṭaya-phalāptaye tato
bhaktimān adhikṛto harau gurau ||
kesava-kasmiri/krama-dipika/1.5.english_close: Certainly, by the descent of Acyuta's sidelong glance. Bhakti directly becomes the cause [of the fruit]. Therefore, for attaining that fourfold result. One possessed of bhakti is qualified with respect to Hari and the guru.

kesava-kasmiri/krama-dipika/2.18.sanskrit_iast:
athavā gopījana iti samasta-jagad-vana-śakti-samudāyaḥ |
tasya svānanyasya svāmī vallabha iti ha nirdiṣṭaḥ ||
kesava-kasmiri/krama-dipika/2.18.english_close: Or else, `gopījana` is the aggregate of the powers that protect the whole world; of that, which is non-other than himself, Vallabha is indeed indicated as the Lord.

kesava-kasmiri/krama-dipika/6.5.locus: Kramadīpikā 6.5, mantroddhāra of the aṣṭādaśākṣara Mukunda-mantra
kesava-kasmiri/krama-dipika/6.5.english_close: I always resort, as refuge, to the two feet of Śrī Mukunda.

kesava-kasmiri/krama-dipika/8.96.sanskrit_iast:
manum imam atihṛdyaṃ yo bhajed bhakti-namro
japa-huta-yajanādyair dhyānavān mantri-mukhyaḥ |
truṭita-sakala-karma-granthir udbuddha-cetāḥ
vrajati sa tu padaṃ tan nitya-śuddhaṃ murāreḥ ||
kesava-kasmiri/krama-dipika/8.96.english_close: Whoever, made bhakti-bowed, would honor this exceedingly delightful mantra. By means of recitation, oblation, worship, and the rest — with meditation. With the knot of all karma severed, his consciousness fully awakened. He, the foremost mantra-practitioner, indeed goes to that eternally-pure station of Murāri.

madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/upodghāta.locus: Siddhānta-Bindu, Upodghāta, on the mahāvākyas as the pramāṇa of ātmatattva
madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/upodghāta.sanskrit_iast:
tasya cātmatattvasya "tattvam asi" "ahaṃ brahmāsmi"
ityādivedāntamahāvākyam eva pramāpakam /
vākyaṃ ca padārthajñānadvāreṇaiva jñāpakam iti
tattvampadārthayoḥ prakṛtavākyānukūlayor anyato 'siddhatvāt
tāv api śāstreṇaiva pramātavyau yūpāhavanīyapadārthavat //
madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/upodghāta.english_close: And of that reality of the self. The great Vedāntic sentences such as 'tat tvam asi' and 'ahaṃ brahmāsmi' alone are the pramāpaka [means of valid cognition]. And a sentence reveals its meaning only by way of cognition of the word-meanings. Therefore, since the meanings of the words 'tat' and 'tvam' — suited to the sentence in question — are not established from elsewhere. Those two too must be known from śāstra alone — just as the meanings of 'yūpa' and 'āhavanīya' must be.

madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/1.prathamo v.locus: Siddhānta-Bindu, Prathamo Vibhāgaḥ, prose under verse 1, defining anirvācyam ajñānam
madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/1.prathamo v.sanskrit_iast:
tasmāt pariśeṣād bhrāntir iyam iti sthite
tatkāraṇam api yogyaṃ kiñcit kalpanīyam /
kalpyamānaṃ ca tadātmany adhyastatayaiva
dharmigrāhakamānena sidhyatīti na jānāmīti
sākṣipratītisiddham anirvācyam ajñānam eva tat /
na cedam abhāvarūpaṃ
jñānasya nityatvena tadabhāvānupapatter uktatvāt //
madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/1.prathamo v.english_close: Therefore, since by elimination it has been established that this is an error. Some suitable cause for it must also be posited. And what is so posited is established precisely as superimposed on that self. It is established by the substrate-grasping cognition expressed as 'I do not know'. That indeed is indefinable ignorance, ascertained by witness-cognition. And this [ignorance] is not of the nature of a mere absence. For, knowledge being eternal, the absence of that knowledge has already been shown to be untenable.

madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.1.sanskrit_iast:
nava-rasa-militaṃ vā kevalaṃ vā pumarthaṃ
paramam iha mukunde bhakti-yogaṃ vadanti /
nirupama-sukha-saṃvid-rūpam aspṛṣṭa-duḥkhaṃ
tam aham akhila-tuṣṭyai śāstra-dṛṣṭyā vyanajmi //
madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.1.english_close: Whether mixed with the nine rasas, or pure on its own, they call it the supreme human end. Here in Mukunda, namely, bhakti-yoga. Of the form of incomparable bliss-consciousness, untouched by sorrow. That [bhakti-yoga] I manifest, by the vision of scripture, for the satisfaction of all.

madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.ṭīkā.1.locus: Bhakti-Rasāyana, Prathama Ullāsa, commentary on kārikā 1, distinguishing bhakti from brahma-vidyā
madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.ṭīkā.1.sanskrit_iast:
dravī-bhāva-pūrvikā hi manaso bhagavad-ākāratā
savikalpaka-vṛtti-rūpā bhaktiḥ /
dravī-bhāvānupetādvitīyātma-mātra-gocarā
nirvikalpaka-mano-vṛttir brahma-vidyā /
bhagavad-guṇa-garimā-granthana-rūpa-grantha-śravaṇaṃ
bhakti-sādhanam /
tat tvam asyādi vedānta-mahā-vākyaṃ
brahma-vidyā-sādhanam //
madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.ṭīkā.1.english_close: Bhakti is the mind's taking on the form of Bhagavat, preceded by melting emotion. It has the form of a determinate (savikalpa) mental modification. Brahma-vidyā, by contrast, is an indeterminate (nirvikalpa) mental modification of the mind. It is unaccompanied by melting emotion, and its object is only the non-dual self. Hearing a text. Whose form is the setting forth of the weight of Bhagavat's qualities. Such hearing is the means of bhakti. The great Vedāntic sentence beginning with 'tat tvam asi' is the means of brahma-vidyā.

madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.13-15.sanskrit_iast:
dravyaṃ karma ca kālaśca svabhāvo jīva eva ca /
yadanugrahataḥ santi na santi yadupekṣayā //
narte tvat kriyate kiñcidityāderna hariṃ vinā /
jñānasvabhāvato 'pi syānmuktiḥ kasyāpi hi kvacit //
ajñānāṃ jñānado viṣṇurjñānināṃ mokṣadaśca saḥ /
ānandadaśca muktānāṃ sa evaiko janārdanaḥ //
madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.13-15.english_close: Substance, action, time, intrinsic nature, and the individual self too — these exist by whose favor. They do not exist by whose disregard. From statements such as 'nothing whatsoever is done without you', [the conclusion follows] that without Hari, liberation would not occur for anyone, anywhere — not even from the very nature of knowledge. Viṣṇu is the giver of knowledge to the ignorant. He is the giver of liberation to the knowers. He, the same one Janārdana, is also the giver of bliss to the liberated.

madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.4.111-112.sanskrit_iast:
jīveśvarabhidā caiva jaḍeśvarabhidā tathā /
jīvabhedo mithaścaiva jaḍajīvabhidā tathā /
mithaśca jaḍabhedo yaḥ prapañco bhedapañcakaḥ //
so 'yaṃ satyo hyanādiśca sādiścennāśamāpnuyāt /
dvaitaṃ na vidyata iti tasmādajñānināṃ matam //
madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.4.111-112.english_close: The difference between jīva and Īśvara, and likewise the difference between the insentient and Īśvara; the mutual difference among jīvas, and likewise the difference between insentient matter and jīva; and the mutual difference among insentient things: that world-manifold is the fivefold difference. This is real and beginningless indeed; if it had a beginning, it would come to destruction. Therefore the statement 'duality does not exist' is the view of the ignorant.

madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69.sanskrit_iast:
śuddhaḥ sākṣī yadā siddho duḥkhitvaṃ vāryate katham /
upajīvyapramāṇaṃ tad bhedagrāhakameva hi /
ato jīveśayorbhedaḥ śrutisāmarthyasusthiraḥ //
tathāpi tu cidānandapūrvāstatsadṛśā guṇāḥ /
sārasvarūpamasyāpi muktāvapyavaśiṣyate //
ato 'bhedavadevaitāḥ śrutayaḥ pravadanti hi /
paurāṇāni ca vākyāni sādṛśyābhedasaṃśrayāt //
sādṛśyācca pradhānatvāt svātantryādapicābhidām /
āhurīśena jīvasya na svarūpābhidāṃ kvacit //
madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69.english_close: When the pure witness-consciousness is established, how can its suffering-status be denied? That [witness-cognition] is indeed a foundational means of knowledge, one that apprehends difference. Therefore the difference between jīva and Lord is made very firm by the force of scripture. Yet, even so, qualities similar to that [Lord]'s — beginning with consciousness and bliss — remain. Even in liberation, they remain for this [jīva] as its essential form. Therefore these scriptural statements indeed speak as if of non-difference. The Purāṇic statements as well [speak so], because they rest on a non-difference grounded in similarity. And because of similarity, predominance, and also sovereignty, they apply a Lord-designation to the jīva. But they never apply a designation of essential identity.

madhva/anuvyakhyana/3.3.1.sanskrit_iast:
vairāgyato bhaktidārḍhyaṃ tenopāsā yadā bhavet /
āparokṣyaṃ bhaved viṣṇoriti pādakramo bhavet //
madhva/anuvyakhyana/3.3.1.english_close: From dispassion comes firmness of devotion; when thereby contemplative worship comes to be, immediate realization of Viṣṇu comes to be. Such is the sequence of the section.

mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-pro.locus: Brahma-Siddhi, opening prose on mbs_1.1, argument that Brahman/the self is bliss by nature
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-pro.sanskrit_iast:
tasmād ātmaprakāśaprakṛṣṭātmasvabhāvam eva brahma iti yuktam /
evaṃ ca laukikānanda etasya mātrā iti yujyate, avacchedāt /
itaś cānandasvabhāva ātmā, parapremāspadatvāt /
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-pro.english_close: Therefore it is fitting that Brahman is precisely of the nature of supreme self-luminosity. Thus worldly pleasure is appropriately understood as only a mere measure of this, because it is delimited. And moreover the self is of the nature of bliss, because it is the locus of supreme love.
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-pro.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/mandana_misra_brahma_siddhi_gretil.txt).

mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-pro.locus: Brahma-Siddhi, opening prose on mbs_1.1, argument that plurality is apprehended only under māyā and denied by śruti
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-pro.sanskrit_iast:
indro māyābhiḥ pururūpa īyate iti vyaktam eva nānārūpāvagamo māyānibandhano darśitaḥ /
tathā vyakto nānātvaniṣedho neha nānāsti kiñcana iti /
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-pro.english_close: "Indra goes as many-formed by māyās": thus it has been clearly shown that the apprehension of manifold forms is bound to māyā. Likewise the negation of plurality is explicit: "here there is not any plurality whatsoever."

mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-pro.locus: Brahma-Siddhi, opening prose on mbs_1.1, definition of avidyā as anirvacanīyā and statement that its locus is the jīvas
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-pro.locus_short: BS 1.1 (avidyā neither sat nor asat; in the jīvas)
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-pro.sanskrit_iast:
nāvidyā brahmaṇaḥ svabhāvaḥ, nārthāntaraṃ, nātyantam asatī, nāpi satī /
evam eveyam avidyā māyā mithyāvabhāsa ity ucyate /
atyantāsattve khapuṣpasadṛśī na vyavahārāṅgam /
tasmād anirvacanīyā /
yat tu kasyāvidyeti, jīvānām iti brūmaḥ /
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-pro.english_close: Avidyā is not Brahman's intrinsic nature, not a different thing, not absolutely non-existent, and not existent either. This very avidyā is called māyā, a false appearance. If it were absolutely non-existent, like a sky-flower, it would not enter into practical transaction. Therefore it is indeterminable. And as for the question "whose avidyā is it?", we say: of the jīvas.

mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.4--tarka-k-a-o.locus: Brahma-Siddhi, mbs_1.4, opening Tarka-kāṇḍa verse on perception and scripture
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.4--tarka-k-a-o.sanskrit_iast:
āhur vidhātṛ pratyakṣaṃ na niṣeddhṛ vipaścitaḥ /
naikatva āgamas tena pratyakṣeṇa virudhyate //
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.4--tarka-k-a-o.english_close: The wise say that perception is an establisher, not a negator. Therefore the scriptural teaching of oneness is not contradicted by that perception.
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.4--tarka-k-a-o.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/mandana_misra_brahma_siddhi_gretil.txt).

mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.35-36--discuss.locus: Brahma-Siddhi, mbs_1.35-36, argument that further contemplative practice is required even after scriptural certainty
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.35-36--discuss.sanskrit_iast:
tannivṛttaye 'sty anyad apekṣyam /
tac ca tattvadarśanābhyāso lokasiddhaḥ /
tathā ca mantavyo nididhyāsitavya ity ucyate /
tasmāt tannivṛttaye viniścitabrahmātmabhāvenāpi sādhanāny apekṣyāṇi /
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.35-36--discuss.english_close: For the cessation of that, something else is required. And that is repeated practice of seeing the truth, something well established in ordinary experience. And thus it is said: 'it is to be reflected upon; it is to be deeply contemplated.' Therefore, even when Brahman-self-identity has been ascertained, further disciplines are required for the cessation of that residual error.
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.35-36--discuss.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/mandana_misra_brahma_siddhi_gretil.txt).

nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1.sanskrit_iast:
jñānasvarūpaṃ ca harer adhīnaṃ
śarīrasaṃyogaviyogayogyam |
aṇuṃ hi jīvaṃ pratidehabhinnaṃ
jñātṛtvavantaṃ cānantam āhuḥ || 1 ||
nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1.english_close: [Its] essential form is consciousness; and that [consciousness] is dependent on Hari. It is fit for both conjunction with and disjunction from a body. The jīva, indeed, is atomic; and it is distinct in each body. They say that it is possessed of knowership and is unending [in number].

nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2.sanskrit_iast:
anādimāyāpariyuktarūpam
enaṃ vidur vai bhagavatprasādāt |
muktaṃ ca bhaktaṃ kila baddham uktaṃ
prabhedabāhulyam athāpi bodhyam || 2 ||
nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2.english_close: Its form is one associated with beginningless māyā. They indeed know this [jīva] thus, by the grace of Bhagavān. It is spoken of as liberated, as devotee, and indeed as bound. And its great abundance of sub-distinctions is also to be understood.

nimbarka/dasa-shloki/4.sanskrit_iast:
svabhāvato 'pāstasamastadoṣam
aśeṣakalyāṇaguṇaikarāśim |
vyūhāṅginaṃ brahma paraṃ vareṇyaṃ
dhyāyema kṛṣṇaṃ kamalekṣaṇaṃ harim || 4 ||
nimbarka/dasa-shloki/4.english_close: From whom all faults have been removed, by his very nature. Who is the single mass of endless auspicious qualities. Who has the vyūhas as his limbs — the supreme Brahman, the most desirable. Let us meditate on him: Kṛṣṇa, the lotus-eyed Hari.

nimbarka/dasa-shloki/5.sanskrit_iast:
aṅke tu vāme vṛṣabhānujāṃ sadā
virājamānām anurūpasaubhagām |
sakhīsahasraiḥ parisevitāṃ mudā
smaret sadā devīṃ sakaleṣṭakāmadām || 5 ||
nimbarka/dasa-shloki/5.english_close: On his left side, [there is] always the daughter of Vṛṣabhānu. Resplendent there, of beauty proportionate [to him]. Attended with delight by thousands of sakhīs. One should always remember the Goddess, the granter of every desired good.

padmapada/pancapadika/V–VI.15–16.locus: Pañcapādikā, V.15–VI.16, on mithyājñāna, avidyā-śakti, and the beginningless status of superimposition
padmapada/pancapadika/V–VI.15–16.sanskrit_iast:
mithyā ca tad ajñānaṃ ca mithyājñānam |
mithyeti anirvacanīyatocyate |
ajñānam iti ca jaḍātmikāvidyāśaktiḥ
jñānaparyudāsenocyate |
tannimittaḥ tadupādāna ity arthaḥ |
kathaṃ punar naimittikavyavahārasya naisargikatvam |
atrocyate |
avaśyam eṣā avidyāśaktir bāhyādhyātmikeṣu vastuṣu
tatsvarūpasattāmātrānubandhinī abhyupagantavyā
anyathā mithyārthāvabhāsānupapatteḥ |
padmapada/pancapadika/V–VI.15–16.english_close: "Mithyājñāna" is that which is both mithyā and ajñāna. By the term "mithyā", indefinability is stated. And by the term "ajñāna", the power of avidyā — whose own nature is insentient. It is stated, by way of the exclusion of knowledge. "Having that as its cause" means: having that as its material cause. How then can a transactional practice that has an occasioning cause be beginningless by nature? Here it is said as follows. This very power of avidyā must necessarily be admitted. With respect to external and internal things, it must be admitted as connected only with the mere existence that is the own-nature of those things. Otherwise, the manifestation of false objects would be impossible.

padmapada/pancapadika/XIX.62–63.locus: Pañcapādikā, XIX.62–63, refining the definition of adhyāsa
padmapada/pancapadika/XIX.62–63.sanskrit_iast:
paratra pūrvadṛṣṭāvabhāsaḥ |
paratra smṛtirūpāvabhāsaḥ |
smṛtirūpaḥ paratra pūrvadṛṣṭāvabhāsaḥ |
padmapada/pancapadika/XIX.62–63.english_close: The appearance elsewhere of what was seen before; the appearance elsewhere of what has the form of memory; [thus, adhyāsa is] the appearance elsewhere, in the form of memory, of what was previously seen.

padmapada/pancapadika/XXIX.108.locus: Pañcapādikā, XXIX.108, reading tat tvam asi through the bimba-pratibimba relation
padmapada/pancapadika/XXIX.108.sanskrit_iast:
tat tvam asi |
na tat tvam asīti syāt |
kintu na tvam asīti bhavet
na rajatam astītivat |
padmapada/pancapadika/XXIX.108.english_close: [The mahāvākya is] "tat tvam asi". It would not [merely amount to] "na tat tvam asi" — "that you are not". Rather, it would amount to "na tvam asi" — "you yourself are not". [And that absurdity would be] just like "there is no silver".

prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/brahmaṇ.locus: Pañcapādikā-Vivaraṇa, brahmaṇo lakṣaṇopalakṣaṇe discussion, p. 212 (scan p. 269)
prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/brahmaṇ.sanskrit_iast:
māyāvidyayoḥ sāmānādhikaraṇyāc ca ।
tasmād anirvacanīya-māyā-viśiṣṭaṃ kāraṇaṃ brahmeti prāptam ॥
prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/brahmaṇ.english_close: And because of the co-reference (sāmānādhikaraṇya) of māyā and avidyā [in the relevant scriptural usage]. Therefore the conclusion is reached that Brahman, as qualified by indeterminable māyā, is the [world-]cause.

prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/jīvasya.sanskrit_iast:
devadatto hi svātmānam andhatvādy-upetaṃ pratibimbe 'vagacchann api tattva-jñānatvān nānuśocati ।
evaṃ brahmāpi svātmani jīve pratibimbe saṃsāraṃ paśyad api tattva-jñānatvān nānuśocati ॥
prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/jīvasya.english_close: For Devadatta, even while cognizing his own self as endowed with blindness and the like in a reflection, does not grieve, because he possesses true knowledge. In the same way, Brahman too, even while seeing saṃsāra in the reflected jīva within its own self, does not grieve, because it possesses true knowledge.

prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/mahāvāk.locus: Pañcapādikā-Vivaraṇa, svayaṃprakāśe 'pi mananādi-sahakārair eva mahāvākyasyāparokṣa-prayojakatvam, p. 103 (scan p. 123)
prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/mahāvāk.sanskrit_iast:
svayaṃprakāśe 'pi mananādi-sahakārair eva mahāvākyasyāparokṣa-prayojakatvam ।
brahmaiva sarva-saṃvid-upādānatvād brahmakāra-śabda-pramāṇa-janya-saṃvedane 'pi brahmāpi prathamam evāparokṣatayāvabhāsate ।
tac ca cittasyātisūkṣmatvād viparyaya-saṃskāra-doṣāc ca pratibaddhaṃ bhrāntyā parokṣavad bhāsate ॥
prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/mahāvāk.english_close: Even in the case of what is self-luminous, the great sentence has the capacity to produce immediacy only through auxiliaries such as reflection. Because Brahman itself is the substrate of every cognition, Brahman too appears from the outset as immediate even in the cognition generated by the verbal pramāṇa taking Brahman as its form. And that, because of the mind's extreme subtlety and because of the defect of contrary latent impressions, appears through error as if indirect.

prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/brahma-.locus: Pañcapādikā-Vivaraṇa, samuccaya discussion in brahma-vicāre kim-ānantaryam atha-śabdāvagamyam, pp. 164-165 (scan pp. 186-187)
prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/brahma-.sanskrit_iast:
avidyayā mṛtyuṃ tīrtvā vidyayāmṛtam aśnuta iti phala-bhedāgamāt ।
tasmān na samuccaye pramāṇam asti ।
sarvānarthopādānānādi-mithyājñāna-nivṛttir eva vijñāne phalavad iti na samuccayāvakāśaḥ ॥
prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/brahma-.english_close: From the scriptural teaching 'having crossed death by avidyā, one attains immortality by vidyā', because the [two] fruits are distinct. Therefore there is no pramāṇa whatsoever for [a jñāna-karma-]combination. Since in liberating cognition the fruit is precisely the cessation of beginningless false cognition — which is the material cause of all misfortune — there is no room left for any such combination.

raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.locus: Tantra-Dīpikā, adhyāya 1 summary on the convergence of Vedic language on Viṣṇu
raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.sanskrit_iast:
te ca śabdā anyatra-prasiddhā ubhayatra-prasiddhā anyatraiva-prasiddhāś ca
sākṣāt paramparayā vā viṣṇum eva sṛṣṭy-ādi-vartṛtva-sādhya-guṇa-pūrṇatvena sādhayanti
raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.english_close: And those words — whether established elsewhere, established in both places, or established only elsewhere. They prove Viṣṇu alone, either directly or through paramparā. They do so by [showing him] to be full of qualities established through creatorship and the rest of the [divine] functions.

raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1.locus: Tantra-Dīpikā, adhyāya 1, pāda 1, discussion of the ānandamayādhikaraṇa under the sūtra bhedavyapadeśāc ca
raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1.sanskrit_iast:
ānandamayo netara iti vartate |
ānandamayād bhedavyapadeśāc cānandamayo netara ity arthaḥ |
"sa eko brahmaṇa ānandaḥ" ityādinā viriñcadeḥ parimitānandam uktvā |
"yato vāco nivartante" ity ānandamayasya pūrṇānandatvokter iti bhāvaḥ ||
raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1.english_close: "Ānandamaya, not the other": that is the construal. Because there is predication of difference from the ānandamaya, the ānandamaya is not the other. By the expression "that is one joy of Brahman" and the like, limited bliss is stated for Viriñci; by "from which words turn back," full blisshood is stated of the ānandamaya: this is the purport.

raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.3.locus: Tantra-Dīpikā, adhyāya 1, pāda 3, discussion of the sūtra suṣuptyutkrāntyor bhedena
raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.3.sanskrit_iast:
na cābhedena sopapattiḥ |
"prājñenātmanā sampariṣvaktaḥ" iti |
"prājñenātmanānvarūḍhaḥ" iti ca |
suṣuptyutkrāntyor bhedena jīveśayor vyapadeśa iti paramparayā yojyam ||
raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.3.english_close: And it is not tenable on the assumption of non-difference. [The śruti says]: "Embraced by the prājña-self". And [the śruti also says]: "mounted upon the prājña-self". Thus, in deep sleep (suṣupti) and in departure (utkrānti), there is designation of the jīva and the Lord as different. So the connection is to be made by sequential linkage (paramparā).

raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.4.locus: Tantra-Dīpikā, adhyāya 1, pāda 4, discussion of the sūtra jñeyatvavacanāc ca
raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.4.sanskrit_iast:
brahmaṇa eva mumukṣu-jñeyatva-vacanād ānumānikāder jñeyatvāvacanāc cety arthaḥ |
"tam evaikaṃ jānathātmānam" iti brahmaṇa eva mumukṣu-jñeyatva-vacanāt ||
raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.4.english_close: The meaning is this: because there is a statement that Brahman alone is the knowable for the seeker of liberation, and because there is no statement of knowability for what is merely inferential and the like. "Know that one Self alone": because there is a statement that Brahman alone is to be known by the seeker of liberation.

sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.11.15.sanskrit_iast:
na tato 'mṛtatāśāsti /
karmaṇo 'jñānahetutaḥ /
mokṣasya jñānahetutvān /
na tadanyad apekṣate //
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.11.15.english_close: From that, no hope of immortality exists. For action has ignorance as its cause. Mokṣa, by contrast, has knowledge as its cause. Therefore it does not depend on anything else.
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.11.15.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7-9.sanskrit_iast:
anyadṛṣṭis tv avidyā syāt /
tannāśo mokṣa ucyate /
jñānenaiva tu so 'pi syād /
virodhitvān na karmaṇā //

karmakāryas tv anityaḥ syād /
avidyākāmakāraṇaḥ /
pramāṇaṃ veda evātra /
jñānasyādhigame smṛtaḥ //

jñānaikārthaparatvāt taṃ /
vākyam ekaṃ tato viduḥ /
ekatvaṃ hy ātmano jñeyaṃ /
vākyārthapratipattitaḥ //
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7-9.english_close: The seeing of something other [than the self] would be avidyā. The destruction of that is what is called mokṣa. And that [destruction] too would come about only through jñāna. For karma, being its [logical] opponent, cannot accomplish it. What is produced by karma would be impermanent. Such a karma-effect has avidyā and desire as its causes. Here the Veda alone is held to be the pramāṇa. It is remembered as such with respect to the attainment of jñāna. Because [the Veda] is directed to a single purport — namely jñāna. They therefore know that mahā-vākya to be a single unified statement. For the oneness of the self is to be known. And it is known through the comprehension of the sentence-meaning.
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7-9.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/mandukya-bhasya/upaniṣad.7.locus: Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad 7, as glossed in Śaṅkara's bhāṣya
sankara/mandukya-bhasya/upaniṣad.7.sanskrit_iast:
nāntaḥprajñaṃ na bahiḥprajñaṃ nobhayataḥprajñaṃ na prajñānaghanam /
na prajñaṃ nāprajñam /
adṛṣṭam avyavahāryam agrāhyam alakṣaṇam acintyam avyapadeśyam /
ekātmapratyayasāraṃ prapañcopaśamaṃ śāntaṃ śivam advaitaṃ caturthaṃ manyante /
sa ātmā sa vijñeyaḥ //
sankara/mandukya-bhasya/upaniṣad.7.english_close: Not inwardly cognizing, not outwardly cognizing, not cognizing both ways, not a mass of cognition; not cognizing, not non-cognizing; unseen, not available for transaction, not graspable, without defining mark, unthinkable, not nameable; whose essence is the conviction of one self, the cessation of phenomenal proliferation, peaceful, auspicious, non-dual, they consider [that] the fourth. That is the self; that is to be known.

sankara/mandukya-bhasya/2.32.locus: Gauḍapāda-Kārikā 2.32, as glossed in Śaṅkara's bhāṣya
sankara/mandukya-bhasya/2.32.sanskrit_iast:
na nirodho na cotpattir /
na baddho na ca sādhakaḥ /
na mumukṣur na vai mukta /
ity eṣā paramārthatā //
sankara/mandukya-bhasya/2.32.english_close: There is no cessation and no origination. There is no one bound and no practitioner. There is no seeker of liberation, and indeed no liberated one. This is the highest truth.

sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/1.319.sanskrit_iast:
āśrayatvaviṣayatvabhāginī nirvibhāgacitir eva kevalā |
pūrvasiddhatamaso hi paścimo nāśrayo bhavati nāpi gocaraḥ ||
sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/1.319.english_close: Only undivided consciousness alone possesses the status of locus and object. For what is later-arising does not become, nor can it become, the locus or the object of the darkness that is already established before it.

sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/1.327.sanskrit_iast:
sukṛtaduṣkṛtakarmaṇi kartṛtāṃ
matigatātmacitipratibimbakam |
vrajati tadvad adaḥ paramātmano
jagati yāti tamaḥpratibimbakam ||
sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/1.327.english_close: The reflection of self-consciousness, the one located in the intellect [buddhi], attains agenthood. It does so with respect to meritorious and demeritorious action. Likewise, that reflection in darkness — belonging to the supreme self — goes forth as Īśvara. It thus enters into relation with the world.

sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/3.275-276.sanskrit_iast:
upādhim aupādhikam āntaraṃ cidābhāsanaṃ citpratibimbakaṃ ca /
cidbimbam evaṃ caturaḥ padārthān vivicya jānīhi tadarthabhājaḥ //
tathā tvamarthe 'pi catuṣṭayaṃ tad vivecanīyaṃ nipuṇena bhūtvā /
matiś cidābhāsanam evam asyāṃ bimbaṃ tadīyaṃ pratibimbakaṃ ca //
sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/3.275-276.english_close: [On the 'tat' side]: there are the upādhi, the inner upādhi-conditioned manifestation of consciousness (cidābhāsa), the reflection of consciousness (cit-pratibimba), and the original consciousness-image (cit-bimba). Discriminate and know, thus, these four referents that bear the meaning of 'tat'. Similarly, in the case of the meaning of 'tvam' too, that fourfold set is to be discriminated — once one has become exact in analysis. There it is the intellect (mati), the manifestation of consciousness (cidābhāsa) in it, the original (bimba) in that, and the reflection (pratibimba) of that original.

sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/3.278.sanskrit_iast:
upādhir antaḥkaraṇaṃ tvamarthe
jīvatvam ābhāsanam atra tadvat /
tadanvitā cit pratibimbam evam
ananvitāṃ bimbam āhuḥ ||
sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/3.278.english_close: In the meaning of 'tvam', the limiting adjunct is the internal organ; the manifestation there is jīva-hood, just as in that other case. Consciousness connected with that is the reflected image; consciousness unconnected with it, they say, is the original image.

srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/1.sanskrit_iast:
śrīkṛṣṇapādayugalaṃ śirasā namāmi
yatrānvitaḥ śrutigaṇo na virodham eti //
srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/1.english_close: To the pair of Śrī Kṛṣṇa's feet, I bow with my head. With reference to which [pair], the host of śrutis does not enter into contradiction.

srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/Upodghāta.locus: Vedānta-Kaustubha, opening prose statement of purpose
srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/Upodghāta.sanskrit_iast:
tadājñayā taduktavartmanā tadanugrahakāmena
tacchiṣyeṇa mayā mṛdumitapado vedāntakaustubhaḥ
tadbhāvārthaprakāśako viduṣām upakārāya viracyate //
srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/Upodghāta.english_close: By his command, by the path taught by him, with desire for his favor. By me, his disciple, the Vedānta-Kaustubha — of gentle and measured wording — [is being composed]. It is the manifester of the intended meaning of that [Brahma-Sūtra]. It is being composed for the benefit of the learned.

srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/1.1.1.sanskrit_iast:
tattvaṃ tāvan trividham |
cid-acid-brahma-bhedāt |
svādhīna-parādhīnau brahmajīvātmānāv ity arthaḥ ||
srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/1.1.1.english_close: Reality, to begin with, is threefold. [It is so] because of the distinction among cit, acit, and Brahman. That is to say: Brahman and the jīva-self stand respectively as svādhīna (independent) and parādhīna (dependent on the other).

srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42.locus: Vedānta-Kaustubha on Brahma-Sūtra 2.3.42, jīva as aṃśa of Paramātman
srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42.sanskrit_iast:
nāyaṃ jīvaḥ śrīpuruṣottamād atyantabhinnaḥ,
nāpy atyantābhinnaḥ, kintu paramātmano 'ṃśaḥ |
aṃśo hy eṣa parasya iti śruteḥ |
aṃśo hi śaktirūpo grāhyaḥ, parāsya śaktiḥ,
jīvo 'lpaśaktir asvatantraḥ iti śruteḥ ||
srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42.english_close: This jīva is not absolutely different from Śrī Puruṣottama. Nor again is it absolutely non-different. Rather, it is an aṃśa (part) of the supreme self. For scripture says, 'this one is indeed a part of the supreme'. And an 'aṃśa' is to be understood as being of the form of a power (śakti). [Thus scripture says]: 'it is the power of the supreme'. And: 'the jīva is a limited power, not independent' — so says scripture.

sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/4.3.sanskrit_iast:
ātmānātmā ca loke 'smin
pratyakṣādi-pramāṇataḥ
siddhau tayor anātmā tu
sarvatraivātma-pūrvakaḥ
sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/4.3.english_close: In this world, the Self and the not-Self [stand thus]. By means of pramāṇas such as perception. They are both established. But, of those two — in such establishment — the not-Self is everywhere preceded by the Self.

sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.9-10.sanskrit_iast:
anvaya-vyatirekābhyāṃ vinā
vākyārtha-bodhanaṃ na syāt
tena vinājñāna-prahāṇaṃ
nopapadyate
vinājñāna-prahāṇena
puruṣārthaḥ su-durlabhaḥ
tasmād yathokta-siddhy-arthaṃ
paro grantho 'vatāryate
sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.9-10.english_close: Without anvaya-vyatireka [reasoning by positive concomitance and exclusion]. There would be no understanding of the sentence-meaning. Without that [understanding]. The removal of ignorance is not possible. Without the removal of ignorance. The human end is very difficult to attain. Therefore, in order to accomplish what has just been stated. A further section [of the treatise] is now introduced.

sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.55-56.sanskrit_iast:
bādhya-bādhaka-bhāvāc ca
pañcāsyoraṇayor iva
eka-deśānavasthānāt
na samuccayatā tayoḥ
ayathā-vastv-avidyā syād
vidyā tasyā virodhinī
samuccayas tayor evaṃ
ravi-śarvarayor iva
sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.55-56.english_close: Because they stand in the relation of what is sublated and what sublates. Just as in the case of a lion and a sheep [together]. And because they do not remain in one place together. Therefore, there is no conjunction of the two. Ignorance would be misapprehension of reality (ayathā-vastv-avidyā). Knowledge is its opponent (virodhin). Their conjunction would [therefore] be thus. Just like the conjunction of the sun and night — that is, impossible.

sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.98-99.sanskrit_iast:
tat-tvam-asy-ādi-vākyānāṃ
svatas-siddhārtha-bodhanāt
arthāntaraṃ na saṃdraṣṭuṃ
śakyate tridaśair api
ataḥ sarvāśramāṇāṃ tu
vāk-manaḥ-kāya-karmabhiḥ
svānuṣṭhitair na muktiḥ syāj
jñānād eva hi sā yataḥ
sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.98-99.english_close: Statements beginning with 'tat tvam asi' reveal a self-established meaning. Therefore, another meaning. Cannot be seen [projected] into them. Not even by the gods. Therefore, for the members of all āśramas. With acts of speech, mind, and body. Even when those acts are self-performed, liberation does not arise. For it [arises] only from knowledge — because it proceeds from that alone.

vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4.sanskrit_iast:
tat brahmaiva samavāyi kāraṇam.
kutaḥ? samanvayāt samyag-anugatatvāt.
asti bhāti priyatvena sac-cid-ānanda-rūpeṇānvayāt.
nāma-rūpayoḥ kārya-dharmatvāt.
vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4.english_close: That Brahman itself is the inhering cause. Why? Because of coherent pervasion, from its proper pervasion as being, shining, and dear, in the form of sat-cit-ānanda; and because name and form have the status of properties belonging to the effect.

vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19.sanskrit_iast:
atra brahmajñānenāvidyā-nivṛttyā puruṣottama-prāptau svarūpa-yogyatā sampādyate.
tato jīve svīyatvena varaṇe bhakti-bhāvāt sahakāri-yogyatā-sampādya puruṣottama-prāptir bhavatīti nirṇeyate.
bhaktyāham ekayā grāhyaḥ.
vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19.english_close: Here, by Brahman-knowledge and by the cessation of ignorance, fitness for one's own true state is accomplished with respect to the attainment of Puruṣottama. Then, when the soul is chosen as His own, and with devotion as its disposition, having brought about the assisting fitness, attainment of Puruṣottama comes to be: thus it is determined. 'I am to be grasped by devotion alone.'

vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26.sanskrit_iast:
tad ātmānaṃ svayam akuruta iti svasyaiva kartṛ-bhāvāt avikṛtatva-śravaṇāc cālaukikatvam.
tathāpi pariṇamate kāryākāreṇa.
avikṛtam eva pariṇamate suvarṇaṃ sarvāṇi ca tejasāni.
vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26.english_close: 'It made itself as itself': because there is the state of its own agency, and because scripture speaks of its being unmodified, this is non-ordinary. Even so, it transforms into the form of the effect. Gold, while itself unmodified, indeed transforms into all golden products.

vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45.sanskrit_iast:
jīvo nāma brahmaṇo 'ṃśaḥ.
kutaḥ? nānāvyapadeśāt.
na hi brahma niraṃśaṃ sāṃśam iti vā kvacid loke siddhaṃ, vedaika-samadhigamyatvāt.
mamaivāṃśo jīvaloke jīvabhūtaḥ sanātana iti smaryate.
vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45.english_close: The jīva indeed is a part of Brahman. Why? Because of manifold designation. For Brahman is not established anywhere in ordinary experience as either partless or as having parts; it is knowable only through the Veda. 'An eternal part of Me in the world of living beings, having become a jīva' is remembered in smṛti.

vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1.maṅga.sanskrit_iast:
cāturvyūhaṃ paraṃ brahma ṣāḍguṇya-parikarmitam |
pañcarātrasya kṛtsnasya prasūtiṃ paryupāsmahe ||
vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1.maṅga.english_close: [He is] the supreme Brahman, [manifested] as the four vyūhas, and adorned with the six divine excellences (ṣāḍguṇya). The source of the whole Pāñcarātra — him we reverentially worship.

vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1.catvā.sanskrit_iast:
āgamasiddhānto mantrasiddhāntas tantrasiddhāntas tantrāntarasiddhānta iti |
catvāraś cete siddhāntā ṛgādivad eva śākhābhedair avantaratantrair bhidyante ||
vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1.catvā.english_close: Thus: "āgama-siddhānta", "mantra-siddhānta", "tantra-siddhānta", and "tantrāntara-siddhānta". These four are the established doctrinal forms; and just like the Ṛk and the other Vedic [recensions], they are divided into śākhā-distinctions and into subordinate tantras.

vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1.vaikh.locus: Pāñcarātra-Rakṣā, prathamo'dhikāraḥ, section on Vaikhānasa-Pāñcarātra non-conflict
vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1.vaikh.sanskrit_iast:
avirodhopayogābhyām ubhayor api śāstrayoḥ |
madhyasthair hi kṛto dharmo vidyāsthāna-niveśanam ||
vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1.vaikh.english_close: Because of the non-conflict and the [respective] usefulness of both śāstras. A normative settlement (dharma) has indeed been made by impartial authorities — namely, the assigning of each to its proper vidyā-sthāna (domain of learning).

vidyaranya/panchadashi/1.15-16.sanskrit_iast:
cidānandamayabrahmapratibimbasamanvitā |
tamo-rajaḥ-sattvaguṇā prakṛtir dvividhā ca sā || 15 ||
sattvaśuddhyaviśuddhibhyāṃ māyāvidye ca te mate |
māyābimbo vaśīkṛtya tāṃ syāt sarvajña īśvaraḥ || 16 ||
vidyaranya/panchadashi/1.15-16.english_close: Prakṛti is endowed with the reflection of Brahman consisting of consciousness and bliss, and is possessed of the guṇas tamas, rajas, and sattva. And that prakṛti is twofold. Through the purity and the impurity of sattva, those two [parts of prakṛti] are regarded as māyā and avidyā [respectively]. The reflection in māyā, having brought that [māyā] under its control, becomes the omniscient Īśvara.

vidyaranya/panchadashi/1.42.sanskrit_iast:
yathā muñjād iveṣīkām ātmā yuktyā samuddhṛtaḥ |
śarīratritayād evaṃ paraṃ brahmaiva jāyate || 42 ||
vidyaranya/panchadashi/1.42.english_close: Just as the inner fibre is drawn out from muñja grass. So too, when the self is extracted by reasoning from the triad of bodies, it [is recognized to] become nothing but the supreme Brahman.

vidyaranya/panchadashi/1.54-55.sanskrit_iast:
tābhyāṃ nirvicikitsārthe cetasaḥ sthāpitasya yat |
ekatānattvam etad dhi nididhyāsanam ucyate || 54 ||
dhyātṛdhyāne parityajya kramād dhyeyaikagocaram |
nivātadīpavac cittaṃ samādhir abhidhīyate || 55 ||
vidyaranya/panchadashi/1.54-55.english_close: When, by those two [śravaṇa and manana], the mind has been established in the matter freed from doubt. The one-pointed continuity that ensues — this indeed is called nididhyāsana. Having left aside meditator and meditation, [and] step by step having only the object of meditation as its range. The mind, then, like a lamp in a windless place, is designated samādhi.

vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/2.3.43.locus: Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya on Brahma-Sūtra 2.3.43, on aṃśo nānāvyapadeśāt
vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/2.3.43.sanskrit_iast:
aṃśo nānāvyapadeśād anyathā cāpi dāsakitavāditvam adhīyate eke //
nityatvādinā sajātīyatvasyopapāditatayā jīvo brahmaṇo 'ṃśaḥ, pitur iva putraḥ //
vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/2.3.43.english_close: There is a part, because multiplicity is predicated, and because the condition of being servant, gambler, and so on is also taught by some. Since common nature is established by eternity and the like, the jīva is a part of Brahman, like a son of a father.

vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/1.1.5.locus: Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya on Brahma-Sūtra 1.1.5, on īkṣater nāśabdam
vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/1.1.5.sanskrit_iast:
tasmāt “māyāṃ tu prakṛtiṃ vidyān māyinaṃ tu maheśvaram /
asyāvayavabhūtais tu vyāptaṃ sarvam idaṃ jagat //”
iti śrutau śrutyantara-smṛti-prasiddha-prakṛter eva māyāśabda-vācyatvaṃ vidhīyate iti.
vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/1.1.5.english_close: Therefore, in the scriptural statement: 'One should know māyā to be prakṛti, and the possessor of māyā to be Maheśvara'. [And further]: 'by what [parts] have become its constituent parts, this whole world is pervaded'. In this śruti, it is prescribed that it is precisely the prakṛti — already known from other śruti and smṛti — that is denoted by the word 'māyā'.

vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.23.locus: Yoga-Vārttika on Yoga-Sūtra 1.23, on īśvara-praṇidhānād vā
vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.23.sanskrit_iast:
īśvara-praṇidhānād vā //
praṇidhānam atra na dvitīya-pādavad japyaprāyaṃ, kiṃ tv asaṃprajñāta-kāraṇībhūta-samādhi-bhāvanā-viśeṣam eva //
vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.23.english_close: Or from devotion-directing oneself to the Lord. Here praṇidhāna is not, as in the second pāda, something consisting chiefly in muttered recitation; rather it is precisely a particular cultivation of samādhi that becomes the cause of asaṃprajñāta [samādhi].

vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.24.locus: Yoga-Vārttika on Yoga-Sūtra 1.24, on kleśa-karma-vipākāśayair aparāmṛṣṭaḥ
vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.24.sanskrit_iast:
kleśa-karma-vipākāśayair aparāmṛṣṭaḥ puruṣa-viśeṣa īśvaraḥ //
avidyā-asmitā-rāga-dveṣābhiniveśāḥ kleśā iti vakṣyate //
vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.24.english_close: Īśvara is a special puruṣa untouched by afflictions, actions, their ripenings, and latent deposits. 'Afflictions' are said to be ignorance, egoity, attachment, aversion, and clinging-to-life.

vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Anubandha-catu.sanskrit_iast:
vedāntānāṃ viṣayo brahma,
ananyasādhāraṇatvāt tatpratipādyatvāc.
brahmaṇaḥ vedāntānāṃ ca
pratipādyapratipādakalakṣaṇaḥ sambandhaḥ.
vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Anubandha-catu.english_close: The subject-matter (viṣaya) of the Vedāntas is Brahman. [This is so] because it is not common to any other source (ananyasādhāraṇatva). And because it is what they expound (tat-pratipādyatva). The relation between Brahman and the Vedāntas is, accordingly, the one characterized as that between what is to be expounded and what expounds it.

vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Prapañcasyānir.sanskrit_iast:
māyeti sadasadbhyām anirvacanīyā avidyocyate.
sā hi yuktyā nirūpyamāṇā na astīti, nāstīti veti vaktuṃ śakyā,
nāpi nāstīti; sadasadvilakṣaṇatvena tadvacanānarhatvāt.
ata evānirvacanīyā, na punar avācyeti.
vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Prapañcasyānir.english_close: By the word 'māyā' is meant ignorance, indeterminable by being and non-being. For when it is analyzed by reasoning, it cannot be stated, 'it is,' or 'it is not'; nor simply 'it is not,' because, by being different from both being and non-being, it is unfit to be described in those terms. Therefore it is indeterminable, not merely unsayable.

vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Anirvacanīya-k.sanskrit_iast:
ato bhrāntau yāvat kiñcid bhāti
tasya sarvasya bhāve 'bhāve ca pramāṇābhāvād
anirvacanīyataiveti siddham.
vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Anirvacanīya-k.english_close: Therefore, in an error-cognition (bhrānti), whatever at all appears. Of that entire thing, there is no pramāṇa establishing either being or non-being. Therefore, indeterminability (anirvacanīyatā) alone is established [as its character].

vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Jaḍe'py avidyā.locus: Iṣṭa-Siddhi, chapter 1, verse-conclusion 140, Jaḍe'py avidyāpādanam, printed p. 212
vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Jaḍe'py avidyā.sanskrit_iast:
tasmād ātmany evāvidyā,
ātmany eva ca,
na tv anātmano 'nātmani ceti siddham.
vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Jaḍe'py avidyā.english_close: Therefore, avidyā is in the Self alone. And it is also of the Self alone [as its locus and content]. It is not in a non-self, nor regarding a non-self. Thus it is established.

vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Jīvanmuktiḥ.sanskrit_iast:
ato jñānodayakāla eva vidvān vimuktaḥ.
aśeṣasaṃsārabījasya ajñānasya
ātmajñānāgninā aśeṣato dagdhatvāt,
jñānān mokṣo niravadya upapadyate.
vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Jīvanmuktiḥ.english_close: Therefore, at the very moment of the rise of knowledge (jñānodaya), the knower is liberated. The reason: ajñāna — the seed of the whole saṃsāra. It has been completely burned by the fire of self-knowledge. Therefore, liberation from [i.e., on the basis of] knowledge follows without defect.

vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/1.5.english_close: By self-valid āmnāyas. That is, [Vedas] devoid of an originating author. Reverently — that is, having infinite auspicious qualities thus declared. I worship the consort of Śrī.

vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/1.prāmāṇyavāda.locus: Tarka-Tāṇḍava, pariccheda 1, prāmāṇyavāda, inference for the intrinsic graspability of cognitive validity
vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/1.prāmāṇyavāda.sanskrit_iast:
jñānaprāmāṇyaṃ svato grāhyaṃ,
parato 'gṛhyamāṇatve sati gṛhyamāṇatvāt /
yadyad anyenāgṛhyamāṇatve sati grāhyaṃ
tat tena grāhyaṃ,
yathā cakṣur anyenāgṛhyamāṇaṃ rūpaṃ
cakṣurgrāhyam //
vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/1.prāmāṇyavāda.english_close: The validity of a cognition is intrinsically graspable. The reason: while not being grasped by anything else, it is nevertheless grasped. [General rule]: whatever, while not being grasped by another. Is [nevertheless] grasped. That is grasped by that very thing itself. Just as a visible form, not grasped by another instrument. Is grasped by the eye.

yamuna/siddhi-trayam/3.sanskrit_iast:
dehendriyamanaḥprāṇadhībhyo 'nyo 'nanyasādhanaḥ /
nityo vyāpī pratikṣetram ātmā bhinnaḥ svataḥ sukhī //
yamuna/siddhi-trayam/3.english_close: [The self is] other than body, senses, mind, vital breaths, and cognitions; and it does not need any other [instrument to manifest itself]. The self is eternal, pervasive, distinct in each bodily field (kṣetra), and by nature blissful.

yamuna/siddhi-trayam/16-18.sanskrit_iast:
pratyakṣaṃ tat prameyatvāt padārthatvād ghaṭādivat /
ekecchānuvidhāyīdam acaitanyāt svadehavat //
ekenābhiṣṭhitāḥ kāryaṃ kurvate sarvacetanāḥ /
dehasambandhasāpekṣakāryakṛttvāt tvagādivat //
ekapradhānapuruṣaṃ vivādādhyāsitaṃ jagat /
cetanācetanātmatvād ekarājakadeśavat //
yamuna/siddhi-trayam/16-18.english_close: That [world] is perceptible (pratyakṣa). [Why?] Because it is an object of knowledge, because it is an entity — just like a pot and the like. This [world] follows one will. [Why?] Because it is non-conscious, just like one's own body. All conscious beings, being controlled by one, perform action. [Why?] Because their agency depends on relation to a body, just like skin and the rest [perform action under the will of the embodied self]. The disputed world has one principal person (eka-pradhāna-puruṣa). [Why?] Because it consists of conscious and non-conscious components, just like a land with a single king.

yamuna/siddhi-trayam/31-35.sanskrit_iast:
tasmāt prapañcasadbhāvo nādvaitaśrutibādhitaḥ //
svapramāṇabalāt siddhaḥ śrutyā cāpy anumoditaḥ //
tenādvitīyaṃ brahmeti śruter artho 'yam ucyate //
dvitīyagaṇanāyogyo nāsīd asti bhaviṣyati //
samo vābhyadiko vāsya yo dvitīyas tu gaṇyate //
yamuna/siddhi-trayam/31-35.english_close: Therefore the real existence (sadbhāva) of the prapañca is not negated by the non-duality śruti. It is established by the force of its own pramāṇa. And it is also approved by śruti. Thus, this is said to be the meaning of the scriptural statement "Brahman is one-without-a-second". There did not exist, does not exist, and will not exist anything countable as a second [to Brahman] — whether equal to it or superior to it.

yamuna/stotra-ratna/22-24.sanskrit_iast:
na dharmaniṣṭho 'smi, na cātmavedī, na bhaktimāṃs tvaccaraṇāravinde |
akiñcano 'nanyagatiḥ śaraṇya ! tvatpādamūlaṃ śaraṇaṃ prapadye ||
na ninditaṃ karma tad asti loke sahasraśo yan na mayā vyadhāyi |
so 'haṃ vipākāvasare mukunda ! krandāmi sampraty agatis tavāgre ||
nimajjato 'nanta ! bhavārṇavāntaś cirāya me kūlam ivāsi labdhaḥ |
tvayāpi labdhaṃ bhagavann idānīm anuttamaṃ pātram idaṃ dayāyāḥ ||
yamuna/stotra-ratna/22-24.english_close: I am not one steadfast in dharma, nor a knower of the self, nor one possessed of devotion at your lotus-feet. Possessing nothing, with no other refuge, O refuge-giver, I take refuge in the root of your feet. There is no blameworthy deed in the world which I have not performed thousands of times. I, at the time of karmic fruition, O Mukunda, now cry out before you, bereft of recourse. For me, long sinking in the depth of the ocean of becoming, O Endless One, you are like a shore at last found; and by you too, O Lord, this unsurpassed vessel of compassion has now been found.

bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.sanskrit_iast: tad dha idaṃ tarhy avyākṛtam āsīt ।
bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.english_close: Then indeed this was in the *avyākṛta* (undifferentiated, not-yet-articulated) state.

bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.sanskrit_iast: atha yo 'nyāṃ devatām upāste 'nyo 'sāv anyo 'ham asmīti na sa veda ॥
bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.english_close: But whoever worships another deity, thinking, "He is one thing; I am another," that person does not know.
One should contemplate the self alone as the world.

bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.sanskrit_iast: dve vāva brahmaṇo rūpe mūrtaṃ caivāmūrtaṃ ca ।
bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.english_close: Brahman has indeed two forms: the formed and the formless.

bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.sanskrit_iast: ātmā vā are draṣṭavyaḥ śrotavyo mantavyo nididhyāsitavyaḥ ।
bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-.english_close: The self, dear one, is to be realized, to be heard about, to be reflected upon, and to be deeply meditated upon.

caitanya/shikshashtakam/1.sanskrit_iast:
ceto-darpaṇa-mārjanaṃ bhava-mahā-dāvāgni-nirvāpaṇaṃ
śreyaḥ-kairava-candrikā-vitaraṇaṃ vidyā-vadhū-jīvanam |
ānandāmbudhi-vardhanaṃ prati-padaṃ pūrṇāmṛtāsvādanaṃ
sarvātma-snapanaṃ paraṃ vijayate śrī-kṛṣṇa-saṅkīrtanam ||
caitanya/shikshashtakam/1.english_close: It cleanses the mirror of consciousness. It extinguishes the great forest-fire of worldly becoming. It diffuses the moonlight over the white lotus of blessedness. It is the life of the bride called knowledge. It enlarges the ocean of bliss. At every step it gives the tasting of full nectar. It bathes the whole self. May the congregational chanting of Śrī Kṛṣṇa be supremely victorious.

caitanya/shikshashtakam/2.sanskrit_iast:
nāmnām akāri bahudhā nija-sarva-śaktis
tatrārpitā niyamitaḥ smaraṇe na kālaḥ |
etādṛśī tava kṛpā bhagavan mamāpi
durdaivam īdṛśam ihājani nānurāgaḥ ||
caitanya/shikshashtakam/2.english_close: Of the names, there was made a multiplicity; into them your own entire power was placed. In remembering them, no fixed time has been imposed. Such is your grace, O Lord, even toward me. Yet such is my ill-fortune here: no attachment has arisen.

caitanya/shikshashtakam/3.sanskrit_iast:
tṛṇād api sunīcena taror iva sahiṣṇunā |
amāninā mānadena kīrtanīyaḥ sadā hariḥ ||
caitanya/shikshashtakam/3.english_close: By one more humble even than a blade of grass, and forbearing like a tree, by one who claims no honour and gives honour, Hari is always to be chanted.

caitanya/shikshashtakam/4.sanskrit_iast:
na dhanaṃ na janaṃ na sundarīṃ
kavitāṃ vā jagad-īśa kāmaye |
mama janmani janmanīśvare
bhavatād bhaktir ahaitukī tvayi ||
caitanya/shikshashtakam/4.english_close: I do not desire wealth, nor people, nor a beautiful woman, nor even poetic brilliance, O Lord of the world. O Lord, birth after birth, for me, let there be devotion to you that is without ulterior cause.

caitanya/shikshashtakam/5.sanskrit_iast:
ayi nanda-tanuja kiṅkaraṃ
patitaṃ māṃ viṣame bhavāmbudhau
kṛpayā tava pāda-paṅkaja-
sthita-dhūlī-sadṛśaṃ vicintaya ||
caitanya/shikshashtakam/5.english_close: O son of Nanda, me, your servant, fallen into the perilous ocean of becoming, by your grace, as resembling the dust resting upon your lotus-feet, regard me.

caitanya/shikshashtakam/6.sanskrit_iast:
nayanaṃ galad-aśru-dhārayā
vadanaṃ gadgada-ruddhayā girā |
pulakair nicitaṃ vapuḥ kadā
tava nāma-grahaṇe bhaviṣyati ||
caitanya/shikshashtakam/6.english_close: When, at the taking of your name, will my eyes be with a streaming current of tears, my mouth with a voice choked and stammering, and my body covered with horripilation? When will that come to be?

caitanya/shikshashtakam/7.sanskrit_iast:
yugāyitaṃ nimeṣeṇa
cakṣuṣā prāvṛṣāyitam |
śūnyāyitaṃ jagat sarvaṃ
govinda-viraheṇa me ||
caitanya/shikshashtakam/7.english_close: A blink has become like an age. My eye has become like the rainy season. The whole world has become void. Such is it for me in separation from Govinda.

caitanya/shikshashtakam/8.sanskrit_iast:
āśliṣya vā pāda-ratāṃ pinaṣṭu mām
adarśanān marma-hatāṃ karotu vā |
yathā tathā vā vidadhātu lampaṭo
mat-prāṇa-nāthas tu sa eva nāparaḥ ||
caitanya/shikshashtakam/8.english_close: Let that libertine embrace me, or let him crush me under his feet. Or, by not appearing, let him make me stricken in the vital core. Let him act in whatever manner whatever. Yet he alone, and no other, is the lord of my life-breath.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.1.sanskrit_iast:
munīndrajāḥ śrāvakamadhyabuddhāḥ
buddhodbhavāḥ khalv api bodhisattvāt |
kāruṇyacittādvayabuddhibodhicittāni hetur jinaputrakāṇām ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.1.english_close: The disciples and the intermediate buddhas are born from the Lords of Sages. The Buddhas too, indeed, arise from a bodhisattva. Compassionate mind, non-dual cognition, and the awakening-mind are the cause of the Victor's sons.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.2.sanskrit_iast:
bījaṃ kṛpā yajjinaśasyarāśeḥ
tadvṛddhaye vārisamā cirāya |
bhogāya pākaś ca yathaiva mānyaḥ,
ādau mamātaḥ karuṇāpraśaṃsā ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.2.english_close: Compassion is the seed of the mass of the Victor's crop. For its growth, it is like water, and that for a long time. For enjoyment, it is like ripening, and so worthy of honor. Therefore my praise of compassion comes first.
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.2.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/candrakirti_madhyamakavatara.txt).

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.3.sanskrit_iast:
ātmābhisaktau tv aham eti pūrvaṃ
rāgodbhave bhāva idaṃ mameti |
arhaṭṭacaryāvadadhīnaloke
kāruṇyavān yo'sti namo'stu tasmai ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.3.english_close: In attachment to self, first there arises the thought, "I." When passion arises, with respect to things there arises, "This is mine." As for the world, dependent on the course of the water-wheel, homage be to the one who is compassionate toward it.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.4.sanskrit_iast:
yad asya citte khalu bodhisattve
jagad vimuktyai karuṇāvaśaṃge |
samantabhadrapraṇidhānanāmni,
pramoditā sā prathameti avasthitā ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.4.english_close: When, in this bodhisattva's mind, held under the sway of compassion for the liberation of the world, there is what bears the name "Samantabhadra-vow," that first state is established as *Pramuditā*.
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.4.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/candrakirti_madhyamakavatara.txt).

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.5.sanskrit_iast:
tataḥ samārabhya tu tatra prāptaḥ
sa bodhisattva iti padābhidheyaḥ ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.5.english_close: From that point onward, once he has arrived there, he is designated by the word "bodhisattva."

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.6.sanskrit_iast:
gotre 'pi bhāvo 'sya tathāgatānāṃ
tyaktaṃ trisaṃyojanam asya sarvam |
prāmoditāṃ prāpya sa bodhisattvaḥ
kṣobdhuṃ samarthaḥ śatalokadhātum ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.6.english_close: He has also entered the lineage of the Tathāgatas. His entire triad of fetters has been abandoned. Having attained *Prāmoditā*, that bodhisattva is capable of shaking a hundred world-systems.
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.6.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/candrakirti_madhyamakavatara.txt).

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.7.sanskrit_iast:
prayāti bhūmeḥ khalu bhūmim ūrdhvaṃ
tadāsya mārgo kugater niruddhaḥ |
pṛthagjanasyāvanisaṃkṣayo'sti
yathāṣṭamāryaḥ kathitas tatha eṣaḥ ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.7.english_close: He goes upward from one ground to a higher ground. Then his path to a bad destiny is blocked. The ground of the ordinary person has been exhausted. This one is said to be like the eighth noble person.
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.7.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/candrakirti_madhyamakavatara.txt).

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.8.sanskrit_iast:
sambodhicittodaye ādike'pi
pratyekabuddhān samunīndravāgjān |
jitvedhate puṇyabalena cāpi
dūraṅgamāyāṃ tu dhiyādhikaḥ syāt ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.8.english_close: Even at the initial arising of the mind of perfect awakening, he surpasses the pratyekabuddhas together with those born from the Sage-Lord's speech. Having overcome them, he flourishes by the power of merit. But on the *Dūraṅgamā* ground he becomes superior in understanding.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.9.sanskrit_iast:
tadātra sambuddhasubodhihetur
bhavet pradhānaṃ hy atireki dānam |
svamāṃsadāne'pi kṛtādaratvād
bhaved adṛṣṭe'py anumānahetuḥ ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.9.english_close: Here, then, the principal and surpassing cause of a Buddha's perfect awakening is generosity. Since they show earnestness even in the giving of their own flesh, it becomes an inferential sign even for what is not directly seen.
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.9.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/candrakirti_madhyamakavatara.txt).

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.10.sanskrit_iast:
sukhābhilāṣī hi janas tu sarvaḥ
sukhaṃ na sampattim apāsya loke |
dhanaṃ tu dānodgatam eva buddhvā
muniḥ pradhānaṃ samuvāca dānam ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.10.english_close: Every person indeed desires happiness. In the world, one does not obtain happiness apart from prosperity. Knowing that wealth arises only from giving, the Sage declared generosity to be primary.
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.10.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/candrakirti_madhyamakavatara.txt).

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.11.sanskrit_iast:
parīttakāruṇyasuduṣṭacittā
vikurvate svārthaparā amī ye |
tadiṣṭasampad vyasanapraśāntyai
samudgatā dānata eva sāpi ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.11.english_close: Even these, whose compassion is slight and whose minds are badly stained, who are intent on their own good, do act in this way. That desired prosperity, for the pacification of misfortune, has arisen from giving alone, even in their case.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.12.sanskrit_iast:
kadācid asminn api dānakāle
drutaṃ hi labdhvāryajanābhisaṅgam |
tataḥ samucchidya bhavapravāhaṃ
sahetukāṃ śāntim ataḥ prayāti ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.12.english_close: Sometimes, even at such a moment of giving, quickly obtaining association with noble persons, one cuts off the stream of becoming, and from that goes to peace together with its causes.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.13.sanskrit_iast:
jagaddhitārthaṃ hi kṛtapratijñāḥ
prayānti modaṃ na cireṇa dānaiḥ ||
dayādayā bhāvamayā yataś ca
sarvābhyudayaniḥśreyasahetur dānam |
tato'sti mūlaṃ khalu dānavārtā ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.13.english_close: Those who have made their vow for the welfare of the world soon come to joy through acts of giving. And because they are constituted by compassion and the like, and because giving is the cause of all flourishing and the highest good, therefore the discourse on giving is indeed fundamental.
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.13.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/candrakirti_madhyamakavatara.txt).

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.14.sanskrit_iast:
yathā tu dehīti niśamya śabdaṃ
sukhodbhavo buddhasute vicintya |
tathā sukhaṃ śāntigate munau na
kim ucyatāṃ sarvasamarpaṇena ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.14.english_close: Consider the joy that arises in a Buddha-son merely on hearing the word, "Give." Such happiness is not found even in a sage who has gone to peace. What then need be said of complete self-offering?

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.15.sanskrit_iast:
pradāya chettuṃ svavapuḥ svaduḥkhāt
svasaṃvidā nārakaduḥkhakādi |
vilokya tannāśayituṃ pareṣāṃ
pariśramaṃ prārabhate sa śīghram ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.15.english_close: Having given his own body to be cut, and from his own pain personally knowing something like the suffering of hell, seeing that, and wishing to destroy it for others, he quickly begins strenuous effort.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.16.sanskrit_iast:
pradeyasaṅgrāhakadātṛśūnyaṃ
vadanti lokottarapāramitīm |
trayīṣu rāgodbhavataḥ pradiṣṭaṃ
tadeva vai laukikapāramitīm ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.16.english_close: That which is empty of the gift, the recipient, and the giver they call the supramundane perfection. That very same thing, when taught as arising through attachment to the triad, is indeed called the worldly perfection.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.17.sanskrit_iast:
tathā pratiṣṭhā jinaputracitte
sadāśraye suprabhakāntim āptā |
ghanaṃ tamisraṃ muditā nirasya
jayaty asau candramaṇir yathā vai ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.17.english_close: Thus established in the mind of a Victor's son, resting on a constant support and having attained splendid radiance, that Joyous Ground, having dispelled dense darkness, shines victorious like a moon-gem.
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.17.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/candrakirti_madhyamakavatara.txt).

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.1.sanskrit_iast: pramāṇaṃ dvividhaṃ meyadvaividhyāc chaktyaśaktitaḥ / arthakriyāyāṃ keśādir nārtho 'narthādhimokṣataḥ //
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.1.english_close: A *pramāṇa* (means of valid cognition) is twofold, because the *meya* (the knowable object) is twofold, in virtue of efficacy and inefficacy. With respect to *arthakriyā* (causal efficacy), hairs and the like are not objects, because there is no settled taking of them as objects.
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.1.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/dharmakirti_pramana_varttika.txt).

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.2.sanskrit_iast: sadṛśāsadṛśatvācca viṣayāviṣayatvataḥ / śabdasyānyanimittānāṃ bhāve dhīḥ sadasattvataḥ //
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.2.english_close: And because there is similarity and dissimilarity, and because there is objecthood and non-objecthood, and because, when a word and other auxiliary causes are present, cognition occurs or does not occur.

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.3.sanskrit_iast: arthakriyāsamarthaṃ yat tad atra paramārthasat / anyat saṃvṛtisat proktaṃ te sva-sāmānyalakṣaṇe //
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.3.english_close: Whatever is capable of causal efficacy, that here is *paramārthasat* (ultimately real). The other is said to be *saṃvṛtisat* (conventionally real). Those two are the particular and the general characteristic.
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.3.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/dharmakirti_pramana_varttika.txt).

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.4.sanskrit_iast: aśaktaṃ sarvam iti ced bījāder aṅkurādiṣu / dṛṣṭā śaktir matā sā cet saṃvṛtyāstu yathā tathā //
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.4.english_close: If it is said that everything is inefficacious, then consider sprouts and the like from seeds and the like. If a power there is admitted because it is observed, then let it stand conventionally, however that may be.
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.4.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/dharmakirti_pramana_varttika.txt).

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.5.sanskrit_iast: sāsti sarvatra ced buddher nānvayavyatirekayoḥ / sāmānyalakṣaṇe 'dṛṣṭeḥ cakṣūrūpādibuddhivat //
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.5.english_close: If that power exists everywhere, then there is no positive and negative concomitance for cognition. For the general characteristic is not observed, just as an eye-cognition [does not apprehend a universal apart from] color and the like.
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.5.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/dharmakirti_pramana_varttika.txt).

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.13.sanskrit_iast:
dvaitasyāgrahaṇaṃ tulyam ubhayoḥ prājñaturyayoḥ
bījanidrāyutaḥ prājñaḥ sā ca turye na vidyate
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.13.english_close: In both, the non-apprehension of duality is the same, namely in the *prājña* and in the *turīya*. But the *prājña* is joined to seed-sleep; and that is not present in the *turīya*.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.14.sanskrit_iast:
svapnanidrāyutāv ādyau prājñas tv asvapnanidrayā
na nidrāṃ naiva ca svapnaṃ turye paśyanti niścitāḥ
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.14.english_close: The first two are joined to dream and sleep; but the *prājña* is joined to dreamless sleep. Those who have ascertained the matter do not see either sleep or dream in the *turīya*.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.15.sanskrit_iast:
anyathā gṛhṇataḥ svapno nidrā tattvam ajānataḥ
viparyāse tayoḥ kṣīṇe turīyaṃ padam aśnute
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.15.english_close: Dream belongs to one who grasps otherwise; sleep belongs to one who does not know the reality. When the inversion constituted by those two has been exhausted, one attains the state called *turīya*.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.16.sanskrit_iast:
anādimāyayā supto yadā jīvaḥ prabudhyate
ajam anidram asvapnam advaitaṃ budhyate tadā
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.16.english_close: When the *jīva* (the embodied living self), asleep through beginningless *māyā* (cognitive projection), awakens, then it knows the unborn, sleepless, dreamless, non-dual.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.17.sanskrit_iast:
prapañco yadi vidyeta nivarteta na saṃśayaḥ
māyāmātram idaṃ dvaitam advaitaṃ paramārthataḥ
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.17.english_close: If the manifold world truly existed, it would cease; of this there is no doubt. This duality is mere *māyā*; in ultimate truth, there is non-duality.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.18.sanskrit_iast:
vikalpo vinivarteta kalpito yadi kenacit
upadeśād ayaṃ vādo jñāte dvaitaṃ na vidyate
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.18.english_close: A conceptual construction would cease, if it had been projected by someone. This discourse is for instruction; when That is known, duality is not found.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.1.sanskrit_iast:
vaitathyaṃ sarvabhāvānāṃ svapne āhur manīṣiṇaḥ
antaḥsthānāt tu bhāvānāṃ saṃvṛtatvena hetunā
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.1.english_close: The wise declare the *vaitathya* (falsity disclosed by analysis) of all entities in dream. They do so because those entities are internally situated, and because they are enclosed: that is the reason.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.2.sanskrit_iast:
adīrghatvāc ca kālasya gatvā deśān na paśyati
pratibuddhaś ca vai sarvas tasmin deśe na vidyate
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.2.english_close: And because the time is not long, one does not, having actually gone there, see those places. And, once awakened, the whole of that is not found in that place.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.3.sanskrit_iast:
abhāvaś ca rathādīnāṃ śrūyate nyāyapūrvakam
vaitathyaṃ tena vai prāptaṃ svapne āhuḥ prakāśitam
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.3.english_close: And the non-existence of chariots and the like is taught, with reasoning standing before it. By that, they say, unreality in dream is indeed established and made manifest.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.4.sanskrit_iast:
antaḥsthānāt tu bhedānāṃ tathā jāgarite smṛtam
yathā tatra tathā svapne saṃvṛtatvaṃ na bhidyate
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.4.english_close: But from the inward location of differentiated objects, the same is held with regard to waking as well. As there, so in dream here, the condition of enclosure does not differ.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.5.sanskrit_iast:
svapnajāgaritasthāne hy ekam āhur manīṣiṇaḥ
bhedānāṃ hi samatvena prasiddhenaiva hetunā
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.5.english_close: The wise indeed say that dream-state and waking-state are one. They say so by the well-known reason, namely the parity of the differentiations.
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.5.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/gaudapada_mandukya_karika_gretil.txt).

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.6.sanskrit_iast:
ādāv ante ca yan nāsti vartamāne 'pi tat tathā
vitathaiḥ sadṛśāḥ santo avitathā iva lakṣitāḥ
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.6.english_close: What is not there at the beginning and at the end is likewise not there even in the middle present. Though being similar to unreal things, they are nevertheless perceived as if they were not unreal.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.1.sanskrit_iast:
upāsanāśrito dharmo jāte brahmaṇi vartate
prāg utpatter ajaṃ sarvaṃ tenāsau kṛpaṇaḥ smṛtaḥ
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.1.english_close: A discipline resting on *upāsanā* operates where Brahman is taken as having come into being. Before origination, all is unborn; therefore that person is regarded as a spiritual pauper.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.2.sanskrit_iast:
ato vakṣyāmy akārpaṇyam ajāti samatāṃ gatam
yathā na jāyate kiṃcij jāyamānaṃ samantataḥ
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.2.english_close: Therefore I shall set forth non-wretchedness: the unborn, gone to sameness. I shall show how nothing whatsoever, though appearing to be born on every side, is in fact not born.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.3.sanskrit_iast:
ātmā hy ākāśavaj jīvair ghaṭākāśair ivoditaḥ
ghaṭādivac ca saṃghātair jātāv etan nidarśanam
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.3.english_close: The Self, indeed, is spoken of by the *jīvas* as though like space, as though by pot-spaces. And, with aggregates such as pots, this serves as the illustration for origination.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.4.sanskrit_iast:
ghaṭādiṣu pralīneṣu ghaṭākāśādayo yathā
ākāśe saṃpralīyante tadvaj jīvā ihātmani
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.4.english_close: Just as, when pots and the like are dissolved, pot-space and the like dissolve back into space, so likewise do the *jīvas* here in the Self.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.5.sanskrit_iast:
yathaikasmin ghaṭākāśe rajodhūmādibhir yute
na sarve saṃprayujyante tadvaj jīvāḥ sukhādibhiḥ
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.5.english_close: Just as, when one pot-space is associated with dust, smoke, and the like, not all are thereby associated; so likewise the *jīvas* are not all associated with pleasure and the like.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.6.sanskrit_iast:
rūpakāryasamākhyāś ca bhidyante tatra tatra vai
ākāśasya na bhedo 'sti tadvaj jīveṣu nirṇayaḥ
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.6.english_close: Forms, functions, and names indeed differ from case to case there. But space has no difference; and so too the settled conclusion holds with regard to the *jīvas*.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.7.sanskrit_iast:
nākāśasya ghaṭākāśo vikārāvayavau yathā
naivātmanaḥ sadā jīvo vikārāvayavau tathā
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.7.english_close: Pot-space is not, in relation to space, either a modification or a part. So too, the *jīva* is never, in relation to the Self, either a modification or a part.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.8.sanskrit_iast:
yathā bhavati bālānāṃ gaganaṃ malinaṃ malaiḥ
tathā bhavaty abuddhānām ātmāpi malino malaiḥ
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.8.english_close: Just as, for children, the sky appears stained by impurities, so too, for the undiscerning, the Self itself appears stained by impurities.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.9.sanskrit_iast:
maraṇe saṃbhave caiva gatyāgamanayor api
sthitau sarvaśarīreṣu ākāśenāvilakṣaṇaḥ
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.9.english_close: In death and in birth, and also in going and coming, and in abiding throughout all bodies, it is not different from space.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.10.sanskrit_iast:
saṃghātāḥ svapnavat sarve ātmamāyāvisarjitāḥ
ādhikye sarvasāmye vā nopapattir hi vidyate
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.10.english_close: All aggregates, like dream-forms, are projected forth by the Self's *māyā*. Whether in superiority or in complete equality, no explanatory coherence is found.
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.10.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/gaudapada_mandukya_karika_gretil.txt).

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.11.sanskrit_iast:
rasādayo hi ye kośā vyākhyātās taittirīyake
teṣāṃ ātmā paro jīvaḥ khaṃ yathā saṃprakāśitaḥ
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.11.english_close: The sheaths beginning with the essence-body, which are indeed explained in the *Taittirīya*, have as their Self the higher *jīva*; it has been shown to be like space.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.12.sanskrit_iast:
dvayor dvayor madhujñāne paraṃ brahma prakāśitam
pṛthivyām udare caiva yathākāśaḥ prakāśitaḥ
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.12.english_close: In the *Madhu* teaching, from pair to pair, the supreme Brahman is made manifest. It is made manifest as space is made manifest in the earth and in the belly.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.13.sanskrit_iast:
jīvātmanor ananyatvam abhedena praśasyate
nānātvaṃ nindyate yac ca tad evaṃ hi samañjasam
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.13.english_close: The non-otherness of *jīva* and Self is praised, in the mode of non-difference. And plurality is censured; and this is indeed coherent in just this way.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.14.sanskrit_iast:
jīvātmanoḥ pṛthaktvaṃ yat prāg utpatteḥ prakīrtitam
bhaviṣyadvṛttyā gauṇaṃ tan mukhyatvaṃ hi na yujyate
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.14.english_close: The separateness of *jīva* and Self which is spoken of prior to origination, that is secondary, spoken by reference to a future course; its literal primacy does not stand.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.15.sanskrit_iast:
mṛllohavisphuliṅgādyaiḥ sṛṣṭir yā coditānyathā
upāyaḥ so 'vatārāya nāsti bhedaḥ kathaṃcana
