madhva/tattvodyota/Pramāṇābhāvopapādanam.english_close: Demonstration of the absence of any *pramāṇa* for the indefinable.

madhva/tattvodyota/Means of Knowledge.english_close: And there is no means of knowledge whatsoever for the indefinable.

madhva/tattvodyota/Word *Mithyā* Does No.english_close: And here too, the claim that the word *mithyā* says “the indefinable” does not help.

madhva/tattvodyota/Pakṣa Bears an Unesta.locus: The Pakṣa Bears an Unestablished Predicate
madhva/tattvodyota/Pakṣa Bears an Unesta.english_close: And, for the inference, the subject-term (*pakṣa*) bears an unestablished qualifier.

madhva/tattvodyota/Shared Universal of *.sanskrit_iast: na ca dhūmatvavat mithyātvam ity ubhayasampratipannaṃ sāmānyam asti।
madhva/tattvodyota/Shared Universal of *.english_close: Nor is there, as there is in the case of smokiness, any universally admitted common property called “*mithyātva*.”

madhva/tattvodyota/Does Not Confer Smoki.english_close: Nor does smokiness deviate by reason of smoke superimposed upon steam.

madhva/tattvodyota/Āgamanirāsaḥ.english_close: Rejection of scriptural testimony as support.

madhva/tattvodyota/Scripture Teaches a C.locus: No Scripture Teaches a Category Beyond Being and Non-Being
madhva/tattvodyota/Scripture Teaches a C.sanskrit_iast: na ca bhāvābhāvavilakṣaṇaṃ kvacid āgamo vakti।
madhva/tattvodyota/Scripture Teaches a C.english_close: And no scripture anywhere speaks of something distinct from both being and non-being.
madhva/tattvodyota/Scripture Teaches a C.source: full_translations/madhva__tattvodyota.md#No Scripture Teaches a Category Beyond Being and Non-Being

madhva/tattvodyota/Iti Śrutyarthavicāraḥ.english_close: Examination of the scriptural meaning of “There was not non-being.”

madhva/tattvodyota/10.129.1.sanskrit_iast: “nāsad āsīn no sad āsīt tadānīṃ nāsīd rajo no vyomā paro yat”
madhva/tattvodyota/10.129.1.english_close: “Then there was not non-being; nor was there being. There was not the aerial expanse; nor that which was beyond the sky.”

madhva/tattvodyota/Closure.english_close: And here too this is from *Ṛgveda-saṃhitā* 10.129.1.

madhva/tattvodyota/One Grants Indefinabi.locus: If One Grants Indefinability by Remainder
madhva/tattvodyota/One Grants Indefinabi.english_close: If indefinability is granted by *pāriśeṣya* (elimination of the remainder).

madhva/tattvodyota/10.129.2.english_close: “That One breathed, windless, by its own power.”

madhva/tattvodyota/of the Remainder Argu.sanskrit_iast: iti tadānīṃ pariśiṣṭatvād brahmaṇa evānirvacanīyatvaṃ syāt।
madhva/tattvodyota/of the Remainder Argu.english_close: Thus, since at that time only Brahman would remain as the residue, indefinability would belong to Brahman alone.

madhva/tattvodyota/Citation.sanskrit_iast: “mūrtaṃ sad iti samproktam amūrtaṃ asad ucyate। mūrtāmūrtetarad brahma na sat tan nāsad ucyate॥”
madhva/tattvodyota/Citation.english_close: “The formed is said to be being; the formless is called non-being. Brahman, which is other than both formed and formless, is not called being; nor is it called non-being.”

madhva/upadhi-khandana/1.english_close: May Nārāyaṇa be propitious, he whose very form is the sole eternal abode of innumerable qualities; he is devoid of every defect without remainder, and is the abode of Kamalā.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/2.sanskrit_iast:
ajñatā 'khila-saṃvettur ghaṭate na kutaścana
upādhi-bhedād ghaṭata iti cet sa svabhāvataḥ // 2 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/2.english_close: Ignorance cannot obtain, from any quarter whatsoever, in one who knows all. If it is said to obtain because of a difference in conditioning adjuncts, then that difference is intrinsic.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/3.sanskrit_iast:
ajñānato vā dvaitasya satyatā svata eva cet
anavasthitir ajñāna-hetau vānyonya-siddhitā // 3 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/3.english_close: Or if the reality of duality is due to ignorance, or else if it is from itself alone, then there is regress in making ignorance the cause, or there is mutual dependence.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/4.sanskrit_iast:
cakrakāpattir athavā bhedaś copādhitaḥ kutaḥ
vidyamānasya bhedasya jñāpako naiva kārakaḥ // 4 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/4.english_close: Otherwise there is circularity; or again, how can difference arise from an adjunct? What makes known a difference already present is not, in that very respect, its producer.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/5.sanskrit_iast:
upādhi-dṛṣṭa-pūrvo hi khe 'pi deśāntarasya saḥ
jñāpako vidyamānasya mūḍha-buddhi-vyapekṣayā // 5 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/5.english_close: An adjunct is indeed something already observed, even in the case of another region of space. It is an indicator of what is already there, and that only with reference to a confused understanding.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/6.sanskrit_iast:
na ced upādhi-sambandha ekadeśe 'tha sarvagaḥ
ekadeśe 'navasthā syāt sarvagaś cen na bhedakaḥ // 6 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/6.english_close: And if the relation to the adjunct is either localised in one part or else all-pervasive, then, if localised, there is regress; and if all-pervasive, it is not a differentiator.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/7.sanskrit_iast:
sukha-duḥkhādi-bhogaś ca svarūpaikye na bhedakaḥ
dṛśyo 'py upādhi-bhede 'pi hasta-pādādigo yathā // 7 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/7.english_close: And experience of pleasure, pain, and the like would not, on a thesis of essential unity, establish difference. For difference through adjuncts is observed even where the substrate is one, as in hands, feet, and the like.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/8.sanskrit_iast:
nānā-dehaga-bhogānusandhānaṃ yogino yathā
na ced bhogānusandhānaṃ tad-icchā yoginaḥ kutaḥ // 8 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/8.english_close: The yogin, as is well known, has an awareness-linking of enjoyments occurring in many bodies. If there were no such linking of experiences, then whence would come the yogin's wish for that [many-bodied condition]?

madhva/upadhi-khandana/9.sanskrit_iast:
anusandhāna-rahita-deha-bāhulyam anyathā
siddham eva hi tat-pakṣe viśeṣo yoginaḥ kutaḥ // 9 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/9.english_close: Otherwise, there would simply be a multiplicity of bodies lacking any connective awareness. But that is already conceded on that view; then what distinctive feature would remain for the yogin?

madhva/upadhi-khandana/10.sanskrit_iast:
siddhau hi karma-bhedasya syād upādhi-vibhinnatā
tat-siddhau caiva tat-siddhir ity anyonya-vyapāśrayaḥ // 10 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/10.english_close: For if difference of karmas were first established, there would then be difference of adjuncts. But if that is what establishes this, and this what establishes that, there is mutual dependence.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/11.sanskrit_iast:
ātma-svabhāva-bhedasya vidoṣatvena cākhilaḥ
pratyakṣādi-virodhāc ca duṣṭaḥ pakṣo 'yam añjasā // 11 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/11.english_close: Since difference belongs to the self's own nature, and since it is free from defect, this entire thesis, and since it also conflicts with perception and the other means of knowledge, is plainly unsound.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/12.sanskrit_iast:
ceṣṭā-liṅgena sātmatve para-dehasya sādhite
anyatvaṃ svātmanas tasmāt sarvair evānubhūyate // 12 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/12.english_close: When, by the sign of activity, the presence of a self in another body has been established, then the otherness of that self from one's own self is, from that very fact, experienced by everyone.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/13.sanskrit_iast:
ajñatā cālpa-śaktitvaṃ duḥkhitvaṃ svalpa-kartṛtā
sarvajñatādi-īśa-guṇa-viruddhā hy anubhūtigāḥ // 13 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/13.english_close: Ignorance, limited power, suffering, and slight agency, these experienced features are indeed opposed to the Lord's qualities such as omniscience.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/14.sanskrit_iast:
sārvajñyādi-guṇā viṣṇoḥ śrutiṣu pratipāditāḥ
satyo 'sya mahimety-ādi-vākyān mṛṣā na ca // 14 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/14.english_close: Qualities such as omniscience are taught, in the śrutis, of Viṣṇu. And the scriptural sentences beginning "his greatness is real" are not false.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/15.sanskrit_iast:
na ca vedokta-mithyātve mānaṃ tan-mānatāpi na
ato 'jñāna-sambhavād eva nādhikāro 'aikya-vādinām // 15 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/15.english_close: Nor is there any valid means of knowledge for the doctrine of scripturally stated falsity; not even for the claim that such a means of knowledge exists. Therefore, since ignorance cannot arise at all, the proponents of identity have no standing.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/16.sanskrit_iast:
ato nājñātam iti ca viṣayo viṣayāntare
ajñābhāvāt phalaṃ kasya yogaḥ śaśa-nṛśṛṅgayoḥ // 16 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/16.english_close: Therefore the claim "it is not known" must concern some other object. In the absence of an ignorancer, for whom would there be any result? The conjunction would be like that of a hare and a human horn.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/17.sanskrit_iast:
durghaṭatvaṃ bhūṣaṇaṃ cet syād avidyātvam ātmanaḥ
andhaṃ tamo 'py alaṅkāro nitya-duḥkhaṃ śiromaṇiḥ // 17 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/17.english_close: If mere hard-to-explain-ness were an ornament, then the self's very being ignorance would follow [as something commendable]. Blind darkness too would be an ornament, and perpetual suffering a crest-jewel.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/18.sanskrit_iast:
ataḥ paro mātrayeti pūrva-śruti-nidarśitaḥ
anyam īśam iti śrutyā bhinna-jīva-dṛśaṃ gataḥ
bhinnatvenātma-sādṛśya-do yad eti śruteḥ sadā // 18 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/18.english_close: Therefore the higher one is indicated by the earlier śruti that speaks of him as beyond measure. By the śruti saying "the Lord is other," he is understood as appearing distinct from the individual self. And the śruti that speaks of attaining likeness to that self always gives resemblance precisely on the basis of difference.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/19.sanskrit_iast:
māyāvāda-tamo-vyāptam iti tattva-dṛśā jagat
bhātaṃ sarvajña-sūryeṇa prītaye śrīpateḥ sadā // 19 //
madhva/upadhi-khandana/19.english_close: The world, having been pervaded by the darkness of Māyāvāda, has been illumined by the sun of the all-knowing seer, always for the delight of the Lord of Śrī.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/20.english_close: Reverence to him whose beautiful form is dense with unobstructed intrinsic bliss; to the husband of Indirā, the giver of participation in eternal bliss.

madhva/upadhi-khandana/Colophon.english_close: Thus the *Upādhi-Khaṇḍana*, composed by the venerable teacher Ānandatīrtha Bhagavatpāda, is complete.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.1.sanskrit_iast:
*varjitaḥ sarvadoṣair yo guṇasarvasvamūrtimān |
svatantro yadvaśāḥ sarve sa viṣṇuḥ paramo mataḥ ||
iti paramopaniṣadi |*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.1.english_close: He who is devoid of all defects, whose very embodiment is the whole treasure of qualities, under whose control all beings stand, that Viṣṇu is held to be supreme. Thus in the *Paramopaniṣad*.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.2.sanskrit_iast:
*nityapūrṇākhilaguṇo nirdoṣaḥ sarvadaiva yaḥ |
svatantraḥ paramo viṣṇur janmamṛtyādivarjitaḥ ||*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.2.english_close: He who is eternally full in all qualities, and faultless at all times without exception, that supreme and independent Viṣṇu is devoid of birth, death, and the rest.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.4.sanskrit_iast:
*nirdoṣaś cet kathaṃ viṣṇur mānuṣeṣūdapadyata |
cintāśramavraṇājñānaduḥkhayug dṛśyate katham ||*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.4.english_close: If Viṣṇu is faultless, then how did he arise among human beings? How is he seen as joined with anxiety, weariness, wounds, ignorance, and suffering?

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.5.sanskrit_iast:
*eṣa me saṃśayo brahman hṛdi śalya ivārpitaḥ |
anuddhāryo'parair martyaiḥ sūktiśaktyā tam uddhara ||*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.5.english_close: This doubt of mine, O Brahman, is lodged in my heart like a barb. It cannot be extracted by other mortals; draw it out by the power of right utterance.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.7.sanskrit_iast:
*strīpuṃmalābhiyogātmadeho viṣṇor na jāyate |
kintu nirdoṣacaitanyasukhāṃ nityāṃ svakāṃ tanum ||*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.7.english_close: For Viṣṇu, a body constituted by conjunction with female and male impurity does not come to be. Rather, [there is] his own eternal body of faultless consciousness and bliss.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.8.sanskrit_iast:
*prakāśayati saiveyaṃ janir viṣṇor na cāparā |
tathāpy asuramohāya pareṣāṃ ca kvacit kvacit ||*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.8.english_close: He manifests it; this alone is Viṣṇu’s birth, and not another kind. Even so, for the delusion of Asuras, and at times also of others, [he appears otherwise].

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.9.sanskrit_iast:
*duḥkhājñānabhramādīn sa darśayec chuddhasadguṇaḥ |
kva vraṇādi kva cājñānaṃ svatantrācintyasadguṇe ||*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.9.english_close: That one, though possessed of pure and auspicious qualities, may display suffering, ignorance, delusion, and the like. But where are wounds and the rest, and where ignorance, in one who is independent and of inconceivable auspicious qualities?

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.10.sanskrit_iast:
*daurlabhyāyaiva mokṣasya darśayet tāny ajo hariḥ |
mithyādarśanadoṣeṇa tena muktiṃ na yānti ca ||*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.10.english_close: Unborn Hari may display those things precisely in order to show the difficulty of liberation. And, because of the fault of false seeing in that matter, people do not attain liberation.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.11.sanskrit_iast:
*tamo yānti ca tenaiva tasmād doṣavivarjitam |
prādurbhāvagataṃ caiva jānīyād viṣṇum añjasā ||
iti brahmāṇḍe |*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.11.english_close: And by that very error they go to darkness. Therefore one should understand Viṣṇu, straightforwardly, as free from defect, and as one whose “birth” consists in manifestation. Thus in the *Brahmāṇḍa*.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.12.sanskrit_iast:
*guṇakriyādayo viṣṇoḥ svarūpaṃ nānyad iṣyate |
ato mitho'pi bhedo na teṣāṃ kvacit kadācana ||*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.12.english_close: Qualities, actions, and the rest of Viṣṇu are held to be his very essence, not something else. Therefore, between them there is never anywhere a mutual difference of the kind that would make them alien to him.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.13.sanskrit_iast:
*svarūpe'pi viśeṣo'sti svarūpatvavad eva tu |
bhedābhāve'pi tenaiva vyavahāraś ca sarvataḥ ||
iti mahopaniṣadi |*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.13.english_close: Even in an essence there is differentiating specification, just as there is essentiality itself. Even where hostile difference is absent, practical discourse proceeds by means of that very specification everywhere. Thus in the *Mahopaniṣad*.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.14.sanskrit_iast:
*abhinnatvam abhedaś ca yathā bhedavivarjitam |
vyavahāryaṃ pṛthak ca syād evaṃ sarve guṇā hareḥ ||*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.14.english_close: Just as non-difference and undividedness are free from difference, yet remain separately available for discourse, so too are all the qualities of Hari.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.15.sanskrit_iast:
*abhedābhinnayor bhedo yadi vā bhedabhinnayoḥ |
anavasthitir eva syān na viśeṣaṇatām atiḥ ||*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.15.english_close: If there were a difference between non-difference and non-separatedness, or between things distinct through difference, there would be only infinite regress; the mind would not reach the status of explanatory specification.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.16.sanskrit_iast:
*mūlasambandham ajñātvā tasmād ekam anantadhā |
vyavahāryaṃ viśeṣeṇa dustarkabalato hareḥ ||*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.16.english_close: Not understanding the fundamental relation, one therefore treats the one Hari as manifold in endless ways, and fit for differentiated discourse by recourse to forced bad reasoning.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.17.sanskrit_iast:
*viśeṣo'pi svarūpaṃ sa svanirvāhakatāsya ca |
iti brahmatarke |*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.17.english_close: Specification too is that very essence, and also the principle by which it sustains itself. Thus in the *Brahma-Tarka*.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.18.english_close: That is one alone, without a second.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.19.english_close: Here there is no plurality whatsoever.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.20.sanskrit_iast: mṛtyoḥ sa mṛtyum āpnoti ya iha nāneva paśyati |
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.20.english_close: He goes from death to death who here sees, as it were, plurality.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.21.sanskrit_iast:
*yathodakaṃ durge vṛṣṭaṃ parvateṣu vidhāvati |
evaṃ dharmān pṛthak paśyaṃs tān evānu vidhāvati ||
ityādiśruteś ca |*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.21.english_close: Just as water, when rained down on rough ground, runs off among the mountains, so one who sees dharmas as separate runs only after those very things. And so too from this and similar *śruti* passages.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.22.sanskrit_iast:
*deśaḥ sarvatra puruṣaḥ svatantraḥ kālanityatā |
ityādiṣu svasambandho yathaiva guṇarūpiṇaḥ ||*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.22.english_close: In expressions such as “space everywhere,” “the person independent,” and “eternity in time,” there is self-relation in just the same way as in what is constituted by qualities.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.23.sanskrit_iast:
*guṇitvaṃ guṇabhoktṛtvaṃ syād viṣṇos tac ca sa svayam |
iti brahmatarke |*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.23.english_close: The state of possessing qualities, and the state of enjoying qualities, belong to Viṣṇu; and that too is he himself. Thus in the *Brahma-Tarka*.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.24.sanskrit_iast:
*viṣṇuṃ sarvaguṇaiḥ pūrṇaṃ jñātvā saṃsāravarjitaḥ |
nirduḥkhānandabhug nityaṃ tatsamīpe sa modate ||*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.24.english_close: Having known Viṣṇu as full of all qualities, one becomes free from saṃsāra. Enjoying bliss without suffering, one rejoices eternally in his proximity.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.25.sanskrit_iast:
*muktānāṃ cāśrayo viṣṇur adhiko'dhipatis tathā |
tadvaśā eva te sarve sarvadaiva sa īśvaraḥ ||
iti paramaśrutiḥ |*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.25.english_close: Viṣṇu is the support of the liberated, and moreover their superior and lord. All of them are indeed under his control; at all times he is the Lord. Thus says the supreme *śruti*.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.26.sanskrit_iast:
*amṛtasyaiṣa setuḥ | so'śnute sarvān kāmān saha brahmaṇā vipaścitā |
ityādi ca |*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.26.english_close: “This is the bridge to immortality.” “He enjoys all desires together with the wise Brahman.” And so forth.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.27.sanskrit_iast:
*nṛpādyāḥ śatadhṛtyantāḥ muktigā uttarottaram |
guṇaiḥ sarvaiḥ śataguṇā modanta iti hi śrutiḥ ||
iti pādme |*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.27.english_close: Those destined for liberation, beginning with kings and extending up to Śatadhṛti, are successively higher and higher. They rejoice, each by all qualities a hundredfold superior to the former: so indeed says scripture. Thus in the *Padma*.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.28.sanskrit_iast: ato niḥśeṣadoṣavarjitaḥ pūrṇānantaguṇo nārāyaṇa iti siddham |
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.28.english_close: Therefore it is established that Nārāyaṇa is devoid of every defect and possessed of complete and infinite qualities.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.29.sanskrit_iast:
*yasya trīṇy uditāni vedavacane divyāni rūpāṇy alaṃ
bat taddarśatam ittham eva nihitaṃ devasya bhargo mahat |
vāyo rāmavaconayaṃ prathakamaṃ pṛkṣo dvitīyaṃ vapuḥ
madhvo yat tu tṛtīyam etad amunā granthaḥ kṛtaḥ keśave ||*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.29.english_close: Of him whose three divine forms are declared in Vedic speech in full, this great radiance of the god has thus been set forth as visible. Vāyu was the first, the body spoken of in Rāma [tradition] the second, and Madhva is the third: by him this treatise has been composed for Keśava.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.30.sanskrit_iast:
*svatantrāyākhileśāya nirdoṣaguṇarūpiṇe |
preyase me supūrṇāya namo nārāyaṇāya te ||*
madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.30.english_close: To the independent one, to the lord of all, to him whose very form is faultless qualities, to my beloved, to the one utterly complete: reverence to you, Nārāyaṇa.

madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.31.english_close: Thus ends the third chapter of the venerable *Viṣṇu-Tattva-Vinirṇaya*, composed by the revered Ācārya Ānandatīrtha Bhagavatpāda.

mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.sanskrit_iast: ānandam ekam amṛtam ajaṃ vijñānam akṣaram / asarvaṃ sarvam abhayaṃ namasyāmaḥ prajāpatim //
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.english_close: We reverence the Lord of creatures as bliss, as one, as deathless, as unborn, as consciousness, as imperishable. We reverence him as not one item among all things, yet as all; and as free from fear.
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/mandana_misra_brahma_siddhi_gretil.txt).

mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.2.sanskrit_iast: āmnāyataḥ prasiddhiṃ ca kavayo 'sya pracakṣate / bhedaprapañcavilayadvāreṇa ca nirūpaṇām //
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.2.english_close: And the sages declare that its ascertainment comes from scripture. They also declare that its determination proceeds by way of the dissolution of the manifold world of difference.

mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.3.sanskrit_iast: saṃhṛtākhilabhedo 'taḥ sāmānyātmā sa varṇitaḥ / hemeva pārihāryādibhedasaṃhārasūcitam //
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.3.english_close: Therefore he has been described as the common underlying reality in whom all differences are withdrawn. It is as gold is indicated through the cancellation of bracelet and other differentiations.

mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.4.sanskrit_iast: āhur vidhātṛ pratyakṣaṃ na niṣeddhṛ vipaścitaḥ / naikatva āgamas tena pratyakṣeṇa virudhyate //
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.4.english_close: The wise say that perception is an establisher, not a canceller. Therefore the scriptural teaching of oneness is not contradicted by that perception.
mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.4.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/mandana_misra_brahma_siddhi_gretil.txt).

mandana/vidhi-viveka/primary anchor.english_close: And this matter is to be understood from the *Vidhi-Viveka* and the *Bhāvanā-Viveka*.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.1.sanskrit_iast:
na svato nāpi parato na dvābhyāṃ nāpy ahetutaḥ
utpannā jātu vidyante bhāvāḥ kva cana ke cana
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.1.english_close: Not from itself, not indeed from another, not from both, and not without cause, are any entities whatsoever ever found to have arisen anywhere.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.2.sanskrit_iast:
catvāraḥ pratyayā hetur ārambaṇam anantaram
tathaivādhipateyaṃ ca pratyayo nāsti pañcamaḥ
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.2.english_close: There are four conditions: cause, object-support, immediate antecedent, and likewise governing condition; there is no fifth condition.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.3.sanskrit_iast:
na hi svabhāvo bhāvānāṃ pratyayādiṣu vidyate
avidyamāne svabhāve parabhāvo na vidyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.3.english_close: For the own-being of entities is not found in conditions and the like. When own-being is absent, other-being is not found either.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.4.sanskrit_iast:
kriyā na pratyayavatī nāpratyayavatī kriyā
pratyayā nākriyāvantaḥ kriyāvantaś ca santy uta
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.4.english_close: An action is not possessed of conditions; nor is an action without conditions. Conditions are not devoid of action; or are they perhaps possessed of action?

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.5.sanskrit_iast:
utpadyate pratītyemān itīme pratyayāḥ kila
yāvan notpadyata ime tāvan nāpratyayāḥ katham
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.5.english_close: “Something arises in dependence on these”: thus, it is said, these are conditions. But so long as these themselves do not arise, how are they not non-conditions?

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.6.sanskrit_iast:
naivāsato naiva sataḥ pratyayo 'rthasya yujyate
asataḥ pratyayaḥ kasya sataś ca pratyayena kim
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.6.english_close: A condition of a non-existent thing is not tenable, nor indeed of an existent thing. For of what non-existent thing would there be a condition? And what would an existent thing need with a condition?

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.7.sanskrit_iast:
na san nāsan na sad asan dharmo nirvartate yadā
kathaṃ nirvartako hetur evaṃ sati hi yujyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.7.english_close: When no dharma is brought forth as existent, as non-existent, or as both existent and non-existent, how, that being so, could a producing cause be tenable?

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.8.sanskrit_iast:
anārambaṇa evāyaṃ san dharma upadiśyate
athānārambaṇe dharme kuta ārambaṇaṃ punaḥ
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.8.english_close: This existent dharma is taught as without an object-support. But if the dharma is without an object-support, whence would an object-support come again?

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.9.sanskrit_iast:
anutpanneṣu dharmeṣu nirodho nopapadyate
nānantaram ato yuktaṃ niruddhe pratyayaś ca kaḥ
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.9.english_close: In dharmas not yet arisen, cessation is not possible. Therefore the immediately antecedent condition is not tenable; and when something is already ceased, what condition would there be?

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.10.sanskrit_iast:
bhāvānāṃ niḥsvabhāvānāṃ na sattā vidyate yataḥ
satīdam asmin bhavatīty etan naivopapadyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.10.english_close: Since there is no inherent existence for entities devoid of own-being, the claim “this comes to be when that exists” is not ultimately tenable.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.11.sanskrit_iast:
na ca vyastasamasteṣu pratyayeṣvasti tat phalam
pratyayebhyaḥ kathaṃ tac ca bhaven na pratyayeṣu yat
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.11.english_close: That result is not present in the conditions taken separately or together. And how could that arise from the conditions, if it is not in the conditions?

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.12.sanskrit_iast:
athāsad api tat tebhyaḥ pratyayebhyaḥ pravartate
apratyayebhyo 'pi kasmān nābhipravartate phalam
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.12.english_close: If, alternatively, that result proceeds from those conditions even while absent, then why does the result not proceed equally from non-conditions?

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.13.sanskrit_iast:
phalaṃ ca pratyayamayaṃ pratyayāścāsvayaṃmayāḥ
phalamasvamayebhyo yattatpratyayamayaṃ katham
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.13.english_close: And if the result is made of conditions, while the conditions are not made of themselves, how could a result made of conditions come from what are not self-made?

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.14.sanskrit_iast:
tasmān na pratyayamayaṃ nāpratyayamayaṃ phalam
saṃvidyate phalābhāvāt pratyayāpratyayāḥ kutaḥ
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.14.english_close: Therefore no result is found that is made of conditions, nor one not made of conditions. Since the result is absent, whence conditions or non-conditions?

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.4.sanskrit_iast:
mamety aham iti kṣīṇe bahirdhādhyātmam eva ca
nirudhyata upādānaṃ tatkṣayāj janmanaḥ kṣayaḥ
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.4.english_close: When “mine” and “I” are exhausted, both with respect to what is external and to what is internal, appropriation ceases; with the exhaustion of that, birth is exhausted.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.5.sanskrit_iast:
karmakleśakṣayān mokṣaḥ karmakleśā vikalpataḥ
te prapañcāt prapañcas tu śūnyatāyāṃ nirudhyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.5.english_close: Liberation comes from the exhaustion of karma and afflictions; karma and afflictions come from conceptual construction. Those come from proliferative elaboration; but proliferative elaboration ceases in emptiness.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.6.sanskrit_iast:
ātmety api prajñapitam anātmety api deśitam
buddhair nātmā na cānātmā kaścid ity api deśitam
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.6.english_close: “Self” has been designated; “not-self” too has been taught. And by the Buddhas it has also been taught that there is no whatever that is either self or not-self.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.7.sanskrit_iast:
nivṛttam abhidhātavyaṃ nivṛttaś cittagocaraḥ
anutpannāniruddhā hi nirvāṇam iva dharmatā
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.7.english_close: What is to be named has fallen away; the field of mind has fallen away. For the nature of things, like nirvāṇa, is unarisen and unceased.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.8.sanskrit_iast:
sarvaṃ tathyaṃ na vā tathyaṃ tathyaṃ cātathyam eva ca
naivātathyaṃ naiva tathyam etad buddhānuśāsanam
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.8.english_close: Everything is true; or else everything is not true; everything is both true and not true. It is neither untrue nor true: this is the Buddhas’ instruction.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.9.sanskrit_iast:
aparapratyayaṃ śāntaṃ prapañcair aprapañcitam
nirvikalpam anānārtham etat tattvasya lakṣaṇam
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.9.english_close: Not dependent on another, peaceful, not proliferated by proliferations, free of conceptual construction and free of plurality of object: this is the mark of reality.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.10.sanskrit_iast:
pratītya yad yad bhavati na hi tāvat tad eva tat
na cānyadapi tattasmānnocchinnaṃ nāpi śāśvatam
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.10.english_close: Whatever comes to be dependently, that is not simply identical with that on which it depends. Yet it is not other than that either; therefore it is neither annihilated nor eternal.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.11.sanskrit_iast:
anekārtham anānārtham anucchedam aśāśvatam
etat tal lokanāthānāṃ buddhānāṃ śāsanāmṛtam
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.11.english_close: Neither one-sidedly one nor one-sidedly many, neither annihilationist nor eternalist, this is the nectar of the teaching of the Buddhas, the lords of the world.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.12.sanskrit_iast:
saṃbuddhānām anutpāde śrāvakāṇāṃ punaḥ kṣaye
jñānaṃ pratyekabuddhānām asaṃsargāt pravartate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.12.english_close: When fully awakened Buddhas do not arise, and when the disciples have also perished, the knowledge of the solitary Buddhas proceeds from non-association.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.8.sanskrit_iast:
dve satye samupāśritya buddhānāṃ dharmadeśanā
lokasaṃvṛtisatyaṃ ca satyaṃ ca paramārthataḥ
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.8.english_close: The Buddhas’ teaching of the Dharma rests upon two truths: the truth of worldly convention and the truth in the ultimate sense.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.9.sanskrit_iast:
ye 'nayor na vijānanti vibhāgaṃ satyayor dvayoḥ
te tattvaṃ na vijānanti gambhīraṃ buddhaśāsane
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.9.english_close: Those who do not understand the distinction between these two truths do not understand the deep reality in the Buddhas’ teaching.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.10.sanskrit_iast:
vyavahāram anāśritya paramārtho na deśyate
paramārtham anāgamya nirvāṇaṃ nādhigamyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.10.english_close: Without relying on conventional practice and discourse, the ultimate is not taught. Without arriving at the ultimate, nirvāṇa is not attained.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.11.sanskrit_iast:
vināśayati durdṛṣtā śūnyatā mandamedhasam
sarpo yathā durgṛhīto vidyā vā duṣprasādhitā
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.11.english_close: Emptiness, when badly seen, destroys the dull-witted person, like a snake badly grasped, or like a spell or discipline badly mastered.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.12.sanskrit_iast:
ataś ca pratyudāvṛttaṃ cittaṃ deśayituṃ muneḥ
dharmaṃ matvāsya dharmasya mandair duravagāhatām
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.12.english_close: And for this reason the sage’s mind turned back from teaching the Dharma, having considered this Dharma difficult for the dull to penetrate.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.13.sanskrit_iast:
śūnyatāyām adhilayaṃ yaṃ punaḥ kurute bhavān
doṣaprasaṅgo nāsmākaṃ sa śūnye nopapadyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.13.english_close: Whatever superimposed fault you once more make out against emptiness, that consequence of defect does not apply to us; in emptiness it does not stand.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.14.sanskrit_iast:
sarvaṃ ca yujyate tasya śūnyatā yasya yujyate
sarvaṃ na yujyate tasya śūnyaṃ yasya na yujyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.14.english_close: Everything works for the one for whom emptiness works. Nothing works for the one for whom emptiness does not work.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.15.sanskrit_iast:
sa tvaṃ doṣān ātmanīyān asmāsu paripātayan
aśvam evābhirūḍhaḥ sann aśvam evāsi vismṛtaḥ
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.15.english_close: You, while casting your own faults upon us, are like one mounted on a horse who has forgotten the horse itself.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.16.sanskrit_iast:
svabhāvād yadi bhāvānāṃ sadbhāvam anupaśyasi
ahetupratyayān bhāvāṃs tvam evaṃ sati paśyasi
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.16.english_close: If you see the existent status of entities as coming from own-being, then, in that case, you see entities as without causes and conditions.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.17.sanskrit_iast:
kāryaṃ ca kāraṇaṃ caiva kartāraṃ karaṇaṃ kriyām
utpādaṃ ca nirodhaṃ ca phalaṃ ca pratibādhase
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.17.english_close: You negate effect and cause, agent, instrument, and action, and also arising, cessation, and result.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.18.sanskrit_iast:
yaḥ pratītyasamutpādaḥ śūnyatāṃ tāṃ pracakṣmahe
sā prajñaptir upādāya pratipat saiva madhyamā
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.18.english_close: That dependent arising, we declare, is emptiness. That is a designation dependent on something taken up; that itself is the middle way.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.19.sanskrit_iast:
apratītya samutpanno dharmaḥ kaścin na vidyate
yasmāt tasmād aśūnyo hi dharmaḥ kaścin na vidyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.19.english_close: No dharma whatever is found to have arisen without dependence. Therefore no dharma whatever is found to be non-empty.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.20.sanskrit_iast:
yady aśūnyam idaṃ sarvam udayo nāsti na vyayaḥ
caturṇām āryasatyānām abhāvas te prasajyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.20.english_close: If all this were non-empty, there would be no arising and no passing away. Then the absence of the four noble truths would follow for you.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.21.sanskrit_iast:
apratītya samutpannaṃ kuto duḥkhaṃ bhaviṣyati
anityam uktaṃ duḥkhaṃ hi tat svābhāvye na vidyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.21.english_close: How could suffering exist if it had arisen without dependence? For suffering is said to be impermanent; that is not found under the regime of own-being.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.22.sanskrit_iast:
svabhāvato vidyamānaṃ kiṃ punaḥ samudeṣyate
tasmāt samudayo nāsti śūnyatāṃ pratibādhataḥ
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.22.english_close: What already exists by own-being, how would it arise again? Therefore origination does not stand if emptiness is denied.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.1.sanskrit_iast:
yadi śūnyam idaṃ sarvam udayo nāsti na vyayaḥ
prahāṇād vā nirodhād vā kasya nirvāṇam iṣyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.1.english_close: If all this were empty, there would be no arising and no passing away. Then, by abandonment or by cessation, whose nirvāṇa would be admitted?

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.2.sanskrit_iast:
yady aśūnyam idaṃ sarvam udayo nāsti na vyayaḥ
prahāṇād vā nirodhād vā kasya nirvāṇam iṣyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.2.english_close: If all this were non-empty, there would be no arising and no passing away. Then, by abandonment or by cessation, whose nirvāṇa would be admitted?

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.3.sanskrit_iast:
aprahīṇam asaṃprāptam anucchinnam aśāśvatam
aniruddham anutpannam etan nirvāṇam ucyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.3.english_close: Not abandoned, not newly attained, not cut off, not eternal, not ceased, not arisen: this is called nirvāṇa.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.4.sanskrit_iast:
bhāvas tāvan na nirvāṇaṃ jarāmaraṇalakṣaṇam
prasajyetāsti bhāvo hi na jarāmaraṇaṃ vinā
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.4.english_close: Nirvāṇa is not, to begin with, a positive entity, for a positive entity has the mark of aging and death. For it would follow that there is no positive entity at all apart from aging and death.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.5.sanskrit_iast:
bhāvaś ca yadi nirvāṇaṃ nirvāṇaṃ saṃskṛtaṃ bhavet
nāsaṃskṛto hi vidyate bhāvaḥ kva cana kaś cana
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.5.english_close: And if nirvāṇa were a positive entity, nirvāṇa would be conditioned. For no positive entity whatever is found anywhere to be unconditioned.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.6.sanskrit_iast:
bhāvaś ca yadi nirvāṇam anupādāya tat katham
nirvāṇaṃ nānupādāya kaścid bhāvo hi vidyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.6.english_close: And if nirvāṇa were a positive entity, how could it be without dependence? For no positive entity whatever is found without depending on something taken up.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.7.sanskrit_iast:
bhāvo yadi na nirvāṇam abhāvaḥ kiṃ bhaviṣyati
nirvāṇaṃ yatra bhāvo na nābhāvas tatra vidyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.7.english_close: If nirvāṇa is not a positive entity, how could it be a negation or absence? Where there is no positive entity, there too an absence is not found.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.8.sanskrit_iast:
yady abhāvaś ca nirvāṇam anupādāya tat katham
nirvāṇaṃ na hy abhāvo 'sti yo 'nupādāya vidyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.8.english_close: And if nirvāṇa were an absence, how could that be without dependence? For no absence is found that exists without depending on something taken up.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.9.sanskrit_iast:
ya ājavaṃjavībhāva upādāya pratītya vā
so 'pratītyānupādāya nirvāṇam upadiśyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.9.english_close: That continuum called *ājavaṃjavībhāva*, whether designated in dependence or as dependently arisen, is taught as nirvāṇa when it is without dependence and without appropriation.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.10.sanskrit_iast:
prahāṇaṃ cābravīc chāstā bhavasya vibhavasya ca
tasmān na bhāvo nābhāvo nirvāṇam iti yujyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.10.english_close: The Teacher spoke of the abandonment of becoming and of non-becoming. Therefore it is not fitting to say that nirvāṇa is either a positive entity or an absence.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.11.sanskrit_iast:
bhaved abhāvo bhāvaś ca nirvāṇam ubhayaṃ yadi
bhaved abhāvo bhāvaś ca mokṣas tac ca na yujyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.11.english_close: If nirvāṇa were both absence and positive entity, liberation too would be both absence and positive entity; and that is not tenable.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.12.sanskrit_iast:
bhaved abhāvo bhāvaś ca nirvāṇam ubhayaṃ yadi
nānupādāya nirvāṇam upādāyobhayaṃ hi tat
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.12.english_close: If nirvāṇa were both absence and positive entity, nirvāṇa would not be without dependence, for both of those are dependent on something taken up.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.13.sanskrit_iast:
bhaved abhāvo bhāvaś ca nirvāṇam ubhayaṃ katham
asaṃskṛtaṃ hi nirvāṇaṃ bhāvābhāvau ca saṃskṛtau
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.13.english_close: How could nirvāṇa be both absence and positive entity? For nirvāṇa is unconditioned, whereas positive entity and absence are conditioned.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.14.sanskrit_iast:
bhaved abhāvo bhāvaś ca nirvāṇa ubhayaṃ katham
tayor abhāvo hy ekatra prakāśatamasor iva
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.14.english_close: How could nirvāṇa be both absence and positive entity? For the two do not occur together in one place, like light and darkness.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.15.sanskrit_iast:
naivābhāvo naiva bhāvo nirvāṇam iti yā 'ñjanā
abhāve caiva bhāve ca sā siddhe sati sidhyati
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.15.english_close: The construal “nirvāṇa is neither absence nor positive entity” is established only if absence and positive entity are themselves established.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.16.sanskrit_iast:
naivābhāvo naiva bhāvo nirvāṇaṃ yadi vidyate
naivābhāvo naiva bhāva iti kena tad ajyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.16.english_close: If nirvāṇa existed as neither absence nor positive entity, by what would it be apprehended or specified as “neither absence nor positive entity”?

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.17.sanskrit_iast:
paraṃ nirodhād bhagavān bhavatīty eva nājyate
na bhavaty ubhayaṃ ceti nobhayaṃ ceti nājyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.17.english_close: After final cessation, the Blessed One is not to be fixed as existing; nor as not existing, nor as both, nor as neither.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.18.sanskrit_iast:
tiṣṭhamāno 'pi bhagavān bhavatīty eva nājyate
na bhavaty ubhayaṃ ceti nobhayaṃ ceti nājyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.18.english_close: Even while remaining here, the Blessed One is not to be fixed as existing; nor as not existing, nor as both, nor as neither.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.19.sanskrit_iast:
na saṃsārasya nirvāṇāt kiṃcid asti viśeṣaṇam
na nirvāṇasya saṃsārāt kiṃcid asti viśeṣaṇam
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.19.english_close: There is not the slightest distinguishing feature of saṃsāra apart from nirvāṇa. There is not the slightest distinguishing feature of nirvāṇa apart from saṃsāra.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.20.sanskrit_iast:
nirvāṇasya ca yā koṭiḥ koṭiḥ saṃsaraṇasya ca
na tayor antaraṃ kiṃcit susūkṣmam api vidyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.20.english_close: And the limit of nirvāṇa is the limit of saṃsāric wandering as well. Between the two, not even the subtlest interval is found.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.21.sanskrit_iast:
paraṃ nirodhād antādyāḥ śāśvatādyāś ca dṛṣṭayaḥ
nirvāṇam aparāntaṃ ca pūrvāntaṃ ca samāśritāḥ
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.21.english_close: The views beginning with “after final cessation” and the views beginning with “eternal” all rely on nirvāṇa, on the far limit, and on the near limit.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.22.sanskrit_iast:
śūnyeṣu sarvadharmeṣu kim anantaṃ kim antavat
kim anantam antavac ca nānantaṃ nāntavac ca kim
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.22.english_close: When all dharmas are empty, what is infinite and what has an end? What is both infinite and finite, and what is neither infinite nor finite?

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.23.sanskrit_iast:
kiṃ tad eva kim anyat kiṃ śāśvataṃ kim aśāśvatam
aśāśvataṃ śāśvataṃ ca kiṃ vā nobhayam apy atha
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.23.english_close: What is the very same, what is different, what is eternal, what is non-eternal? What is both eternal and non-eternal, or again what is neither?

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.24.sanskrit_iast:
sarvopalambhopaśamaḥ prapañcopaśamaḥ śivaḥ
na kva cit kasyacit kaścid dharmo buddhena deśitaḥ
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.24.english_close: The pacification of all apprehension, the pacification of proliferation: that is the auspicious peace. No dharma whatsoever has been taught by the Buddha anywhere to anyone.

nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/21.sanskrit_iast:
hetupratyayasāmagryāṃ ca pṛthak cāpi madvaco na yadi
nanu śūnyatvaṃ siddhaṃ bhāvānām asvabhāvatvāt
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/21.english_close: Even if my statement is not in the aggregate of causes and conditions, nor separately either, still the emptiness of entities is surely established, because they are without own-being.
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/21.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/nagarjuna_vigraha_vyavartani.txt).

nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/22.sanskrit_iast:
yaś ca pratītyabhāvo bhāvānāṃ śūnyateti sā proktā
yaḥ pratītyabhāvo bhavati hi tasyāsvabhāvatvam
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/22.english_close: And that dependent arising of entities, that is what is called emptiness. For whatever comes to be dependently, of that there is lack of own-being.
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/22.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/nagarjuna_vigraha_vyavartani.txt).

nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/23.sanskrit_iast:
nirmitako nirmitakaṃ māyāpuruṣaḥ svamāyayā sṛṣṭam
pratiṣedhayeta yadvat pratiṣedho 'yaṃ tathaiva syāt
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/23.english_close: Just as an apparition, a magical person, might negate another apparition created by his own magic, so too this negation could be just so.
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/23.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/nagarjuna_vigraha_vyavartani.txt).

nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/24.sanskrit_iast:
na svābhāvikam etad vākyaṃ tasmān na vādahānir me
nāsti ca vaiṣamikatvaṃ viśeṣahetuś ca na nigadyaḥ
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/24.english_close: This statement is not intrinsically grounded; therefore there is no collapse of my position. And there is no asymmetry here; nor is any special reason to be stated.

nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/25.sanskrit_iast:
mā śabdavad iti nāyaṃ dṛṣṭānto yas tvayā samārabdhaḥ
śabdena hi tac chabdasya vāraṇaṃ naivam etac ca
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/25.english_close: This analogy, "like the prohibitive word *mā*," is not the example you can sustain. For there a word prevents a word; this, however, is not like that.

nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/26.sanskrit_iast:
naiḥsvābhāvyānāṃ cen naiḥsvābhāvyena vāraṇaṃ yadi hi
naiḥsvābhāvyanivṛttau svābhāvyaṃ hi prasiddhaṃ syāt
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/26.english_close: If indeed there were a stopping of non-own-being things by means of non-own-being, then, when non-own-being ceased, own-being would indeed be established.

nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/27.sanskrit_iast:
atha vā nirmitakāyāṃ yathā striyāṃ striyam ity asadgrāham
nirmitakaḥ pratihanyāt kasya cid evaṃ bhaved etat
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/27.english_close: Or again, just as with regard to an illusory woman, the false apprehension "a woman!" might be countered by an apparition, so could this be in just that way for someone.

nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/28.sanskrit_iast:
atha vā sādhyasamo 'yaṃ hetur na hi vidyate dhvaneḥ sattā
saṃvyavahāraṃ ca vayaṃ nānabhyupagamya kathayāmaḥ
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/28.english_close: Or else, this reason is fallacious by being equal to the probandum; for the sound has no intrinsic existence. And we do not speak without accepting conventional transaction.

nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/29.sanskrit_iast:
yadi kā cana pratijñā syān me tata eṣa me bhaved doṣaḥ
nāsti ca mama pratijñā tasmān naivāsti me doṣaḥ
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/29.english_close: If I had any thesis at all, then this fault would belong to me. But I have no thesis; therefore there is no fault at all for me.
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/29.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/nagarjuna_vigraha_vyavartani.txt).

nimbarka/dasa-shloki/3.sanskrit_iast:
*aprākṛtaṃ prākṛtarūpakaṃ ca
kālasvarūpaṃ tad acetanaṃ matam |
māyāpradhānādipadapravācyaṃ
śuklādibhedāś ca same 'pi tatra || 3 ||*
nimbarka/dasa-shloki/3.english_close: The insentient is held to be non-prākṛta, and also of prākṛta form. It is also of the nature of time. It is denoted by terms such as *māyā* and *pradhāna*. And within that one same domain there are distinctions such as white and the rest.

nimbarka/dasa-shloki/6.sanskrit_iast:
*upāsanīyaṃ nitarāṃ janaiḥ sadā
prahāṇaye 'jñānatamo'nuvṛtteḥ |
sanandanādyair munibhis tathoktaṃ
śrīnāradāyākhilatattvasākṣiṇe || 6 ||*
nimbarka/dasa-shloki/6.english_close: He is to be worshipped in constant *upāsanā* (meditative attendance), most especially, by people always, for the removal of the continuing darkness of ignorance. Thus was it taught by the sages beginning with Sanandana to Śrī Nārada, witness to the whole reality.

nimbarka/dasa-shloki/7.sanskrit_iast:
*sarvaṃ hi vijñānam ato yathārthakaṃ
śrutismṛtibhyo nikhilasya vastunaḥ |
brahmātmakatvād iti vedavinmataṃ
trirūpatāpi śrutisūtrasādhitā || 7 ||*
nimbarka/dasa-shloki/7.english_close: For this reason every cognition is veridical. So say *śruti* and *smṛti* with regard to every entity whatsoever. Because each thing has Brahman for its self: such is the view of the knowers of Veda. And even its threefoldness is established by *śruti* and by the sūtras.

nimbarka/dasa-shloki/8.sanskrit_iast:
*nānyā gatiḥ kṛṣṇapadāravindāt
sandṛśyate brahmaśivādivanditāt |
bhaktecchayopāttasucintyavigrahād
acintyaśakter avicintyasāśayāt || 8 ||*
nimbarka/dasa-shloki/8.english_close: No other refuge is seen than Kṛṣṇa’s lotus feet. They are adored by Brahmā, Śiva, and the rest. He assumes a form well suited to meditation in accordance with the devotee’s desire. His *acintya-śakti* (inconceivable power), and his inward ground, are beyond thought.

nimbarka/dasa-shloki/9.sanskrit_iast:
*kṛpāsya dainyādiyuji prajāyate
yayā bhavet premaviśeṣalakṣaṇā |
bhaktir hy ananyādhipater mahātmanaḥ
sā cottamā sādhanarūpikāparā || 9 ||*
nimbarka/dasa-shloki/9.english_close: His grace arises in one joined with humility and the like. By that, there comes to be a *bhakti* marked by a distinctive love. For the great Lord who has no other master, that devotion indeed is directed. And that is the higher devotion; the other is merely of the nature of a means.

nimbarka/dasa-shloki/10.sanskrit_iast:
*upāsyarūpaṃ tad upāsakasya ca
kṛpāphalaṃ bhaktirasas tataḥ param |
virodhino rūpam athaitad āpter
jñeyā ime 'rthā api pañca sādhubhiḥ || 10 ||*
nimbarka/dasa-shloki/10.english_close: The nature of the worshipped object, and also that of its worshipper. The fruit of grace, and then beyond that the savor of *bhakti*. The nature of the obstacles, and then the nature of the attainment of this. These five matters too are to be known by the holy aspirants.

nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.4.sanskrit_iast: aṃśo nānāvyapadeśād anyathā cāpi dāśakitavāditvam adhīyate eke ||
nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.4.english_close: (The individual self is) a part, because scripture speaks in the register of plurality or difference, and also otherwise. And some recensions read [Brahman as] being fishermen, gamblers, and the like.
And also because of the wording of the mantra.
And it is also remembered in *smṛti* (remembered scripture and authoritative tradition).

nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/Upani.locus: Chāndogya Upaniṣad 3.12.6 (the mantra cited for BS 2.3.43)
nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/Upani.sanskrit_iast: tāvān asya mahimā tato jyāyāṃś ca pūruṣaḥ | pādo'sya sarvā bhūtāni tripād asyāmṛtaṃ divīti ||
nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/Upani.english_close: Such is his greatness. And the *puruṣa* (supreme person) is greater than that. All beings are one quarter of him. His three quarters are immortal in heaven.

nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/15.7a.english_close: An eternal part of me, in the world of living beings, having become a living self.

padmapada/pancapadika/V.15.1.sanskrit_iast: mithyā ca tad ajñānaṃ ca mithyājñānam |
padmapada/pancapadika/V.15.1.english_close: That which is both *mithyā* (indeterminable, not straightforwardly real or unreal) and *ajñāna* (ignorance) is *mithyājñāna*.

padmapada/pancapadika/V.15.3.english_close: And by the word "*ajñāna*" there is meant the *avidyāśakti* (power of ignorance) whose nature is insentient.

padmapada/pancapadika/V.15.4.english_close: It is stated by exclusion from knowledge.

padmapada/pancapadika/V.15.5.english_close: The meaning is: it has that as its occasioning cause, and that as its material cause.

padmapada/pancapadika/VI.16.1.english_close: But then how can a transactional order that is causally occasioned possess *naisargikatva* (beginningless, congenital status)?

padmapada/pancapadika/VI.16.3.english_close: This *avidyāśakti* must indeed be admitted with respect to external and internal things.

padmapada/pancapadika/VI.16.4.english_close: It must be admitted as attached only to the bare existence that belongs to their very nature.

padmapada/pancapadika/VI.16.5.english_close: Otherwise, the appearance of false objects would be inexplicable.

prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 65a.sanskrit_iast: devadatto hi svātmānam andhatvādy-upetaṃ pratibimbe 'vagacchann api tattva-jñānatvān nānuśocati ।
prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 65a.english_close: For Devadatta, indeed, even while cognizing his own self, as possessed of blindness and the like, in a *pratibimba* (reflection-image), does not grieve, because he possesses knowledge of the truth.

prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 65b.sanskrit_iast: evaṃ brahmāpi svātmani jīve pratibimbe saṃsāraṃ paśyad api tattva-jñānatvān nānuśocati ॥
prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 65b.english_close: In the same way, Brahman too, even while seeing *saṃsāra* (bondage and transmigratory suffering) in the *jīva* (individual self) as a reflection in its own self, does not grieve, because it possesses knowledge of the truth.

prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 103a.english_close: Even in the case of what is *svayaṃprakāśa* (self-luminous), it is precisely through auxiliaries such as reflection that the *mahāvākya* (great Upaniṣadic sentence) has the power to produce immediacy.

prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 103b.sanskrit_iast: brahmaiva sarva-saṃvid-upādānatvād brahmakāra-śabda-pramāṇa-janya-saṃvedane 'pi brahmāpi prathamam evāparokṣatayāvabhāsate ।
prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 103b.english_close: Because Brahman itself is the material basis of every cognition, Brahman too appears from the very first as immediate even in the cognition generated by the verbal *pramāṇa* (means of valid knowledge) that has Brahman for its form.

prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 103c.sanskrit_iast: tac ca cittasyātisūkṣmatvād viparyaya-saṃskāra-doṣāc ca pratibaddhaṃ bhrāntyā parokṣavad bhāsate ॥
prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 103c.english_close: And that, because of the extreme subtlety of the mind and because of the defect constituted by residual impressions of error, being obstructed, appears through delusion as though it were indirect.

prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 164a.sanskrit_iast: avidyayā mṛtyuṃ tīrtvā vidyayāmṛtam aśnuta iti phala-bhedāgamāt ।
prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 164a.english_close: From the scriptural declaration, "having crossed death by *avidyā* (ignorance, here in the lower or preparatory sense), one attains immortality by *vidyā* (liberating knowledge)," because scripture teaches a difference of fruits.

prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 164b.english_close: Therefore there is no valid warrant for a *samuccaya* (co-functioning combination of means).

prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 164c.sanskrit_iast: sarvānarthopādānānādi-mithyājñāna-nivṛttir eva vijñāne phalavad iti na samuccayāvakāśaḥ ॥
prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 164c.english_close: Since, in realized knowledge, the fruit is precisely the cessation of beginningless false cognition that is the substrative cause of all misfortune, there is no room for any combination.

prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 212a.english_close: And because of the co-reference of *māyā* (cosmic projecting and veiling power) and *avidyā*.

prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 212b.english_close: Therefore the conclusion is reached that Brahman, qualified by *anirvacanīya* (indeterminable as either real or unreal) *māyā*, is the cause.

raghavendra/nyaya-sudha-parimala/1.english_close: Salutation to him whose form is exalted through full and innumerable qualities, whose fame is holy. Salutation to the husband of Śrī, whose very form is self-bliss bestowed upon devotees.

raghavendra/nyaya-sudha-parimala/2.english_close: Salutation to Brahmā, whose form is eternally beautiful and exalted through full and innumerable qualities. Salutation to Brahmā, whose fame is praised by the illustrious Brahmā-and-Indra line [reading of the middle compound tentative].

raghavendra/nyaya-sudha-parimala/3.sanskrit_iast: viśvaṃ yasya vaśe sarvam aniśaṃ śaṅkarādikam | tasya vāyos tṛtīyāṃśam āśraye śamavāptaye || 3 ||
raghavendra/nyaya-sudha-parimala/3.english_close: He in whose power the whole world lies, with Śaṅkara and the rest, at all times. I take refuge in that third portion of Vāyu, for the attainment of tranquillity.

raghavendra/nyaya-sudha-parimala/4.sanskrit_iast: sat-padmabandhavo yasya gāvo viśva-tamo-nudaḥ | sa syāt tattva-prakāśāya jayatīrtha-ravir mama || 4 ||
raghavendra/nyaya-sudha-parimala/4.english_close: He whose rays are the friends of good lotuses and the dispellers of the world’s darkness. May that sun, Jayatīrtha, be for the illumination of truth for me.

raghavendra/nyaya-sudha-parimala/5.sanskrit_iast: pada-vākya-pramāṇa-jño durmata-dhvānta-bhāskaraḥ | vyāsatīrtha-munīndro me bhūyāj jñāna-samṛddhaye || 5 ||
raghavendra/nyaya-sudha-parimala/5.english_close: The knower of word, sentence, and valid means of cognition; the sun to the darkness of bad doctrine. May the sage-lord Vyāsatīrtha be for the enrichment of knowledge in me.

raghavendra/nyaya-sudha-parimala/6.sanskrit_iast: asmad-deśika-pādānāṃ praṇamya caraṇāmbujam | śrīman-nyāya-sudhā-vākya-vyākhyāṃ kuryāṃ yathā-mati || 6 ||
raghavendra/nyaya-sudha-parimala/6.english_close: Having bowed to the lotus-foot of my own teacher. I should make, according to my understanding, an explanation of the wording of the venerable *Nyāya-Sudhā*.

raghavendra/nyaya-sudha-parimala/prose s.english_close: While explaining the first verse, at the beginning of the treatise, he performs an auspicious opening. It takes the form of salutation to a deity endowed with the properties that ground worthiness-to-be-saluted, desirability, and authority.

raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1, adhyāya s.sanskrit_iast: te ca śabdā anyatra-prasiddhā ubhayatra-prasiddhā anyatraiva-prasiddhāś ca
raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1, adhyāya s.english_close: Those words, moreover, are words established elsewhere, words established in both places, and words established only elsewhere.

raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1, adhyāya s.sanskrit_iast: sākṣāt paramparayā vā viṣṇum eva sṛṣṭy-ādi-vartṛtva-sādhya-guṇa-pūrṇatvena sādhayanti
raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1, adhyāya s.english_close: They establish Viṣṇu alone, either directly or by *paramparā* (mediated succession), as full of qualities established through creatorship and the rest of the divine functions.

raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1, *bhedavya.english_close: The reading stands as: "The *ānandamaya* (the bliss-full one) is not the other."

raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1, *bhedavya.sanskrit_iast: ānandamayād bhedavyapadeśāc cānandamayo netara ity arthaḥ |
raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1, *bhedavya.english_close: The meaning is this: because there is a predication of difference from the *ānandamaya*, the *ānandamaya* is not the other.

raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1, *bhedavya.sanskrit_iast: "sa eko brahmaṇa ānandaḥ" ityādinā viriñcadeḥ parimitānandam uktvā |
raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1, *bhedavya.english_close: Having stated, by the expression beginning "That is one joy of Brahman," a limited bliss in Viriñci and the rest,

raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1, *bhedavya.sanskrit_iast: "yato vāco nivartante" ity ānandamayasya pūrṇānandatvokter iti bhāvaḥ ||
raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1, *bhedavya.english_close: By the statement "from which words turn back," there is a statement of the *ānandamaya*'s full blisshood. Such is the purport.

raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.3, *suṣuptyu.english_close: And there is no cogency on the basis of non-difference.

raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.3, *suṣuptyu.sanskrit_iast: suṣuptyutkrāntyor bhedena jīveśayor vyapadeśa iti paramparayā yojyam ||
raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.3, *suṣuptyu.english_close: The designation of the *jīva* (individual self) and the Lord, as distinct in *suṣupti* and *utkrānti*, is thus to be construed by sequential linkage.

raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.4, *jñeyatva.sanskrit_iast: brahmaṇa eva mumukṣu-jñeyatva-vacanād ānumānikāder jñeyatvāvacanāc cety arthaḥ |
raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.4, *jñeyatva.english_close: The meaning is this: because there is a statement that Brahman alone has *jñeyatva* (the status of being the proper object of knowledge) for the *mumukṣu* (seeker of liberation), and because there is no statement of knowability for what is merely *ānumānika* (inferentially posited) and the like.

raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.4, *jñeyatva.sanskrit_iast: "tam evaikaṃ jānathātmānam" iti brahmaṇa eva mumukṣu-jñeyatva-vacanāt ||
raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.4, *jñeyatva.english_close: "Know that one self alone" - because there is a statement that Brahman alone is the proper object of knowledge for the *mumukṣu*.

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/notice 1.english_close: The earlier teachers abridged the extensive exposition on the *Brahma-Sūtra*-s composed by the venerable Bodhāyana.

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/notice 2.english_close: In accordance with that view, the very words of the sūtra-s will be explained.

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/1.1.1.english_close: Now, therefore, the desire to know Brahman.

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/1.english_close: Here, this word *atha* has the sense of immediate succession.

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/2.english_close: The word *ataḥ* conveys the sense of causal connection with what has occurred.

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/3.english_close: For one who has studied the Veda together with its auxiliaries and head-texts, who through the cognition that mere ritual action has a small and unstable fruit has come to desire liberation, the inquiry into Brahman, whose fruit is infinite and abiding, arises thereafter.

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/4.english_close: "The desire to know Brahman": that is the compound *brahmajijñāsā*.

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/5.sanskrit_iast: brahmaṇa iti karmaṇi ṣaṣṭhī. kartṛkarmaṇoḥ kṛti (Pāṇini 2.3.65).
ramanuja/shri-bhasya/5.english_close: In "brahmaṇaḥ," the genitive is in the sense of the object. "With a kṛt-formation, the genitive may denote agent or object" (Pāṇini 2.3.65).

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/6.english_close: By the word *brahman* there is denoted the supreme Person, whose very nature excludes every defect, and who possesses innumerable hosts of auspicious qualities of unsurpassed excellence.

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/7.english_close: For everywhere the word *brahman* applies because of connection with the attribute of greatness.

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/8.sanskrit_iast: bṛhattvaṃ ca svarūpeṇa guṇaiś ca, yatrānavadhikātiśayaṃ so 'sya mukhyo 'rthaḥ.
ramanuja/shri-bhasya/8.english_close: And greatness consists both in essential nature and in qualities; where that greatness is unsurpassed, that is the word's primary meaning.

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/9.english_close: And that is precisely the Lord of all.

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/10.english_close: Therefore the word *brahman* functions primarily only in its proper sense here.

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/11.english_close: Therefore Brahman, as the object of inquiry, is the Lord of all.

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/12.english_close: A desire to know is what "inquiry" means.

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/13.sanskrit_iast: icchāyā iṣyamāṇa-pradhānatvād iṣyamāṇaṃ jñānam iha vidhīyate.
ramanuja/shri-bhasya/13.english_close: Because in desire the thing desired is primary, what is enjoined here is the knowledge being sought.

ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§1.english_close: Salutation to Viṣṇu, who is qualified by the totality of conscious and non-conscious entities, who reclines upon Śeṣa. Salutation to him who is the treasury of stainless and infinite auspicious qualities.

ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§2.sanskrit_iast:
paraṃ brahmaivājñaṃ bhramaparigataṃ saṃsarati
tatparopādhyālīḍhaṃ vivaśam aśubhasyāspadam iti
śrutinyāyāpetaṃ jagati vitataṃ mohanam idaṃ
tamo yenāpāstaṃ sa hi vijayate yāmunamuniḥ
ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§2.english_close: "The supreme Brahman itself, as ignorant, seized by error, transmigrates; it is overrun by adjuncts other than itself, helpless, the abode of evil" such is this delusion, spread throughout the world, devoid of scripture and reasoning. The sage Yāmuna, by whom that darkness was driven away, he indeed triumphs.

ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§3.english_close: At the head of the mass of scriptural texts that instruct for the welfare of the whole world, this meaning has been established: meditation upon, worship of, prostration before, and the like, directed to the pair of feet of the supreme Person, and preceded by knowledge of the true nature of the *jīva* and the supreme Self and by the proper carrying out of the duties of *varṇa* and *āśrama*, are supremely dear to him; and the fruit is the attainment of him.

ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4.sanskrit_iast: asya jīvātmano 'nādyavidyāsaṃcitapuṇyapāparūpakarmapravāhahetukabrahmādisuranaratiryaksthāvarātmakacaturvidhadehapraveśakṛtatattadabhimānajanitāvarjanīyabhavabhayavidhvaṃsanāya dehātiriktātmasvarūpatatsvabhāvatadantarayāmiparamātmasvarūpatatsvabhāvatadupāsanatatphalabhūtātmasvarūpāvirbhāvapūrvakānavadhikātiśayānandabrahmānubhavajñāpane pravṛttaṃ hi vedāntavākyajātam, tat tvam asi | ayam ātmā brahma | ya ātmani tiṣṭhann ātmano 'ntaro yam ātmā na veda yasyātmā śarīraṃ ya ātmānam antaro yamayati sa ta ātmāntaryāmy amṛtaḥ | eṣa sarvabhūtāntarātmāpahatapāpmā divyo deva eko nārāyaṇaḥ | tam etaṃ vedānuvacanena brāhmaṇā vividiṣanti yajñena dānena tapasānāśakena | brahmavid āpnoti param | tam evaṃ vidvān amṛta iha bhavati nānyaḥ panthā ayanāya vidyata ity ādikam
ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4.english_close: For the destruction of the inescapable fear of becoming, born from the several identifications produced by this individual self's entry into the four kinds of bodies consisting in Brahmā, gods, humans, animals, and immovables, an entry caused by the stream of karmas in the form of merit and demerit accumulated through beginningless ignorance, the whole body of Vedāntic sentences is indeed set in motion to make known: the self's nature as distinct from body; its intrinsic character; the nature of the supreme Self who is its inner controller; that supreme Self's intrinsic character; the worship of him; and the experience of Brahman as limitless and unsurpassed bliss, preceded by the manifestation of the self's own nature that is the fruit of that worship. "That thou art." "This self is Brahman." "He who stands in the self, who is within the self, whom the self does not know, whose body is the self, who from within controls the self, he is thy Self, the inner controller, the immortal." "This one, the inner Self of all beings, free from evil, divine, the one god, is Nārāyaṇa." "By recitation of the Veda, by sacrifice, by gift, and by austerity unaccompanied by self-seeking, the Brāhmaṇas desire to know that very one." "The knower of Brahman attains the supreme." "One who knows him thus becomes immortal here; there is no other path for going there" and the like.

ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§5.sanskrit_iast: jīvātmanaḥ svarūpaṃ devamanuṣyādiprakṛtipariṇāmaviśeṣarūpanānāvidhabhedarahitaṃ jñānānandaikaguṇaṃ, tasyaitasya karmakṛtadevādibhede 'padhvaste svarūpabhedo vācām agocaraḥ svasaṃvedyaḥ, jñānasvarūpam ity etāvad eva nirdeśyam | tac ca sarveṣām ātmanāṃ samānam
ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§5.english_close: The essential nature of the individual self is free from the many distinctions constituted by the particular transformations of matter such as gods, humans, and the rest; it has knowledge and bliss alone as its defining qualities. When the distinctions such as godhood, produced by karma, have been destroyed, the self's own specific nature is beyond the reach of speech and knowable only by itself; it can be indicated only thus far: "it is of the nature of knowledge." And this is common to all selves.

ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6.sanskrit_iast: evaṃvidhacidacidātmakaprapañcasyodbhavasthitipralayasaṃsāranirvartanaikahetubhūtaḥ samastaheyapratyanīkānantakalyāṇatayā ca svetarasamastavastuvilakṣaṇasvarūpo 'navadhikātiśayāsaṃkhyeyakalyāṇaguṇagaṇaḥ sarvātmaparabrahmaparajyotiḥparatattvaparamātmasadādiśabdabhedair nikhilavedāntavedyo bhagavān nārāyaṇaḥ puruṣottama ity antaryāmisvarūpam | asya ca vaibhavapratipādanaparāḥ śrutayaḥ svetarasamastacidacidvastujātāntarātmatayā nikhilaniyamanaṃ tacchaktitadaṃśatadvibhūtitadrūpataccharīratattanuprabhṛtibhiḥ śabdais tatsāmānādhikaraṇyena ca pratipādayanti
ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6.english_close: The nature of the inner controller is this: Bhagavān Nārāyaṇa, the supreme Person, who is the single cause of the origination, subsistence, dissolution, and continuance of transmigratory existence for this cosmos constituted by sentient and non-sentient reality; whose own nature is distinct from all other things whatsoever because he is opposed to every defect and possessed of infinite auspiciousness; who is a host of innumerable and unsurpassed auspicious qualities; and who is known through the whole Vedānta under the diverse words "the Self of all," "the supreme Brahman," "the supreme light," "the supreme reality," "the supreme Self," "sat," and the like. And the śrutis that aim to proclaim his glory teach, by terms such as "his power," "his part," "his manifestation," "his form," "his body," "his person," and the like, and also by their co-reference with him, that he is the inner self of every class of sentient and non-sentient entity other than himself and that he governs them all.

rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1..sanskrit_iast: etasya bhagavad-bhakti-rasāmṛta-payonidheḥ / catvāraḥ khalu vakṣyante bhāgāḥ pūrvādayaḥ kramāt
rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1..english_close: Of this ocean of the nectar of the *rasa* (relish) of devotion to Bhagavān, four divisions, beginning with the *pūrva* section, will now indeed be stated in order.

rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1..sanskrit_iast: tatra pūrve vibhāge 'smin bhakti-bheda-nirūpake / anukrameṇa vaktavyaṃ laharīṇāṃ catuṣṭayam
rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1..english_close: Therein, in this prior division which sets forth the kinds of *bhakti*, a set of four waves is to be described in sequence.

rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1..sanskrit_iast: ādyā sāmānya-bhakty-āḍhyā dvitīyā sādhanānvitā / bhāvāśritā tṛtīyā ca turyā prema-nirūpikā
rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1..english_close: The first is rich in general devotion. The second is furnished with *sādhana* (disciplined practice). The third rests on *bhāva* (devotional affect). And the fourth expounds *prema* (consummated love).

rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1..sanskrit_iast: tatrādau suṣṭhu vaiśiṣṭyam asyāḥ kathayituṃ sphuṭam / lakṣaṇaṃ kriyate bhakter uttamāyāḥ satāṃ matam
rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1..english_close: There, at the outset, in order to state clearly the distinctive character of this devotion, the definition is given of *uttamā-bhakti*, as understood by the good.

rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1..sanskrit_iast: anyābhilāṣitā-śūnyaṃ jñāna-karmādy-anāvṛtam / ānukūlyena kṛṣṇānuśīlanaṃ bhaktir uttamā
rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1..english_close: The cultivation of Kṛṣṇa, carried on with favorability, devoid of other desire, and not overlaid by *jñāna* (knowledge-discipline), *karma* (ritual or action-discipline), and the like, is *uttamā-bhakti*.

rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1..sanskrit_iast: yathā śrī-nārada-pañcarātre — sarvopādhi-vinirmuktaṃ tat-paratvena nirmalam / hṛṣīkeṇa hṛṣīkeśa-sevanaṃ bhaktir ucyate
rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1..english_close: As it is said in the revered *Nārada-Pañcarātra*: devotion is called service to Hṛṣīkeśa, by the senses, when one is free from all limiting designations and pure through orientation to him.

rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1..sanskrit_iast: śrī-bhāgavatasya tṛtīya-skandhe ca — ahaituky avyavahitā yā bhaktiḥ puruṣottame
rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1..english_close: And in the third *skandha* of the *Śrī-Bhāgavata*: that devotion to the Supreme Person which is without ulterior motive and without interruption.

rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1..sanskrit_iast: sālokya-sārṣṭi-sāmīpya-sārūpyaikatvam apy uta / dīyamānaṃ na gṛhṇanti vinā mat-sevanaṃ janāḥ
rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1..english_close: Even when *sālokya* (dwelling in the same world), *sārṣṭi* (shared lordly power), *sāmīpya* (nearness), *sārūpya* (likeness of form), and even *ekatva* (oneness) are being offered, people do not accept them without service to me.
That alone is declared to be the ultimate state known as *bhakti-yoga*.
The delineation of the excellence of devotion found in the verse beginning "*sālokya*," because it makes manifest the purity of devotion, comes to rest in the definition.

sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.11.14.sanskrit_iast:
dehaliṅgātmanā kāryā vāsanārūpiṇā kriyā
netinetyātmarūpatvān na me kāryā kriyā kvacit
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.11.14.english_close: Action is to be done by one whose self is [taken to be] body and subtle body, by one constituted by latent dispositions. But because my nature is the self taught by "not this, not this," no action at all is to be done by me.
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.11.14.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.11.16.sanskrit_iast:
amṛtaṃ cābhayaṃ nārtaṃ netītyātmā priyo mama
viparītam ato 'nyad yat tyajet tat sakriyaṃ tataḥ
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.11.16.english_close: The self, taught as "not this, not this," is immortal, fearless, untroubled, and dear to me. Therefore, whatever else is contrary to that, one should abandon, together with action.
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.11.16.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.4.sanskrit_iast:
ātmalābhāt paro nānyo lābhaḥ kaścana vidyate
yadarthā vedavādāś ca smārtāś cāpi tu yāḥ kriyāḥ
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.4.english_close: No gain whatever exists higher than the attainment of the self. It is for the sake of that that the rites grounded in Vedic statement, and likewise those grounded in Smṛti, are [undertaken].
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.4.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.5.sanskrit_iast:
ātmārtho 'pi hi yo lābhaḥ sukhāyeṣṭo viparyayaḥ
ātmalābhaḥ paraḥ prokto nityatvād brahmavedibhiḥ
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.5.english_close: Even the gain that is desired for happiness as though it were for the self is a reversal. The attainment of the self is said by knowers of Brahman to be the highest, because it is permanent.
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.5.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.6.sanskrit_iast:
svayaṃ labdhasvabhāvatvāl lābhas tasya na cānyataḥ
anyāpekṣas tu yo lābhaḥ so 'nyadṛṣṭisamudbhavaḥ
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.6.english_close: Because it is of the nature of something already obtained in oneself, its gain is not from elsewhere. But any gain that depends on another arises from seeing something as other.
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.6.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7.sanskrit_iast:
anyadṛṣṭis tv avidyā syāt tannāśo mokṣa ucyate
jñānenaiva tu so 'pi syād virodhitvān na karmaṇā
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7.english_close: The seeing of something other would be *avidyā*; the destruction of that is called liberation. And that too would come about only through knowledge, not through action, because action is its opposite.
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.8.sanskrit_iast:
karmakāryas tv anityaḥ syād avidyākāmakāraṇaḥ
pramāṇaṃ veda evātra jñānasyādhigame smṛtaḥ
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.8.english_close: What is produced by action would be impermanent, having ignorance and desire as its causes. Here the Veda alone is held to be the means of valid knowledge for the attainment of knowledge.
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.8.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.9.sanskrit_iast:
jñānaikārthaparatvāt taṃ vākyam ekaṃ tato viduḥ
ekatvaṃ hy ātmano jñeyaṃ vākyārthapratipattitaḥ
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.9.english_close: Because it is directed to a single purport, namely knowledge, they therefore know that sentence to be one. For the oneness of the self is what is to be known, and it is known from the apprehension of the sentence-meaning.
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.9.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/vivekacudamani/18.sanskrit_iast: sādhanāny atra catvāri kathitāni manīṣibhiḥ / yeṣu satsv eva sanniṣṭhā yadabhāve na sidhyati //
sankara/vivekacudamani/18.english_close: Here four disciplines have been stated by the wise. Only when these are present does firm establishment attain completion; in the absence of any one of them, it does not succeed.
sankara/vivekacudamani/18.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/shankara_vivekacudamani_gretil.txt).

sankara/vivekacudamani/19.sanskrit_iast:
ādau nityānityavastuvivekaḥ parigamyate
ihāmutraphalabhogavirāgastadanantaram
śamādiṣaṭkasampattirmumukṣutvamiti sphuṭam //
sankara/vivekacudamani/19.english_close: First, discrimination between the eternal and the non-eternal reality is to be grasped. Thereafter comes dispassion toward enjoyment of fruits here and hereafter. Then the sixfold wealth beginning with *śama*; and then, plainly, the desire for liberation.
sankara/vivekacudamani/19.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/vivekacudamani/20.sanskrit_iast: brahma satyaṃ jaganmithyety evaṃrūpo viniścayaḥ / so 'yaṃ nityānityavastuvivekaḥ samudāhṛtaḥ //
sankara/vivekacudamani/20.english_close: “Brahman is real; the world is *mithyā*”: a determination of this form is what is here called discrimination between the eternal and the non-eternal.
sankara/vivekacudamani/20.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/vivekacudamani/21.sanskrit_iast: tadvairāgyaṃ jihāsā yā darśanaśravaṇādibhiḥ / dehādibrahmaparyante hyanitye bhogavastuni //
sankara/vivekacudamani/21.english_close: That dispassion is the wish to abandon the object of enjoyment that is impermanent, extending from the body up to Brahmā, through seeing, hearing, and the like.
sankara/vivekacudamani/21.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.
