sankara/vivekacudamani/22.sanskrit_iast: virajya viṣayavrātāddoṣadṛṣṭyā muhurmuhuḥ / svalakṣye niyatāvasthā manasaḥ śama ucyate //
sankara/vivekacudamani/22.english_close: Having become detached from the mass of objects, through repeated seeing of their defects again and again, the mind’s settled abiding in its own proper aim is called *śama*.
sankara/vivekacudamani/22.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/vivekacudamani/23.sanskrit_iast:
viṣayebhyaḥ parāvartya sthāpanaṃ svasvagolake
ubhayeṣāmindriyāṇāṃ sa damaḥ parikīrtitaḥ
bāhyānālambanaṃ vṛttereṣoparatiruttamā //
sankara/vivekacudamani/23.english_close: Turning them back from the objects, and placing each within its own proper sphere, that is declared to be *dama* for both classes of the senses. The mental modification’s non-reliance on external supports: this is the highest *uparati*.
sankara/vivekacudamani/23.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/vivekacudamani/24.sanskrit_iast: sahanaṃ sarvaduḥkhānāmapratīkārapūrvakam / cintāvilāparahitaṃ sā titikṣā nigadyate //
sankara/vivekacudamani/24.english_close: Endurance of all sufferings, not preceded by retaliatory counter-measures, and free from anxiety and lamentation: that is called *titikṣā*.
sankara/vivekacudamani/24.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/vivekacudamani/25.sanskrit_iast: śāstrasya guruvākyasya satyabuddhyavadhāraṇam / sā śraddhā kathitā sadbhiryayā vastūpalabhyate //
sankara/vivekacudamani/25.english_close: Determinate holding of scripture and the teacher’s word by means of the judgment “this is true”: that is called *śraddhā* by the good, by which the Reality is apprehended.
sankara/vivekacudamani/25.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/vivekacudamani/26.sanskrit_iast: sarvadā sthāpanaṃ buddheḥ śuddhe brahmaṇi sarvadā / tatsamādhānamityuktaṃ na tu cittasya lālanam //
sankara/vivekacudamani/26.english_close: The constant fixing of the intellect in the pure Brahman, always, is called *samādhāna*; not the pampering of the mind.
sankara/vivekacudamani/26.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/vivekacudamani/27.english_close: The desire to release the bonds, imagined by ignorance, extending from egoity to body, through the realization of one’s own true nature, is *mumukṣutva*.
sankara/vivekacudamani/27.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/vivekacudamani/28.sanskrit_iast: mandamadhyamarūpāpi vairāgyeṇa śamādinā / prasādena guroḥ seyaṃ pravṛddhā sūyate phalam //
sankara/vivekacudamani/28.english_close: Even if it is of weak or middling kind, this, when strengthened by dispassion, by *śama* and the rest, and by the teacher’s grace, bears fruit.
sankara/vivekacudamani/28.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/vivekacudamani/29.sanskrit_iast: vairāgyaṃ ca mumukṣutvaṃ tīvraṃ yasya tu vidyate / tasminnevārthavantaḥ syuḥ phalavantaḥ śamādayaḥ //
sankara/vivekacudamani/29.english_close: But in the one for whom dispassion and the desire for liberation are intense, in him alone the disciplines beginning with *śama* are meaningful and fruitful.
sankara/vivekacudamani/29.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/vivekacudamani/30.sanskrit_iast: etayor mandatā yatra viraktatvamumukṣayoḥ / marau salīlavattatra śamāderbhānamātratā //
sankara/vivekacudamani/30.english_close: Where these two are weak, namely dispassion and the desire for liberation, there the appearance of *śama* and the rest is only an appearance, like water in a desert.
sankara/vivekacudamani/30.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/vivekacudamani/31.sanskrit_iast: mokṣakāraṇasāmagryāṃ bhaktir eva garīyasī / svasvarūpānusandhānaṃ bhaktirityabhidhīyate //
sankara/vivekacudamani/31.english_close: Among the total assemblage of the causes of liberation, *bhakti* indeed is the weightiest. *Bhakti* is said to be sustained recollection and inquiry into one’s own true nature.
sankara/vivekacudamani/31.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/vivekacudamani/32.sanskrit_iast:
svātmatattvānusandhānaṃ bhaktirityapare jaguḥ
uktasādhanasaṃpannastattvajijñāsurātmanaḥ
upasīdedguruṃ prājñyaṃ yasmādbandhavimokṣaṇam //
sankara/vivekacudamani/32.english_close: Others have said that *bhakti* is inquiry into the truth of one’s own Self. The seeker who desires knowledge of the Self’s truth, furnished with the disciplines already stated, should approach a wise teacher, from whom there is release from bondage.
sankara/vivekacudamani/32.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/vivekacudamani/33.sanskrit_iast:
śrotriyo 'vṛjino 'kāmahato yo brahmavittamaḥ
brahmaṇy uparataḥ śānto nirindhana ivānalaḥ
ahetukadayāsindhurbandhurānamatāṃ satām //
sankara/vivekacudamani/33.english_close: He is one learned in scripture, sinless, not struck down by desire, the best knower of Brahman. Withdrawn into Brahman, peaceful, like a fire without fuel. An ocean of causeless compassion, a friend to the good who bow before him.
sankara/vivekacudamani/33.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sankara/vivekacudamani/34.sanskrit_iast: tamārādhya guruṃ bhaktyā prahvapraśrayasevanaiḥ / prasannaṃ tamanuprāpya pṛcchejjñātavyamātmanaḥ //
sankara/vivekacudamani/34.english_close: Having propitiated that teacher with *bhakti*, with bowed humility and with acts of service, and having found him gracious, one should ask what is to be known concerning the Self.
sankara/vivekacudamani/34.verification_note: Primary text not yet on disk in clean form; locus is preserved, passage awaits acquisition.

sarvajnatman/samkshepa-sariraka/I.319.sanskrit_iast:
*āśrayatvaviṣayatvabhāginī nirvibhāgacitir eva kevalā |
pūrvasiddhatamaso hi paścimo nāśrayo bhavati nāpi gocaraḥ ||*
sarvajnatman/samkshepa-sariraka/I.319.english_close: Only undivided consciousness itself, and it alone, shares the status of locus and object. For what arises later does not become the locus, nor even the object-field, of the darkness already established beforehand.

sarvajnatman/samkshepa-sariraka/I.327.sanskrit_iast:
*sukṛtaduṣkṛtakarmaṇi kartṛtāṃ
matigatātmacitipratibimbakam |
vrajati tadvad adaḥ paramātmano
jagati yāti tamaḥpratibimbakam ||*
sarvajnatman/samkshepa-sariraka/I.327.english_close: In meritorious and demeritorious action, the reflection of self-consciousness lodged in the intellect comes into agenthood. Likewise, that reflection in darkness, belonging to the supreme Self, goes forth in relation to the world.

sarvajnatman/samkshepa-sariraka/III.275.sanskrit_iast:
*upādhim aupādhikam āntaraṃ cidābhāsanaṃ citpratibimbakaṃ ca /
cidbimbam evaṃ caturaḥ padārthān vivicya jānīhi tadarthabhājaḥ //*
sarvajnatman/samkshepa-sariraka/III.275.english_close: The limiting adjunct, the adjunct-conditioned inner manifestation of consciousness, the reflection of consciousness, and the original consciousness-image as well: having discriminated thus these four referents, know them as the bearers of the meaning of *tat*.

sarvajnatman/samkshepa-sariraka/III.276.sanskrit_iast:
*tathā tvamarthe 'pi catuṣṭayaṃ tad vivecanīyaṃ nipuṇena bhūtvā /
matiś cidābhāsanam evam asyāṃ bimbaṃ tadīyaṃ pratibimbakaṃ ca //*
sarvajnatman/samkshepa-sariraka/III.276.english_close: Similarly, in the meaning of *tvam* too, that fourfold set is to be discriminated, once one has become exact in analysis. There it is the intellect, the manifestation of consciousness in it, the original image in that, and its reflected image as well.

sarvajnatman/samkshepa-sariraka/III.278.sanskrit_iast:
*upādhir antaḥkaraṇaṃ tvamarthe
jīvatvam ābhāsanam atra tadvat /
tadanvitā cit pratibimbam evam
ananvitāṃ bimbam āhuḥ ||*
sarvajnatman/samkshepa-sariraka/III.278.english_close: On the *tvam* side, the limiting adjunct is the internal organ. There, as in the preceding case, the manifestation is individual-selfhood. Consciousness as connected with that is thus the reflected image. Consciousness as unconnected with it, they say, is the original image.

srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/1a.english_close: I bow with my head to the pair of Śrī Kṛṣṇa's feet.

srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/1b.english_close: Connected with whom, the host of *śrutis* (revealed Vedic scriptures) does not enter into contradiction.

srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.english_close: By me, his disciple, the *Vedānta-Kaustubha* - gentle and measured in wording -

srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/3.english_close: as making his intended meaning manifest, is composed for the benefit of the learned.

srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/1.1.1a.english_close: The *tattva* (reality), to begin with, is threefold.

srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/1.1.1b.english_close: because of the distinction among *cit* (sentient selves), *acit* (insentient reality), and Brahman.

srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/1.1.1c.english_close: This is the meaning: Brahman and the *jīvātman* are the independent and the dependent.

srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42a.english_close: This *jīva* is not absolutely different from Śrī Puruṣottama.

srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42b.english_close: Nor is it absolutely non-different. Rather, it is an *aṃśa* (part, share) of the supreme self.

srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42c.sanskrit_iast: aṃśo hy eṣa parasya iti śruteḥ |
srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42c.english_close: For this one is indeed a part of the supreme: so says scripture.

srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42d.sanskrit_iast: aṃśo hi śaktirūpo grāhyaḥ, parāsya śaktiḥ,
srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42d.english_close: For a part is to be understood as of the form of a *śakti* (power): "his supreme power..."

srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42e.english_close: "[The] *jīva* is a limited power, not independent": so says scripture.

sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.9.sanskrit_iast:
anvaya-vyatirekābhyāṃ vinā vākyārtha-bodhanam
na syāt tena vinājñāna-prahāṇaṃ nopapadyate
sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.9.english_close: Without *anvaya-vyatireka* (agreement-and-difference reasoning), there is no grasp of *vākyārtha* (sentence-meaning). Without that, the removal of *ajñāna* (ignorance) does not become possible.

sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.10.sanskrit_iast:
vinājñāna-prahāṇena puruṣārthaḥ su-durlabhaḥ
tasmād yathokta-siddhy-arthaṃ paro grantho 'vatāryate
sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.10.english_close: Without the removal of ignorance, the human end is exceedingly difficult to attain. Therefore, for the sake of establishing what has just been stated, a further section of the treatise is introduced.

sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.11.sanskrit_iast:
varcaskaṃ tv anna-kāryatvād yathānātmeti gamyate
tad-bhāgaḥ sendriyo dehas tadvat kim iti nekṣyate
sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.11.english_close: Just as excrement is understood to be not-Self because it is an effect of food, why is the body, together with the senses, which is a portion of that very food, not seen in the same way?

sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.12.sanskrit_iast:
prāg anātmaiva jagdhaṃ sad ātmatām eti avidyayā
srag-ālepanavad dehaṃ tasmāt paśyed vivikta-dhīḥ
sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.12.english_close: Before being eaten, it is simply not-Self; once eaten, it comes to the status of selfhood only through ignorance. Therefore the man of discriminating understanding should see the body as separate, as one would a garland or an unguent.

vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.4b.english_close: Why? Because of coherent pervasion; because of right pervasion throughout.

vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.4c.english_close: It is; it shines; it does so as dear, because of pervasion in the form of *sat-cid-ānanda* (being-consciousness-bliss).

vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.4d.english_close: Because name and form have the status of properties belonging to the effect.

vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.12-19a.english_close: Here, by Brahman-knowledge and by the cessation of ignorance, fitness for one’s own proper state is accomplished with respect to the attainment of *Puruṣottama*.

vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.12-19b.sanskrit_iast: tato jīve svīyatvena varaṇe bhakti-bhāvāt sahakāri-yogyatā-sampādya puruṣottama-prāptir bhavatīti nirṇeyate.
vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.12-19b.english_close: Thereafter, when the soul is chosen as His own, and from the state of devotion, having effected the auxiliary fitness, the attainment of *Puruṣottama* comes to be: thus it is determined.

vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.12-19c.english_close: By devotion alone am I to be grasped.

vallabha/anubhasya/1.4.26a.sanskrit_iast: tad ātmānaṃ svayam akuruta iti svasyaiva kartṛ-bhāvāt avikṛtatva-śravaṇāc cālaukikatvam.
vallabha/anubhasya/1.4.26a.english_close: “‘It made itself as itself’”: because agency belongs to it alone, and because scripture teaches its unmodifiedness, the matter is non-ordinary.

vallabha/anubhasya/1.4.26b.english_close: Even so, it transforms into the form of the effect.

vallabha/anubhasya/1.4.26c.sanskrit_iast: avikṛtam eva pariṇamate suvarṇaṃ sarvāṇi ca tejasāni.
vallabha/anubhasya/1.4.26c.english_close: Gold, remaining itself unmodified, indeed transforms into all golden products.

vallabha/anubhasya/2.3.43-45a.english_close: The *jīva* indeed is a part of Brahman.

vallabha/anubhasya/2.3.43-45c.sanskrit_iast: na hi brahma niraṃśaṃ sāṃśam iti vā kvacid loke siddhaṃ, vedaika-samadhigamyatvāt.
vallabha/anubhasya/2.3.43-45c.english_close: For Brahman is not established anywhere in ordinary experience as either partless or as possessed of parts, since it is ascertainable only through the Veda.

vallabha/anubhasya/2.3.43-45d.sanskrit_iast: mamaivāṃśo jīvaloke jīvabhūtaḥ sanātana iti smaryate.
vallabha/anubhasya/2.3.43-45d.english_close: “An eternal part of Me, having become a living self in the world of living beings” thus is remembered in *smṛti*.

vasubandhu/trimsika/1.sanskrit_iast: ātmadharmopacāro hi vividho yaḥ pravartate / vijñānapariṇāme 'sau pariṇāmaḥ sa ca tridhā //
vasubandhu/trimsika/1.english_close: The manifold attribution of self and of *dharma*s that goes on takes place in the transformation of cognition; and that transformation is threefold.

vasubandhu/trimsika/2.sanskrit_iast: vipāko mananākhyaśca vijñaptirviṣayasya ca / tatrālayākhyaṃ vijñānaṃ vipākaḥ sarvabījakam //
vasubandhu/trimsika/2.english_close: [That transformation is] maturation, that called *manana* (mentation), and the presentation of the object. Of these, the cognition called *ālaya* is maturation, and it is possessed of all seeds.

vasubandhu/trimsika/3.english_close: And that is such that its appropriation and its presentation of location are not distinctly cognized. It is always conjoined with contact, attention, awareness, apperception, and volition.

vasubandhu/trimsika/4.sanskrit_iast: upekṣā vedanā tatrānivṛtāvyākṛtaṃ ca tat / tathā sparśādayastacca vartate srotasaughavat //
vasubandhu/trimsika/4.english_close: There, the feeling is equanimous; and that is unobscured and ethically indeterminate. So too are contact and the rest; and that proceeds like the rush of a stream.

vasubandhu/trimsika/5.sanskrit_iast: tasya vyāvṛtirarhatve tadāśritya pravartate / tadālambaṃ manonāma vijñānaṃ mananātmakam //
vasubandhu/trimsika/5.english_close: Its cessation occurs in arhatship. Depending on that, and taking that as its object, there proceeds the cognition called *manas*, whose nature is mentation.

vasubandhu/trimsika/6.english_close: It is always accompanied by four afflictions, obscuring yet ethically indeterminate, namely those called self-view, self-delusion, self-conceit, and self-attachment.

vasubandhu/trimsika/7.sanskrit_iast: yatrajastanmayairanyaiḥ sparśādyaiścārhato na tat / na nirodhasamāpattau mārge lokottare na ca //
vasubandhu/trimsika/7.english_close: Together with the other factors, such as contact, that are born where it is born and are of its own kind, that is not present in the arhat. Nor is it present in the attainment of cessation; nor on the supramundane path.

vasubandhu/trimsika/8.sanskrit_iast: dvitīyaḥ pariṇāmo 'yaṃ tṛtīyaḥ ṣaḍvidhasya yā / viṣayasyopalabdhiḥ sā kuśalākuśalādvayā //
vasubandhu/trimsika/8.english_close: This is the second transformation. The third is the apprehension of the object, and that is sixfold, wholesome, unwholesome, or neither.

vasubandhu/trimsika/9.english_close: That is associated with the universal, the determinate, and the wholesome mental factors. It is associated likewise with the afflictions, the subsidiary afflictions, and the three feelings.

vasubandhu/trimsika/10.sanskrit_iast: ādyāḥ sparśādayaśchandādhimokṣasmṛtayaḥ saha / samādhidhībhyāṃ niyatāḥ śraddhātha hrirapatrapā //
vasubandhu/trimsika/10.english_close: The first are contact and the rest. Desire, resolve, and memory, together with concentration and understanding, are the determinate factors. Then [come] faith, shame, and moral embarrassment.

vasubandhu/trimsika/11.sanskrit_iast: alobhādi trayaṃ vīryaṃ praśrabdhiḥ sāpramādikā / ahiṃsā kuśalāḥ kleśā rāgapratighamūḍhayaḥ //
vasubandhu/trimsika/11.english_close: The triad beginning with non-greed, vigor, pliancy, together with heedfulness and the allied factors, and non-harming are wholesome. The afflictions are passion, hostility, and delusion.

vasubandhu/trimsika/12.sanskrit_iast: mānadṛgvicikitsāśca krodhopanahane punaḥ / mrakṣaḥ pradāśa īrṣyātha mātsaryaṃ saha māyayā //
vasubandhu/trimsika/12.english_close: [Further afflictions are] conceit, false view, and doubt. Then again [come] anger and resentment, concealment, malice, envy, avarice, together with deceit.

vasubandhu/trimsika/13.sanskrit_iast: śāṭhyaṃ mado 'vihiṃsā hrīratrapā styānamuddhavaḥ / āśraddhamatha kausīdyaṃ pramādo muṣitā smṛtiḥ //
vasubandhu/trimsika/13.english_close: Guile, intoxicated pride, harmfulness, shamelessness, lack of embarrassment, torpor, and agitation, then lack of faith, sloth, carelessness, and stolen-away mindfulness.
vasubandhu/trimsika/13.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/vasubandhu_trimsika.txt).

vasubandhu/trimsika/14.sanskrit_iast: vikṣepo 'samprajanyaṃ ca kaukṛtyaṃ middhameva ca / vitarkaśca vicāraścetyupakleśā dvaye dvidhā //
vasubandhu/trimsika/14.english_close: Distraction, non-alertness, remorse, and sleep as well, and thought-initialization and discursive examination: these are the indeterminate factors, two and two.

vasubandhu/trimsika/15.sanskrit_iast: pañcānāṃ mūlavijñāne yathāpratyayamudbhavaḥ / vijñānānāṃ saha na vā taraṅgāṇāṃ yathā jale //
vasubandhu/trimsika/15.english_close: In the root-cognition of the five, there is arising according to conditions. The consciousnesses arise together, or not, like waves in water.

vasubandhu/trimsika/16.english_close: The arising of mental consciousness occurs always, except among the non-percipient beings, and except in the two attainments, from sleep, from fainting, and from the mindless condition.

vasubandhu/trimsika/17.sanskrit_iast: vijñānapariṇāmo 'yaṃ vikalpo yadvikalpyate / tena tannāsti tenedaṃ sarvaṃ vijñaptimātrakam //
vasubandhu/trimsika/17.english_close: This transformation of cognition is conceptual construction. What is constructed does not exist by that [construction]; therefore all this is only cognitive presentation.

vasubandhu/trimsika/18.sanskrit_iast: sarvabījaṃ hi vijñānaṃ pariṇāmastathā tathā / yātyanyonyavaśād yena vikalpaḥ sa sa jāyate //
vasubandhu/trimsika/18.english_close: For cognition, possessed of all seeds, undergoes transformation in this way and that. Under mutual influence, from that there arises just such and such conceptual construction.

vasubandhu/trimsika/19.sanskrit_iast: karmaṇo vāsanā grāhadvayavāsanayā saha / kṣīṇe pūrvavipāke 'nyadvipākaṃ janayanti tat //
vasubandhu/trimsika/19.english_close: The perfumings of karma, together with the perfuming of the duality of grasper and grasped, when the prior maturation is exhausted, generate another maturation.

vasubandhu/trimsika/20.sanskrit_iast: yena yena vikalpena yadyad vastu vikalpyate / parikalpita evāsau svabhāvo na sa vidyate //
vasubandhu/trimsika/20.english_close: By whatever conceptual construction whatever thing is constructed, that is precisely the imagined nature; it does not exist.

vasubandhu/trimsika/21.sanskrit_iast: paratantrasvabhāvastu vikalpaḥ pratyayodbhavaḥ / niṣpannastasya pūrveṇa sadā rahitatā tu yā //
vasubandhu/trimsika/21.english_close: But the dependent nature is conceptual construction arising from conditions. The perfected [nature] is that which is its perpetual absence of the former.

vasubandhu/trimsika/22.sanskrit_iast: ata eva sa naivānyo nānanyaḥ paratantrataḥ / anityatādivad vācyo nādṛṣṭe 'smin sa dṛśyate //
vasubandhu/trimsika/22.english_close: For that very reason, it is neither different from the dependent [nature] nor non-different from it. It is to be spoken of as one speaks of impermanence and the like; when this is not seen, that is not seen.

vasubandhu/trimsika/23.sanskrit_iast: trividhasya svabhāvasya trividhāṃ niḥsvabhāvatām / sandhāya sarvadharmāṇāṃ deśitā niḥsvabhāvatā //
vasubandhu/trimsika/23.english_close: With reference to the threefold absence of own-nature belonging to the threefold nature, the absence of own-nature of all *dharma*s has been taught.

vasubandhu/trimsika/24.sanskrit_iast: prathamo lakṣaṇenaiva niḥsvabhāvo 'paraḥ punaḥ / na svayambhāva etasyetyaparā niḥsvabhāvatā //
vasubandhu/trimsika/24.english_close: The first is without own-nature by its very defining mark. The next, again, is another kind of absence of own-nature: it is not self-produced.

vasubandhu/trimsika/25.sanskrit_iast: dharmāṇāṃ paramārthaśca sa yatastathatāpi saḥ / sarvakālaṃ tathābhāvāt saiva vijñaptimātratā //
vasubandhu/trimsika/25.english_close: And that is the ultimate of the *dharma*s; because it is suchness, that too is it. Because it is always thus, that itself is *vijñapti-mātratā*.

vasubandhu/trimsika/26.english_close: So long as cognition does not become established in *vijñapti-mātratā*, the latent tendency of the duality of grasping does not cease.

vasubandhu/trimsika/27.sanskrit_iast: vijñaptimātramevedamityapi hyupalambhataḥ / sthāpayannagrataḥ kiñcit tanmātre nāvatiṣṭhate //
vasubandhu/trimsika/27.english_close: Even when there is the apprehension, "This indeed is mere cognitive presentation," if one places even something at the front, one does not become established in mere-that.
vasubandhu/trimsika/27.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/vasubandhu_trimsika.txt).

vasubandhu/trimsika/28.sanskrit_iast: yadālambanaṃ vijñānaṃ naivopalabhate tadā / sthitaṃ vijñānamātratve grāhyābhāve tadagrahāt //
vasubandhu/trimsika/28.english_close: When cognition no longer apprehends any object-support, then it is established in mere cognition, because with the absence of the grasped there is no grasping of that.

vasubandhu/trimsika/29.sanskrit_iast: acitto 'nupalambho 'sau jñānaṃ lokottaraṃ ca tat / āśrayasya parāvṛttirdvidhā dauṣṭhulyahānitaḥ //
vasubandhu/trimsika/29.english_close: That is non-minded non-apprehension; and that is supramundane knowledge. It is the turning-about of the basis, through the abandonment of the twofold grossness.

vasubandhu/trimsika/30.sanskrit_iast: sa evānasravo dhāturacintyaḥ kuśalo dhruvaḥ / sukho vimuktikāyo 'sau dharmākhyo 'yaṃ mahāmuneḥ //
vasubandhu/trimsika/30.english_close: That itself is the undefiled element, inconceivable, wholesome, and stable. It is blissful. It is the body of liberation. It is this of the Great Sage that is called the *dharma*-body.

vasubandhu/vimsatika/Proem.sanskrit_iast: mahāyāne traidhātukaṃ vijñaptimātraṃ vyavasthāpyate | cittamātraṃ bho jinaputrā yad uta traidhātukam iti sūtrāt | cittaṃ mano vijñānaṃ vijñaptiś ceti paryāyāḥ | cittam atra sasaṃprayogam abhipretam | mātram ity arthapratiṣedhārtham |
vasubandhu/vimsatika/Proem.english_close: In the Mahāyāna, the threefold world is established as *vijñaptimātra* (nothing but representation). "'The threefold world, O sons of the Victor, is mind-only'": so says the sūtra. "*Citta*, *manas*, *vijñāna*, and *vijñapti* are synonyms." Here *citta* is intended together with its associates. "Only" is for the sake of excluding an object.

vasubandhu/vimsatika/1.sanskrit_iast:
vijñaptimātram evedam asadarthāvabhāsanāt |
yadvat taimirikasyāsat keśoṇḍukādidarśanaṃ || 1 ||
vasubandhu/vimsatika/1.english_close: This indeed is only representation, because unreal objects appear. It is as when a man afflicted with ophthalmia sees unreal hairs, globules, and the like.

vasubandhu/vimsatika/Heading.english_close: At this point an objection is raised: "There is no object at all."

vasubandhu/vimsatika/2.sanskrit_iast:
na deśakālaniyamaḥ saṃtānāniyamo na ca |
na ca kṛtyakriyā yuktā vijñaptir yadi nārthataḥ || 2 ||
vasubandhu/vimsatika/2.english_close: There would be no restriction of place, no restriction of time, and no restriction across continua. Nor would practical efficacy be coherent, if representation were without an object.

vasubandhu/vimsatika/on Vvs 2.sanskrit_iast: kim uktaṃ bhavati | yadi vinā rūpādyarthena rūpādivijñaptir utpadyate na rūpādyarthāt | kasmāt kvacid deśa utpadyate na sarvatra | tatraiva ca deśe kadācid utpadyate na sarvadā | taddeśakālapratiṣṭhitānāṃ sarveṣāṃ saṃtānāniyama utpadyate na kevalam ekasya | yathā taimirikāṇāṃ saṃtāne keśādyābhāso nānyeṣāṃ | kasmād yat taimirikaiḥ keśabhramarādi dṛśyate tena keśādikriyā na kriyate na ca tad anyair na kriyate | yad annapānavastraviṣāyudhādi svapne dṛśyate tenānnādikriyā na kriyate na ca tad anyair na kriyate | gandharvanagareṇāsattvān nagarakriyā na kriyate na ca tad anyair na kriyate | tasmād asadabhāvāvabhāsane deśakālaniyamaḥ saṃtānāniyamaḥ kṛtyakriyā ca na yujyate | na khalu na yujyate | yasmāt |
vasubandhu/vimsatika/on Vvs 2.english_close: What is meant is this. If a representation of form and the like arises without an object such as form and the like, and not from such an object, then why does it arise in one place and not everywhere? Why, in that very place, does it arise at one time and not always? Why does there arise a regularity across the continua of all those situated in that place and time, and not of one alone, whereas the appearance of hairs and the like occurs in the continuum of the taimirikas and not of others? Why is no action involving hairs and the like performed by means of what the taimirikas see as hairs, bees, and the like, and why is no such action performed by others either? What food, drink, clothing, poison, weapon, and the like are seen in a dream: by means of that no eating and the like is performed, nor by others either. Because a city of gandharvas does not exist, no city-action is performed by means of it, nor by others either. Therefore, when what is non-existent appears, restriction of place and time, restriction across continua, and practical efficacy do not fit. No: it is not the case that they do not fit. For:

vasubandhu/vimsatika/and Preta Replies.sanskrit_iast: deśādiniyamaḥ siddhaḥ svapnavat | svapna iva svapnavat | kathaṃ tāvat | svapne vināpy arthena kvacid eva deśe kiṃcid bhramarārāmastrīpurūṣādikaṃ dṛśyate na sarvatra | tatraiva ca deśe kadācid dṛśyate na sarvakālam iti siddho vināpy arthena deśakālaniyamaḥ | pretavat punaḥ | saṃtānāniyamaḥ siddha iti vartate | pretānām iva pretavat | kathaṃ siddhaḥ samaṃ | sarvaiḥ pūyanadyādidarśane || 3 || pūyapūrṇā nadī pūyanadī | ghṛtaghaṭavat | tulyakarmavipākāvasthā hi pretāḥ sarve 'pi pūyapūrṇāṃ nadīṃ paśyanti naika eva | yathā pūyapūrṇām evaṃ mūtrapurīṣādipūrṇāṃ daṇḍāsidharaiś ca puruṣair adhiṣṭhitām ity ādigrahaṇena | evaṃ saṃtānāniyamo vijñaptinām asaty apy arthe siddhaḥ ||
vasubandhu/vimsatika/and Preta Replies.english_close: Restriction of place and the rest is established, as in a dream. As in a dream, that is to say. How so? In a dream, even without an object, something or other, such as bees, pleasure-gardens, women, and men, is seen only in a certain place, not everywhere. And in that very place it is seen at some time, not at all times. Thus, even without an object, restriction of place and time is established. Again, restriction across continua is established, as in the case of pretas. It is established, that is to say, just as among pretas. How is it established? In the seeing by all alike of a river of pus and the like. A river full of pus is a "river of pus," like a jar of ghee. For pretas, all of them being in the condition of an equal karmic maturation, all see a river full of pus, not only one. And by the "and the like" one should include a river full of urine and excrement, and one occupied by men bearing clubs and swords. Thus regularity across continua is established for representations even though the object is unreal.

vasubandhu/vimsatika/and Hell.sanskrit_iast: svapnopaghātavat kṛtyakriyā siddha iti veditavyaṃ | yathā svapne dvayasamāpattim antareṇa śukravisargalakṣaṇaḥ svapnopaghātaḥ | evaṃ tāvad anyānyair dṛṣṭāntair deśakālaniyamādicatuṣṭayaṃ siddham | narakavat punaḥ | sarvaṃ siddham iti veditavyaṃ | narakeṣv iva narakavat | kathaṃ siddham | narakapālādidarśane taiś ca bādhane || 4 || yathā hi narakeṣu nārakāṇāṃ narakapālādidarśanaṃ deśakālaniyamena siddhaṃ | śvavāyasāyasaparvatādyāgamanagamanadarśanaṃ cety ādigrahaṇena | sarveṣāṃ ca naikasyaiva taiś ca tadbādhanaṃ siddham asatsv api narakapālādiṣu samānasvakarmavipākādhipatyāt | tathānyatrāpi sarvam etad deśakālaniyamādicatuṣṭayaṃ siddham iti veditavyaṃ ||
vasubandhu/vimsatika/and Hell.english_close: Practical efficacy is to be understood as established, as in dream-affection. For just as in a dream there is a dream-affection marked by seminal emission even without sexual intercourse, so too, by other examples of the same sort, the whole fourfold set beginning with restriction of place and time is established. Again, everything is to be understood as established, as in hell. How is it established? In the seeing of hell-guards and the like, and in being tormented by them. For in the hells the seeing by the hell-beings of hell-guards and the like is established with restriction of place and time. By the "and the like" one should include the seeing of dogs, crows, iron mountains, and the like coming and going. And the torment inflicted by them is established for all, not for one alone, even though the hell-guards and the rest are unreal, because the maturation of each one's own karma exercises the same domination. So too elsewhere this whole fourfold set beginning with restriction of place and time should be understood as established.

vasubandhu/vimsatika/Hell-Guards, Animal.sanskrit_iast: kiṃ punaḥ kāraṇaṃ narakapālās te ca śvāno vāyasāś ca sattvā neṣyante | ayogāt | na hi te nārakā yujyante | tathaiva tadduḥkhāpratisaṃvedanāt | parasparaṃ yātayatām ime nārakā ime narakapālā iti vyavasthā na syāt | tulyākṛtipramāṇabalānāṃ ca parasparaṃ yātayatāṃ na tathā bhayaṃ syāt | dāhaduḥkhaṃ ca pradīptāyām ayomayyāṃ bhūmāv asahamānāḥ kathaṃ tatra parān yātayeyuḥ | anārakāṇāṃ vā narake kutaḥ saṃbhavaḥ | kathaṃ tāvat tiraścāṃ svargasaṃbhavaḥ | evaṃ narakeṣu tiryakpretaviśeṣāṇāṃ narakapālādīnāṃ saṃbhavaḥ syāt | tiraścāṃ saṃbhavaḥ svarge yathā na narake tathā | na pretānāṃ yatas tajjaṃ duḥkhaṃ nānubhavanti te || 5 ||
vasubandhu/vimsatika/Hell-Guards, Animal.english_close: But why are those hell-guards, and those dogs and crows, not admitted to be sentient beings? Because that is unsuitable. For they do not fit as hell-beings. Likewise, because they do not experience that suffering. If beings were mutually tormenting one another, there could be no settled distinction, "these are the hell-beings; these are the hell-guards." And if beings of equal shape, size, and strength were mutually tormenting one another, there would not be that kind of fear. And if they themselves could not endure the pain of burning on a blazing iron ground, how could they there torment others? Or how could non-hell-beings arise in hell? Well then, how is there the arising of animals in heaven? In that same way there might be in hell the arising of special animals or pretas, namely hell-guards and the like. As the arising of animals is in heaven and not in hell, so too the arising of pretas is not there, because they do not experience the suffering produced there.

vasubandhu/vimsatika/Karma, Vāsanā, and.sanskrit_iast: teṣāṃ tarhi nārakāṇāṃ karmabhis tatra bhūtaviśeṣāḥ saṃbhavanti varṇākṛtipramāṇabalaviśiṣṭā ye narakapālādisaṃjñāṃ pratilabhante | tathā ca pariṇamanti | yad dvidhāṃ hastavikṣepādikriyāṃ kurvanto dṛśyante bhayotpādanārthaṃ | yathā meṣākṛtayaḥ parvatā āgacchanto gacchanto 'yaḥśālmalīvane ca kaṇṭakā adhomukhībhavanta ūrdhvamukhībhavantaś ceti | na te na saṃbhavanty eva | yadi tatkarmabhis tatra bhūtānāṃ saṃbhavas tathā | iṣyate pariṇāmaś ca kiṃ vijñānasya neṣyate || 6 || vijñānasyaiva tatkarmabhis tathā pariṇāmaḥ kasmān neṣyate kiṃ punar bhūtāni kalpyante || api ca | karmaṇo vāsanānyatra phalam anyatra kalpyate | tatraiva neṣyate yatra vāsanā kiṃ nu kāraṇaṃ || 7 || yena hi karmaṇā nārakāṇāṃ tatra tādṛśo bhūtānāṃ saṃbhavaḥ kalpyate pariṇāmaś ca tasya karmaṇo vāsanā teṣāṃ vijñānasaṃtānasaṃniviṣṭā nānyatra | yatraiva ca vāsanā tatraiva tasyāḥ phalaṃ tādṛśo vijñānapariṇāmaḥ kiṃ neṣyate | yatra vāsanā nāsti tatra tasyāḥ phalaṃ kalpyata iti kim atra kāraṇaṃ || āgamaḥ kāraṇaṃ | yadi vijñānam eva rūpādipratibhāsaṃ syān na rūpādiko 'rthas tadā rūpādyāyatanāstitvaṃ bhagavatā noktaṃ syāt | akāraṇam etad yasmāt | rūpādyāyatanāstitvaṃ tadvineyajanaṃ prati | abhiprāyavaśād uktam upapādukasattvavat || 8 || yathāsti sattva upapāduka ity uktaṃ bhagavatā abhiprāyavaśāc cittasaṃtatyanucchedam āyatyām abhipretya | nāstīha sattva ātmā vā dharmās tv ete sahetukāḥ iti vacanāt | evaṃ rūpādyāyatanāstitvam apy uktaṃ bhagavatā taddeśanāvineyajanam adhikṛtyety ābhiprāyikaṃ tad vacanaṃ ||
vasubandhu/vimsatika/Karma, Vāsanā, and.english_close: "Then, for those hell-beings, special elements arise there from their karmas, distinguished by color, shape, size, and strength, and these acquire the designation 'hell-guards' and the like. And they transform in that way, since they are seen making twofold gestures of the hands and the like for the sake of producing fear, just as sheep-shaped mountains are seen coming and going, and just as in the forest of iron śālmalī the thorns become downward-facing and upward-facing." No: those things do not come into being in that way at all. If it is admitted that by those karmas there is there an arising of elements, and also a transformation, why is a transformation of consciousness not admitted? Why is precisely a transformation of consciousness by those karmas not admitted? Why instead are elements imagined? And again: the impression of the karma is posited in one place, the fruit in another. Why is it not admitted precisely there where the impression is? What reason is there? For by the karma through which such an arising of elements there for the hell-beings is imagined, and also a transformation, the impression of that karma is lodged in their continuum of consciousness, not elsewhere. And precisely where the impression is, why is not its fruit there, namely such a transformation of consciousness, admitted? If the fruit of that impression is imagined where the impression is not, what reason is there for that? "'Scripture' is the reason. If only consciousness were the appearance of form and the rest, and there were no external thing such as form and the rest, then the Blessed One would not have taught the existence of the sense-spheres of form and the rest." That is no reason. For: "The existence of the sense-spheres of form and the rest was spoken, with regard to those disciples to be trained by that teaching, by force of intention, just as [the teaching of] a spontaneously born being." Just as the Blessed One, by force of intention, spoke of "a being that is spontaneously born," intending the non-interruption of the stream of consciousness in the future, because of the statement, "Here there is no being and no self; these are only causal dharmas," so too the existence of the sense-spheres of form and the rest was spoken by the Blessed One with regard to those to be trained by that teaching. That statement is therefore intentional.
vasubandhu/vimsatika/Karma, Vāsanā, and.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/vasubandhu_vimsatika.txt).

vasubandhu/vimsatika/9 and Entry into *P.sanskrit_iast: ko 'trābhiprāyaḥ | yataḥ svabījād vijñaptir yadābhāsā pravartate | dvividhāyatanatvena te tasyā munir abravīt || 9 || kim uktaṃ bhavati | rūpapratibhāsā vijñaptir yataḥ svabījāt pariṇāmaviśeṣaprāptād utpadyate tac ca bījaṃ yat pratibhāsā ca sā te tasyā vijñapteś cakṣūrūpāyatanatvena yathākramam bhagavān abravīt | evaṃ yāvat spraṣṭavyapratibhāsā vijñaptir yataḥ svabījāt pariṇāmaviśeṣaprāptād utpadyate tac ca bījaṃ yat pratibhāsā ca sā te tasyā kāyaspraṣṭavyāyatanatvena yathākramam bhagavān abravīd ity ayam abhiprāyaḥ || evaṃ punar abhiprāyavaśena deśayitvā ko guṇāḥ | tathā pudgalanairātmyapraveśo hi | tathā hi deśyamāne pudgalanairātmyaṃ praviśanti | dvayād vijñānaṣaṭkaṃ pravartate | na tu kaścid eko draṣṭāsti na yāvan manteti evaṃ viditvā ye pudgalanairātmyadeśanāvineyās te pudgalanairātmyaṃ praviśanti |
vasubandhu/vimsatika/9 and Entry into *P.english_close: What is the intention here? When a representation, bearing a given appearance, proceeds from its own seed, the Sage spoke of those two as the twofold sense-sphere of that representation. What is meant is this. When a representation that has the appearance of form arises from its own seed, a seed that has reached a specific transformation, then that seed and that appearance, those two, the Blessed One described, in order, as the eye-sphere and the form-sphere of that representation. So too, up to the representation that has the appearance of the tangible: when it arises from its own seed that has reached a specific transformation, that seed and that appearance, those two, the Blessed One described, in order, as the body-sphere and the tangible-sphere of that representation. This is the intention. What advantage is there in teaching in this way, by force of intention? There is entry into *pudgala-nairātmya* (the non-selfhood of the person). For when it is taught in this way, those fit to be disciplined by the teaching of the person's non-selfhood enter into that non-selfhood. They understand that from the two the sixfold consciousness proceeds, but that there is no one single seer, nor indeed one single thinker, and so on.
vasubandhu/vimsatika/9 and Entry into *P.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/buddhist/vasubandhu_vimsatika.txt).

vasubandhu/vimsatika/10 and Entry into *.sanskrit_iast: anyathā punaḥ | deśanā dharmanairātmyapraveśaḥ | anyatheti vijñaptimātradeśanā | kathaṃ dharmanairātmyapraveśaḥ | vijñaptimātram idaṃ rūpādidharmapratibhāsam utpadyate na tu rūpādilakṣaṇo dharmaḥ ko 'py astīti viditvā | yadi tarhi sarvathā dharmo nāsti tad api vijñaptimātraṃ nāstīti kathaṃ tarhi vyavasthāpyate | na khalu sarvathā dharmo nāstīty evaṃ dharmanairātmyapraveśo bhavati | api tu | kalpitātmanā || 10 || yo bālair dharmāṇāṃ svabhāvo grāhyagrāhakādiḥ parikalpitas tena kalpitenātmanā teṣāṃ nairātmyaṃ na tv anabhilāpyenātmanā yo buddhānāṃ viṣaya iti | evaṃ vijñaptimātrasyāpi vijñaptyantaraparikalpitenātmanā nairātmyapraveśāt vijñaptimātravyavasthāpanayā sarvadharmāṇāṃ nairātmyapraveśo bhavati na tu tadastitvāpavādāt | itarathā hi vijñapter api vijñaptyantaram arthaḥ syād iti vijñaptimātratvaṃ na sidhyetārthavatītvād vijñaptīnāṃ ||
vasubandhu/vimsatika/10 and Entry into *.english_close: Again, on another reading, the teaching is an entry into *dharma-nairātmya* (the non-selfhood of dharmas). "On another reading" means: as a teaching of *vijñaptimātra* (representation-only). How is it an entry into the non-selfhood of dharmas? By understanding that this alone is representation, arising with the appearance of dharmas such as form, whereas there is no dharma at all having the defining character of form and the rest. "If then there is in every way no dharma, then this representation-only too does not exist; how then is it established?" Entry into the non-selfhood of dharmas does not occur by teaching that there is absolutely no dharma at all. Rather, it occurs with respect to an imagined selfhood. The own-nature of dharmas that childish people imagine, namely object, subject, and the like, it is with that imagined selfhood that their selflessness is intended, not with an inexpressible selfhood which is the object of the Buddhas. Thus, since even representation-only is entered as selfless with respect to a selfhood imagined by another representation, the establishment of representation-only brings about entry into the selflessness of all dharmas, and not a denial of their existence. Otherwise, for representation too, another representation would become its object. Then representation-only would not be established, because representations would have objective content.

vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1.1.sanskrit_iast:
cāturvyūhaṃ paraṃ brahma ṣāḍguṇya-parikarmitam |
pañcarātrasya kṛtsnasya prasūtiṃ paryupāsmahe ||
vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1.1.english_close: We reverentially attend upon the supreme Brahman as the four *vyūha*-s (emanational forms), adorned with *ṣāḍguṇya* (the six divine excellences). We reverentially attend upon him as the source of the entire Pāñcarātra.

vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1, p. 2.sanskrit_iast: atra tāvat pratyakṣita-samasta-vedārtha-tattva-sthitibhiḥ pārāśarya-prabhṛtibhir mahābhāratādiṣu bhagavacchāstrasya sārvabhaumaṃ prāmāṇyaṃ pratyapādi |
vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1, p. 2.english_close: Here, to begin with, the universal *prāmāṇya* (authoritativeness) of the Lord's scripture was set forth in the *Mahābhārata* and related texts by Pārāśarya and the others, who had directly seen the settled truth of the whole meaning of the Veda.

vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1, p. 3.sanskrit_iast:
āgamasiddhānto mantrasiddhāntas tantrasiddhāntas tantrāntarasiddhānta iti |
catvāraś cete siddhāntā ṛgādivad eva śākhābhedair avantaratantrair bhidyante ||
vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1, p. 3.english_close: "Āgama-siddhānta," "mantra-siddhānta," "tantra-siddhānta," and "tantrāntara-siddhānta": thus [the four are named]. These four *siddhānta*-s are differentiated, just like the Ṛg and the rest, by branch-divisions and by subordinate tantras.

vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1, p. 2.sanskrit_iast:
avirodhopayogābhyām ubhayor api śāstrayoḥ |
madhyasthair hi kṛto dharmo vidyāsthāna-niveśanam ||
vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1, p. 2.english_close: On the strength of the non-contradiction and the usefulness of both *śāstra*-s, impartial authorities have indeed laid down the norm: the assigning of each to its proper domain of learning and practice.

vedanta-desika/rahasya-traya-sara/103–10.sanskrit_iast: pratitantram āvatu - maṟṟumuḷḷa siddhāntikaḷ oruvarum icaiyātē taṉṉuṭaiya siddhāntattukkē asādhāraṇamāṉa artham.
vedanta-desika/rahasya-traya-sara/103–10.english_close: "*Pratitantra*" means: a thesis peculiar to one's own system, to which none of the adherents of the other systems gives assent.

vedanta-desika/rahasya-traya-sara/104.sanskrit_iast: iṅgu vedāntikaḷāna nammuṭaiya darśanattukkē asādhāraṇamumāy, pradhānamumāna artham eteniṉil, cetanācetanangaḷukkum īśvaranukkumuṇṭāna śarīrātmabhāva-sambandhādigaḷ.
vedanta-desika/rahasya-traya-sara/104.english_close: Here, if one asks what the thesis is that is both distinctive to our Vedāntic system and principal within it, it is the relations beginning with the body-self relation that obtain between sentient beings, non-sentient things, and Īśvara.

vedanta-desika/rahasya-traya-sara/104–10.sanskrit_iast: cetanācetanangaḷukku śarīratvamāvatu - niyamana īśvaranaip paṟṟi dhāryamumāy, niyāmyamumāy, śeṣamumāna dravyamāy irukkai.
vedanta-desika/rahasya-traya-sara/104–10.english_close: And the sentient and the non-sentient have bodyhood in this sense: with respect to the regulating Īśvara, they are what is borne, what is controlled, and a substance that is subordinate.

vedanta-desika/sarvartha-siddhi/1.2.sanskrit_iast:
nānā-siddhānta-nīti-śrama-vimala-dhiyo 'nanta-sūres tanūjo
vaiśvāmitrasya pautro vitata-makha-vidheḥ puṇḍarīkākṣa-sūreḥ |
śrutvā rāmānujāryāt sad-asad api tatas tattva-muktā-kalāpaṃ
vyātānīd veṅkaṭeśo varada-guru-kṛpā-lambhita-uddāma-bhūmā || 2 ||
vedanta-desika/sarvartha-siddhi/1.2.english_close: [He was] the son of Ananta Sūri, whose intelligence had been made clear by the labor of mastering many doctrinal positions. [He was] the grandson, in the Vaiśvāmitra line, of Puṇḍarīkākṣa Sūri, performer of expansive sacrifices. Having heard, from Rāmānujārya, both what is *sat* (authoritative and to be appropriated) and what is *asat* (the contrary), then Veṅkaṭeśa unfolded the *Tattva-Muktā-Kalāpa*, his amplitude of standing having been won through the grace of his teacher Varada.

vedanta-desika/sarvartha-siddhi/ad 1.2a.english_close: To make what is about to be undertaken worthy of trust, he shows the distinctiveness of the speaker's lineage, by the word “many”.

vedanta-desika/sarvartha-siddhi/ad 1.2b.sanskrit_iast: sat — prāmāṇikaṃ mumukṣor upādeyaṃ ca; tad anyad asat |
vedanta-desika/sarvartha-siddhi/ad 1.2b.english_close: “*Sat*” means what is authoritative, and also what is to be appropriated by the seeker of liberation. What is other than that is *asat*.

vedanta-desika/sarvartha-siddhi/ad 1.2c.sanskrit_iast: satas sattvenāsataś cāsattvena śravaṇam iheṣṭam, tataḥ — śravaṇād eva hetoḥ || 2 ||
vedanta-desika/sarvartha-siddhi/ad 1.2c.english_close: Here, the hearing of what is *sat* precisely as *sat*, and of what is *asat* precisely as *asat*, is what is desired. Therefore, because hearing itself is the cause.

vedanta-desika/tattva-mukta-kalapa/verse.sanskrit_iast:
jayati sakalavidyāvāhinījanmaśailo janipathaparivṛttiśrāntaviśrāntiśākhī |
nikhilakumatimāyāśarvarībālasūryo nigamajaladhivelāpūrṇacandro yatīndraḥ || 1 ||
vedanta-desika/tattva-mukta-kalapa/verse.english_close: *Yatīndra* triumphs: he is the mountain from which the river of all learning is born, the branch of repose for those wearied by circling along the road of birth. He is the young sun to the night that consists in the delusive *māyā* of every false doctrine, the full moon that fills out the shoreline of the ocean of Vedic revelation.

vidyaranya/jivanmukti-viveka/opening.sanskrit_iast: keyaṃ jīvanmuktiḥ, kiṃ vā tatpramāṇam, kathaṃ vā tatsiddhiḥ; ity etasya praśnatrayasyottaraṃ nirūpitam. siddhyā vā kiṃ prayojanam ity asya caturthapraśnasyottaram idānīm abhidhīyate — jñānarakṣā, tapaḥ, visaṃvādābhāvaḥ, duḥkhanāśaḥ, sukhāvirbhāvaś ca santi pañca prayojanāni.
vidyaranya/jivanmukti-viveka/opening.english_close: “What is this *jīvanmukti* (liberation while living)? Or what is the valid means of knowing it? Or how is its accomplishment to be understood?” The answer to this triad of questions has been set out. Now the answer to the fourth question, namely, “What purpose is served by that accomplishment?”, is stated: the safeguarding of knowledge, austerity, absence of discord, destruction of suffering, and manifestation of bliss are five purposes.

vidyaranya/jivanmukti-viveka/and reply o.locus: Objection and reply on the need to protect knowledge (p. 130)
vidyaranya/jivanmukti-viveka/and reply o.sanskrit_iast: nanu pramāṇotpannasya tattvajñānasya ko nāma bādhaprasaṅgo yena rakṣāpekṣyeta iti ced ucyate: cittaviśrāntyabhāve saṃśayaviparyayau prasajyetām.
vidyaranya/jivanmukti-viveka/and reply o.english_close: If it be objected: “For knowledge of reality that has arisen from a valid means of cognition, what possible occasion of obstruction could there be, such that protection should be required?” the reply is this: In the absence of mental repose, doubt and error would arise.
vidyaranya/jivanmukti-viveka/and reply o.source: full_translations/vidyaranya__jivanmukti-viveka.md#Objection and reply on the need to protect knowledge (p. 130)

vidyaranya/jivanmukti-viveka/citation on.sanskrit_iast:
na rāghava tavāsty ajñeyaṃ jñānavatāṃ vara |
svayaṃ sūkṣmayā buddhyā sarvaṃ vijñātavān asi ||
bhagavadvyāsaputrasya śukasyeva matis tava |
viśrāntimātram evādya jñātasyāpekṣate ||
vidyaranya/jivanmukti-viveka/citation on.english_close: O Rāghava, there is nothing unknown to you, best of the knowers. By your own subtle intellect you have come to know everything. Your understanding is like that of Śuka, the divine Vyāsa’s son. What has been known now requires only repose.

vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.15.english_close: *Prakṛti* (primordial causal matrix), conjoined with the reflection of Brahman consisting of consciousness and bliss, possessing the *guṇas* (constitutive strands) of *tamas*, *rajas*, and *sattva*, is twofold; and that indeed is she.

vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.16.sanskrit_iast:
sattvaśuddhāviśuddhibhyāṃ māyā'vidye ca te mate
māyābimbovaśīkṛtya tāṃ syātsarvajña īśvaraḥ ॥ 16 ॥
vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.16.english_close: By the purity and impurity of *sattva*, the two are considered to be *māyā* and *avidyā*; the reflection in *māyā*, having brought that under control, becomes the omniscient *Īśvara*.

vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.17.english_close: But another, being under the power of *avidyā*, becomes manifold because of its diversity; that is the causal body, and the one who bears identification there is *Prājña*.

vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.42.sanskrit_iast:
yathāmuñjādiṣīkaivamātmā yuktyā samuddhṛtaḥ
śarīratritayāddhīraiḥ paraṃ brahmaiva jāyate ॥ 42 ॥
vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.42.english_close: Just as the inner fibre is drawn out from *muñja* grass, so the self, drawn out by reasoning, by the steady-minded from the triad of bodies, becomes nothing other than the supreme Brahman.

vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.43.sanskrit_iast:
parāparātmanorevaṃ yuktyā sambhāvitaikatā
tattvamasyādivākyaiḥ sā bhāgatyāgena lakṣyate ॥ 43 ॥
vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.43.english_close: Thus the unity of the higher Self and the lower self, rendered intelligible by reasoning, is indicated by sentences beginning with "tat tvam asi," through the abandonment of incompatible parts.

vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.44.sanskrit_iast:
jagato yadupādānaṃ māyāmādāya tāmasīm
nimittaṃ śuddhasattvāṃ tāmucyate brahma tadgirā ॥ 44 ॥
vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.44.english_close: That which, taking up *māyā* in its tamasic form, is the material cause of the world, and that same which, taking her as pure *sattva*, is the efficient cause, is called Brahman by that word.

vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.45.sanskrit_iast:
yadā malinasattvāṃ tāṃ kāmakarmādidūṣitām
ādatte tatparaṃ brahma tvaṃ padena tadocyate ॥ 45 ॥
vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.45.english_close: When that supreme Brahman takes up that one as possessed of impure *sattva*, defiled by desire, action, and the rest, then that is denoted by the word "tvam."

vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.46.sanskrit_iast:
tritayīmapi tāṃ muktvā parasparavirodhinīm
akhaṇḍaṃ saccidānandaṃ mahāvākyena lakṣyate ॥ 46 ॥
vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.46.english_close: Having abandoned even that triad, mutually opposed to one another, the undivided *sat-cit-ānanda* (being-consciousness-bliss) is indicated by the great sentence.

vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.47.sanskrit_iast:
so'yamityādivākyeṣu virodhāttadidantvayoḥ
tyāgena bhāgayoreka āśrayo lakṣyate yathā ॥ 47 ॥
vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.47.english_close: In sentences such as "this is that," because of the incompatibility of "that-ness" and "this-ness," by abandoning those two portions, a single substratum is indicated, as in that familiar case.

vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.48.sanskrit_iast:
māyāvidye vihāyaivamupādhī parajīvayoḥ
akhaṇḍaṃ saccidānandaṃ paraṃ brahmaiva lakṣyate ॥ 48 ॥
vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.48.english_close: Thus, having abandoned *māyā* and *avidyā*, the limiting adjuncts of the supreme and the individual self, the undivided being-consciousness-bliss alone, the supreme Brahman, is indicated.

vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.53.sanskrit_iast:
itthaṃ vākyaistadarthānusandhānaṃ śravaṇaṃ bhavet
yuktyā sambhāvitatvānusandhānaṃ mananantu tat ॥ 53 ॥
vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.53.english_close: Thus, the sustained tracing of the meaning of those sentences by means of the sentences is called *śravaṇa* (hearing); and the tracing of their plausibility by reasoning, that indeed is *manana* (reflective consideration).

vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.54.sanskrit_iast:
tābhyāṃ nirvicikitse'rthe cetasaḥ sthāpitasya yat
ekatānatvametaddhi nididhyāsanamucyate ॥ 54 ॥
vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.54.english_close: Of the mind established, by those two, in a matter free from doubt, whatever one-streamed continuity there is, that indeed is called *nididhyāsana* (deep contemplative assimilation).

vidyaranya/panchadashi/Pañcadaśī 1.55.english_close: Abandoning, step by step, meditator and meditation, and becoming directed to the object of meditation alone, a mind like a lamp in a windless place is called *samādhi*.

vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/2.3.44.english_close: Because of the wording of the mantras.

vimuktatman/ishta-siddhi/1.1, *Prapañcas.english_close: By the term *māyā* (the power of appearance), *avidyā* (ignorance) is called *anirvacanīyā* (indeterminable) with respect to *sat* and *asat*.

vimuktatman/ishta-siddhi/1.1, *Prapañcas.sanskrit_iast: sā hi yuktyā nirūpyamāṇā na astīti, nāstīti veti vaktuṃ śakyā,
vimuktatman/ishta-siddhi/1.1, *Prapañcas.english_close: For that, when it is being examined by reasoning, cannot be stated as "it is," nor as "it is not," nor again as "either so."
Nor, again, can it be simply "it is not"; because, by its being different from both being and non-being, it is unfit to be spoken of in those terms.
For that very reason, it is indeterminable, and not merely unsayable.

vimuktatman/ishta-siddhi/1.1, *Anubandha.english_close: Brahman is the subject-matter of the Vedāntas.
Because Brahman is not commonly available from any other source, and because it is what those texts teach.
And between Brahman and the Vedāntas,
the relation is one characterized as that between what is to be taught and that which teaches it.

vimuktatman/ishta-siddhi/1.9, *Jīvanmukt.english_close: Therefore, at the very moment when knowledge arises, the knower is liberated.
of *ajñāna* (nescience), which is the seed of the whole of saṃsāra,
because it has been burned without remainder by the fire of self-knowledge,
from knowledge, liberation follows without defect.

vimuktatman/ishta-siddhi/1.140, *Jaḍe'py.english_close: Therefore *avidyā* is in the Self alone.
and in the Self alone indeed [again].
But it is not of a non-self, nor in a non-self: thus it is established.

vimuktatman/ishta-siddhi/1.9, *Anirvacan.english_close: Therefore, in an error-cognition, whatever at all appears,
because, with respect to the being and the non-being of that whole appearing item, there is no *pramāṇa* (means of valid cognition),

vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/1.sanskrit_iast:
*nikhilaguṇanikāyaṃ nityanirdhūtaheyaṃ
śubhatamamatimeyaṃ śuddhasaukhyāptyupāyam /
sakalanigamageyaṃ sarvaśabdābhidheyaṃ
navajaladharākāyaṃ naumi lakṣmīsahāyam //*
vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/1.english_close: I bow to the consort of Lakṣmī, whose body is a mass of all qualities. He whose rejectable features are eternally shaken off; he who is knowable to the most excellent intelligence; he who is the means to the attainment of pure bliss. He who is sung by the whole Veda. He who is denoted by every word; he whose form is that of a fresh rain-cloud.
vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/1.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/_kashmir_saivism/AdvaitasiddhiVsNyayamrta.txt).

vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/2.sanskrit_iast:
*vighnaughavāraṇaṃ satyāśeṣaviśvasya kāraṇam /
karuṇāsindhum ānandatīrthabandhuṃ hariṃ bhaje //*
vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/2.english_close: I worship Hari, the warder-off of the flood of obstacles, the cause of the entire real universe. I worship Hari, the ocean of compassion, the friend of Ānandatīrtha.
vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/2.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/_kashmir_saivism/AdvaitasiddhiVsNyayamrta.txt).

vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/3.sanskrit_iast:
*abhramaṃ bhaṅgarahitam ajaḍaṃ vimalaṃ sadā /
ānandatīrtham atulaṃ bhaje tāpatrayāpaham //*
vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/3.english_close: I worship the peerless Ānandatīrtha: free from error, free from fracture, not dull, and always stainless. I worship the peerless Ānandatīrtha, the remover of the threefold suffering.
vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/3.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/_kashmir_saivism/AdvaitasiddhiVsNyayamrta.txt).

vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/4.sanskrit_iast:
*citraiḥ padaiś ca gambhīrair vākyair mānair akhaṇḍitaiḥ /
gurubhāvaṃ vyañjayantī bhāti śrījayatīrthavāk //*
vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/4.english_close: With lucid words, and with profound sentences, and with proofs that are unbroken by refutation, the speech of Śrī Jayatīrtha shines, making manifest the guru’s intention.
vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/4.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/_kashmir_saivism/AdvaitasiddhiVsNyayamrta.txt).

vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/5.sanskrit_iast:
*samutsārya tamaḥstomaṃ sanmārgaṃ samprakāśya ca /
sadā viṣṇupadāsaktaṃ seve brahmaṇyabhāskaram //*
vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/5.english_close: Having driven away the mass of darkness, and having fully illumined the good path, I serve Brahmaṇyatīrtha, sun-like, always attached to the feet of Viṣṇu.
vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/5.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/_kashmir_saivism/AdvaitasiddhiVsNyayamrta.txt).

vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/6.english_close: I salute Lakṣmīnārāyaṇa Muni, rich in auspicious qualities such as knowledge, dispassion, and devotion. I salute him as my intellectual preceptor.
vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/6.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/_kashmir_saivism/AdvaitasiddhiVsNyayamrta.txt).

vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/7.english_close: Having churned the milk-ocean that is the śāstra of Śrī Madhva with the mountain Mandara that is intellect, the Nectar of Reason is drawn up, for the satisfaction of the learned.
vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/7.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/_kashmir_saivism/AdvaitasiddhiVsNyayamrta.txt).

vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/8.sanskrit_iast:
*vikṣiptasaṅgrahāt kvāpi kvāpy uktasyopapādanāt /
anuktakathanāt kvāpi saphalo 'yaṃ śramo mama //*
vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/8.english_close: In some places, because of collecting together what is scattered; and in some places, because of demonstrating what has been stated; and in some places, because of stating what has not been stated, this labour of mine is fruitful.
vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/8.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/_kashmir_saivism/AdvaitasiddhiVsNyayamrta.txt).

vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/heading.english_close: The *prāmāṇyavāda* is: the definition of *svatastva* (intrinsicness) with respect to apprehension, according to the school's own view.

vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/Prāmāṇyavāda,.english_close: *Svatastva* is the invariability of being the object of a *sākṣijñāna* (witness-cognition) whose object is that very cognition which is the locus of graspable validity, when that witness-cognition is not accompanied by the complex that would present a defeater to that graspable validity.
The *sākṣin* (witness) is, in this dialectical setting, precisely of the nature of the *anuvyavasāya* (reflexive cognition) admitted by the logicians.

vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/Prāmāṇyavāda,.locus: Prāmāṇyavāda, utpatti-svatastva-lakṣaṇa (pu. 18; cited by the commentary as p. 154)
vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/Prāmāṇyavāda,.english_close: *Svatastva* is origination dependent solely on the producer of the cognition.
vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/Prāmāṇyavāda,.source: full_translations/vyasatirtha__tarka-tandava.md#Prāmāṇyavāda, utpatti-svatastva-lakṣaṇa (pu. 18; cited by the commentary as p. 154)

vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/Prāmāṇyavāda,.english_close: But as for the proof of intrinsicness:

vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/Prāmāṇyavāda,.sanskrit_iast: jñānaprāmāṇyaṃ svato grāhyaṃ, parato 'gṛhyamāṇatve sati gṛhyamāṇatvāt / yadyad anyenāgṛhyamāṇatve sati grāhyaṃ tat tena grāhyaṃ / yathā cakṣur anyenāgṛhyamāṇaṃ rūpaṃ cakṣurgrāhyam //
vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/Prāmāṇyavāda,.english_close: The validity of a cognition is graspable intrinsically, because it is grasped even when it is not being grasped by something else. Whatever is grasped, when not being grasped by another, is grasped by that very thing. For example: a visible form, when not grasped by something else, is grasped by the eye itself.

vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/conclusion.sanskrit_iast: tasmāt prāmāṇyasya svatastvād apauruṣeyo 'pi vedaḥ pramāṇam eva //
vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/conclusion.english_close: Therefore, because validity is intrinsic, the Veda too, though unauthored, is indeed a *pramāṇa* (means of knowledge).
vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/conclusion.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/vyasatirtha_tarka_tandava_gretil.txt).

yamuna/atma-siddhi/2.sanskrit_iast:
*viruddhamatayo 'nekāḥ santy ātmaparamātmanoḥ /
atas tatpariśuddhyartham ātmasiddhir vidhīyate //*
yamuna/atma-siddhi/2.english_close: Many conflicting doctrines concerning the *ātman* (self) and the *paramātman* (supreme self) exist. Therefore, for the sake of clarifying that matter, the *Ātma-Siddhi* is being composed.
yamuna/atma-siddhi/2.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/yamuna_atma_siddhi_gretil.txt).

yamuna/atma-siddhi/3.sanskrit_iast:
*dehendriyamanaḥprāṇadhībhyo 'nyo 'nanyasādhanaḥ /
nityo vyāpī pratikṣetram ātmā bhinnaḥ svataḥ sukhī //*
yamuna/atma-siddhi/3.english_close: The *ātman* is other than body, senses, mind, vital breath, and cognitions; and it is *ananyasādhana* (not requiring another revealer, that is, self-manifest). It is eternal, pervasive, distinct in each embodied field, and by its own nature happy.
yamuna/atma-siddhi/3.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/yamuna_atma_siddhi_gretil.txt).

yamuna/atma-siddhi/4.english_close: Surely the “I”-cognition and the “this”-cognition do not belong, in one and the same case, to one single entity.
yamuna/atma-siddhi/4.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/yamuna_atma_siddhi_gretil.txt).

yamuna/atma-siddhi/5.sanskrit_iast:
*aparārthaṃ svam ātmānam ātmārthe 'nyac ca jānataḥ /
saṅghātatvāt parārthe 'smin dehe katham ivātmadhīḥ //*
yamuna/atma-siddhi/5.english_close: For one who knows his own self as not for another, and what is other as for the sake of the self, how could there be a cognition of self with respect to this body, which is for another because it is a composite?

yamuna/atma-siddhi/6.sanskrit_iast:
*asphuṭatve 'pi bhedasya śarīre tadasaṃbhavāt /
tadguṇāntaravaidharmyād api jñānaṃ na tadguṇaḥ //*
yamuna/atma-siddhi/6.english_close: Even if the difference is not distinctly manifest, because that cognition cannot arise in the body, and also because it is unlike the body's other qualities, cognition is not a quality of that body.
yamuna/atma-siddhi/6.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/yamuna_atma_siddhi_gretil.txt).

yamuna/atma-siddhi/7.sanskrit_iast:
*utpattimattvāt sanniveśaviśeṣataḥ /
rūpādimattvād bhūtatvād deho nātmā ghaṭādivat //*
yamuna/atma-siddhi/7.english_close: Because it is produced, because it is a particular configuration, because it possesses color and the rest, and because it is an elemental thing, the body is not the self, like a pot and the like.
yamuna/atma-siddhi/7.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/yamuna_atma_siddhi_gretil.txt).

yamuna/atma-siddhi/8.sanskrit_iast:
*sacchidratvād adehitvād dehatvān mṛtadehavat /
ityādisādhanair nyāyyair niṣedhyā varṣmaṇaś citiḥ //*
yamuna/atma-siddhi/8.english_close: Because it has apertures, because it is not itself the embodied subject, and because it is a body, as in the case of a dead body, the consciousness of the body is to be denied by such and similar proper inferences.
yamuna/atma-siddhi/8.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/yamuna_atma_siddhi_gretil.txt).

yamuna/catuh-shloki/1.sanskrit_iast:
kāntas te puruṣottamaḥ phaṇipatiḥ śayyāsanaṃ vāhanaṃ vedātmā vihageśvaro yavanikā māyā jaganmohinī
brahmādisuravrajaḥ sadayitas tvaddāsadāsīgaṇaḥ śrīr ity eva ca nāma te bhagavati, brūmaḥ kathaṃ tvāṃ vayam
yamuna/catuh-shloki/1.english_close: Puruṣottama is your beloved. The lord of serpents is your couch, seat, and vehicle. The lord of birds, whose very being is the Veda, is yours. Māyā, the deluder of the world, is your curtain. The host of gods beginning with Brahmā, together with their beloveds, is the company of your male and female servants. "Śrī" alone is your name, O Blessed Lady. How are we to speak of you?

yamuna/catuh-shloki/2.sanskrit_iast:
yasyās te mahimānam ātmana iva tvadvallabho 'pi prabhur nālaṃ mātum iyattayā niravadhiṃ nityānukūlaṃ svataḥ
tām tvāṃ dāsa iti, prapanna iti ca stoṣyāmy ahaṃ nirbhayo lokaikeśvari lokanāthadayite dānte dayāṃ te vidan
yamuna/catuh-shloki/2.english_close: Even your beloved, the Lord himself, is not equal to measuring your greatness as though it were his own, delimiting it by any fixed extent; for it is boundless and by nature ever favorable. Knowing your mercy, I shall praise that you, fearlessly, as "your servant" and as "one who has surrendered," O sole sovereign of the world, O beloved of the Lord of the world, O gentle one.

yamuna/catuh-shloki/3.sanskrit_iast:
śreyo tvatkaruṇānirīkṣaṇasudhāsandhukṣaṇād rakṣyate naṣṭaṃ prāk tadalābhas tribhuvanaṃ saṃpraty anantodayam
śreyo na hy aravindalocanamanaḥkāntāprasādād ṛte saṃsṛtyakṣaravaiṣṇavādhvasu nṛṇāṃ saṃbhāvyate karhicit
yamuna/catuh-shloki/3.english_close: Well-being is preserved by the kindling effected through the nectar of your compassionate glance. The three worlds, formerly ruined through lacking that, now possess unending rise. For no blessed good for human beings is ever thinkable, on the paths of transmigratory existence, the imperishable end, or the Vaiṣṇava end, apart from the favor of the mind-beloved of the lotus-eyed Lord.

yamuna/catuh-shloki/4.sanskrit_iast:
śāntānantamahāvibhūti paraṃ yad brahma rūpaṃ harer mūrtaṃ brahma tato 'pi tat priyataraṃ rūpaṃ yad atyadbhutam
yāny anyāni yathāsukhaṃ viharato rūpāṇi sarvāṇi tāny āhuḥ svair anurūparūpavibhavair gāḍhopagūḍhāni te
yamuna/catuh-shloki/4.english_close: That form of Hari which is the supreme Brahman, tranquil, infinite, and of vast glory; that embodied Brahman; and that form which is dearer even than that, which is utterly wondrous. And all the other forms in which he sports at pleasure: they say that all of them are tightly embraced by you, through your own powers taking forms corresponding to each.

yamuna/isvara-siddhi/14.sanskrit_iast:
atra brūmo na kāryatvaṃ kṣityādau śakyanihnavam |
sabhāgatvāt kriyāvattvān mahattvena viśeṣitāt ||
yamuna/isvara-siddhi/14.english_close: Here we say: the status of being an effect cannot be denied in earth and the like. That is because they have parts, because they possess activity, and because they are marked by magnitude.

yamuna/isvara-siddhi/15.sanskrit_iast:
tādṛśād eva mūrtatvād bāhyapratyakṣatānvitāt |
sasāmānyaviśeṣatvād ityādibhyo ghaṭādivat ||
yamuna/isvara-siddhi/15.english_close: And precisely because of corporeality of that very sort, because of being endowed with external perceptibility, because of possessing universal and particular, and because of such further features, [its effect-status is established] like that of a pot and the like.

yamuna/isvara-siddhi/16.sanskrit_iast:
pratyakṣaṃ tat prameyatvāt padārthatvād ghaṭādivat /
ekecchānuvidhāyīdam acaitanyāt svadehavat ||
yamuna/isvara-siddhi/16.english_close: That is perceptible, because it is an object of knowledge, because it is an entity, like a pot and the like. This follows one will, because it is non-conscious, like one's own body.

yamuna/isvara-siddhi/17.sanskrit_iast:
ekenābhiṣṭhitāḥ kāryaṃ kurvate sarvacetanāḥ |
dehasambandhasāpekṣakāryakṛttvāt tvagādivat ||
yamuna/isvara-siddhi/17.english_close: All conscious beings, being governed by one, carry out activity. That is because their agency depends upon connection with a body, like skin and the rest.

yamuna/isvara-siddhi/18.english_close: The world that is under dispute has one principal person. That is because it has conscious and non-conscious entities as its constitution, like a realm under one king.

yamuna/samvit-siddhi/31.english_close: Therefore the real existence of the *prapañca* (articulated world-manifold) is not negated by the scripture that teaches non-duality.

yamuna/samvit-siddhi/32.english_close: It is established by the force of its own *pramāṇa* (valid means of knowledge); and it is also ratified by scripture.

yamuna/samvit-siddhi/33.english_close: Therefore the meaning of the scriptural sentence, "*Brahman is advitīya* (*without a second*)," is stated as follows.

yamuna/samvit-siddhi/34.english_close: No being fit to be counted as a second has existed, exists, or will exist.

yamuna/samvit-siddhi/35.sanskrit_iast: samo vābhyadiko vāsya yo dvitīyas tu gaṇyate
yamuna/samvit-siddhi/35.english_close: Whether equal to it, or superior to it, that indeed is what is counted as a second.

yamuna/samvit-siddhi/36.english_close: For this world is merely a fraction of the deployment of his manifested glory and power.

yamuna/samvit-siddhi/37.english_close: How could that come to occupy the status of being denoted by the word "second"?

yamuna/samvit-siddhi/38.english_close: Just as, "Today on earth the Cola king, the emperor, is without a second" may be said.

yamuna/samvit-siddhi/39.english_close: Such a statement is directed toward excluding a king equal to him.

yamuna/samvit-siddhi/40.english_close: It is not, however, a statement that negates his servants, his sons, his wives, and the rest.

yamuna/stotra-ratna/20.sanskrit_iast:
tvadāśritānāṃ jagadudbhavasthitapraṇāśasaṃsāravimocanādayaḥ
bhavanti līlāvidhayaś ca vaidikās tvadīyagambhīramano'nusāriṇaḥ
yamuna/stotra-ratna/20.english_close: For those who have taken refuge in you, the creation, maintenance, and dissolution of the world, release from *saṃsāra* (transmigratory bondage), and the rest become modes of play; and they are Veda-grounded, following the depth of your own intention.

yamuna/stotra-ratna/21.sanskrit_iast:
namo namo vāṅmanasātibhūmaye namo namo vāṅmanasaikabhūmaye
namo namo 'nantamahāvibhūtaye namo namo 'nantadayaikasandhive
yamuna/stotra-ratna/21.english_close: Salutation, salutation to the one beyond speech and mind; salutation, salutation to the one who is the single ground of speech and mind. Salutation, salutation to the one of endless great manifestations; salutation, salutation to the one whose single, unbroken bent is endless compassion.

yamuna/stotra-ratna/22.sanskrit_iast:
na dharmaniṣṭho 'smi, na cātmavedī, na bhaktimāṃs tvaccaraṇāravinde
akiñcano 'nanyagatiḥ śaraṇya ! tvatpādamūlaṃ śaraṇaṃ prapadye
yamuna/stotra-ratna/22.english_close: I am not steadfast in *dharma*; nor am I a knower of the self; nor am I possessed of devotion at your lotus-feet. Having nothing, with no other refuge, O giver of refuge, I take the root of your feet as refuge.

yamuna/stotra-ratna/23.sanskrit_iast:
na ninditaṃ karma tad asti loke sahasraśo yan na mayā vyadhāyi
so 'haṃ vipākāvasare mukunda ! krandāmi sampraty agatis tavāgre
yamuna/stotra-ratna/23.english_close: There is no blameworthy deed in the world that has not been done by me thousands of times. I, that very man, at the moment of karmic fruition, O Mukunda, cry out now before you, without recourse.

yamuna/stotra-ratna/24.sanskrit_iast:
nimajjato 'nanta ! bhavārṇavāntaś cirāya me kūlam ivāsi labdhaḥ
tvayāpi labdhaṃ bhagavann idānīm anuttamaṃ pātram idaṃ dayāyāḥ
yamuna/stotra-ratna/24.english_close: To me, long sinking in the depth of the ocean of becoming, O Ananta, you are like a shore at last found. And by you too, O Lord, this unsurpassed recipient of compassion has now been found.

yamuna/stotra-ratna/25.sanskrit_iast:
abhūtapūrvaṃ mama bhāvi kiṃ vā sarvaṃ sahe me sahajaṃ hi duḥkham
kiṃ tu tvadagre śaraṇāgatānāṃ parābhavo nātha ! na te 'nurūpaḥ
yamuna/stotra-ratna/25.english_close: Or what unprecedented thing is still to happen to me? I can endure everything; suffering is indeed native to me. But the downfall of those who have come for refuge before you, O Lord, is not fitting for you.

yamuna/stotra-ratna/26.sanskrit_iast:
nirāsakasyāpi na tāvad utsahe maheśa ! hātuṃ tava pādapaṅkajam
ruṣā nirasto 'pi śiśuḥ stanandhayo na jātu mātuś caraṇau jihāsati
yamuna/stotra-ratna/26.english_close: Even as one cast off, I still do not dare, O great Lord, to abandon your lotus-foot. Even a suckling child, though pushed away in anger, never wishes to leave its mother's feet.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.1.english_close: Now, therefore, the inquiry into dharma.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.2.english_close: Dharma is the object characterized by injunction.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.3.1.english_close: Because dharma has language as its root, the non-verbal should not be expected there.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.13.english_close: By this, the transformations of property, mark, and state in the elements and senses are explained.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.14.english_close: The substrate conforms to properties that are quiescent, arisen, and unexpressed.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/4.29.sanskrit_iast: prasaṃkhyāne 'py akusīdasya sarvathā vivekakhyāter dharmameghaḥ samādhiḥ
patanjali/yoga-sutra/4.29.english_close: For one who is not attached even to discriminative knowledge, through complete discriminative insight, there is the samādhi called dharma-megha.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/40.sanskrit_iast: pūrvotpannam asaktaṃ niyataṃ mahadādisūkṣmaparyantam / saṃsarati nirupabhogaṃ bhāvair adhivāsitaṃ liṅgam //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/40.english_close: The subtle body, produced previously, unattached, fixed, extending from mahat down to the subtle elements, transmigrates without enjoyment, permeated by the bhāvas.
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/40.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/samkhya/samkhya_karika_isvarakrsna.txt).

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/43.sanskrit_iast: sāṃsiddhikāś ca bhāvāḥ prākṛtikā vaikṛtāś ca dharmādyāḥ / dṛṣṭāḥ karaṇāśrayiṇaḥ kāryāśrayiṇaś ca kalalādyāḥ //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/43.english_close: The states beginning with dharma are seen as innate, natural, and derivative; they rest in the instruments, and, like the embryonic stages and the rest, they also rest in the effect.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/44.sanskrit_iast: dharmeṇa gamanam ūrdhvaṃ gamanam adhastād bhavaty adharmeṇa / jñānena cāpavargo viparyayād iṣyate bandhaḥ //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/44.english_close: By dharma there is ascent upward; by adharma there is descent downward. By knowledge there is release; by the opposite, bondage is held to occur.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.1.english_close: Now, therefore, we shall explain dharma.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.2.english_close: That from which the accomplishment of flourishing and the highest good arises is dharma.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.15.sanskrit_iast: dravyāśrayy aguṇavān saṃyogavibhāgeṣv akāraṇam anapekṣa iti guṇalakṣaṇam
kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.15.english_close: The mark of a quality is this: it resides in a substance, is not itself possessed of qualities, is not a cause with respect to conjunction and disjunction, and is non-dependent.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.16.sanskrit_iast: ekadravyam aguṇaṃ saṃyogavibhāgeṣv anapekṣaṃ kāraṇam iti karmalakṣaṇam
kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.16.english_close: The mark of motion is this: it resides in a single substance, is without qualities, is non-dependent, and is a cause with respect to conjunction and disjunction.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.1.english_close: The non-living extended entities are dharma, adharma, space, and matter.
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.1.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/comparator/umasvati_tattvartha_sutra.txt).

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.17.english_close: The function of dharma and adharma is to assist motion and rest.
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.17.verification_note: IAST fragment not found in best-match source file (sanskrit/comparator/umasvati_tattvartha_sutra.txt).

mathara/samkhya-karika-vrtti/22.sanskrit_iast: prakṛtiḥ pradhānam adhikurute | brahma avyaktaṃ bahudhātmakaṃ māyeti paryāyāḥ | tasyāḥ prakṛter mahān utpadyate prathamaḥ kaścit |
mathara/samkhya-karika-vrtti/22.english_close: Prakṛti acts as the pradhāna. 'Brahma', 'avyakta', 'bahudhātmaka', and 'māyā' are synonyms. From that prakṛti the mahat first arises.

mathara/samkhya-karika-vrtti/68.sanskrit_iast: prāpte śarīrabhede caritārthatvāt pradhānavinivṛttau / aikāntikam ātyantikam ubhayaṃ kaivalyam āpnoti // ... pradhānaṃ ca kṛtaprayojanaṃ bhūyo 'nyat śarīraṃ dvitīyaṃ nārabhate viduṣaḥ |
mathara/samkhya-karika-vrtti/68.english_close: When the body is broken and pradhāna has ceased because its task is accomplished, one attains kaivalya that is both definitive and final. Pradhāna, having fulfilled its purpose, does not undertake another body for the knower.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.24-1.25.sanskrit_iast: kleśakarmavipākāśayair aparāmṛṣṭaḥ puruṣaviśeṣa īśvaraḥ // ... yatra kāṣṭhāprāptir jñānasya sa sarvajñaḥ | sa ca puruṣaviśeṣa iti |
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.24-1.25.english_close: Īśvara is a distinct puruṣa untouched by afflictions, action, karmic fruition, and latent deposits. Where knowledge reaches its unsurpassable limit, there is omniscience; that too belongs to this distinct puruṣa.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/4.22.sanskrit_iast: na pātālaṃ na ca vivaraṃ girīṇāṃ naivāndhakāraṃ kukṣayo nodadhīnām / guhā yasyāṃ nihitaṃ brahma śāśvataṃ buddhivṛttim aviśiṣṭāṃ kavayo vedayante //
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/4.22.english_close: It is not in the underworld, nor in a mountain cleft, nor in darkness, nor in the belly of the oceans. The sages know the eternal brahma hidden in the cave, as not distinct from the mode of intellect.

gautama/nyaya-sutra/4.1.19.english_close: Īśvara is the cause, because one observes that human acts do not by themselves yield their fruits.

vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/1.1.22.sanskrit_iast: etām avasthām aparyantam apavargaṃ vedayante 'pavargavidaḥ / tad abhayam ajaram amṛtyupadaṃ brahma kṣemaprāptir iti /
