vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/1.1.22.english_close: Those who know apavarga describe this endless state as apavarga. It is fearlessness, freedom from decay, the station beyond death, the attainment of brahma-security.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.8-10.sanskrit_iast: dve satye samupāśritya buddhānāṃ dharmadeśanā / lokasaṃvṛtisatyaṃ ca satyaṃ ca paramārthataḥ // ... vyavahāram anāśritya paramārtho na deśyate / paramārtham anāgamya nirvāṇaṃ nādhigamyate //
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.8-10.english_close: The Buddhas teach the Dharma by relying on two truths: worldly convention and ultimate truth. Without depending on ordinary transaction, the ultimate is not taught; without arriving at the ultimate, nirvāṇa is not attained.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.7-9.sanskrit_iast: nivṛttam abhidhātavyaṃ nivṛttaś cittagocaraḥ / anutpannāniruddhā hi nirvāṇam iva dharmatā // aparapratyayaṃ śāntaṃ prapañcair aprapañcitam / nirvikalpam anānārtham etat tattvasya lakṣaṇam //
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.7-9.english_close: What is to be spoken of is stilled, and the range of mind is stilled. Dharmatā, like nirvāṇa, is neither produced nor destroyed. Independent of another, peaceful, not proliferated by proliferations, without conceptual construction and without plurality: this is the mark of reality.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.189-190.sanskrit_iast: paramārtho hi nirvāṇaṃ param etat prayojanam / tasya yā śūnyatā tena sā paramārthaśūnyatā // nirvāṇe bhāvabuddhīnāṃ bhāvagrāhanivṛttaye / deśitā paramārthajñaiḥ śūnyatā pāramārthikā //
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.189-190.english_close: Ultimate meaning is nirvāṇa, the highest aim. Its emptiness is called ultimate emptiness. To stop the grasping of entities with respect to nirvāṇa, those who know the ultimate teach a specifically ultimate emptiness.

vasubandhu/trimsika/23-28.sanskrit_iast: trividhasya svabhāvasya trividhāṃ niḥsvabhāvatām / sandhāya sarvadharmāṇāṃ deśitā niḥsvabhāvatā // ... dharmāṇāṃ paramārthaśca sa yatastathatāpi saḥ / sarvakālaṃ tathābhāvāt saiva vijñaptimātratā // ... yadālambanaṃ vijñānaṃ naivopalabhate tadā / sthitaṃ vijñānamātratve grāhyābhāve tadagrahāt //
vasubandhu/trimsika/23-28.english_close: The teaching of insubstantiality is given with reference to the three natures of all dharmas. The ultimate of dharmas is that which is suchness; because it is always thus, that itself is consciousness-only. When consciousness no longer apprehends an object, it stands in consciousness-only, since there is no grasped object to be taken.

vasubandhu/vimsatika/10.sanskrit_iast: vijñaptimātram idaṃ rūpādidharmapratibhāsam utpadyate na tu rūpādilakṣaṇo dharmaḥ ko 'py astīti viditvā ... yo bālair dharmāṇāṃ svabhāvo grāhyagrāhakādiḥ parikalpatas tena kalpitenātmanā teṣāṃ nairātmyaṃ na tv anabhilāpyenātmanā yo buddhānāṃ viṣaya iti |
vasubandhu/vimsatika/10.english_close: One knows that this appearance of form and the like arises as cognition-only, and that no dharma having the character of external form truly exists. The intrinsic nature imagined by the immature as object, subject, and the like is what is denied; what is denied is that imagined selfhood, not the ineffable domain of the Buddhas.

abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/43.sanskrit_iast: tad brahma paraṃ śuddhaṃ śāntaṃ abhedātmakaṃ samaṃ sakalam / amṛtaṃ satyaṃ śaktau viśrāmyati bhāsvarūpāyām //
abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/43.english_close: That is the supreme brahma: pure, peaceful, nondual, even, and whole; immortal and real, it comes to rest in śakti, which is of the nature of luminosity.

ksemaraja/spanda-karika-commentary/1.5.1.sanskrit_iast: mūḍhabhāva aiśvaryātmakavimarśaśūnyaprakāśamātratattvo brahmarūpo 'pi yatra nāsti yac chrutyantavidaḥ pratipannāḥ vijñānaṃ brahma iti tasyāpi svātantryātmakaspandaśaktiṃ vinā jaḍatvāt // ... napuṃsakamidaṃ nātha paraṃ brahma phalet kiyat /
ksemaraja/spanda-karika-commentary/1.5.1.english_close: Even a brahma-form consisting in mere illumination, devoid of reflective sovereignty, is not ultimately adequate there; what the knowers of the Upaniṣads grasp as 'consciousness is brahma' would, without the spanda-power of freedom, be inert. What fruit could a neuter supreme brahma yield?

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/isk_17.sanskrit_iast: saṅghātaparārthatvāt triguṇādiviparyayād adhiṣṭhānāt / puruṣo 'sti bhoktṛbhāvāt kaivalyārthapravṛtteś ca //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/isk_17.english_close: The *puruṣa* exists because aggregates are for another, because of the inversion from the three guṇas and the like, because of an underlying support, because of enjoyership, and because activity is directed toward *kaivalya*.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/isk_18.sanskrit_iast: janmamaraṇakaraṇānāṃ pratiniyamād ayugapatpravṛtteś ca / puruṣabahutvaṃ siddhaṃ traiguṇyaviparyayāc caiva //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/isk_18.english_close: The plurality of *puruṣas* is established from the specific allotment of birth, death, and instruments, from the non-simultaneity of activity, and again from the inversion relative to the guṇas.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/isk_19.sanskrit_iast: tasmāc ca viparyāsāt siddhaṃ sākṣitvam asya puruṣasya / kaivalyaṃ mādhyasthyaṃ draṣṭṛtvam akartṛbhāvaś ca //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/isk_19.english_close: From that inversion are established for this *puruṣa* witnesshood, isolation, neutrality, seerhood, and non-agency.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/isk_59.sanskrit_iast: raṅgasya darśayitvā nivartate nartakī yathā nṛtyāt / puruṣasya tathātmānaṃ prakāśya vinivartate prakṛtiḥ //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/isk_59.english_close: As a dancer withdraws after displaying herself upon the stage, so prakṛti withdraws after revealing herself to the *puruṣa*.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/ys_2.20.english_close: The seer is pure seeing; though pure, he beholds the mental presentations.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/4.18.sanskrit_iast: yadi cittavat prabhur api puruṣaḥ pariṇamet tatas tadviṣayāś cittavṛttayaḥ śabdādiviṣayavaj jñātājñātāḥ syuḥ sadājñātatvaṃ tu manasas tatprabhoḥ puruṣasyāpariṇāmitvam anumāpayati.
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/4.18.english_close: If the lord, the *puruṣa*, were to change like the mind, then the mind's modifications would be known and unknown like sound and the other objects; but their being always known implies the non-transformation of the *puruṣa*, the lord of that mind.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/4.34.sanskrit_iast: kṛtabhogāpavargāṇāṃ puruṣārthaśūnyānāṃ yaḥ pratiprasavaḥ kāryakāraṇātmakānāṃ guṇānāṃ tat kaivalyaṃ, svarūpapratiṣṭhā punar buddhisattvānabhisaṃbandhāt puruṣasya citiśaktir eva kevalā, tasyāḥ sadā tathaivāvasthānaṃ kaivalyam iti.
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/4.34.english_close: The reabsorption of the guṇas, now emptied of purpose for the *puruṣa* whose enjoyment and release are complete, is *kaivalya*; or rather, because the *puruṣa* is no longer connected with buddhi-sattva, its power of consciousness alone remains in its own form, and its abiding always thus is *kaivalya*.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.10.english_close: Desire, aversion, effort, pleasure, pain, and cognition are marks of the self.

vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/1.1.10.sanskrit_iast: yajjātīyasyārthasya sannikarṣāt sukham ātmopalabdhavān, tajjātīyam evārthaṃ paśyann upādātum icchati, seyam ādātum icchā ekasyānekārthadarśino darśanapratisandhānād bhavati liṅgam ātmanaḥ.
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/1.1.10.english_close: Having experienced pleasure in connection with an object of a certain kind, one desires to take up an object of that very kind upon seeing it again; that desire, arising from the reconnection of the perceptions of one and the same experiencer across many objects, is a mark of the self.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/3.2.4.english_close: Breathing out and in, blinking and opening the eyes, vital life, movement of the mind, the modifications of the other senses, pleasure and pain, desire and aversion, and effort: these are marks of the self.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/3.2.13.sanskrit_iast: aham iti pratyagātmani bhāvāt paratrābhāvād arthāntarapratyakṣaḥ
kanada/vaisesika-sutra/3.2.13.english_close: Because the cognition "I" occurs with regard to the inward self and not elsewhere, it is the direct perception of a distinct entity.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/4.1.3.sanskrit_iast: tadutsarge karmāṇi puruṣārthāya śāstrasyānatiśaṅkyatvān na ca dravyaṃ cikīrṣyate tenārthenābhisambandhāt kriyāyāṃ puruṣaśrutiḥ
jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/4.1.3.english_close: Where there is no special restriction, acts are for the sake of the person; the śāstra is not otherwise to be suspected, one does not wish to produce the material itself, and because the action is connected with that end scripture mentions the person with respect to the act.

shabara/mimamsa-bhasya/ms_1.1.2.sanskrit_iast: tayā yo lakṣyate, so 'rthaḥ puruṣaṃ niḥśreyasena saṃyunaktīti pratijānīmahe.
shabara/mimamsa-bhasya/ms_1.1.2.english_close: We maintain that the entity indicated by that injunction is what connects the person with the highest good.

shabara/mimamsa-bhasya/ms_1.1.4.sanskrit_iast: satīndriyārthasaṃbandhe yā puruṣasya buddhir jāyate, tat pratyakṣam.
shabara/mimamsa-bhasya/ms_1.1.4.english_close: When there is contact between sense and object, the cognition that arises in the person is perception.

shabara/mimamsa-bhasya/icchayātmānam-upa.locus: Śabara-Bhāṣya, self inferred from desire and recognition
shabara/mimamsa-bhasya/icchayātmānam-upa.sanskrit_iast: icchayātmānam upalabhāmahe. katham? upalabdhapūrve hy abhiprete bhavatīcchā, nānupalabdhapūrve ... anyedyur dṛṣṭe 'paredyuḥ aham idam adarśam iti bhavati pratyayaḥ. pratyagātmani caitad bhavati, na paratra.
shabara/mimamsa-bhasya/icchayātmānam-upa.english_close: We apprehend the self through desire. How so? Desire arises toward what has previously been apprehended, not toward what has not been apprehended; and after seeing something on one day, on the next there is the cognition, "I saw this." That cognition belongs to the inward self, not to another.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: kartari jñātari svātmany ādisiddhe maheśvare / ajaḍātmā niṣedhaṃ vā siddhiṃ vā vidadhīta kaḥ //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: When Maheśvara is already established in one's own self as agent and knower, who could meaningfully effect either his denial or his proof, given that he is no inert thing?

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: citiḥ pratyavamarśātmā parā vāk svarasoditā / svātantryam etan mukhyaṃ tad aiśvaryaṃ paramātmanaḥ //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: Consciousness is of the nature of reflexive self-apprehension, the supreme speech arising from its own essence; this is freedom, and this is the principal sovereignty of the supreme self.

ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/9.english_close: Through the contraction of his powers, that consciousness becomes the impurity-covered transmigrant.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/jiva-ajiva-tat.english_close: Soul, non-soul, influx, bondage, stoppage, shedding, and liberation are the fundamental realities.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/upayogo-laksan.english_close: Conscious engagement is its defining mark.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/bandha.sanskrit_iast: sakaṣāyatvāj jīvaḥ karmaṇoyogyān pudgalān ādatte sa bandhaḥ.
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/bandha.english_close: Because it is accompanied by passions, the *jīva* takes in material particles fit for karma; that is bondage.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/kevala-moksha.sanskrit_iast: mohakṣayāj jñānadarśanāvaraṇāntarāyakṣayāc ca kevalam. bandhahetvabhāvanirjarābhyāṃ kṛtsna karmavipramokṣo mokṣaḥ.
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/kevala-moksha.english_close: From the destruction of delusion and of the karmas that obstruct knowledge, vision, and efficacy comes omniscience; through the absence of the causes of bondage and the shedding of karma there is complete release from all karmic matter, which is liberation.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/17.sanskrit_iast: saṅghātaparārthatvāt triguṇādiviparyayād adhiṣṭhānāt / puruṣo 'sti bhoktṛbhāvāt kaivalyārthapravṛtteś ca //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/17.english_close: Puruṣa exists because the composite exists for another, because of the opposite of the three guṇa-s and the rest, because of superintendence, because of the condition of being an experiencer, and because of striving for isolation.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/18.sanskrit_iast: janmamaraṇakaraṇānāṃ pratiniyamād ayugapatpravṛtteś ca / puruṣabahutvaṃ siddhaṃ traiguṇyaviparyayāc caiva //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/18.english_close: The plurality of puruṣa-s is established because births, deaths, and instruments are individually ordered, because activities do not occur all at once, and because of the opposite of the three guṇa-s.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/19.sanskrit_iast: tasmāc ca viparyāsāt siddhaṃ sākṣitvam asya puruṣasya / kaivalyaṃ mādhyasthyaṃ draṣṭṛtvam akartṛbhāvaś ca //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/19.english_close: And from that contrariety, the witnesshood of this puruṣa is established, along with isolation, neutrality, the status of seer, and non-agency.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/20.sanskrit_iast: tasmāt tatsaṃyogād acetanaṃ cetanāvad iva liṅgam / guṇakartṛtve 'pi tathā karteva bhavaty udāsīnaḥ //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/20.english_close: From conjunction with that, the non-conscious subtle body appears as if conscious; and although the guṇa-s are the agents, the indifferent one becomes as if an agent.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/21.sanskrit_iast: puruṣasya darśanārthaṃ kaivalyārthaṃ tathā pradhānasya / paṅgvandhavad ubhayor api saṃyogas tatkṛtaḥ sargaḥ //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/21.english_close: For the sake of the puruṣa's seeing, and likewise for the sake of isolation, the conjunction of the two occurs like the lame and the blind; from that creation arises.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/65.sanskrit_iast: tena nivṛttaprasavām arthavaśāt saptarūpavinivṛttām / prakṛtiṃ paśyati puruṣaḥ prekṣakavad avasthitaḥ svasthaḥ //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/65.english_close: Then the puruṣa, poised in itself like a spectator, sees prakṛti, whose productivity has ceased and which, because its purpose is fulfilled, has withdrawn its seven forms.

ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/10.english_close: Even so, like that [Lord], it performs the five acts.

ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/13.sanskrit_iast: tatparijñāne cittam eva antarmukhībhāvena cetanapadādhyārohāt citiḥ ||
ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/13.english_close: When that is recognized, thought itself, by turning inward and ascending to the status of the conscious subject, is consciousness.

abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/36.sanskrit_iast: jaladharadhūmarajobhir malinīkriyate yathā na gaganatalam / tadvan māyāvikṛtibhir aparāmṛṣṭaḥ paraḥ puruṣaḥ //
abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/36.english_close: Just as the surface of the sky is not stained by clouds, smoke, and dust, so the supreme puruṣa is untouched by the transformations of māyā.

abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/92.sanskrit_iast: svargamayo nirayamayas tad ayaṃ dehāntarālagaḥ puruṣaḥ / tadbhaṅge svaucityād dehāntarayogam abhyeti //
abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/92.english_close: Made of heaven, made of hell, this puruṣa abiding between bodies takes on connection with another body, when that one is broken, according to its own congruity.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.21.english_close: The function of souls is mutual support.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/8.2.sanskrit_iast: sakaṣāyatvāj jīvaḥ karmaṇoyogyān pudgalān ādatte sa bandhaḥ
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/8.2.english_close: Because it is accompanied by passions, the soul appropriates matter fit for karma; that is bondage.

gautama/nyaya-sutra/1.1.2.english_close: When suffering, birth, activity, defect, and false cognition are successively eliminated, apavarga follows from the elimination of what comes immediately after each.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/6.2.17-19.sanskrit_iast:
icchādveṣapūrvikā dharmādharmayoḥ pravṛttiḥ |
tataḥ saṃyogo vibhāgaś ca |
ātmakarmasu mokṣo vyākhyātaḥ |
kanada/vaisesika-sutra/6.2.17-19.english_close: Activity with respect to dharma and adharma is preceded by desire and aversion. From that there is conjunction and disjunction. Mokṣa is explained in connection with the self's actions.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/5.2.20.english_close: When that is absent, there is absence of conjunction and non-arising; that is mokṣa.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/71-72.english_close: Dharma is produced by enjoined action. Adharma, by contrast, is produced by prohibited action.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.2.1.sanskrit_iast: āmnāyasya kriyārthatvād ānarthakyam atadarthānāṃ tasmād anityam ucyate
jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.2.1.english_close: Because Vedic recitation is for the sake of action, statements not bearing on action would be purposeless; therefore the item under discussion is said to be non-eternal.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.2.19.sanskrit_iast: vidhir vā syād apūrvatvād vādamātrama hy anarthakam
jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.2.19.english_close: Or it should be an injunction because of novelty; a merely explanatory statement would indeed be purposeless.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/2.1.1.sanskrit_iast: bhāvārthāḥ karmaśabdās tebhyaḥ kriyā pratīyetaiṣa hy artho vidhīyate
jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/2.1.1.english_close: Terms denoted by the word karma have bhāva as their meaning; from them action is to be understood, for this indeed is what is enjoined.

shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/7.4.1-apurv.locus: Śabara Bhāṣya on Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra 7.4.1, on apūrva and deferred fruition
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/7.4.1-apurv.sanskrit_iast: yajir apūrvaṃ sādhayati | tataś cāpūrvāt kālāntare phalaṃ bhavatīti |
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/7.4.1-apurv.english_close: The sacrifice produces apūrva, and then from that apūrva the fruit comes about at a later time.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3..sanskrit_iast:
eṣa pramātā māyāndhaḥ saṃsārī karmabandhanaḥ /
vidyābhijñāpitaiśvaryaś cidghano mukta ucyate //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3..english_close: This cognizer, blinded by māyā, is a transmigrant bound by karma. One whose lordship has been made manifest by recognition through knowledge, compact mass of consciousness, is called liberated.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3..sanskrit_iast:
bhinnavedyaprathātraiva māyākhyaṃ janmabhogadam /
kartary abodhe kārmaṃ tu māyāśaktyaiva tat trayam //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3..english_close: The manifestation there of differentiated objects is the impurity called māyā, the giver of birth and enjoyment. But when there is unawareness in the agent, the karmic impurity is that very triad brought about by māyā-śakti.

abhinavagupta/isvara-pratyabhijna-vimars.sanskrit_iast: tatra kartur abodharūpasya dehāder bhinnavedyaprathane sati dharmādharmarūpaṃ kārmaṃ malam, yato janma bhogaś ca sa ca niyatāvadhikaḥ, iti jātyāyurbhogaphalaṃ karma ity uktaṃ bhavati.
abhinavagupta/isvara-pratyabhijna-vimars.english_close: There, when differentiated objects such as body and the rest are manifest for an agent whose condition is unawareness, the karmic impurity is of the nature of dharma and adharma, because it is the cause of birth and enjoyment, and of these within a fixed limit; thus karma is spoken of as having birth, lifespan, and enjoyment as its fruit.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/6.1-2.english_close: The activity of body, speech, and mind is karmic yoga. That is āsrava.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/8.1-3.sanskrit_iast:
mithyādarśanāviratipramādakaṣāyayogā bandhahetavaḥ.
sakaṣāyatvāj jīvaḥ karmaṇo yogyān pudgalān ādatte sa bandhaḥ.
prakṛtisthityanubhavapradeśās tadvidhayaḥ.
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/8.1-3.english_close: False view, non-restraint, negligence, passion, and yoga are the causes of bondage. Because it is passion-laden, the soul takes up material particles fit for karma; that is bondage. Its modes are prakṛti, duration, fruition-intensity, and quantity of space-points.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/9.1-3.sanskrit_iast:
āsravanirodhaḥ saṃvaraḥ.
sa guptisamitidharmānuprekṣāparīṣahajayacāritraiḥ.
tapasā nirjarā ca.
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/9.1-3.english_close: The stoppage of āsrava is saṃvara. It is effected by restraints, carefulnesses, virtues, contemplations, conquest of afflictions, and conduct. And there is nirjarā through tapas.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/10.2.english_close: From the absence of the causes of bondage and from nirjarā, mokṣa is the complete release from all karma.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/9.sanskrit_iast: asadakaraṇād upādānagrahaṇāt sarvasambhavābhāvāt / śaktasya śakyakaraṇāt kāraṇabhāvāc ca sat kāryam //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/9.english_close: Because the non-existent cannot be produced, because a material basis is selectively appropriated, because not everything arises from everything, because a capable cause produces only what it can produce, and because the effect has the character of the cause, the effect is real in the cause.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/9-commentary.sanskrit_iast: iha loke sad eva sad bhavati | asataḥ karaṇaṃ nāsti ... tasmāt paśyāmaḥ sarvasambhavābhāvād api mahadādi kāryaṃ pradhāne sad eva sad bhavatīti.
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/9-commentary.english_close: In ordinary experience, only what is real comes forth from what is real; production from the unreal does not occur. Therefore mahat and the rest are to be understood as already real in pradhāna before manifestation.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/4.12.english_close: Past and future exist in their own form, because the properties differ according to temporal course.

vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/4.12.sanskrit_iast: nāsty asataḥ saṃbhavaḥ, na cāsti sato vināśa iti ... tasmād atītānāgataṃ svarūpato 'stīti ... sataś ca phalasya nimittaṃ vartamānīkaraṇe samarthaṃ nāpūrvopajanane.
vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/4.12.english_close: There is no arising from what is non-existent and no destruction of what is existent. Hence past and future exist in their own form, and a causal condition is competent only to make an already real result present, not to produce an unprecedented entity.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.2.4-8.sanskrit_iast: bhāvaḥ sāmānyam eva | dravyatvaṃ guṇatvaṃ karmatvaṃ ca sāmānyāni viśeṣāś ca | anyatrāntyebhyo viśeṣebhyaḥ | sad iti yato dravyaguṇakarmasu | dravyaguṇakarmabhyo 'rthāntaraṃ sattā |
kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.2.4-8.english_close: Positive being is a universal. Dravyahood, qualityhood, and motionhood are universals and also differentiators, except from the ultimate particulars. The use of the term 'sat' across substance, quality, and motion points to existence, which is something distinct from those three.

shabara/shabara-bhasya/1.1.4.sanskrit_iast: satsaṃprayoge puruṣasyendriyāṇāṃ buddhijanma tat pratyakṣam ... sataś caitad upalambhanam, nāsataḥ.
shabara/shabara-bhasya/1.1.4.english_close: Perception is the arising of cognition in a person when the senses are in contact with what is present. What is apprehended in that way is the existent, not the non-existent.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: sā sphurattā mahāsattā deśakālāviśeṣinī / saiṣā sāratayā proktā hṛdayaṃ parameṣṭhinaḥ //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: That pulsation is the great being, undelimited by space and time. As the very essence, it is spoken of as the heart of the supreme Lord.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2..sanskrit_iast: yad asat tad asadyuktā nāsataḥ satsvarūpatā / sato 'pi na punaḥ sattā-lābhenārtho 'tha cocyate //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2..english_close: What is unreal is conjoined only with unreality; the state of real being does not arise from the unreal. Nor is something already real meaningfully said to become real by later obtaining existence.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.8.english_close: And by being, number, place, touch, time, interval, mode, and quantity.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.29.english_close: What is endowed with origination, decay, and persistence is real.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/31.sanskrit_iast:
svāṃ svāṃ pratipadyante parasparākūtahaitukīṃ vṛttim /
puruṣārtha eva heturna kenacitkāryate karaṇam //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/31.english_close: They undertake their respective functions, each occasioned by the intention of the others. The purpose of puruṣa alone is the motive; the instrument is not made to act by anything else.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/32.sanskrit_iast:
karaṇaṃ trayodaśavidham, tadāharaṇadhāraṇaprakāśakaram /
kāryaṃ ca tasya daśadhā, hāryaṃ dhāryaṃ prakāśyaṃ ca //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/32.english_close: The instrument is of thirteen kinds, effecting bringing, holding, and illuminating. Its field is tenfold: what is to be brought, held, and illuminated.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/33.sanskrit_iast:
antaḥkaraṇaṃ trividhaṃ daśadhā bāhyaṃ trayasya viṣayākhyam /
sāmpratakālaṃ bāhyaṃ trikālamābhyantaraṃ karaṇam //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/33.english_close: The internal instrument is threefold; the external is tenfold and is called the object-domain of those three. The external instrument operates in the present time; the internal instrument is tri-temporal.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.47-48.english_close: From saṃyama on grasping, own-form, egoity, connectedness, and purposiveness there is mastery of the senses. These three siddhis are attained through conquest of the fivefold form of the instrument.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/4.14.sanskrit_iast: prakhyākriyāsthitiśīlānāṃ guṇānāṃ grahaṇātmakānāṃ karaṇabhāvenaikaḥ pariṇāmaḥ śrotram indriyam, grāhyātmakānāṃ śabdatanmātrabhāvenaikaḥ pariṇāmaḥ śabdo viṣaya iti.
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/4.14.english_close: Of the guṇas whose character is illumination, activity, and stability, one transformation in the mode of instrumentality, having the nature of grasping, is the auditory sense; of those having the nature of the grasped, one transformation in the form of the sound-tanmātra is sound as object.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/37-41.sanskrit_iast:
asādhāraṇam kāraṇaṃ karaṇam /
... tad etattrividhakāraṇamadhye yad asādhāraṇaṃ kāraṇaṃ tad eva karaṇam //
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/37-41.english_close: The non-generic cause is the instrument. Among the three kinds of cause, precisely that cause which is non-generic is the instrument.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/42-43.sanskrit_iast:
tatra pratyakṣajñānakaraṇaṃ pratyakṣam /
... evaṃ sannikarṣaṣaṭkajanyaṃ jñānaṃ pratyakṣam / tatkaraṇam indriyam / tasmād indriyaṃ pratyakṣapramāṇam iti siddham //
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/42-43.english_close: There, perception is that whose instrument is perceptual cognition. The cognition produced from the sixfold contact is perception; its instrument is the sense-organ, and therefore the sense-organ is the perceptual pramāṇa.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/47.sanskrit_iast:
svārthānumitiparārthānumityor liṅgaparāmarśa eva karaṇam /
tasmāl liṅgaparāmarśā'numānam //
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/47.english_close: For both inference for oneself and inference for another, the instrument is precisely consideration of the sign; therefore inference is [defined through] sign-consideration.

vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/1.1.9.sanskrit_iast: tasya bhogāyatanaṃ śarīram / bhogasādhanānīndriyāṇi / bhoktavyā indriyārthāḥ / bhogo buddhiḥ / sarvārthopalabdhau nendriyāṇi prabhavantīti sarvaviṣayam antaḥkaraṇaṃ manaḥ /
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/1.1.9.english_close: Its abode of enjoyment is the body. The means of enjoyment are the senses. The objects of the senses are what is to be enjoyed. Enjoyment is cognition. Since the senses do not suffice for the apprehension of all objects, mind is the inner organ whose range is all objects.

vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/1.1.15.sanskrit_iast: nācetanasya karaṇasya buddher jñānaṃ bhavitum arhati, tad dhi cetanaṃ syāt, ekaś cāyaṃ cetano dehendriyasaṃghātavyatirikta iti.
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/1.1.15.english_close: The cognition of an insentient instrument such as buddhi cannot itself be knowledge, for then that buddhi would have to be conscious. The conscious subject is one and is distinct from the bodily and sensory aggregate.

sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika-commentary/.sanskrit_iast: pramāṇatvaṃ pramākāraṇatvam ity arthaḥ. karaṇasādhanaś ca pramāṇaśabda iti.
sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika-commentary/.english_close: Pramāṇa-status means being the cause of true cognition. And the word pramāṇa is formed with reference to instrumentality.

sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika-commentary/.sanskrit_iast: yadi hy anugrāhakāpekṣā svaśāktiṃ vihanyāt sarvam eva laukikaṃ vaidikaṃ karaṇaṃ karaṇatāṃ jahyāta. ... yatkāryasiddhau sannihitaṃ tatkaraṇam itarad bāhyam itikartavyateti.
sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika-commentary/.english_close: If dependence on an assisting factor destroyed its own power, every worldly and Vedic instrument would lose its instrumentality. What stands immediately in the accomplishment of the act is the instrument; the rest is the ancillary specification called itikartavyatā.

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.241.sanskrit_iast:
niṣpāditakriye kañcid viśeṣamasamādadhat /
karmaṇyaindriyamanyad vā sādhanaṃ kimitīṣyate //
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.241.english_close: When no further distinguishing feature is established in an already accomplished operation, why should one posit another sensory or other instrument with respect to that act?

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.318.sanskrit_iast:
kriyākaraṇayoraikyavirodha iti ced asat /
dharmabhedābhyupagamād vastvabhinnamitīṣyate //
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.318.english_close: If one objects that there is a contradiction in action and instrument being one, that is unsound, since one accepts difference of properties while holding the entity to be non-different.

ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/9-comment.sanskrit_iast: jñānaśaktiḥ krameṇa saṃkocāt bhede sarvajñatvasya kiṃcijjñatvāpteḥ antaḥkaraṇabuddhīndriyatāpattipūrvaṃ atyantasaṃkocagrahaṇena bhinnavedyaprathārūpaṃ māyīyaṃ malam | kriyāśaktiḥ krameṇa bhede sarvakartṛtvasya kiṃcitkartṛtvāpteḥ karmendriyarūpasaṃkocagrahaṇapūrvaṃ atyantaparimitatāṃ prāptā śubhāśubhānuṣṭānamayaṃ kārmaṃ malam |
ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/9-comment.english_close: Through progressive contraction in the domain of difference, cognitive power, descending from omniscience to limited knowledge, becomes inner organ, intellect, and sense-organ form, and so appears as the māyīya impurity. Action-power, descending from universal agency to limited agency, contracts into the form of the action-organs and thus appears as the kārma impurity.

ksemaraja/spanda-nirnaya/2.1.8-9.sanskrit_iast: nanu karaṇānāṃ mantrāṇāṃ ca tat udayādau tulye kimiti karaṇāni na sarvajñādirūpāṇi || ucyate parameśvaro māyāśaktyā śarīrakaraṇāni bhedamayāni nirmimīte vidyāśaktyā tv ākāśīyavicitravācakaparāmarśaśarīrān mantrān ||
ksemaraja/spanda-nirnaya/2.1.8-9.english_close: If the arising of bodily instruments and mantras is alike, why are the instruments not omniscient and the like? The answer is that the Lord, through māyā-śakti, fashions bodily instruments as differentiated, whereas through vidyā-śakti he fashions mantras whose body is a manifold expressive awareness.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/indriya-prakar.sanskrit_iast:
pañcendriyāṇi /
dvividhāni /
nirvṛtyupakaraṇe dravyendriyam /
labdhyupayogau bhāvendriyam //
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/indriya-prakar.english_close: There are five senses. They are of two kinds. The material sense-faculty is the sense as constituted in its instrumental support. The psychical sense-faculty is attainment and use.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/23.sanskrit_iast: adhyavasāyo buddhir dharmo jñānaṃ virāga aiśvaryam / sāttvikam etad rūpaṃ tāmasam asmād viparyastam //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/23.english_close: Buddhi is ascertainment. Dharma, knowledge, dispassion, and lordly power are its sāttvika form; the contrary to this is tāmasa.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/64.sanskrit_iast: evaṃ tattvābhyāsān nāsmi na me nāham ity apariśeṣam / aviparyayād viśuddhaṃ kevalam utpadyate jñānam //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/64.english_close: Thus, through repeated practice concerning the tattvas, there arises, exhaustively, the purified and isolated knowledge: 'I am not this, it is not mine, I am not [the tattvas],' free from error.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.6-9.sanskrit_iast: pramāṇaviparyayavikalpanidrāsmṛtayaḥ // pratyakṣānumānāgamāḥ pramāṇāni // viparyayo mithyājñānam atadrūpapratiṣṭham // śabdajñānānupātī vastuśūnyo vikalpaḥ //
patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.6-9.english_close: The vṛttis are pramāṇa, error, conceptual construction, sleep, and memory. Perception, inference, and testimony are the pramāṇas. Error is false cognition founded on what is not the thing's own form. Conceptual construction follows verbal cognition while being empty of a real object.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.20.english_close: For the others, [samādhi is preceded] by faith, vigor, memory, samādhi, and insight.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.48-49.sanskrit_iast: ṛtaṃbharā tatra prajñā // śrutānumānaprajñābhyām anyaviṣayā viśeṣārthatvāt //
patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.48-49.english_close: There, the insight is truth-bearing. Because it has a particular object, it has a different object from the insight that comes from scripture and inference.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.26-28.sanskrit_iast: vivekakhyātir aviplavā hānopāyaḥ // tasya saptadhā prāntabhūmiḥ prajñā // yogāṅgānuṣṭhānād aśuddhikṣaye jñānadīptir ā vivekakhyāteḥ //
patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.26-28.english_close: Uninterrupted discriminative insight is the means of cessation. Its insight has seven final stages. From the practice of the limbs of yoga, when impurity is destroyed, there is the shining forth of knowledge up to discriminative insight.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/34-36.sanskrit_iast: sarvavyavahārahetur buddhir jñānam / sā dvividhā smṛtiranubhavaś ca / saṃskāramātrajanyaṃ jñānaṃ smṛtiḥ / tadbhinnaṃ jñānam anubhavaḥ // sa dvividho yathārtho 'yathārthaś ca / tadvati tatprakārako 'nubhavo yathārthaḥ //
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/34-36.english_close: Cognition, called buddhi or jñāna, is the cause of all practical activity. It is twofold: memory and experience. Memory is cognition produced solely by a latent impression; the other cognition is experience. Experience is further divided into veridical and non-veridical.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/64-65.sanskrit_iast: ayathārthānubhavas trividhaḥ saṃśayaviparyayatarkabhedāt / ekasmin dharmini viruddhanānādharmavaiśiṣṭyāvagāhi jñānaṃ saṃśayaḥ / mithyājñānaṃ viparyayaḥ // smṛtir api dvividhā / yathārthāyathārthā ca //
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/64-65.english_close: Non-veridical cognition is threefold: doubt, error, and tarka. A cognition that apprehends one substrate as qualified by incompatible diverse properties is doubt. False cognition is error. Memory too is twofold: veridical and non-veridical.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/3.2.1-3.sanskrit_iast: ātmendriyārthasannikarṣe jñānasyābhāvo bhāvaś ca manaso liṅgam / prayatnāyaugapadyāj jñānāyaugapadyāc caikaṃ manaḥ //
kanada/vaisesika-sutra/3.2.1-3.english_close: The presence and absence of cognition, when self, sense, and object are in contact, are a mark of mind. And because effort and cognition do not arise simultaneously, mind is one.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/8.3-5.sanskrit_iast: jñānanirdeśe jñānaniṣpattir uktā / guṇakarmasv asannikṛṣṭeṣu jñānaniṣpatter dravyaṃ kāraṇaṃ kāraṇakāraṇaṃ ca / sāmānyaviśeṣeṣu sāmānyaviśeṣābhāvāt tata eva jñānam //
kanada/vaisesika-sutra/8.3-5.english_close: Once cognition has been indicated, its production has been stated. Since cognition arises with respect to qualities and motions that are not directly conjoined, substance is its cause and the cause of that cause. With respect to universals and particulars, cognition arises from that very basis.

vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.2.1.sanskrit_iast: mithyājñānaṃ vai khalu moho na tattvajñānasyānutpattimātram / tac ca mithyājñānaṃ yatra viṣaye pravartamānaṃ saṃsārabījaṃ bhavati sa viṣayas tattvato jñeya iti / apavargo 'dhigantavyas tasyādhigamopāyas tattvajñānam / evaṃ catasṛbhir vidhābhiḥ prameyaṃ vibhaktam āsevamānasyābhyasyato bhāvayataḥ samyagdarśanaṃ yathābhūtāvabodhas tattvajñānam utpadyate //
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.2.1.english_close: False cognition is indeed delusion, not merely the absence of true cognition. Wherever that false cognition operates and becomes the seed of saṃsāra, that object must be known as it really is. Apavarga is to be attained, and its means of attainment is true knowledge. For one who repeatedly studies and cultivates the prameyas under the four headings, right vision arises as knowledge of things as they really are.

shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.2-codan.locus: Śabara Bhāṣya on Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra 1.1.2, explaining codanā as the source of dharma-cognition
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.2-codan.sanskrit_iast: codanā hi bhūtaṃ bhavantaṃ bhaviṣyantaṃ sūkṣmaṃ vyavahitaṃ viprakṛṣṭam ity evaṃjātīyakam arthaṃ śaknoty avagamayitum, nānyat kiṃcanendriyam //
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.2-codan.english_close: For injunction is able to make known an object of just this sort: past, present, future, subtle, intervened, or remote. No sense-organ whatever can make such a thing known.

shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.4-praty.locus: Śabara Bhāṣya on Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra 1.1.4, defining perception
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.4-praty.sanskrit_iast: satsaṃprayoge puruṣasyendriyāṇāṃ buddhijanma tat pratyakṣam / satīndriyārthasaṃbandhe yā puruṣasya buddhir jāyate, tat pratyakṣam //
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.4-praty.english_close: Perception is the arising of cognition when a person's senses are in contact with what exists. The cognition that arises for a person when there is contact between sense and object is perception.

shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.5-sabda.locus: Śabara Bhāṣya on Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra 1.1.5, on Vedic verbal cognition as pramāṇa
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.5-sabda.sanskrit_iast: autpattikas tu śabdasyārthena saṃbandhas tasya jñānam upadeśo 'vyatirekaś cārthe 'nupalabdhe, tat pramāṇaṃ bādarāyaṇasya, anapekṣatvāt // ... yac ca nāma jñānam utpannaṃ na viparyeti, na tac chakyate vaktuṃ naitad evam iti ... tasmāt tat pramāṇam, anapekṣatvāt //
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.5-sabda.english_close: The relation of word to meaning is innate; instruction and the non-deviation of the resulting cognition, where the object is otherwise unperceived, constitute its pramāṇa-status according to Bādarāyaṇa, because it does not depend on anything further. A cognition that has arisen and does not subsequently deviate cannot be dismissed as 'not so'; therefore that is a pramāṇa, precisely because it is non-dependent.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: tathā hi jaḍabhūtānāṃ pratiṣṭhā jīvadāśrayā / jñānaṃ kriyā ca bhūtānāṃ jīvatāṃ jīvanaṃ matam // tatra jñānaṃ svataḥsiddhaṃ kriyā kāyāśritā satī / parair apy upalakṣyeta tayānyajñānam ūhyate //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: For inert things are grounded in what depends on the living. Knowledge and action are held to be the very life of living beings. There, knowledge is self-established; action, although dependent on the body, can be observed by others, and from it another's knowledge is inferred.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: jñānaṃ ca citsvarūpaṃ cet tad anityaṃ kim ātmavat / athāpi jaḍam etasya katham arthaprakāśatā // athārthasya yathā rūpaṃ dhatte buddhis tathātmanaḥ / caitanyam ajaḍā saivaṃ jāḍye nārthaprakāsatā //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: If knowledge has the nature of consciousness, why would it be impermanent like the self? But if it is inert, how could it illuminate an object? If intellect takes on the form of the object and likewise the self's consciousness, then it is not inert; for in inertness there could be no illumination of the object.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: svabhāvam avabhāsasya vimarśaṃ vidur anyathā / prakāśo 'rthoparakto 'pi sphaṭikādijaḍopamaḥ // ātmāta eva caitanyaṃ citkriyā citikartṛtā / tātparyeṇoditas tena jaḍāt sa hi vilakṣaṇaḥ // citiḥ pratyavamarśātmā parā vāk svarasoditā / svātantryam etan mukhyaṃ tad aiśvaryaṃ paramātmanaḥ //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: They know reflexive awareness to be the very nature of manifestation; otherwise light, even when colored by an object, would be like an inert crystal. From the self alone come consciousness, the activity of consciousness, and conscious agency, and by that it is distinguished from the inert. Consciousness is of the nature of reflexive apprehension, the supreme speech arising from its own savor; this is the chief freedom, the lordly power of the supreme self.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: māyāśaktyā vibhoḥ saiva bhinnasaṃvedyagocarā / kathitā jñānasaṃkalpādhyavasāyādināmabhiḥ // sākṣātkārakṣaṇe 'py asti vimarśaḥ katham anyathā / dhāvanādy upapadyeta pratisaṃdhānavarjitam //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: Through the Lord's power of māyā, that very consciousness, as having differentiated knowables for its field, is designated by names such as cognition, conception, and determination. Even in the moment of direct experience there is reflexive awareness; otherwise running and the rest, devoid of reconnection, would be unintelligible.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.1.english_close: Right vision, right knowledge, and right conduct are the path to liberation.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9-12.sanskrit_iast: mati-śrutāvadhi-manaḥparyaya-kevalāni jñānam / tatpramāṇe / ādye parokṣam / pratyakṣam anyat //
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9-12.english_close: Knowledge is of five kinds: sensory-cognitive, scriptural, clairvoyant, telepathic, and omniscient. These are the pramāṇas. The first two are indirect; the others are direct.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.13-14.sanskrit_iast: matiḥ smṛtiḥ saṃjñā cintābhinibodha ity anarthāntaram / tadindriyānindriyanimittam //
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.13-14.english_close: Mati is also called memory, recognition, thought, and determination. It is caused by sense and non-sense conditions.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.20.english_close: Scriptural knowledge is indeed preceded by mati-knowledge and is of many forms, in twelve divisions.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/17-19.sanskrit_iast: saṅghātaparārthatvāt triguṇādiviparyayād adhiṣṭhānāt / puruṣo 'sti bhoktṛbhāvāt kaivalyārthapravṛtteś ca // janmamaraṇakaraṇānāṃ pratiniyamād ayugapatpravṛtteś ca / puruṣabahutvaṃ siddhaṃ traiguṇyaviparyayāc caiva // tasmāc ca viparyāsāt siddhaṃ sākṣitvam asya puruṣasya / kaivalyaṃ mādhyasthyaṃ draṣṭṛtvam akartṛbhāvaś ca //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/17-19.english_close: Puruṣa exists because the composite exists for another, because of the opposite of the three guṇa-s and the rest, because of superintendence, because of enjoyership, and because of striving for kaivalya. The plurality of puruṣa-s is established because births, deaths, and instruments are individually ordered, because activity does not occur all at once, and because of the opposite of the three guṇa-s. From that very contrariety are established its witnesshood, neutrality, seerhood, and non-agency.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/62-65.sanskrit_iast: tasmān na badhyate nāpi mucyate nāpi saṃsarati kaścit / saṃsarati badhyate mucyate ca nānāśrayā prakṛtiḥ // rūpaiḥ saptabhir evaṃ badhnāty ātmānam ātmanā prakṛtiḥ / saiva ca puruṣasyārthaṃ prati vimocayaty ekarūpeṇa // evaṃ tattvābhyāsān nāsmi na me nāham ity apariśeṣam / aviparyayād viśuddhaṃ kevalam utpadyate jñānam // tena nivṛttaprasavām arthavaśāt saptarūpavinivṛttām / prakṛtiṃ paśyati puruṣaḥ prekṣakavad avasthitaḥ svasthaḥ //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/62-65.english_close: Therefore no one is really bound, released, or transmigrating; it is prakṛti, resting on various bases, that transmigrates, is bound, and is released. By seven forms prakṛti binds itself by itself, and by a single form it releases itself for the sake of puruṣa. Through repeated practice of the tattva-s there arises the pure knowledge, leaving nothing over, that 'I am not, nothing is mine, I am not this.' Then the puruṣa, established in itself like a spectator, sees prakṛti, whose production has ceased and whose seven forms have withdrawn because its purpose is fulfilled.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.17-25.sanskrit_iast: draṣṭṛdṛśyayoḥ saṃyogo heyahetuḥ // prakāśakriyāsthitiśīlaṃ bhūtendriyātmakaṃ bhogāpavargārthaṃ dṛśyam // viśeṣāviśeṣaliṅgamātrāliṅgāni guṇaparvāṇi // draṣṭā dṛśimātraḥ śuddho 'pi pratyayānupaśyaḥ // tadartha eva dṛśyasyātmā // kṛtārthaṃ prati naṣṭam apy anaṣṭaṃ tadanyasādhāraṇatvāt // svasvāmiśaktyoḥ svarūpopalabdhihetuḥ saṃyogaḥ // tasya hetur avidyā // tadabhāvāt saṃyogābhāvo hānaṃ taddṛśeḥ kaivalyam //
patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.17-25.english_close: The conjunction of seer and seen is the cause of what is to be eliminated. The seen, consisting of the elements and the senses and characterized by illumination, activity, and stability, exists for experience and liberation. The seer is pure seeing alone, though it apprehends the mental presentations. The conjunction of owned and owner exists for the sake of realizing their true natures; its cause is ignorance. When that ceases, conjunction ceases: that cessation is release, the seer's kaivalya.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.24.sanskrit_iast: avidyādayaḥ kleśāḥ. kuśalākuśalāni karmāṇi. tatphalaṃ vipākaḥ. tadanuguṇā vāsanā āśayāḥ. te ca manasi vartamānāḥ puruṣe vyapadiśyante, sa hi tatphalasya bhokteti.
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.24.english_close: Avidyā and the rest are the afflictions; meritorious and unmeritorious deeds are actions; their result is maturation; and the corresponding latent traces are dispositions. Though these abide in mind, they are attributed to puruṣa, because it is the enjoyer of their fruit.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.9-10.sanskrit_iast: ātmaśarīrendriyārthabuddhimanaḥpravṛttidoṣapretyabhāvaphaladuḥkhāpavargāḥ tu prameyam // icchādveṣaprayatnasukhaduḥkhajñānāni ātmanaḥ liṅgam iti //
aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.9-10.english_close: Self, body, senses, objects, cognition, mind, activity, faults, post-mortem continuity, result, suffering, and release are the knowables. Desire, aversion, effort, pleasure, pain, and cognition are the marks of the self.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/17.sanskrit_iast: jñānādhikaraṇam ātmā / sa dvividhaḥ paramātmā jīvātmā ca / tatreśvaraḥ sarvajñaḥ paramātmaika eva / jīvātmā pratiśarīraṃ bhinno vibhur nityaś ca //
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/17.english_close: The self is the substratum of cognition. It is twofold: the supreme self and the individual self. There is only one supreme self, Īśvara, omniscient; the individual self is distinct in each body, all-pervasive, and eternal.

sabara/shabara-bhasya/1.1.2.sanskrit_iast: tena yaḥ puruṣaṃ niḥśreyasena saṃyunakti, sa eva dharmaśabdenocyate.
sabara/shabara-bhasya/1.1.2.english_close: That which joins the person to the highest good is precisely what is denoted by the word dharma.

kumarila/sloka-varttika/5.9.11-15.sanskrit_iast: asti hi paralokaphalabhoktā cetanaḥ karmaṇāṃ kartā, sa svargaṃ lokaṃ yāsyatīti.
kumarila/sloka-varttika/5.9.11-15.english_close: There is indeed a conscious doer of actions, an enjoyer of other-worldly fruit; he will go to heaven.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3..sanskrit_iast: eṣa pramātā māyāndhaḥ saṃsārī karmabandhanaḥ / vidyābhijñāpitaiśvaryaś cidghano mukta ucyate // svāṅgarūpeṣu bhāveṣu pramātā kathyate patiḥ / māyāto bhediṣu kleśakarmādikaluṣaḥ paśuḥ // svātantryahānir bodhasya svātantryasyāpy abodhatā / dvidhāṇavaṃ malam idaṃ svasvarūpāpahānitaḥ //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3..english_close: This subject, blinded by māyā, is a transmigrating being bound by karma; when its lordship is disclosed by knowledge, it is called liberated, a compact mass of consciousness. As apprehending realities as its own limbs, the subject is called Lord; as divided by māyā, tainted by afflictions, karma, and the rest, it is the paśu. The loss of the freedom of consciousness, and ignorance of that freedom itself, is the twofold āṇava impurity arising from the loss of one's own nature.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.4.english_close: Soul, non-soul, influx, bondage, stoppage, shedding, and liberation are the real principles.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/2.8-10.sanskrit_iast: upayogo lakṣaṇam // sa dvividho 'ṣṭacaturbhedaḥ // saṃsāriṇo muktāś ca //
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/2.8-10.english_close: Its defining mark is upayoga. That is twofold, with eightfold and fourfold divisions. Souls are worldly and liberated.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/3.14.english_close: It is of atomic measure, because scripture states its making of that [body].

kapila/samkhya-sutra/5.69-71.sanskrit_iast: na vyāpakatvaṃ manasaḥ karaṇatvād indriyatvād vā vāsyādivac cakṣurādivac ca / sakriyatvāt gatiśruteḥ / na nirbhāgatvaṃ tadyogāt ghaṭavat
kapila/samkhya-sutra/5.69-71.english_close: Mind is not pervasive, because it is an instrument, or an organ, like an adze or like the eye and the rest. It is active, because scripture speaks of its movement. It is not partless, because it stands in conjunction, like a pot.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika-commentary/11.sanskrit_iast: tad yathā vyaktād visadṛśaṃ pradhānaṃ tathā pradhānasadharmā puruṣaḥ | tathā hi ahetumān nityo vyāpī niṣkriya eko 'nāśrito 'liṅgo niravayavaḥ svatantra iti
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika-commentary/11.english_close: Just as the pradhāna is unlike the manifest, so the puruṣa has the same properties as the pradhāna: it is uncaused, eternal, pervasive, inactive, one, unsupported, without a mark, partless, and independent.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.40.sanskrit_iast: paramāṇuparamamahattvānto 'sya vaśīkāraḥ / sūkṣme niviśamānasya paramāṇvantaṃ sthitipadaṃ labhata iti / sthūle niviśamānasya paramamahattvāntaṃ sthitipadaṃ cittasya
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.40.english_close: Its mastery extends from the atom to the greatest magnitude. When it settles on the subtle, the mind attains stability up to the atom; when it settles on the gross, the mind's stability reaches up to the greatest magnitude.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/4.10.sanskrit_iast: ghaṭaprāsādapradīpakalpaṃ saṃkocavikāsi cittaṃ śarīraparimāṇākāramātram ity apare pratipannāḥ / vṛttir evāsya vibhunaś cittasya saṃkocavikāsinīty ācāryaḥ
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/4.10.english_close: Others hold that citta, like the light of a jar-lamp or palace-lamp, contracts and expands and is only of the form of body-sized measure. The teacher says rather that it is only the vṛtti of this pervasive citta that contracts and expands.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/7.1.17-18.sanskrit_iast: tadviparītam aṇu / aṇu mahad iti tasmin viśeṣabhāvād viśeṣābhāvāc ca
kanada/vaisesika-sutra/7.1.17-18.english_close: The opposite of that is the atomic. 'Atomic' and 'great' are [so called] because a differentiating feature is present there, and absent [in the other case].

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/7.1.29-30.english_close: So too the self [is great/pervasive]. Because that is absent, mind is atomic.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/17-18.sanskrit_iast: jñānādhikaraṇam ātmā / sa dvividhaḥ paramātmā jīvātmā ca / tatreśvaraḥ sarvajñaḥ paramātmaika eva / jīvātmā pratiśarīraṃ bhinno vibhur nityaś ca // sukhādyupalabdhisādhanam indriyaṃ manaḥ / tac ca pratyātmaniyatatvād anantaṃ paramāṇurūpaṃ nityaṃ ca
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/17-18.english_close: The self is the substrate of cognition. It is twofold: supreme self and living self; the Lord alone is the supreme self, while the living self is distinct in each body, pervasive, and eternal. Mind is the internal organ for the apprehension of pleasure and the rest; because there is one for each self, it is innumerable, atomic in form, and eternal.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/25.sanskrit_iast: mānavyavahārakāraṇaṃ parimāṇaṃ / navadravyavṛtti / tac caturvidham / aṇu mahad dīrghaṃ hrasvaṃ ceti
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/25.english_close: Magnitude is the cause of measure-talk in ordinary usage. It resides in the nine dravyas, and it is fourfold: atomic, great, long, and short.

kumarila/sloka-varttika/5.9.29-30.sanskrit_iast: paramāṇukārayamakaṃ hi kāṇādā dvyaṇukādikrameṇa jagato nirmāṇam ātiṣṭhante / na cāṇutvena parasparam anatiśayānair aṇubhir asaṅkīrṇākārajagad utpādayituṃ śakyate / ataḥ santi kecanāṇusamavāyino viśeṣā nāma ye tān itaretarato vyāvartayantīti saṅgirante / ... aṇuṣu tv ekajātīyeṣv api kāryavibhāgād rūpam eva vibhaktam anumāsyāmahe
kumarila/sloka-varttika/5.9.29-30.english_close: The followers of Kaṇāda undertake the construction of the world from atoms, beginning with dyads. But atoms, being equal in atomicity, cannot produce the differentiated world-form without confusion; hence they speak of certain viśeṣas inhering in atoms to differentiate them from one another. For atoms of a single kind too, however, we infer differentiation from the difference of effects and manifest form alone.

vasubandhu/vimsatika/11.sanskrit_iast: na tad ekaṃ na cānekaṃ viṣayaḥ paramāṇuśaḥ / na ca te saṃhatā yasmāt paramāṇur na sidhyati
vasubandhu/vimsatika/11.english_close: That object is neither one nor many in the atomic manner. Nor are those atoms an aggregate, because the atom itself is not established.

vasubandhu/vimsatika/12-14.sanskrit_iast: ṣaṭkena yugapad yogāt paramāṇoḥ ṣaḍaṃśatā / ... ṣaṇṇāṃ samānadeśatvāt piṇḍaḥ syād aṇumātrakaḥ // ... paramāṇor asaṃyogāt tatsaṃghāte 'sti kasya saḥ // ... anyo na piṇḍaś cen na tasya te
vasubandhu/vimsatika/12-14.english_close: If an atom is joined simultaneously with six others, it would have six parts. If all were in the same place, the whole mass would be only atom-sized. Since the atom has no conjunction, whose conjunction could there be in the aggregate? And if there is no aggregate other than atoms, those properties cannot belong to it.

abhinavagupta/ipv/3.2.9.sanskrit_iast: kartṛtāyoge 'pi bodhānāṃ karmottīrṇānāṃ vidyeśvaratve 'pi bhinnavedyayogān māyāmalam asty eva / pūrṇakartṛtvābhāvād īśvarād bhinnā anyonyaṃ ca pūrvavad ata evaiṣām aṇutvam api syāt
abhinavagupta/ipv/3.2.9.english_close: Even for consciousnesses that are beyond karma and have the status of vidyeśvaras, there is still māyā-mala because of relation to differentiated objects. Because full agency is absent, they remain different from Īśvara and from one another as before; for that very reason they would still have aṇutva.

abhinavagupta/ipv/3.2.11.sanskrit_iast: saṃsāriṇām etac ca cittattvaṃ kartṛtārūpaṃ śūnyaprāṇādau jaḍe devasya kalāśaktyopodbalyamānam upasarjanatvenāvasthānāt parimitam / ataś cātraiva mukhyam aṇutvam
abhinavagupta/ipv/3.2.11.english_close: For saṃsārins, this very cittattva, which is of the nature of agency, becomes limited because it abides subordinately in inert conditions such as emptiness and life-breath, being only reinforced there by the power of kalā. Hence it is precisely here that the primary sense of aṇutva lies.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/pudgala-anavah.locus: Tattvārtha-Sūtra, pudgala section: 'aṇavaḥ skandhāś ca' / 'bhedasaṃghātebhya utpadyante'
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/pudgala-anavah.sanskrit_iast: sparśa-rasa-gandha-varṇavantaḥ pudgalāḥ / śabda-saukṣmya-sthaulya-saṃsthāna-bhedatas chāyātapodyotavantaś ca / aṇavaḥ skandhāś ca / bhedasaṃghātebhya utpadyante
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/pudgala-anavah.english_close: Pudgalas possess touch, taste, smell, and color; they also possess sound, subtlety, grossness, configuration, division, shadow, heat, and light. They are atoms and aggregates. They arise from disjunction and conjunction.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/jiva-pradesa-s.english_close: For souls, [division extends] into innumerable fractions and so on. Through contraction and expansion of spatial units, they are like a lamp.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/59.sanskrit_iast: raṅgasya darśayitvā nivartate nartakī yathā nṛtyāt / puruṣasya tathātmānaṃ prakāśya vinivartate prakṛtiḥ //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/59.english_close: As a dancer withdraws after displaying herself upon the stage, so prakṛti withdraws after revealing herself to the puruṣa.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/5.61.english_close: The self is not nondual, because the sign gives apprehension of its difference.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.17.english_close: The conjunction of seer and seen is the cause of what is to be removed.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.18.english_close: The seen, made up of elements and senses, has the character of illumination, activity, and stability, and exists for experience and release.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.25.english_close: With its absence, there is absence of conjunction; that is cessation, the isolation of the seer.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.35.sanskrit_iast: sattvapuruṣayor atyantāsaṃkīrṇayoḥ pratyayāviśeṣo bhogaḥ parārthāt svārthasaṃyamāt puruṣajñānam ||
patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.35.english_close: Experience is the non-discrimination of sattva and puruṣa, though they are utterly unmixed; because one exists for another, from saṃyama on what is for itself there is knowledge of puruṣa.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.55.english_close: When sattva and puruṣa are equally pure, there is kaivalya.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.5.sanskrit_iast: rūparasagandhasparśāḥ saṃkhyāḥ parimāṇāni pṛthaktvaṃ saṃyogavibhāgau paratvāparatve buddhayaḥ sukhaduḥkhe icchādveṣau prayatnaś ca guṇāḥ |
kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.5.english_close: Colour, taste, smell, touch, number, magnitude, distinctness, conjunction, disjunction, remoteness and proximity, cognitions, pleasure and pain, desire and aversion, and effort are qualities.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/2.english_close: Substance, quality, motion, universal, particularity, inherence, and absence are the seven categories.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/26.english_close: Distinctness is the cause of separate cognition; it resides in all substances.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/77.sanskrit_iast: nityam ekam anekānugataṃ sāmānyam / dravyaguṇakarmavṛtti / tad dvividhaṃ parāparabhedāt / paraṃ sattā / aparaṃ dravyatvādiḥ //
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/77.english_close: Universal is eternal, one, and present in many; it resides in substance, quality, and motion. It is twofold as higher and lower: higher is being, lower is substancehood and the like.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/79.sanskrit_iast: nityasaṃbandhaḥ samavāyaḥ / ayutasiddhavṛttiḥ / yayor dvayor madhya ekam avinaśyadaparāśritam evāvatiṣṭhate tāv ayutasiddhau / yathāvayavāvayavinau guṇaguṇinau kriyākriyāvantau jātivyaktī viśeṣanityadravye ceti //
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/79.english_close: Inherence is an eternal relation; it occurs where the relata are inseparably established. Such relata are part and whole, quality and qualified, action and actor, universal and particular, and particularity with an eternal substance.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: prakāśasyeva nānyasya bhedinas tv avabhāsanam //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: The manifestation of what is differentiated belongs only to the luminous itself, and not to anything else.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: āntaratvāt pramātraikye naiṣāṃ bhedanibandhanā / arthakriyāpi bāhyatve sā bhinnābhāsabhedataḥ //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: Because of interiority and the unity of the subject, their difference does not rest on an external divider; even practical efficacy, when externality is posited, depends on the distinctness of manifested appearances.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2..english_close: Such a cognition is not erroneous, because its object is of the nature of difference-and-non-difference.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4..sanskrit_iast: meyaṃ sādhāraṇaṃ muktaḥ svātmābhedena manyate / maheśvaro yathā baddhaḥ punar atyantabhedavat //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4..english_close: The liberated one regards the common object as non-different from his own self, like Maheśvara; the bound one, by contrast, as if absolutely different.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/3.76.english_close: From repeated practice of the tattvas, through the rejection 'not this, not this', there is the accomplishment of discrimination.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/67.sanskrit_iast: samyagjñānādhigamād dharmādīnām akāraṇaprāptau / tiṣṭhati saṃskāravaśāc cakrabhramavad dhṛtaśarīraḥ //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/67.english_close: With the attainment of right knowledge, dharma and the other factors cease to be causally operative; yet, by force of residual impressions, one remains embodied like a wheel that keeps turning.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.26.english_close: Uninterrupted discriminative insight is the means of cessation.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/2.26.sanskrit_iast: sattvapuruṣānyatāpratyayo vivekakhyātiḥ ... sā vivekakhyātir aviplavā hānopāyaḥ
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/2.26.english_close: Vivekakhyāti is the cognition of the distinctness of sattva and puruṣa; that discriminative insight, when uninterrupted, is the means of cessation.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.52.english_close: From saṃyama on the moment and its sequence there arises knowledge born of discrimination.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.54.sanskrit_iast: tārakaṃ sarvaviṣayaṃ sarvathāviṣayam akramaṃ ceti vivekajaṃ jñānam
patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.54.english_close: Knowledge born of discrimination is salvific, has all things as its object, knows them in every way, and is non-sequential.

gautama/nyaya-sutra/1.1.1.english_close: From true knowledge of the means of knowledge, objects of knowledge, doubt, purpose, example, established conclusion, syllogistic members, reasoning, ascertainment, debate, wrangling, destructive criticism, fallacious reason, quibble, futile rejoinder, and points of defeat, there is the attainment of the highest good.

vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.2.4.sanskrit_iast: sadasator upalambhād vidyā dvividhā, sadasator anupalambhād avidyāpi dvividhā / upalambhyamāne 'vayavini vidyādvaividhyāt saṃśayaḥ, anupalabhyamāne cāvidyādvaividhyāt saṃśayaḥ /
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.2.4.english_close: Knowledge is twofold because there is cognition of both what exists and what does not; ignorance too is twofold because there is non-cognition of both what exists and what does not. When the whole is cognized, doubt arises because knowledge is twofold; when it is not cognized, doubt arises because ignorance is twofold.

vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.2.46.sanskrit_iast: tasyāpavargasyādhigamāya yamaniyamābhyām ātmasaṃskāraḥ / yamaḥ samānam āśramiṇāṃ dharmasādhanam, niyamas tu viśiṣṭam / ātmasaṃskāraḥ punar adharmahānaṃ dharmopacayaś ca / yogaśāstrāc cādhyātmavidhiḥ pratipattavyaḥ /
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.2.46.english_close: For the attainment of liberation there is purification of the self by yama and niyama: yama is the common means of dharma for the āśramins, while niyama is the specific one. This purification of the self is the removal of adharma and the increase of dharma. And from the Yoga-śāstra the discipline concerning the self is to be learned.

vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.2.47.sanskrit_iast: jñāyate 'neneti jñānam ātmavidyāśāstraṃ tasya grahaṇam adhyayanadhāraṇe, abhyāsaḥ satatakriyādhyayanaśravaṇacintanāni / tadvidyaiś ca saha saṃvāda iti prajñāparipākārtham, paripākas tu saṃśayacchedanam avijñātārthabodho 'dhyavasitābhyanujñānam iti /
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.2.47.english_close: Knowledge, that by which something is known, is the treatise on self-knowledge; its acquisition consists in study and retention, and its repeated exercise consists in constant practice, study, hearing, and reflection. Dialogue with those who know that science serves the ripening of insight; and that ripening is the cutting of doubt, the understanding of what was not understood, and assent to what has been determined.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3..sanskrit_iast: īśvaro bahir unmeṣo nimeṣo 'ntaḥ sadāśivaḥ / sāmānādhikaraṇyaṃ ca sadvidyāhamidaṃdhiyoḥ //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3..english_close: Īśvara is the outward opening; Sadāśiva is the inward closing. And in Sadvidyā there is common referentiality between the cognitions 'I' and 'this'.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3..sanskrit_iast: tasyaiśvaryasvabhāvasya paśubhāve prakāśikā / vidyāśaktiḥ tirodhānakarī māyābhidhā punaḥ //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3..english_close: For that essential lordship, when in the state of the bound soul, the illuminating power is vidyāśakti; the concealing power, by contrast, is called māyā.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9.english_close: Knowledge is of the kinds called sensory-mental, scriptural, clairvoyant, telepathic, and omniscient.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/10.1.english_close: Omniscience arises from the destruction of delusion and from the destruction of the karmic obscurations of knowledge, vision, and obstruction.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/57.sanskrit_iast:
vatsavivṛddhinimittaṃ kṣīrasya yathā pravṛttir ajñasya /
puruṣavimokṣanimittaṃ tathā pravṛttiḥ pradhānasya //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/57.english_close: Just as milk, though unconscious, acts for the calf's growth, so the activity of pradhāna is for the sake of the puruṣa's release.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/62.sanskrit_iast:
tasmān na badhyate nāpi mucyate nāpi saṃsarati kaścit /
saṃsarati badhyate mucyate ca nānāśrayā prakṛtiḥ //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/62.english_close: Therefore no one is bound, released, or transmigrates; prakṛti, resting on diverse bases, transmigrates, is bound, and is released.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/63.sanskrit_iast:
rūpaiḥ saptabhir evaṃ badhnāty ātmānam ātmanā prakṛtiḥ /
saiva ca puruṣasyārthaṃ prati vimocayaty ekarūpeṇa //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/63.english_close: Through seven forms, prakṛti thus binds itself by itself, and for the sake of puruṣa it releases itself by a single form.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.5.english_close: Avidyā is the taking of the non-eternal, impure, painful, and non-self to be eternal, pure, pleasant, and self.

vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/4.13.sanskrit_iast: sarvam idaṃ guṇānāṃ saṃniveśaviśeṣamātram iti paramārthatas guṇātmanaḥ. tathā ca śāstrānuśāsanam: guṇānāṃ paramaṃ rūpaṃ na dṛṣṭipatham ṛcchati, yat tu dṛṣṭipathaṃ prāptaṃ tan māyā iva sutucchakam.
vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/4.13.english_close: All this is, in ultimate terms, merely a particular arrangement of the guṇa-s. Scripture accordingly teaches: the supreme form of the guṇa-s does not come into the range of sight; what has come into the range of sight is like māyā, exceedingly insubstantial.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.2.english_close: With the successive cessation of suffering, birth, activity, defect, and false cognition, liberation follows from the cessation of each immediately prior member.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/4.2.32.english_close: Or [it is] like māyā, a gandharva-city, or a mirage.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/4.2.35.english_close: The destruction of false apprehension comes from knowledge of reality, like the destruction of the dream-object conceit upon waking.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/9.25-27.sanskrit_iast:
indriyadoṣāt saṃskārāc cāvidyā /
tad duṣṭaṃ jñānam /
aduṣṭaṃ vidyā //
kanada/vaisesika-sutra/9.25-27.english_close: From defect of sense and from saṃskāra comes avidyā. That is defective cognition. Non-defective cognition is vidyā.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/7.34.sanskrit_iast:
yathā māyā yathā svapno gandharvanagaraṃ yathā /
tathotpādas tathā sthānaṃ tathā bhaṅga udāhṛtam //
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/7.34.english_close: Like māyā, like a dream, like a gandharva-city: so are origination, duration, and destruction taught.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/23.9.sanskrit_iast:
aśubhaṃ vā śubhaṃ vāpi kutas teṣu bhaviṣyati /
māyāpuruṣakalpeṣu pratibimbasameṣu ca //
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/23.9.english_close: How could the impure or the pure arise in things that are like magical persons and like reflections?

nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/66.sanskrit_iast:
sa yadi svabhāvataḥ syād bhāvo na syāt pratītya samudbhūtaḥ /
yaś ca pratītya bhavati grāho nanu śūnyatā saiva //
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/66.english_close: If a thing existed by its own nature, it would not be dependently arisen. But whatever apprehension arises dependently, that indeed is emptiness.

abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/17.sanskrit_iast:
adhunaiva kiṃcid evedam eva sarvātmanaiva jānāmi /
māyāsahitaṃ kañcukaṣaṭkam aṇor antaraṅgam idam uktam //
abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/17.english_close: I know this now only in part, not in its all-form. This inner equipment of the atomic subject is said to consist of māyā together with the six kañcukas.

ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/6.english_close: The māyā-subject is constituted of that.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/27.sanskrit_iast: ubhayātmakam atra manaḥ saṅkalpakam indriyaṃ ca sādharmyāt / guṇapariṇāmaviśeṣān nānātvaṃ grāhyabhedāc ca //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/27.english_close: Here the mind is of both kinds, being a conceptualizer and also an organ because of homology; plurality arises from distinctions in the transformations of the guṇas and from difference in the objects grasped.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.57.english_close: From non-discrimination of pradhāna comes the other non-discrimination; when that is removed, the rest are removed.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/2.27.english_close: Plurality is due to difference in the transformations of the guṇas, as with distinct states.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.49.sanskrit_iast: nirdhūtarajastamomalasya buddhisattvasya pare vaiśāradye parasyāṃ vaśīkārasaṃjñāyāṃ vartamānasya sattvapuruṣānyatākhyātimātrarūpapratiṣṭhasya sarvabhāvādhiṣṭhātṛtvam. sarvātmāno guṇā vyavasāyavyavaseyātmakāḥ svāminaṃ kṣetrajñaṃ pratyaśeṣadṛśyātmatvenopasthitā ity arthaḥ. sarvajñātṛtvaṃ sarvātmanāṃ guṇānāṃ śāntoditāvyapadeśyadharmatvena vyavasthitānām akramopārūḍhaṃ vivekajaṃ jñānam ity arthaḥ.
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.49.english_close: For one established only in the cognition of the otherness of sattva and puruṣa, once the stains of rajas and tamas have been shaken off from the sattva of the intellect, there is lordship over all states. The guṇas stand forth wholly as the field for their master, the knower of the field. Omniscience is a discriminative knowledge that ranges over all their states.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.55.sanskrit_iast: yadā nirdhūtarajastamomalaṃ buddhisattvaṃ puruṣasyānyatāpratītimātrādhikāraṃ dagdhakleśabījaṃ bhavati tadā puruṣasya śuddhisārūpyam ivāpannaṃ bhavati, tadā puruṣasyopacaritabhogābhāvaḥ śuddhiḥ. etasyām avasthāyāṃ kaivalyaṃ bhavati.
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.55.english_close: When the sattva of the intellect, with the impurity of rajas and tamas shaken off, becomes fit only for the apprehension of the puruṣa's otherness and has its seed of kleśa burned, it comes, as it were, into likeness with the purity of the puruṣa. In that state there is kaivalya.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.2.3-6.sanskrit_iast: sāmānyaṃ viśeṣa iti buddhyapekṣam | bhāvaḥ sāmānyam eva | dravyatvaṃ guṇatvaṃ karmatvaṃ ca sāmānyāni viśeṣāś ca | anyatrāntyebhyo viśeṣebhyaḥ |
kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.2.3-6.english_close: Universal and particular are relative to cognition. Being is just the universal. Dravyatva, guṇatva, and karmatva are universals, and there are also particulars apart from the ultimate particulars.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/7.2.1.english_close: Unity is a distinct entity from color, taste, smell, and touch; so too is separateness.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/78.english_close: The differentiators called viśeṣas inhere in eternal substances and function as delimiters.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/2.2.19.sanskrit_iast: tadbhedāt karmaṇo 'bhyāso dravyapṛthaktvād anarthakaṃ hi syād bhedo dravyaguṇībhāvāt |
jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/2.2.19.english_close: Because of that difference there is repetition of the rite, on account of the distinctness of the material; otherwise the difference would be purposeless, since the material stands as a subordinate qualifier.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/2.4.8.english_close: Across recensions there may be a difference of rites because one observes difference in name, form, specific property, repetition, censure, incapacity, concluding statement, expiation, or some other purpose.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: māyāśaktyā vibhoḥ saiva bhinnasaṃvedyagocarā / kathitā jñānasaṃkalpādhyavasāyādināmabhiḥ //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: By the power of māyā of the all-pervading One, that very [consciousness] becomes a domain of distinct objects of awareness; it is designated by names such as cognition, conception, determination, and the like.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.24-28.sanskrit_iast: śabda-saukṣmya-sthaulya-saṃsthāna-bhedatas-chāyātapodyotavantaś ca. aṇavaḥ skandhāś ca. bhedasaṃghātebhya utpadyante. bhedād aṇuḥ. bhedasaṃghātābhyāṃ cākṣuṣaḥ.
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.24-28.english_close: Matter possesses sound, fineness, grossness, shape, divisibility, shade, heat, and light. There are atoms and aggregates. They arise from division and aggregation. An atom comes from division. What is visible arises from division and aggregation.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.29-30.english_close: This is the mark of a real substance: the real is endowed with origination, cessation, and persistence.

abhinavagupta/tantraloka/13.118-119.sanskrit_iast:
anapekṣya śive bhaktiḥ śaktipāto 'phalārthinām /
yā phalārthitayā bhaktiḥ sā karmādyamapekṣate //
tato 'tra syāt phale bhedo nāpavarge tvasau tathā /
bhogāpavargadvitayābhisaṃdhātur api sphuṭam //
abhinavagupta/tantraloka/13.118-119.english_close: Bhakti to Śiva, for those who do not seek fruits, is śaktipāta and depends on nothing else. The bhakti that is motivated by fruit-seeking depends on karma and the like. Therefore there is difference here in the fruit, though not so in liberation, even for one who plainly intends both enjoyment and release.

abhinavagupta/tantraloka/13.285-287.sanskrit_iast:
purāṇe 'pi ca tasyaiva prasādād bhaktir iṣyate /
yayā yānti parāṃ siddhiṃ tadbhāvagatamānasāḥ //
evakāreṇa karmādisāpekṣatvaṃ niṣidhyate /
prasādo nirmalībhāvas tena saṃpūrṇarūpatā //
ātmanā tena hi śivaḥ svayaṃ pūrṇaḥ prakāśate /
śivībhāvamahāsiddhisparśavandhye tu kutracit //
abhinavagupta/tantraloka/13.285-287.english_close: Even in the Purāṇa, bhakti is held to arise from his grace alone; by it, those whose minds have entered his state attain the highest accomplishment. By the restrictive particle, dependence on karma and the like is denied; grace is inner purification, and by it there is fullness. Through that very state, Śiva, full in himself, shines forth of himself, and apart from it there is no contact with the great accomplishment of becoming Śiva.

abhinavagupta/tantraloka/15.31.sanskrit_iast:
samayācārapāśaṃ tu nirbījāyāṃ viśodhayet /
dīkṣāmātreṇa muktiḥ syād bhaktyā deve gurau sadā //
abhinavagupta/tantraloka/15.31.english_close: In the nirbīja initiation one should purify the bond of samayācāra. Liberation comes by initiation alone, always together with devotion to the deity and the guru.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.149.english_close: The plurality of puruṣas follows from the ordered distribution of birth and the rest.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.154.english_close: There is no conflict with the non-dual scripture, because it is directed to the genus.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/6.46.english_close: If it is an upādhi, then once that is admitted duality appears again.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.50.sanskrit_iast: tad eteṣāṃ guṇānāṃ manasi karmakleśavipākasvarūpeṇābhivyaktānāṃ caritārthānāṃ pratiprasave puruṣasyātyantiko guṇaviyogaḥ kaivalyaṃ tadā svarūpapratiṣṭhā citiśaktir eva puruṣa iti
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.50.english_close: When these guṇas, manifested in the mind as karma, affliction, and fruition, have fulfilled their task and subside back, the absolute disjunction of puruṣa from the guṇas is kaivalya; then puruṣa stands in its own nature as pure consciousness-power.

vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/2.2.31.sanskrit_iast: yad idam anyad iti manyase tat svārthenānanyatvād anyan na bhavati, evam anyatāyā abhāvaḥ
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/2.2.31.english_close: What you take to be 'other' does not become non-other merely through being non-different from its own nature; thus the abolition of otherness does not follow.

vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/2.2.32.sanskrit_iast: anyasmād ananyatām upapādayati bhavān, upapādya cānyat pratyācaṣṭe ... tasmāt tayor anyānanyaśabdayor itaro 'nanyaśabda itaram anyaśabdam apekṣamāṇaḥ sidhyati
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/2.2.32.english_close: You establish non-otherness only in relation to something other, and having done so you still speak of the other; therefore the terms 'other' and 'non-other' are each established only in dependence upon the other.

vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.1.43.sanskrit_iast: sarvam ekam ity anapavargeṇa pratijñāya kasyacid ekatvam ucyate, tatra vyapavṛkto 'vayavaḥ sādhanabhūto nopapadyate
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.1.43.english_close: If one first lays down the unrestricted thesis 'everything is one' and then speaks of the unity of something in particular, no distinct component remains available as a probative member.

abhinavagupta/tantrasara/2.4.sanskrit_iast: tasmāt samastam idam ekaṃ cinmātratattvaṃ kālena akalitaṃ deśena aparicchinnam upādhibhir amlānam ākṛtibhir aniyantritaṃ śabdair asaṃdiṣṭaṃ pramāṇair aprapañcitaṃ ... tad eva ca aham tatraiva antar mayi viśvaṃ pratibimbitam
abhinavagupta/tantrasara/2.4.english_close: Therefore all this is one reality consisting only of consciousness, untouched by time, unbounded by space, unstained by limiting adjuncts, and not parceled out by language or proofs; that very reality am I, and within me the universe is reflected.

abhinavagupta/tantrasara/3.1-3.8.sanskrit_iast: sarvam idaṃ bhāvajātaṃ bodhagagane pratibimbamātraṃ pratibimbalakṣaṇopetatvāt ... evaṃ yathā etat pratibimbitaṃ bhāti tathaiva viśvaṃ parameśvaraprakāśe ... viśvātmakatvaṃ parameśvarasya
abhinavagupta/tantrasara/3.1-3.8.english_close: This whole manifold of entities is only a reflection in the sky of awareness; just as a reflection appears there, so too the universe appears in the light of the Lord, and the Lord is of the very nature of the universe.

ksemaraja/siva-sutra-varttika/2.3.1.english_close: Vidyā is of the nature of the realization of supreme non-duality.

ksemaraja/siva-sutra-varttika/3.6.18.sanskrit_iast: śivo 'ham advitīyo 'haṃ samādhiḥ sa paraḥ smṛtaḥ
ksemaraja/siva-sutra-varttika/3.6.18.english_close: ‘I am Śiva, I am without a second’: that is remembered as the highest samādhi.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/19-20.sanskrit_iast:
tasmāc ca viparyāsāt siddhaṃ sākṣitvam asya puruṣasya /
kaivalyaṃ mādhyasthyaṃ draṣṭṛtvam akartṛbhāvaś ca //

tasmāt tatsaṃyogād acetanaṃ cetanāvad iva liṅgam /
guṇakartṛtve 'pi tathā karteva bhavaty udāsīnaḥ //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/19-20.english_close: Therefore, from that inversion it is established that this puruṣa is a witness; and likewise isolation, neutrality, seerhood, and non-agenthood. Therefore, through conjunction with that, the non-conscious subtle body appears as if conscious; though the guṇas are the agents, the indifferent puruṣa appears as if a doer.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika-comm/20.sanskrit_iast: yasmāc cetanasvabhāvaḥ puruṣaḥ, tasmāt tatsaṃyogād acetanaṃ mahadādi liṅgam adhyavasāyābhimānasaṅkalpālocanādiṣu vṛttiṣu cetanāvat pravartate | ... sattvādayo guṇāḥ kartāras taiḥ saṃyuktaḥ puruṣo 'pi akartāpi kartā bhavati | kartṛsaṃsargāt karteva | paraṃ paramārthatayā akartā puruṣaḥ //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika-comm/20.english_close: Since the puruṣa is conscious by nature, the non-conscious subtle body beginning with mahat behaves as if conscious in functions such as determination, egoity, intention, and cognition through conjunction with it. The guṇas beginning with sattva are the doers; the puruṣa, though non-agent, appears as a doer through association with those agents. In ultimate truth, however, the puruṣa is not an agent.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika-comm/62.sanskrit_iast: puruṣo na badhyate sarvagatatvāt, avikāratvāt, niṣkriyatvāt, akartṛtvāt | ... saṃsarati badhyate mucyate ca nānāśrayā prakṛtiḥ | prakṛtir ātmānaṃ badhnāti, mocayati, saṃsarati ca |
