isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika-comm/62.english_close: The puruṣa is not bound, because it is all-pervasive, unchanging, inactive, and non-agent. It is prakṛti, taking on various bases, that transmigrates, is bound, and is released; prakṛti binds itself, releases itself, and transmigrates.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/2.18.sanskrit_iast: ayaṃ tu khalu triṣu guṇeṣu kartṛṣv akartari ca puruṣe tulyātulyajātīye caturthe tatkriyāsākṣiṇy upanīyamānān sarvabhāvān upapannān anupaśyann adarśanam anyac chaṅkata iti. ... evaṃ bandhamokṣau buddhāv eva vartamānau puruṣe vyapadiśyete, sa hi tatphalasya bhokteti.
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/2.18.english_close: Here, when the three guṇas are the agents and the puruṣa is the non-agent, the fourth, witnessing their activity, sees all states being brought forward and wonders whether there is any other non-seeing principle. Thus bondage and release, though occurring in buddhi alone, are ascribed to puruṣa, because it is the experiencer of their fruit.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.20.english_close: The seer is pure seeing alone, though it witnesses the mental presentations.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.9.sanskrit_iast: tathā pratiṣiddhavastudharmo niṣkriyaḥ puruṣaḥ, tiṣṭhati bāṇaḥ sthāsyati sthita iti. gatinivṛttau dhātvarthamātraṃ gamyate.
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.9.english_close: Likewise, the puruṣa is inactive, though one speaks as if of a property belonging to it, as in expressions such as 'the arrow stands' or 'will stand'; what is understood is only the verbal meaning after motion has ceased.

gautama/nyaya-sutra/1.1.10.english_close: Desire, aversion, effort, pleasure, pain, and cognition are marks of the self.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/5.1.1.english_close: Action in the hand arises from conjunction with the self and from effort.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/6.2.17.english_close: The activity that yields dharma and adharma is preceded by desire and aversion.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/3.1.5-6.sanskrit_iast:
phalaṃ ca puruṣārthatvāt /
puruṣaś ca karmārthatvāt /
jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/3.1.5-6.english_close: And the fruit is for the sake of the person. And the person too is for the sake of the act.

shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.2.sanskrit_iast: yo hi yāgam anutiṣṭhati, taṃ dhārmika iti samācakṣate. yaś ca yasya kartā, sa tena vyapadiśyate, yathā pācako lāvaka iti. tena yaḥ puruṣaṃ niḥśreyasena saṃyunakti, sa eva dharmaśabdenocyate.
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.2.english_close: The one who performs a sacrifice is called righteous. And whoever is the doer of something is designated by that, as one says 'cook' or 'reaper'. Therefore that which connects a person with the highest good is what is denoted by the word dharma.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/2.3.14.english_close: The verbal root *yaj* is understood through its connection with substance, fruit, and experiencer, because these stand in relation to the act.

shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/7.4.1.sanskrit_iast: yajatis tu dravyaphalabhoktṛsaṃyogād eṣāṃ karmasaṃbandhād ... yajir apūrvaṃ sādhayati. tataś cāpūrvāt kālāntare phalaṃ bhavatīti.
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/7.4.1.english_close: The root *yaj* is understood through the connection of these with substance, fruit, and experiencer, because they are connected with the act. And the verbal root *yaj* accomplishes apūrva; from that apūrva the fruit arises later in time.

abhinavagupta/tantraloka/14.24-27.sanskrit_iast:
itthaṃ sṛṣṭisthitidhvaṃsatirobhāvamanugrahaḥ /
iti pañcasu kartṛtvaṃ śivatvaṃ saṃvidātmanaḥ //

pañcakṛtyasvatantratvasaṃpūrṇasvātmamāninaḥ /
...

jñātṛtvameva śivatā svātantryaṃ tadihocyate /
kulālavattu kartṛtvaṃ na mukhyaṃ tadadhiṣṭhiteḥ //
abhinavagupta/tantraloka/14.24-27.english_close: Thus creation, maintenance, dissolution, concealment, and grace: in these five, agency and Śiva-hood belong to consciousness itself. Śiva-hood is precisely knowerhood, and that is here called freedom; potter-like agency is not primary, because it depends on that underlying reality.

abhinavagupta/tantraloka/9.8.sanskrit_iast:
vastutaḥ sarvabhāvānāṃ karteśānaḥ paraḥ śivaḥ /
asvatantrasya kartṛtvaṃ nahi jātūpapadyate //
abhinavagupta/tantraloka/9.8.english_close: In reality the supreme Śiva, Lord, is the doer of all states of being; agency can never belong to what is not free.

ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/34.sanskrit_iast: svātantryam atha kartṛtvaṃ mukhyam īśvaratāpi ca
ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/34.english_close: Freedom, then, is primary agency and also lordship.

ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/9-10.sanskrit_iast:
cidvat tac chaktisaṃkocāt malāvṛtaḥ saṃsārī //
... kriyāśaktiḥ krameṇa bhede sarvakartṛtvasya kiṃcitkartṛtvāpteḥ ... kārmaṃ malam ...

tathāpi tadvat pañcakṛtyāni karoti //
ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/9-10.english_close: Because of the contraction of its powers, though consciousness-like, the soul covered by impurity becomes a transmigrating individual. In the domain of difference, its power of action contracts from universal agency to limited agency and becomes the karmic impurity. Even so, like that higher reality, it still performs the five acts.

abhinavagupta/tantraloka/30.2-3.sanskrit_iast:
pratibuddhā hi te mantrā vimarśaikasvabhāvakāḥ /
svatantrasyaiva ciddhāmnaḥ svātantryāt kartṛtāmayāḥ //

yamāviśanti cācāryaṃ taṃ tādātmyanirūḍhitaḥ /
svatantrīkurvate yānti karaṇānyapi kartṛtām //
abhinavagupta/tantraloka/30.2-3.english_close: Those mantras, when awakened, are of the single nature of reflexive awareness, consisting in agency through the freedom of the autonomous ground of consciousness alone. Entering the teacher, they make him autonomous through firm identity with that reality, and even the instruments attain agency.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/8.1-2.sanskrit_iast:
mithyādarśanāviratipramādakaṣāyayogā bandhahetavaḥ //
sakaṣāyatvāj jīvaḥ karmaṇoyogyān pudgalān ādatte sa bandhaḥ //
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/8.1-2.english_close: False view, non-restraint, negligence, passions, and yoga are the causes of bondage. Because it is passion-laden, the soul takes up those particles of matter fit to become karma; that is bondage.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.20--na-avidyato-p.english_close: Nor does bondage arise from avidyā either, since bondage from a non-entity is impossible.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika-with-commenta.sanskrit_iast: jñānasya viparyayo 'jñānaṃ, tenājñānenāhaṃ darśanīyaḥ subhago 'haṃ bhoktāham iti manyate | tenājñānena manuṣyatiryagdeveṣv ātmānaṃ nibadhnāti |
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika-with-commenta.english_close: The contrary of knowledge is ignorance. Through that ignorance one thinks, 'I am admirable; I am fortunate; I am the enjoyer.' Through that ignorance one binds oneself among humans, animals, and gods.

vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.2.1--mithyajna.sanskrit_iast: mithyājñānaṃ vai khalu moho na tattvajñānasyānutpattimātram | tac ca mithyājñānaṃ yatra viṣaye pravartamānaṃ saṃsārabījaṃ bhavati sa viṣayas tattvato jñeya iti | kiṃ punas tan mithyājñānam? anātmany ātmagrahaḥ, ahaṃ asmīti moho 'haṅkāra iti |
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.2.1--mithyajna.english_close: Delusion is in fact false cognition, not merely the non-arising of true cognition. Wherever such false cognition operates, its object must be known as it really is, since that false cognition is the seed of saṃsāra. And what is that false cognition? It is the grasping of self in what is not self, the delusion 'I am this,' namely egoity.

shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.5--mith.sanskrit_iast: yadā kṣudādibhir upahataṃ mano bhavati, indriyaṃ vā timirādibhiḥ, saukṣmyādibhir vā bāhyo viṣayaḥ, tato mithyājñānam, anupahateṣu saṃyagjñānam | indriyamanorthasaṃnikarṣo hi jñānasya hetuḥ, asati tasmin ajñānāt | tadantargato doṣo mithyājñānasya hetuḥ | duṣṭeṣu hi jñānaṃ mithyā bhavati |
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.5--mith.english_close: When the mind is impaired by hunger and the like, or a sense-faculty by cataract and the like, or the external object by subtlety and the like, then there is false cognition; when these are unimpaired, there is correct cognition. Sense, mind, and object in conjunction are the cause of cognition; in their failure there is non-knowing. The defect within that complex is the cause of false cognition, for cognition in defective conditions becomes false.

shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/3.8.24--mit.sanskrit_iast: tasmād etan mithyādarśanam, yasya hi darśanasya pramāṇaṃ nāsti, vyāmohaḥ saḥ | yathā śuktikāyāṃ rajatavijñānam |
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/3.8.24--mit.english_close: Therefore this is false seeing. For any seeing that has no pramāṇa is confusion, as in the cognition of silver in a shell.

abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/25.sanskrit_iast: ajñānatimirayogād ekam api svaṃ svabhāvam ātmānam | grāhyagrāhakanānāvaicitryeṇāvabudhyeta ||
abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/25.english_close: Because of conjunction with the darkness of ignorance, the self, though one in its own nature, apprehends itself under the manifold variety of object and subject.

abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/54.sanskrit_iast: lokavyavahārakṛtāṃ ya ihāvidyām upāsate mūḍhāḥ | te yānti janmamṛtyū dharmādharmārgalābaddhāḥ ||
abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/54.english_close: Those deluded ones who cultivate the avidyā that produces worldly transaction go to birth and death, fastened by the bolt of dharma and adharma.

abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/60.sanskrit_iast: mokṣasya naiva kiṃcid dhāmāsti na cāpi gamanam anyatra | ajñānagranthibhidā svaśaktyabhivyaktatā mokṣaḥ ||
abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/60.english_close: Liberation has no separate place, nor is there any going elsewhere. Liberation is the cutting of the knot of ignorance and the manifestation of one's own power.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.31.english_close: Erroneous cognition also occurs in sensory-mental knowledge, scriptural knowledge, and clairvoyance.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/8.1.english_close: False view, non-restraint, negligence, passions, and activity are the causes of bondage.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/8.4.english_close: The first division [of karmic bondage] consists of knowledge-obscuring, perception-obscuring, feeling-producing, deluding, lifespan, body-making, status, and obstructive karmas.

jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§19.sanskrit_iast: tena brahma hṛdā iti sarva-lokāśrayatva-buddhi-vṛtti-prerakatvādi-sāmyāt | dharma-vistara ity atra dharma-śabdaḥ parama-dharma-paraḥ | dharmaḥ projjhita-kaitavo'tra paramaḥ [bhp 1.1.2] ity atraiva pratipāditatvāt | sa ca bhagavad-dhyānādi-lakṣaṇa eveti purastād vyaktībhaviṣyati ||19||
jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§19.english_close: By the parallel with 'tena brahma hṛdā', in the phrase 'expansion of dharma' the word dharma refers to the highest dharma. That has already been taught in 'here the highest dharma, free from deceit, is set forth'; and it will become clear further on that it is characterized by meditation on Bhagavān and the like.

baladeva/tattva-sandarbha-tippani/§23.english_close: The dharma taught there has the defining character of Kṛṣṇa-saṅkīrtana.

abhinavagupta/tantraloka/1.82-84.sanskrit_iast: tattvagrāmasya sarvasya dharmaḥ syādanapāyavān / ātmaiva hi svabhāvātmetyuktaṃ śrītriśiromate // hṛdisthaṃ sarvadehasthaṃ svabhāvasthaṃ susūkṣmakam / sāmūhyaṃ caiva tattvānāṃ grāmaśabdena kīrtitam // ātmaiva dharma ityuktaḥ śivāmṛtapariplutaḥ / prakāśāvasthitaṃ jñānaṃ bhāvābhāvādimadhyataḥ //
abhinavagupta/tantraloka/1.82-84.english_close: The unfailing dharma of the whole cluster of tattvas is the self itself, as the Śrītriśiro-mata says. That cluster is heart-abiding, present in every body, established in its own nature, extremely subtle, and is the collective of the tattvas. The self itself, flooded by Śiva's nectar, is said to be dharma: knowledge established in luminosity amid being, non-being, and the rest.

abhinavagupta/tantraloka/9.291-294.sanskrit_iast: yathā ca vistṛte vastre yugapadbhāti citratā / tathaiva yogināṃ dharmasāmastyenāvabhāti bhūḥ // gandhādiśabdaparyantacitrarūpā dharā tataḥ / upāyabhedādbhātyeṣā kramākramavibhāgataḥ // tata eva kramavyaktikṛto dhībheda ucyate / ṣaṣṭhīprayogo dhībhedādbhedyabhedakatā tathā // tena dharmātirikto'tra dharmī nāma na kaścana / tatrānekaprakārāḥ syurgandharūparasāḥ kṣitau //
abhinavagupta/tantraloka/9.291-294.english_close: Just as variegation appears all at once on an extended cloth, so for yogins the earth appears through the totality of its dharmas. This earth is variegated from smell up to sound and appears according to differences of method, whether successive or non-successive. Hence there is here no dharmin distinct from dharma; in earth there are only the many forms of smell, color, and taste.

abhinavagupta/tantraloka/15.23-29.sanskrit_iast: sādhako dvividhaḥ śaivadharmā lokojjhitasthitiḥ // lokadharmī phalākāṃkṣī śubhasthaścāśubhojjhitaḥ / dvidhā mumukṣurnirbījaḥ samayādivivarjitaḥ // bālabāliśavṛddhastrībhogabhugvyādhitādikaḥ / anyaḥ sabījo yasyetthaṃ dīkṣoktā śivaśāsane // vidvaddvandvasahānāṃ tu sabījā samayātmikā / dīkṣānugrāhikā pālyā viśeṣasamayāstu taiḥ // abhāvaṃ bhāvayetsamyakkarmaṇāṃ prācyabhāvinām / mumukṣornirapekṣasya prārabdham ekaṃ na śādhayet // sādhakasya tu bhūtyartham itthameva viśodhayet / śivadharmiṇy asau dīkṣā lokadharmāpahāriṇī // adharmarūpiṇām eva na śubhānāṃ tu śodhanam / lokadharmiṇy asau dīkṣā mantrārādhanavarjitā //
abhinavagupta/tantraloka/15.23-29.english_close: The aspirant is of two kinds: one established in Śaiva-dharma and one moving within loka-dharma. The loka-dharmin seeks results and remains within auspicious action after abandoning the inauspicious. For the Śaiva-dharmin who seeks liberation without dependence, initiation does not work upon anything except the already-begun karma; for the sādhaka it purifies for the sake of attainment. That initiation belonging to the Śaiva-dharmin removes loka-dharma, whereas the initiation belonging to the loka-dharmin is without mantra-worship and purifies only what is of the form of adharma.

jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§9.sanskrit_iast: athaivaṃ sūcitānāṃ śrī-kṛṣṇa-tad-vācya-vācakatā-lakṣaṇa-sambandha-tad-bhajana-lakṣaṇa-vidheya-saparyāyābhidheya-tat-prema-lakṣaṇa-prayojanākhyānām arthānāṃ nirṇayāya tāvat pramāṇaṃ nirṇīyate | tatra puruṣasya bhramādi-doṣa-catuṣṭaya-duṣṭatvāt sutarām alaukikācintya-svabhāva-vastu-sparśāyogyatvāc ca tat-pratyakṣādīny api sadoṣāṇi ||
jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§9.english_close: Thus, for determining the meanings previously indicated — Kṛṣṇa, the relation of signifier and signified, the prescribed practice of his worship, the means, and the end called love — the pramāṇa is first determined. There, because the human subject is corrupted by the four defects beginning with error, and because he is unfit to touch a reality whose nature is alaukika and acintya, even perception and the rest are defective.

jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§10.sanskrit_iast: tatas tāni na pramāṇīty anādi-siddha-sarva-puruṣa-paramparāsi sarva-laukikālaukika-jñāna-nidānatvād aprākṛta-vacana-lakṣaṇo veda evāsmākaṃ sarvātīta-sarvāśraya-sarvācintyāścarya-svabhāvaṃ vastu vividiṣatāṃ pramāṇam ||
jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§10.english_close: Therefore those are not pramāṇas here; rather, because it is beginninglessly established in the succession of all persons, because it is the source of all worldly and trans-worldly knowledge, and because it is characterized as non-material speech, the Veda alone is our pramāṇa for those who wish to know a reality whose nature is all-transcending, the support of all, and altogether inconceivably wondrous.

jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§27.sanskrit_iast: tad evaṃ parama-niḥśreyasa-niścayāya śrī-bhāgavatam eva paurvāparyāvirodhena vicāryate | tatrāsmin sandarbha-ṣaṭkātmake granthe sūtra-sthānīyam avatārikā-vākyaṃ viṣaya-vākyaṃ śrī-bhāgavata-vākyam ||
jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§27.english_close: Thus, for settling the highest good, the Śrī-Bhāgavata alone is examined in a way free of contradiction with what precedes and follows. In this sixfold Sandarbha, the Bhāgavata sentence serves as the topic-statement standing in the place of the sūtra.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/4.sanskrit_iast: dṛṣṭam anumānam āptavacanaṃ ca sarvapramāṇasiddhatvāt / trividhaṃ pramāṇam iṣṭaṃ prameyasiddhiḥ pramāṇād dhi //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/4.english_close: Perception, inference, and authoritative testimony: because all the means of knowledge are accounted for there, a threefold pramāṇa is accepted, for the establishment of the knowable depends on pramāṇa.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/4-commentary.sanskrit_iast: pratyakṣānumānābhyāṃ yo 'rthaḥ sādhayituṃ na śakyas tatrāptavacanam ... nanv arthāpattiḥ sambhavo 'bhāvaḥ pratibhā aitihyam upamānaṃ ceti prabhṛtīni santi bahūni pramāṇāntarāṇi katham atra trividhaṃ pramāṇam iti saṅgatiḥ | atrocyate sarvapramāṇasiddhatvāt | sarvāṇi hi pramāṇāni pramāṇatraye 'viruddhāni ... tasmāt triṣv evānyad antarbhavatīti yuktam abhihitaṃ trividhaṃ pramāṇam |
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/4-commentary.english_close: Where an object cannot be established by perception and inference, there authoritative testimony operates. But if one asks about further pramāṇas such as postulation, possibility, non-apprehension, intuition, report, and comparison, the reply is that all such pramāṇas are non-discrepant with the triad, so the rest are included within the three alone.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.87-1.101.sanskrit_iast: dvayorekatarasyavāpy asannikṛṣṭārthaparicchittiḥ pramā, tatsādhakatamaṃ yat tat trividhaṃ pramāṇaṃ | yat saṃbaddhaṃ sat tadākārollekhi vijñānaṃ tat pratyakṣaṃ | pratibandhadṛśaḥ pratibaddhajñānam anumānaṃ | āptopadeśaḥ śabdaḥ |
kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.87-1.101.english_close: True cognition is the ascertainment of an object not immediately present to one or both parties; that which is most effective in bringing it about is the threefold pramāṇa. Cognition marking the form of a present connected object is perception; constrained cognition arising from a seen entailment is inference; the teaching of a trustworthy person is verbal testimony.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.6-1.7.english_close: The mental modifications are: valid cognition, error, conceptual construction, sleep, and memory. The pramāṇas are perception, inference, and authoritative transmission.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.7.sanskrit_iast: indriyapraṇālikayā cittasya bāhyavastūparāgāt tadviṣayā sāmānyaviśeṣātmano 'rthasya viśeṣāvadhāraṇapradhānā vṛttiḥ pratyakṣaṃ pramāṇam ... anumeyasya tulyajātīyeṣv anuvṛtto bhinnajātīyebhyo vyāvṛttaḥ saṃbandho yas tadviṣayā sāmānyāvadhāraṇapradhānā vṛttir anumānam ... āptena dṛṣṭo 'numito vārthaḥ paratra svabodhasaṃkrāntaye śabdenopadiśyate, śabdāt tadarthaviṣayā vṛttiḥ śrotur āgamaḥ |
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.7.english_close: Perception is the cognition whose leading function is the determinate ascertainment of an object, general and particular, through the mind’s being colored by an external thing via the channel of the senses. Inference is the cognition whose leading function is universal ascertainment through a relation that extends over similar cases and excludes dissimilar ones. Āgama is the listener’s cognition of an object conveyed by the word of a trustworthy person who has seen or inferred it.

gautama/nyaya-sutra/1.1.3-7.sanskrit_iast: pratyakṣānumānopamānaśabdāḥ pramāṇāni / indriyārthasannikarṣotpannaṃ jñānam avyapadeśyam avyabhicāri vyavasāyātmakaṃ pratyakṣam / atha tatpūrvakaṃ trividham anumānaṃ pūrvavat śeṣavat sāmānyatodṛṣṭaṃ ca / prasiddhasādharmyāt sādhyasādhanam upamānam / āptopadeśaḥ śabdaḥ /
gautama/nyaya-sutra/1.1.3-7.english_close: Perception, inference, comparison, and testimony are the pramāṇas. Perception is cognition produced by the contact of sense and object, non-verbal, non-errant, and determinate. Inference, comparison, and testimony are then defined respectively as cognition based on a prior mark, the establishment of what is to be established through known similarity, and the instruction of a trustworthy person.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/36.sanskrit_iast: yathārthānubhavaś caturvidhaḥ pratyakṣānumityupamitiśabdabhedāt / tatkaraṇam api caturvidhaṃ pratyakṣānumānopamānaśabdabhedāt //
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/36.english_close: Valid cognition is fourfold, divided as perception, inferential cognition, comparative cognition, and verbal cognition. Its instruments are likewise fourfold: perception, inference, comparison, and testimony.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/62.sanskrit_iast: vākyaṃ dvividham / vaidikaṃ laukikaṃ ca / vaidikam īśvaroktatvāt sarvam eva pramāṇam / laukikaṃ tv āptoktaṃ pramāṇam / anyad apramāṇam //
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/62.english_close: Sentence is twofold: Vedic and worldly. The Vedic is entirely pramāṇa because it is spoken by God; the worldly is pramāṇa only when spoken by a trustworthy person. Other speech is not pramāṇa.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/9.18-20,10.19-21.sanskrit_iast: asyedaṃ kāryaṃ kāraṇaṃ saṃbandhy ekārthasamavāyi virodhi ceti laiṅgikam / etena śābdaṃ vyākhyātam / hetur apadeśo liṅgaṃ nimittaṃ pramāṇaṃ kāraṇam ity anarthāntaram / ... laiṅgikaṃ pramāṇaṃ vyākhyātam / ... tadvacanād āmnāyaprāmāṇyam iti |
kanada/vaisesika-sutra/9.18-20,10.19-21.english_close: Inference is articulated through the relations ‘this is its effect,’ ‘this is its cause,’ ‘this is connected with it,’ ‘this inheres in the same thing,’ and ‘this is its contrary’; by this, verbal testimony too is explained. ‘Hetu’, ‘apadeśa’, ‘liṅga’, ‘nimitta’, ‘pramāṇa’, and ‘kāraṇa’ are here treated as not different, and the authority of the sacred tradition is affirmed on the basis of its statement.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.5.sanskrit_iast: autpattikas tu śabdasyārthena saṃbandhas tasya jñānam upadeśo 'vyatirekaś cārthe 'nupalabdhe tatpramāṇaṃ bādarāyaṇasyānapekṣatvāt
jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.5.english_close: The relation of word to meaning is original; its cognition is instruction, and its non-deviation holds. Where the object is not otherwise available, that relation is the pramāṇa, since according to Bādarāyaṇa it does not depend on anything else.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.3.2-3.sanskrit_iast: api vā kartṛsāmānyāt pramāṇam anumānaṃ syāt / virodhe tv anapekṣyaṃ syād asati hy anumānam
jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.3.2-3.english_close: Or else, on the basis of common agency, inference might be a pramāṇa. But in case of conflict it is to be disregarded, for then inference simply does not obtain.

nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/31-33.sanskrit_iast: yadi ca pramāṇatas teṣāṃ teṣāṃ prasiddhir arthānām / teṣāṃ punaḥ prasiddhiṃ brūhi kathaṃ teṣāṃ pramāṇānāṃ // anyaiḥ yadi pramāṇaiḥ pramāṇasiddhir bhavaty anavasthā syān / nādeḥ siddhis tatrāsti naiva madhyasya nāntasya // teṣām atha pramāṇair vinā prasiddhiḥ vihīyate vādaḥ / vaiṣamikatvaṃ tasmin viśeṣahetuś ca vaktavyaḥ //
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/31-33.english_close: If the establishment of things is through pramāṇas, then one must explain the establishment of those pramāṇas in turn. If pramāṇas are established by other pramāṇas, an infinite regress follows; if they are established without pramāṇa, the thesis itself is abandoned and asymmetry results unless a special reason is supplied.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: smṛtau yaiva svasaṃvittiḥ pramāṇaṃ svātmasaṃbhave / pūrvānubhavasadbhāve sādhanaṃ saiva nāparam //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: In memory, that very self-awareness is the pramāṇa for one’s own prior occurrence; for the existence of the prior experience, that alone is the means, and nothing else.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2..sanskrit_iast: idam etādṛg ity evaṃ yad vaśād vyavatiṣṭhate vastu pramāṇaṃ tat so 'pi svābhāso 'bhinavodayaḥ // so 'ntas tathāvimarśātmā deśakālādyabhedini ekābhidhānaviṣaye mitir vastuny abādhitā //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2..english_close: That by whose force a thing is fixed as ‘this, of such-and-such a kind’ is pramāṇa; and that is a self-appearance, a fresh arising. When interiorized as reflexive awareness with distinctions of place and time excluded in a single object of designation, there is unbaffled ascertainment of the thing.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2..sanskrit_iast: pramātari purāṇe tu sarvadā bhātavigrahe kiṃ pramāṇaṃ navābhāsaḥ sarvapramitibhāgini //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2..english_close: But in the ancient subject, whose form is ever-luminous, what pramāṇa could a new appearance be, when that subject already participates in every cognition?

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.6.english_close: There is apprehension by means of pramāṇas and standpoints.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/46-48.sanskrit_iast: eṣa pratyayasargo viparyayāśaktituṣṭisiddhyākhyaḥ / guṇavaiṣamyavimardena tasya bhedās tu pañcāśat // pañca viparyayabhedā bhavanty aśakteś ca karaṇavaikalyāt / aṣṭāviṃśatibhedā tuṣṭir navadhāṣṭadhā siddhiḥ // bhedas tamaso 'ṣṭavidho mohasya ca daśavidho mahāmohaḥ / tāmisro 'ṣṭādaśadhā tathā bhavaty andhatāmisraḥ //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/46-48.english_close: This creation of cognitions is named viparyaya, incapacity, contentment, and accomplishment; through the clash produced by the inequality of the guṇas it has fifty divisions. There are five divisions of viparyaya. The forms of tamas are eightfold, of moha and mahāmoha tenfold, and of tāmisra and andhatāmisra eighteenfold.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.8.english_close: Viparyaya is false cognition, established in what is not that object's form.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.8.sanskrit_iast: sa kasmān na pramāṇam. yataḥ pramāṇena bādhyate. bhūtārthaviṣayatvāt pramāṇasya. tatra pramāṇena bādhanam apramāṇasya dṛṣṭam. tadyathā dvicandradarśanaṃ sadviṣayeṇaikacandradarśanena bādhyata iti. seyaṃ pañcaparvā bhavaty avidyā.
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.8.english_close: Why is this not pramāṇa? Because it is sublated by pramāṇa; pramāṇa has a real object. The sublation of non-pramāṇa by pramāṇa is seen, as when the cognition of two moons is sublated by the cognition of one moon in the real object. This is the five-jointed avidyā.

vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/1.1.2-prose-on-m.locus: Nyāya-Bhāṣya on Nyāya-Sūtra 1.1.2, prose on mithyājñāna and tattvajñāna
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/1.1.2-prose-on-m.sanskrit_iast: tatrātmādyapavargaparyantaprameye mithyājñānam anekaprakārakaṃ varttate ... ātmani tāvan nāstīti, anātmani ātmeti duḥkhe sukham iti anitye nityam iti ... etasmān mithyājñānād anukūleṣu rāgaḥ, pratikūleṣu dveṣaḥ ... tattvajñānaṃ tu khalu mithyājñānaviparyayeṇa vyākhyātam.
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/1.1.2-prose-on-m.english_close: Across the prameyas from self to apavarga there operates false cognition of many kinds: 'the self does not exist,' 'the non-self is the self,' 'pain is pleasure,' 'the impermanent is permanent,' and so on. From this false cognition arise attachment to the favourable and aversion to the unfavourable. Tattvajñāna is explained precisely as the contrary of false cognition.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/64.sanskrit_iast: ayathārthānubhavas trividhaḥ saṃśayaviparyayatarkabhedāt ... mithyājñānaṃ viparyayaḥ / yathā śuktāv idaṃ rajatam iti /
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/64.english_close: Erroneous cognition is threefold as doubt, viparyaya, and tarka. Viparyaya is false cognition, as in 'this is silver' in a shell.

shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.2-prose.locus: Śabara-Bhāṣya on Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra 1.1.2, prose on mithyāpratyaya and avitathatva
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.2-prose.sanskrit_iast: na ca niścitam avagamyamānam idaṃ mithyā syāt. yo hi janitvā pradhvaṃsate naitad evam iti, sa mithyāpratyayaḥ. na caiṣa kālāntare puruṣāntare 'vasthāntare deśāntare vā viparyeti. tasmād avitathaḥ.
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.2-prose.english_close: What is definitely understood cannot be false in that way. A false cognition is one which, after arising, is later cancelled as 'it is not thus.' But this one does not reverse at another time, in another person, condition, or place; therefore it is non-false.

shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.5-prose.locus: Śabara-Bhāṣya on Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra 1.1.5, prose on shell-silver and mithyājñāna
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.5-prose.sanskrit_iast: bādhakaṃ hi yatra jñānam utpadyate naitad evam, mithyā-jñānam idam iti ... yadā kṣudādibhir upahataṃ mano bhavati, indriyaṃ vā timirādibhiḥ, saukṣmyādibhir vā bāhyo viṣayaḥ, tato mithyājñānam, anupahateṣu saṃyagjñānam.
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.5-prose.english_close: Where a cancelling cognition arises, 'this is not so; this is false cognition,' there is misperception. When the mind is impaired by hunger and the like, or the sense by darkness and the like, or the external object by subtlety and the like, there is false cognition; where these are unimpaired, there is correct cognition.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/13.1.sanskrit_iast: tan mṛṣā moṣadharma yad bhagavān ity abhāṣata / sarve ca moṣadharmāṇaḥ saṃskārās tena te mṛṣā //
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/13.1.english_close: What the Blessed One declared to be deceptive in nature is mṛṣā; and because all conditioned things are deceptive in nature, therefore they are mṛṣā.

nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/13.sanskrit_iast: bālānām iva mithyā mṛgatṛṣṇāyāṃ yathājalagrāhaḥ / evaṃ mithyāḥ grāhaḥ syāt te pratiṣidhyate hy asataḥ //
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/13.english_close: Just as children falsely grasp water in a mirage, so your grasp is false; it is refuted because its object is non-existent.

vasubandhu/vimsatika/17-dream-awakening.sanskrit_iast: svapnadṛgviṣayābhāvaṃ nāprabuddho 'vigacchati || evaṃ vitathavikalpābhyāsavāsanānidrayā prasupto lokaḥ svapna ivābhūtam arthaṃ paśyan na prabuddhas tadabhāvaṃ yathāvan nāvagacchati.
vasubandhu/vimsatika/17-dream-awakening.english_close: One not awakened does not ascertain the absence of the object seen in dream. Likewise the world, asleep through the latent tendency produced by habituation to false conceptualization, sees an unreal object as in a dream and, not yet awakened, does not apprehend its absence as it really is.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2..sanskrit_iast: rajataikavimarśe 'pi śuktau na rajatasthitiḥ / upādhideśāsaṃvādād dvicandre 'pi nabho 'nyathā // ipv: rajate śuktau ca rajatāvamarśaikyena rajatatve 'pi punaḥ śuktideśasaṅgatibādhenopādhisaṃvādābhāvāt tadbuddhir asthairyād bhrāntā / dvicandre 'pi nabhodeśāsaṃvādān mithyā //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2..english_close: Even where there is a unitary apprehension of silver, there is no silver in the shell; because place and adjunct do not correspond, as also with the second moon, the cognition is unstable and erroneous. The ĪPV gloss makes the point explicit: because there is no conformity to the shell's place and adjunct, that cognition is bhrāntā, and in the case of the second moon it is mithyā.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3..sanskrit_iast: bhinnavedyaprathātraiva māyākhyaṃ janmabhogadam / kartary abodhe kārmaṃ tu māyāśaktyaiva tat trayam // ipv: atraiva dvidhāṇave vedyam abhinnam api bhedena yadā bhāti tadāto 'pi viparyāsān nāmnā māyīyaṃ malam / ahetūnām api karmaṇāṃ janmādihetubhāvaviṣayaviparyāsād abodhātmakakartṛgataṃ kārmam / tanmalatrayanirmāṇe prabhor icchā māyāśaktir ucyate //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3..english_close: Here itself the manifestation of distinct objects is called māyā and gives birth and enjoyment; when there is non-apprehension in the agent, the karmic impurity too, and that threefold bond, arise by māyā-śakti. The ĪPV gloss says that when what is non-different appears as different, that very viparyāsa is the māyīya impurity.

abhinavagupta/tantraloka/1.331.sanskrit_iast: mithyājñānaṃ timiramasamān dṛṣṭidoṣānprasūte tatsadbhāvādvimalamapi tadbhāti mālinyadhāma / yattu prekṣyaṃ dṛśi parigataṃ taimirīṃ doṣamudrāṃ dūraṃ runddhetprabhavatu kathaṃ tatra mālinyaśaṅkā //
abhinavagupta/tantraloka/1.331.english_close: False cognition is like an eye-disease, produced by unequal defects of vision; because that defect is present, even what is pure appears as the abode of impurity. But if the seen object can remove the tainted mark in the eye, how could impurity be suspected there?

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/56.sanskrit_iast: ity eṣa prakṛtikṛto mahadādiviṣayabhūtaparyantaḥ / pratipuruṣavimokṣārthaṃ svārtha iva parārtha ārambhaḥ //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/56.english_close: Thus this evolution produced by prakṛti, extending from mahat down to the elements and their objects, though for another, proceeds as if for itself, for the sake of each puruṣa's liberation.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/68.sanskrit_iast: prāpte śarīrabhede caritārthatvāt pradhānavinivṛttau / aikāntikam ātyantikam ubhayaṃ kaivalyam āpnoti //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/68.english_close: When bodily disjunction occurs, and pradhāna has ceased because its purpose has been fulfilled, one attains kaivalya in both senses: certain and absolute.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/4.34.sanskrit_iast: puruṣārthaśūnyānāṃ guṇānāṃ pratiprasavaḥ kaivalyaṃ svarūpapratiṣṭhā vā citiśaktir iti
patanjali/yoga-sutra/4.34.english_close: Kaivalya is the reabsorption of the guṇas once they are empty of purpose for the puruṣa; or it is the power of consciousness established in its own nature.

utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3.sanskrit_iast: eṣa pramātā māyāndhaḥ saṃsārī karmabandhanaḥ / vidyābhijñāpitaiśvaryaś cidghano mukta ucyate //
utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3.english_close: This knower, blinded by māyā, is a saṃsārin bound by karma; when his lordship is disclosed by vidyā, he is said to be liberated, a compact mass of consciousness.

utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4.sanskrit_iast: meyaṃ sādhāraṇaṃ muktaḥ svātmābhedena manyate / maheśvaro yathā baddhaḥ puna atyantabhedavat //
utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4.english_close: The liberated regards the common object as non-different from his own self, like Maheśvara; the bound one, by contrast, takes it as utterly different.

utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4.sanskrit_iast: tad atra nidadhat padaṃ bhuvanakartṛtām ātmano vibhāvya śivatāmayīm aniśam āviśan siddhyati //
utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4.english_close: Establishing his footing there, realizing the self as world-creating lordship, and continually entering the state consisting of Śiva-hood, one attains accomplishment.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/9.1.english_close: The stopping of influx is saṃvara.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/26.sanskrit_iast: buddhī'ndriyāṇi cakṣuḥ śrotra-ghrāṇa-rasana-tvag-ākhyāni / vāk-pāṇi-pāda-pāyūpasthān karm'endriyāṇy āhuḥ //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/26.english_close: The faculties of cognition are called eye, ear, nose, tongue, and skin; the faculties of action are said to be speech, hands, feet, anus, and genitals.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/17-commentary.sanskrit_iast: iha madhurāmlatiktalavaṇakaṭukaṣāyāḥ ṣaḍ rasāḥ | etaiḥ ṣaḍbhī rasair yuktaṃ bhojanaṃ dṛṣṭvā bhoktā sādhyate | asti bhoktā yasyedaṃ bhojanam | evam idaṃ vyaktāvyaktaṃ dṛṣṭvā sādhayāmo 'sty asau paramātmā puruṣo yasyedaṃ bhoktur vyaktāvyaktaṃ bhogyam iti |
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/17-commentary.english_close: Here there are six tastes: sweet, sour, bitter, salty, pungent, and astringent. Seeing food furnished with these six tastes, one establishes an enjoyer: there is an enjoyer for whom this food exists. So too, seeing the manifest and unmanifest, we establish a puruṣa for whom this manifest-unmanifest is the enjoyed.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/22-commentary.sanskrit_iast: tatra śabdatanmātrād ākāśam, sparśatanmātrād vāyuḥ, rūpatanmātrāt tejaḥ, rasatanmātrād āpaḥ, gandhatanmātrāt pṛthivī ityādikrameṇa pūrvapūrvānupraveśenaikadvitricatuṣpañcaguṇāny ākāśādipṛthvīparyantāni mahābhūtānīti sṛṣṭikramaḥ |
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/22-commentary.english_close: From sound-tanmātra comes space, from touch-tanmātra air, from form-tanmātra fire, from taste-tanmātra water, and from smell-tanmātra earth; thus, in sequence, the great elements arise.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.35-bhasya.sanskrit_iast: nāsikāgre dhārayato 'sya yā divyagandhasaṃvit sā gandhapravṛttiḥ | jihvāgre rasasaṃvit | tāluni rūpasaṃvit | jihvāmadhye sparśasaṃvit | jihvāmūle śabdasaṃvid ity etā vṛttaya utpannāś cittaṃ sthitau nibadhnanti, saṃśayaṃ vidhamanti, samādhiprajñāyāṃ ca dvārībhavantīti |
patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.35-bhasya.english_close: For one who fixes attention at the tip of the nose there arises a divine cognition of smell; at the tip of the tongue, a cognition of taste; at the palate, a cognition of form; in the middle of the tongue, a cognition of touch; at the root of the tongue, a cognition of sound. These arisen modes bind the mind to steadiness, shatter doubt, and become doorways to samādhi-wisdom.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.45-bhasya.sanskrit_iast: pārthivasyāṇor gandhatanmātraṃ sūkṣmo viṣayaḥ | āpyasya rasatanmātram | taijasasya rūpatanmātram | vāyavīyasya sparśatanmātram | ākāśasya śabdatanmātram iti |
patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.45-bhasya.english_close: For the earthy atom the subtle object is smell-tanmātra; for the watery, taste-tanmātra; for the fiery, form-tanmātra; for the airy, touch-tanmātra; for ether, sound-tanmātra.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/2.1.1-2.sanskrit_iast: rūparasagandhasparśavatī pṛthivī | rūparasasparśavatya āpo dravāḥ snigdhāś ca |
kanada/vaisesika-sutra/2.1.1-2.english_close: Earth possesses color, taste, smell, and touch. Waters, fluid and unctuous, possess color, taste, and touch.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.12.english_close: Smell, taste, sight, touch, and hearing are the sense-faculties, arising from the elements.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/3.1.56-62.english_close: Because there are five sense-objects, there are five senses ... and among smell, taste, color, touch, and sound, earth possesses the series up through touch.

kumarila/sloka-varttika/commentary-line-.sanskrit_iast: ātmamanaḥ saṃyogo hi rasādijñānasādhāraṇo nāvyabhicāritayā rūpadarśanakāraṇam iti śakyate 'vagantum, cakṣuṣaḥ punar asādhāraṇatayaiva rūpajñāne kāraṇatvam |
kumarila/sloka-varttika/commentary-line-.english_close: The conjunction of self and mind is common to cognitions of taste and the rest, and so cannot be understood, through invariable concomitance, as the cause of seeing form; the eye, however, is the cause of form-cognition precisely through its distinctive role.

shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/4.1.23.sanskrit_iast: saṃsargasaniṣpatteḥ, tatra hi dadhipayasoḥ saṃsṛṣṭayo rasa upalabhyate, tena tatra dadhipayasī ity anumānaṃ bhavati, vājine tiktakaṭuko rasaḥ |
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/4.1.23.english_close: Because the mixture is what is produced there: in that case a taste is perceived in the curds-and-milk combined, and from that one infers the presence of curds and milk there; in the vājina, by contrast, the taste is bitter-pungent.

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/3.9-10.sanskrit_iast: ekasāmagryadhīnasya rūpāde rasato gatiḥ / hetudharmānumānena dhūmendhanavikāravat // śaktipravṛttyā na vinā rasaḥ saivānyakāraṇam / ity atītaikakālānāṃ gatistastatkāryaliṅgajā //
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/3.9-10.english_close: From taste one can arrive at form and the rest, since they depend on a single causal assemblage, like inferring fire from smoke as a modification of fuel. Taste does not occur without the operation of that causal power, which is also the cause of the others; thus knowledge of non-simultaneous items arises from the sign furnished by their respective effects.

ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/4-vrtti.english_close: Thereupon it expands also as the whole series of tattvas, worlds, entities, and cognizers, in the form of a saturation by the savor of consciousness.

ksemaraja/shiva-sutra-vimarshini/3.20.6.english_close: One should moisten even the middle state with the rasa of the bliss of turiya.

ksemaraja/shiva-sutra-vimarshini/3.21.3-.english_close: With the rasa of the wonder of inward reflective awareness, immersed here in the rasa of turiya, he should quiet the condition of being a cognizer of his own body and the rest.

ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/RVas_35.locus: Vedārtha-Saṃgraha (RVas_35), on the cause itself entering another state as effect
ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/RVas_35.sanskrit_iast: ekam eva kāraṇabhūtaṃ dravyam avasthāntarayogena kāryam ity ucyata ... kāraṇam evāvasthāntarāpannaṃ kāryam ity ucyata
ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/RVas_35.english_close: One and the same causal substance is called the effect through entry into another state; the cause itself, having reached another state, is what is called the effect.

ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/RVas_58-60.locus: Vedārtha-Saṃgraha (RVas_58-60), hostile reconstruction of a Bhedābheda consequence
ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/RVas_58-60.sanskrit_iast: īśvaraḥ svarūpeṇaiva suranaratiryaksthāvarādibhedenāvasthita iti hi tadātmakatvavarṇanaṃ kriyate ... evaṃ sarvajīvagatasukhaduḥkhādi sarvam īśvaragatam eva syāt ... īśvarasya svarūpeṇa tādātmyavarṇane syād ayaṃ doṣaḥ
ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/RVas_58-60.english_close: The position described is that the Lord himself stands as gods, humans, animals, and immovables; thus all the pleasures and pains found in all jīvas would belong to the Lord himself. This difficulty arises if identity with the Lord is stated at the level of his very essence.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.92-1.94.sanskrit_iast: īśvarāsiddheḥ / muktāmuktayoḥ atyatarābhāvān na tatsiddhiḥ / ubhayathāpy asatkaratvaṃ
kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.92-1.94.english_close: Īśvara is unestablished. Since he can be neither wholly liberated nor wholly unliberated, that postulate is not established. In either case the hypothesis is ineffective.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/5.2-5.9.sanskrit_iast: neśvarādhiṣṭhite phalaniṣpattiḥ karmaṇā tatsiddheḥ / svopakārādadhiṣṭhānaṃ lokavat / na rāgādṛte tatsiddhiḥ pratiniyatakāraṇatvāt / tadyoge 'pi na nityamuktaḥ
kapila/samkhya-sutra/5.2-5.9.english_close: The production of results does not depend on governance by Īśvara, because that is established through karma itself. Governance is for one's own benefit, as in the world. Nor is such governance established without desire, since action has determinate causes; and even if such conjunction were admitted, he would not be eternally liberated.

vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.1.19-21.sanskrit_iast: īśvaraḥ kāraṇaṃ puruṣakarmāphalyadarśanāt ... yadadhīnaṃ sa īśvaraḥ ... puruṣakāram īśvaro 'nugṛhṇāti, phalāya puruṣasya yatamānasyeśvaraḥ phalaṃ sampādayati ... guṇaviśiṣṭam ātmāntaram īśvaraḥ
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.1.19-21.english_close: Īśvara is the cause, because human action is seen not always to attain its result; the fruition of action depends on that being, and that is Īśvara. The Lord favors human effort and brings about its result; Īśvara is a distinct self qualified by special properties.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/59-62.sanskrit_iast: asmāt padād ayam artho boddhavya itīśvarasaṃketaḥ śaktiḥ ... vaidikam īśvaroktatvāt sarvam eva pramāṇam
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/59-62.english_close: The power of a word is the convention, 'from this word this meaning is to be understood,' and that convention belongs to Īśvara. The Veda, being spoken by Īśvara, is entirely authoritative.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/74.sanskrit_iast: buddhīcchāprayatnā dvividhāḥ / nityā anityāś ca / nityā īśvarasya / anityā jīvasya
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/74.english_close: Cognition, desire, and effort are of two kinds, eternal and non-eternal. The eternal belong to Īśvara; the non-eternal belong to the jīva.

sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika/1.96-98.locus: Ślokavārttika commentary, opening section 96-98, on apauruṣeyatva and rejection of a Vedic author
sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika/1.96-98.sanskrit_iast: ye tu naiyāyikādayaḥ prāmāṇyasiddhyarthaṃ kartṛmattvaṃ vedānāṃ kalpayanti ... samyagvādibhis tāvad īśvaraḥ kartā ... na kartṛmanto vedā iti
sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika/1.96-98.english_close: Those Naiyāyikas and others who posit authorship of the Veda for the sake of its authority do not establish that hypothesis. For the opposing theorists the creator is Īśvara; yet no compelling reasoning for that construction is seen. Therefore the Vedas are not authored.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/8.2-4.sanskrit_iast: sadbhūtasya kriyā nāsti kartā ca syād akarmakaḥ / karoti yady asadbhūto 'sadbhūtaṃ karma kārakaḥ / ahetukaṃ bhavet karma kartā cāhetuko bhavet / hetāv asati kāryaṃ ca kāraṇaṃ ca na vidyate / tadabhāve kriyā kartā kāraṇaṃ ca na vidyate
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/8.2-4.english_close: If what truly exists had action, the agent would be without action; if a non-existent agent acted upon a non-existent act, both act and agent would be causeless. Without cause, effect and instrument do not exist; and without them, action, agent, and cause do not exist.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/15.1.sanskrit_iast: na saṃbhavaḥ svabhāvasya yuktaḥ pratyayahetubhiḥ / hetupratyayasaṃbhūtaḥ svabhāvaḥ kṛtako bhavet
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/15.1.english_close: The arising of svabhāva from conditions and causes is not tenable, for a svabhāva produced from causes and conditions would be something made.

vasubandhu/trimsika/21-24.sanskrit_iast: paratantrasvabhāvastu vikalpaḥ pratyayodbhavaḥ / trividhasya svabhāvasya trividhāṃ niḥsvabhāvatām / sandhāya sarvadharmāṇāṃ deśitā niḥsvabhāvatā / prathamo lakṣaṇenaiva niḥsvabhāvo 'paraḥ punaḥ / na svayambhāva etasyety aparā niḥsvabhāvatā
vasubandhu/trimsika/21-24.english_close: The dependent nature is conceptual construction arising from conditions. With respect to the three natures, a threefold lack of own-being is taught for all dharmas. The second lack of own-being is precisely that this has no self-produced nature.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: citiḥ pratyavamarśātmā parā vāk svarasoditā / svātantryam etan mukhyaṃ tad aiśvaryaṃ paramātmanaḥ // sā sphurattā mahāsattā deśakālāviśeṣinī / saiṣā sāratayā proktā hṛdayaṃ parameṣṭhinaḥ // ātmānam ata evāyaṃ jñeyīkuryāt pṛthaksthiti / jñeyaṃ na tu tadaunmukhyāt khaṇḍyetāsya svatantratā // svātantryāmuktātmānaṃ svātantryād advayātmanaḥ / prabhur īśādisaṃkalpair nirmāya vyavahārayet
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: Consciousness, whose essence is reflexive awareness, is the supreme speech; this freedom is the very lordly power of the supreme Self. This is the Heart of the Supreme Lord. By that very freedom the Lord objectifies himself and, through conceptions such as 'Īśvara,' enters worldly discourse.

ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/1-4.sanskrit_iast: citiḥ svatantrā viśvasiddhihetuḥ ... svecchayā svabhittau viśvam unmīlayati ... citisaṃkocātmā cetano 'pi saṃkucitaviśvamayaḥ
ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/1-4.english_close: Consciousness, being free, is the cause of the manifestation of the universe. By her own will she unfolds the universe on her own ground. The individual conscious subject, as a contraction of consciousness, is likewise a contracted form of the whole universe.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/5.sanskrit_iast: prativiṣayādhyavasāyo dṛṣṭaṃ trividham anumānam ākhyātam / tal liṅgaliṅgipūrvakam āptaśrutir āptavacanaṃ tu //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/5.english_close: Perceptual ascertainment with respect to each object is perception, and inference is declared to be threefold. Trustworthy śruti, however, is authoritative utterance.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/6.sanskrit_iast: sāmānyatas tu dṛṣṭād atīndriyāṇāṃ pratītir anumānāt / tasmād api cāsiddhaṃ parokṣam āptāgamāt sādhyam //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/6.english_close: From what is generally observed there is inferential cognition of the supersensible; and what, though beyond direct perception, is not established even from that is to be proved from reliable tradition.

vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/1.7.sanskrit_iast: āptena dṛṣṭo 'numito vārthaḥ paratra svabodhasaṃkrāntaye śabdenopadiśyate, śabdāt tadarthaviṣayā vṛttiḥ śrotur āgamaḥ. yasyāśraddheyārtho vaktā na dṛṣṭānumitārthaḥ sa āgamaḥ plavate. mūlavaktari tu dṛṣṭānumitārthe nirviplavaḥ syāt.
vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/1.7.english_close: An object seen or inferred by a trustworthy person is taught by speech so that his cognition may pass to another; the hearer's cognition of that object arising from the speech is āgama. If the speaker's object is not trustworthy and has not been seen or inferred, that putative āgama collapses; but where the original speaker has seen or inferred the object, it is free from error.

vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/1.49.sanskrit_iast: śrutam āgamavijñānaṃ tat sāmānyaviṣayam. na hy āgamena śakyo viśeṣo 'bhidhātum, kasmāt, na hi viśeṣeṇa kṛtasaṃketaḥ śabda iti. ... tasmāc chrutānumānaprajñābhyām anyaviṣayā sā prajñā viśeṣārthatvād iti.
vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/1.49.english_close: Scriptural cognition is āgama-cognition, and it has a general object; a particular cannot be expressed by āgama, because no word is conventionally fixed with regard to a particular. Therefore that insight has an object different from scriptural and inferential cognition, because its object is a particular.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.7.english_close: Testimony is the instruction of a trustworthy person.

vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/1.1.7.sanskrit_iast: āptaḥ khalu sākṣātkṛtadharmā yathādṛṣṭasyārthasya cikhyāpayiṣayā prayukta upadeṣṭā.
vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/1.1.7.english_close: A trustworthy person is one who has directly apprehended the matter and, desiring to make known the object as he has seen it, undertakes to teach it.

vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/p92-agniho.locus: Nyāya-Sūtra-Bhāṣya, p.92, example of Vedic testimony on supersensible svarga
vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/p92-agniho.sanskrit_iast: vyavasthā punaḥ — "agnihotra juhuyāt svargakāmaḥ" iti, laukikasya svarge na liṅgadarśanam, na pratyakṣam.
vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/p92-agniho.english_close: As for the restricted case: 'One desiring heaven should perform the agnihotra.' For an ordinary person, with respect to heaven there is no perception of a sign and no perception.

shabara/shabara-bhasya/1.1.2.sanskrit_iast: codanā hi bhūtaṃ bhavantaṃ bhaviṣyantaṃ sūkṣmaṃ vyavahitaṃ viprakṛṣṭam ity evaṃjātīyakam arthaṃ śaknoty avagamayitum, nānyat kiṃcanendriyam. ... na tu vedavacanasya mithyātve kiṃcana pramāṇam asti.
shabara/shabara-bhasya/1.1.2.english_close: The injunction can make known an object of this kind - past, present, future, subtle, concealed, remote - which no sense can grasp. ... But there is no proof at all that Vedic utterance is false.

vasubandhu/vimsatika/8-abhiprayavasat.locus: Vimśatikā 8, on scriptural instruction by pedagogical intention
vasubandhu/vimsatika/8-abhiprayavasat.sanskrit_iast: rūpādyāyatanāstitvaṃ tadvineyajanaṃ prati / abhiprāyavaśād uktam upapādukasattvavat // 8 //
vasubandhu/vimsatika/8-abhiprayavasat.english_close: The existence of the sense-fields beginning with form was taught, with respect to those disciples to be trained by that teaching, by force of intention, just like the teaching of a spontaneously born being.

vasubandhu/vimsatika/9-svabijad-vijnapti.locus: Vimśatikā 9, on cognition's own seed and the twofold sense-field
vasubandhu/vimsatika/9-svabijad-vijnapti.sanskrit_iast: yataḥ svabījād vijñaptir yadābhāsā pravartate / dvividhāyatanatvena te tasyā munir abravīt // 9 //
vasubandhu/vimsatika/9-svabijad-vijnapti.english_close: When a cognition proceeds from its own seed together with the appearance that belongs to it, the Sage spoke of those two as its twofold sense-field.

abhinavagupta/tantraloka/35.23-24.sanskrit_iast:
eka evāgamaścāyaṃ vibhunā sarvadarśinā / darśito yaḥ pravṛtte ca nivṛtte ca pathi sthitaḥ //
dharmārthakāmamokṣeṣu pūrṇāpūrṇādibhedataḥ / vicitreṣu phaleṣveka upāyaḥ śāmbhavāgamaḥ //
abhinavagupta/tantraloka/35.23-24.english_close: This āgama is one, shown by the all-seeing Lord, and established on both the path of engagement and the path of withdrawal. Across the diverse fruits of dharma, artha, kāma, and mokṣa, the one Śāmbhava āgama is the means.

abhinavagupta/tantraloka/35.30.sanskrit_iast: eka evāgamastasmāttatra laukikaśāstrataḥ / prabhṛtyāvaiṣṇavādbauddhācchaivātsarvaṃ hi niṣṭhitam //
abhinavagupta/tantraloka/35.30.english_close: Therefore the āgama is one; from worldly teaching onward, through Vaiṣṇava, Buddhist, and Śaiva systems, everything is grounded there.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9-11.english_close: Knowledge is of five kinds: mati, śruta, avadhi, manaḥparyaya, and kevala. These are the means of valid cognition. The first two are indirect.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/11-14.sanskrit_iast: triguṇam aviveki viṣayaḥ sāmānyam acetana prasavadharmi / vyaktaṃ tathā pradhānaṃ tadviparītas tathā ca pumān // prītyaprītiviṣādātmakāḥ prakāśapravṛttiniyamārthāḥ / anyo 'nyābhibhavāśrayajananamithunavṛttayaś ca guṇāḥ // sattvaṃ laghu prakāśakam iṣṭam upaṣṭambhakaṃ calaṃ ca rajaḥ / guru varaṇakam eva tamaḥ pradīpavac cārthato vṛttiḥ // avivekyādiḥ siddhas traiguṇyāt tadviparyayābhāvāt / kāraṇaguṇātmakatvāt kāryasyāvyaktam api siddham //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/11-14.english_close: The manifest and the primal source are tri-guṇic, non-discriminating, objective, common, non-conscious, and productive; puruṣa is the opposite. The guṇas are of the nature of pleasure, pain, and delusion, and of illumination, activity, and restraint; they operate by mutual overpowering, support, generation, and coupling. Sattva is light and illuminating, rajas mobile and stimulating, tamas heavy and obscuring. Because the effect is constituted by the guṇas of the cause, the unmanifest too is established.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/60.sanskrit_iast: nānāvidhair upāyair upakāriṇy anupakāriṇaḥ puṃsaḥ / guṇavaty aguṇasya satas tasyārtham apārthakaṃ carati //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/60.english_close: For the sake of the guṇaless puruṣa, though he does nothing for her, prakṛti, endowed with guṇas, moves in many ways as a benefactor.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.16.english_close: That higher [dispassion] is indifference to the guṇas through knowledge of puruṣa.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.18-19.sanskrit_iast: prakāśakriyāsthitiśīlaṃ bhūtendriyātmakaṃ bhogāpavargārthaṃ dṛśyam // viśeṣāviśeṣaliṅgamātrāliṅgāni guṇaparvāṇi //
patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.18-19.english_close: The seen is of the nature of illumination, activity, and stability; it consists of the elements and the senses and exists for experience and release. Its stages are the particular, the non-particular, the mere mark, and the unmarked: these are the phases of the guṇas.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/4.32-34.sanskrit_iast: tataḥ kṛtārthānāṃ pariṇāmakramasamāptir guṇānām // kṣaṇapratiyogī pariṇāmāparāntanirgrāhyaḥ kramaḥ // puruṣārthaśūnyānāṃ guṇānāṃ pratiprasavaḥ kaivalyaṃ svarūpapratiṣṭhā vā citiśaktir iti //
patanjali/yoga-sutra/4.32-34.english_close: Then, for the guṇas whose purpose has been accomplished, the sequence of transformations comes to an end. Sequence is what is grasped at the limit of transformation, correlated with moments. Kaivalya is the reabsorption of the guṇas that have become devoid of purpose for puruṣa, or else the establishment of the power of consciousness in its own nature.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.14.english_close: Smell, taste, color, touch, and sound are the qualities of earth and the other elements, and they are the corresponding objects [of the senses].

vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/p.156-157.locus: Nyāya-Bhāṣya, p. 156-157 (on desire and the self)
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/p.156-157.sanskrit_iast: yathecchādibhir ātmā, icchādayo guṇāḥ, guṇāś ca dravyasaṃsthānāḥ, tad tad eṣāṃ sthānaṃ sa ātmeti
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/p.156-157.english_close: As [the self is inferred] from desire and the like: desire and the rest are qualities, and qualities are located in substances; their locus is that very substance, namely the self.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.4.16.english_close: Because guṇas exist for the sake of something else.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/7.3.17.english_close: A guṇa-vidhi should not apprehend [further matters], because of parity.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/7.3.34.english_close: Or figuratively, because it is not a remainder of the sacrifice and because the guṇas are what is denoted.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.167.sanskrit_iast: avayavaguṇarāgā lakṣāṇānīndhanādyā avayaviguṇiraktā lakṣyam agnyādayo 'rthāḥ / vihitarathavicārāt saptadhā santi naite sati tu taditarasmin santi lokaprasiddhyā //
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.167.english_close: Things such as fuel and the rest are tied to parts and qualities, while things such as fire are tied to wholes and quality-bearers. But once the chariot-analysis has been carried out in seven ways, these do not exist [ultimately]; when that other standpoint is in place, they exist by worldly convention.

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.226-229.sanskrit_iast: guṇapradhānādhigamaḥ sahāpyabhimato yadi / sampūrṇāṅgo na gṛhyeta sakṛnnāpi guṇādimān // vivakṣāparatantratvād viśeṣaṇaviśeṣayayoḥ / yadṅgabhāvenopāttaṃ tat tenaiva hi gṛhyate // svato vastvantarābhedād guṇāder bhedakasya ca / agrahād ekabuddhiḥ syāt paśyato 'pi parāparam // guṇādibhedagrahaṇān nānātvapratipad yadi / astu nāma tathāpyeṣāṃ bhavet sambandhisaṅkaraḥ //
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.226-229.english_close: If it is claimed that quality and principal thing are apprehended together, then even a complete whole would not be grasped in a single act, nor anything possessed of qualities. Since qualifier and qualified depend on the intended construal, whatever is taken as a component is grasped only in that very way. Because qualities and the like are not really different from another thing, and because no differentiator is separately apprehended, there would be a single cognition even when one sees what is before and after. If plurality is claimed on the basis of grasping differences such as qualities, then even so there would be a confusion of related items.

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/3.44-45.sanskrit_iast: no ced bhrāntinimittena saṃyojyeta guṇāntaram / śuktau vā rajatākāro rūpasādharmyadarśanāt // tasmād dṛṣṭasya bhāvasya dṛṣṭa evākhilo guṇaḥ / bhrānter niścīyate neti sādhanaṃ sampravartate //
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/3.44-45.english_close: Otherwise one would have to attach some further quality as the cause of error, as when the appearance of silver in nacre arises from seeing a similarity of form. Therefore, for the thing that is seen, the whole of its qualities is seen there itself; it is from error that the contrary conclusion is constructed.

abhinavagupta/tantraloka/8.253-259.sanskrit_iast: mūrtayaḥ suśivā vīro mahādevāṣṭakaṃ vapuḥ / upariṣṭāddhiyo 'dhaś ca prakṛter guṇasaṃjñitam // tattvaṃ tatra tu saṃkṣubdhā guṇāḥ prasuvate dhiyam / na vaiṣamyam anāpannaṃ kāraṇaṃ kāryasūtaye // guṇasāmyatmikā tena prakṛtiḥ kāraṇaṃ bhavet / nanvevaṃ sāpi saṃkṣobhaṃ vinā tān viṣamān guṇān // kathaṃ suvīta tatrādye kṣobhe syād anavasthitiḥ / sāṃkhyasya doṣa evāyaṃ yadi vā tena te guṇāḥ // avyaktam iṣṭāḥ sāmyaṃ tu saṅgamātraṃ na cetarat / asmākaṃ tu svatantreśa-tathecchākṣobhasaṃgatam // avyaktaṃ buddhitattvasya kāraṇaṃ kṣobhitā guṇāḥ / nanu tattveśvarecchāto yaḥ kṣobhaḥ prakṛteḥ purā // tadeva buddhitattvaṃ syāt kim anyaiḥ kalpitair guṇaiḥ / naitat kāraṇatārūpaparāmarśāvarodhi yat //
abhinavagupta/tantraloka/8.253-259.english_close: Below buddhi there is the level called guṇa within prakṛti. There, once disturbed, the guṇas produce intellect, for a cause does not generate an effect without entering disequilibrium. If prakṛti is the equilibrium of guṇas, however, then without a prior disturbance it could not yield unequal guṇas, and the Sāṃkhya defect of regress follows. For us, by contrast, the unmanifest is joined to disturbance through the will of the free Lord; indeed that primordial disturbance itself would amount to buddhi, so merely imagined guṇas need not be posited as an autonomous ultimate cause.

abhinavagupta/tantraloka/8.272-274.sanskrit_iast: guṇānāṃ yat paraṃ sāmyaṃ tadavyaktaṃ guṇordhvataḥ / krodheśacaṇḍasaṃvartā jyotiḥpiṅgalasūrakau // pañcāntakaikavīrau ca śikhodaścāṣṭa tatra te / gahanaṃ puruṣanidhānaṃ prakṛtirmūlaṃ pradhānamavyaktam // guṇakāraṇamityete māyāprabhavasya paryāyāḥ //
abhinavagupta/tantraloka/8.272-274.english_close: The supreme equilibrium of the guṇas is the unmanifest, above the guṇa-level. 'Prakṛti', 'root', 'pradhāna', 'unmanifest', and 'cause of the guṇas' are names for that level. These are designations for a stratum that arises from māyā.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.37-39.sanskrit_iast: guṇaparyayavat dravyam // dravyāśrayā nirguṇā guṇāḥ // tadbhāvaḥ pariṇāmaḥ //
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.37-39.english_close: Substance is possessed of qualities and modes. Qualities are dependent on substance and are themselves without qualities. Their becoming is transformation.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/46-49.sanskrit_iast:
eṣa pratyayasargo viparyayāśaktituṣṭisiddhyākhyaḥ /
guṇavaiṣamyavimardena tasya bhedās tu pañcāśat //
pañca viparyayabhedā bhavanty aśakteś ca karaṇavaikalyāt /
aṣṭāviṃśatibhedā tuṣṭir navadhāṣṭadhā siddhiḥ //
bhedas tamaso 'ṣṭavidho mohasya ca daśavidho mahāmohaḥ /
tāmisro 'ṣṭādaśadhā tathā bhavaty andhatāmisraḥ //
ekādaśendriyavadhāḥ saha buddhivadhair aśaktir uddiṣṭā /
saptadaśa vadhā buddher viparyayāt tuṣṭisiddhīnām //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/46-49.english_close: This creation of cognitions is called error, incapacity, contentment, and accomplishment; through the conflict and inequality of the guṇas it has fifty divisions. There are five kinds of error, and incapacity, due to defect in the instruments, has twenty-eight divisions. Incapacity is specified as the eleven failures of the senses together with the failures of the buddhi.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/2.6.sanskrit_iast:
dṛgdarśanaśaktyor ekātmatevāsmitā ||
puruṣo dṛkśaktir buddhir darśanaśaktir ity etayor ekasvarūpāpattir ivāsmitā kleśa ucyate. bhoktṛbhogyaśaktyor atyantavibhaktayor atyantāsaṃkīrṇayor avibhāgaprāptāv iva satyāṃ bhogaḥ kalpate. svarūpapratilambhe tu tayoḥ kaivalyam eva bhavati kuto bhoga iti.
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/2.6.english_close: Asmitā is, as it were, the identity of the power of seeing and the power of cognition. Puruṣa is the seeing-power, buddhi the cognitive power. Experience is possible only when these utterly distinct powers seem to become non-different; when each regains its own form, there is only kaivalya.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.21.sanskrit_iast:
kāyarūpasaṃyamāt tadgrāhyaśaktistambhe cakṣuḥprakāśāsaṃprayoge 'ntardhānam ||
kāyasya rūpe saṃyamād rūpasya yā grāhyā śaktis tāṃ pratiṣṭabhnāti. grāhyaśaktistambhe sati cakṣuṣprakāśāsaṃprayoge 'ntardhānam utpadyate yoginaḥ.
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.21.english_close: By saṃyama on the form of the body, when its perceptibility-power is arrested and there is no conjunction with the light of the eye, invisibility arises. The yogin stops the body's grāhya-śakti, and with that arrest invisibility occurs.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/59.sanskrit_iast: āptavākyaṃ śabdaḥ / āptas tu yathārthavaktā / vākyaṃ padasamūhaḥ / yathā gām ānayeti / śaktaṃ padam / asmāt padād ayam artho boddhavya itīśvarasaṃketaḥ śaktiḥ //
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/59.english_close: Verbal testimony is the speech of a trustworthy person, and a sentence is a collection of words, as in 'bring the cow'. A word is śakta when a meaning is to be understood from that word; that fixed convention by Īśvara is śakti.

udayana/nyaya-kusumanjali/1.116-117.sanskrit_iast: kathaṃ tarhi tṛṇāraṇimaṇibhyo bhavannāśuśukṣaṇirekajātīyaḥ? ekaśaktimatvāditi cenna; yadi hi vijātīyeṣv apy ekajātīyakāryakaraṇaśaktiḥ samaveyāt, na kāryāt kāraṇaviśeṣaḥ kvāpy anumīyeta; kāraṇavyāvṛttyā ca na tajjātīyasyaiva kāryasya vyāvṛttir avasīyeta.
udayana/nyaya-kusumanjali/1.116-117.english_close: How, then, does one and the same kind of fire arise from grass, fire-sticks, and jewel? If you say 'because they possess one power', no: if the power to produce one kind of effect inhered even in heterogeneous things, no specific cause could ever be inferred from its effect, nor could one determine that only that same kind of effect is excluded when the cause is excluded.

udayana/nyaya-kusumanjali/1.130.sanskrit_iast: siddhyatu bhūtadharma eva gurutvādivad atīndriyaḥ / avaśyaṃ tvayāpy etad aṅgīkaraṇīyam / katham anyathā mantrādibhiḥ pratibandhaḥ ... akiñcitkarasya pratibandhakatvāyogāt; kiñcitkaratve cātīndriyaśakteḥ svīkārāt / ato 'tīndriyaṃ kiñcid dāhānuguṇam anugrāhakam agner unnīyate.
udayana/nyaya-kusumanjali/1.130.english_close: Let there be admitted, like heaviness and the like, an imperceptible property belonging to the element itself; you too must accept this, for otherwise how could there be inhibition by mantra and the like? Since something wholly inefficacious cannot be a blocker, if it is efficacious one must accept an imperceptible śakti. Therefore an imperceptible factor favorable to burning is inferred in fire.

sabara/mimamsa-bhasya/1.1.25-26.sanskrit_iast: na hy anapekṣitapadārthasya vākyāntyavarṇasya pūrvavarṇajanitasaṃskārarahitasya śaktir asti padārthebhyo 'rthāntare vartitum iti ... padāni hi svaṃ svaṃ padārtham abhidhāya nivṛttavyāpārāṇi. athedānīṃ padārthā avagatāḥ santo vākyārthaṃ gamayanti ... tasmāt padārthapratyaya eva vākyārthaḥ. nāsya padasamudāyena saṃbandhaḥ.
sabara/mimamsa-bhasya/1.1.25-26.english_close: There is no power in the final phoneme of a sentence, independent of the word-meanings and lacking the saṃskāra produced by the earlier phonemes, to move toward a meaning other than those meanings. Words, having denoted their own meanings, cease their operation; then the already-grasped word-meanings convey the sentence-meaning. Therefore the sentence-meaning is just the cognition of the word-meanings, not something grounded in a separate power of the word-aggregate.

sabara/mimamsa-bhasya/1.3.28.sanskrit_iast: gośabdam uccārayitukāmena kenacid aśaktyā gāvīty uccāritam. apareṇa jñātaṃ sāsnādimān asya vivakṣitaḥ, tad arthaṃ gaur ity uccārayitukāmo gāvīty uccārayati. tataḥ śikṣitvāpare 'pi sāsnādim iti vivakṣite gāvīty uccārayanti. tena gāvyādibhyaḥ sāsnādimān avagamyate. anurūpo hi gāvyādigośabdasya.
sabara/mimamsa-bhasya/1.3.28.english_close: Someone, being unable to pronounce the word go, utters gāvī. Another understands that the horned animal is intended, and wanting to utter gauḥ for that meaning, he too says gāvī. After learning this, others as well say gāvī when the horned one is intended. Thus the horned animal is understood from gāvī and the like, for such forms are analogous to the word go.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.57-58.sanskrit_iast:
jātasya śakter na hi sambhavo 'sti
nājātarūpasya ca śaktir asti|
viśeṣaṇaṃ nāsti vinā viśeṣyaṃ
vandhyāsutasyāpi ca tatprasaṅgaḥ||
bhaviṣyatā ced vyapadeśa iṣṭaḥ
śaktiṃ vinā nāsti hi bhāvitāsya|
parasparāyāśrayaṇī ca siddhir
asiddhir eveti vadanti santaḥ||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.57-58.english_close: A power cannot belong to what has already arisen, nor is there any power in what has not yet arisen; there is no qualifier without a qualified thing, otherwise the same would follow even for the son of a barren woman. If one prefers to speak of it as future, that future state cannot exist without the power, and the establishment of the two would rest on mutual dependence; the wise therefore call it non-establishment itself.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.59.sanskrit_iast:
niruddhaśakteḥ paripākataḥ syād
yady anyaśakteḥ parasambhavaḥ syāt|
santānināṃ tatra mitho 'sti bhedaḥ
sarvasya sarvaprabhavas tataḥ syāt||
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.59.english_close: If another thing were to arise from the maturation of a power that had ceased, or from a different power, then there would be mutual difference among causal continua there, and from that it would follow that everything could arise from everything.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast:
ghaṭo 'yam ity adhyavasā nāmarūpātirekiṇī
pareśaśaktir ātmeva bhāsate na tv idantayā //
ipv: ayam iti ghaṭa iti vādhyavasāyo bhinnaprakāśamānanāmarūpātiriktaś citiśaktimaya evātmevābhedena avabhāsate //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: The determinative cognition 'this is a pot', exceeding mere name and form, is the power of the supreme Lord and shines as the very Self, not as an objectified 'this'. The commentary adds that such cognition appears, beyond distinct name-and-form manifestation, as the self itself made of citi-śakti.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2..sanskrit_iast:
eṣa cānantaśaktitvād evam ābhāsayaty amūn
bhāvān icchāvaśād eṣā kriyā nirmātṛtāsya sā //
ipv: pramātā cidrūpo 'nantaśaktir īśvaraḥ svecchāvaśāt tathā tān bhāvān ābhāsayet / saiva cecchāśaktir nirmātṛtākhyā kriyā tasya //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2..english_close: Because he possesses infinite power, he manifests these entities in this way according to his will; that is his activity, his status as maker. The commentary says: the conscious subject, Īśvara, is of infinite power and manifests those entities by his own free will; that very will-power is his kriyā, called nirmātṛtā.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3..sanskrit_iast:
tasyaiśvaryasvabhāvasya paśubhāve prakāśikā
vidyāśaktiḥ tirodhānakarī māyābhidhā punaḥ //
bhede tv ekarase bhāte 'haṃtayānātmanīkṣite
śūnye buddhe śarīre vā māyāśaktir vijṛmbhate //
ipv: saṃsāriṇām aiśvaryasya svātmanaspratyabhijñānaṃ vidyayā, paśubhāvo māyayā //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3..english_close: For that one whose nature is lordship, vidyā-śakti makes that lordship manifest in the state of the bound creature, while the power called māyā performs concealment. When undivided reality appears as divided, and selfhood is seen in void, intellect, or body, māyā-śakti expands. The commentary summarizes: self-recognition of one's lordship belongs to vidyā; the animal condition belongs to māyā.

jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§273.sanskrit_iast: te pāṇḍavāḥ sādhu yathā styāt tathā kṛta-sarvārthā vaśīkṛta-dharmārtha-kāma-mokṣā api vaikuṇṭhasya śrī-kṛṣṇasya caraṇāmbujam eva ātyantikaṃ parama-puruṣārthaṃ jñātvā tad eva manasā dhārayāmāsuḥ | nārāyaṇaḥ śrī-kṛṣṇaḥ |
jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§273.english_close: The Pāṇḍavas, having accomplished all ends and mastered dharma, artha, kāma, and mokṣa, understood the lotus feet of Śrī Kṛṣṇa of Vaikuṇṭha alone to be the ultimate supreme human end and fixed their minds on that alone. Nārāyaṇa is Śrī Kṛṣṇa.

jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/bhp-10.35.16-.locus: Ṣaṭ-Sandarbha, gloss on Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 10.35.16-17 (page 151 in the on-disk text)
jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/bhp-10.35.16-.sanskrit_iast: atra viṣṇuṃ sarvatraiva sphurantaṃ śrī-kṛṣṇam ity arthaḥ |
jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/bhp-10.35.16-.english_close: Here 'Viṣṇu' means Śrī Kṛṣṇa manifesting everywhere.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/5.2.english_close: The production of the result does not depend on an Īśvara-overseer, because it is accomplished by karma.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/5.10.english_close: Its establishment fails because there is no pramāṇa.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.24.english_close: Īśvara is a special puruṣa untouched by afflictions, actions, their fruition, and latent deposits.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.23.sanskrit_iast: praṇidhānād bhaktiviśeṣād āvarjita īśvaras tam anugṛhṇāty abhidhyānamātreṇa
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.23.english_close: By that dedication, a special form of devotion, Īśvara is drawn toward him and favors him by mere contemplation.

udayana/nyaya-kusumanjali/5.685-689.sanskrit_iast: sa evaṃ bhagavān śruto 'numitaśca kaiścit sākṣādapi dṛśyate prameyatvāder ghaṭavat ... tam imaṃ jyotiṣṭomādibhiriṣṭaiḥ, prāsādādinā pūrtena, śītātapasahanādinā tapasā, ahiṃsādibhir yamaiḥ, śaucasantoṣādibhir niyamaiḥ, āsanaprāṇāyāmādinā yogena maharṣayo 'pi vividiṣanti
udayana/nyaya-kusumanjali/5.685-689.english_close: Thus the Blessed Lord is known from scripture and inference, and by some is even directly seen, as a knowable object like a pot. This very one the great seers seek to know through sacrifice, charitable works, austerity, yamas, niyamas, and yoga.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/6.3.18-19.english_close: Not so for deity, fire, word, and act, because they are connected with another purpose; and in the case of the deity, because it is for the sake of that.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/9.1.9.sanskrit_iast: api vā śabdapūrvatvād yajñakarma pradhānaṃ syād guṇatve devatāśrutiḥ
jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/9.1.9.english_close: Or rather, because it is preceded by the word, the sacrificial act should be primary; the deity-text is of the status of a guṇa.

abhinavagupta/ipv/3.1.2-comment.sanskrit_iast: yastu sadāśivabhaṭṭāraka īśvarabhaṭṭārakaśca dhyeyopāsyādirūpatayā sa brahmaviṣṇutulyaḥ pṛthageva mantavyo na tu nāmasārūpyāt bhramitavyam
abhinavagupta/ipv/3.1.2-comment.english_close: But the venerable Sadāśiva and Īśvara, insofar as they are objects of meditation and worship, must be understood as distinct, like Brahmā and Viṣṇu; one must not be misled by mere similarity of names.

abhinavagupta/ipv/3.2.1-comment.sanskrit_iast: tatraitanmātṛtāmātrasthitau rudro'dhidaivatam / bhinnaprameyaprasare brahmaviṣṇū vyavasthitau ... bhinnasya tu prameye tatsaṃtānapravahaṇalakṣaṇe prasare 'dhiṣṭhātṛdevatā viṣṇuḥ
abhinavagupta/ipv/3.2.1-comment.english_close: In the state of bare subjecthood Rudra is the presiding deity; in the spread of differentiated objects Brahmā and Viṣṇu are established. In that spread of differentiated objects, the presiding deity is Viṣṇu.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/10.sanskrit_iast: hetumad anityam avyāpi sakriyam anekam āśritaṃ liṅgam / sāvayavaṃ paratantraṃ vyaktaṃ viparītam avyaktam //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/10.english_close: The manifest is caused, impermanent, non-pervasive, active, manifold, dependent, marked, composite, and subordinate; the unmanifest is the reverse.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.44.sanskrit_iast: sāmānyaviśeṣasamudāyo 'tra dravyam. dviṣṭho hi samūhaḥ pratyastamitabhedāvayavānugataḥ śarīraṃ vṛkṣo yūthaṃ vanam iti. śabdenopāttabhedāvayavānugataḥ samūha ubhaye devamanuṣyāḥ samūhasya devā eko bhāgo manuṣyā dvitīyo bhāgas tābhyām evābhidhīyate samūhaḥ. sa punar dvividho yutasiddhāvayavo 'yutasiddhāvayavaś ca. ... atha kim eṣāṃ sūkṣmarūpaṃ, tanmātraṃ bhūtakāraṇaṃ, tasyaiko 'vayavaḥ paramāṇuḥ ...
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.44.english_close: Here substance is a collection of universal and particular. A composite is a group carried by differentiated parts, such as body, tree, herd, or forest; another kind of group is designated through its differentiated parts, as when gods and humans constitute one aggregate with distinct shares. That aggregate is of two kinds, with separably established parts and inseparably established parts; even at the subtle level the atom is treated as a part within the analysis of the element-cause.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/2.1.31-37.sanskrit_iast: pratyakṣam anumānam ekadeśagrahaṇād upalabdheḥ // na, pratyakṣeṇa yāvat tāvat apy upalambhāt // na caikadeśopalabdhir avayavisadbhāvāt // sādhyatvād avayavini sandehaḥ // sarvāgrahaṇam avayavyasiddheḥ // dhāraṇākarṣaṇopapatteś ca // senāvanavat grahaṇam iti cet na atīndriyatvāt aṇūnām //
aksapada/nyaya-sutra/2.1.31-37.english_close: An opponent claims that perception is really inference because it apprehends only one part. Not so: there is direct apprehension of what is apprehended, and one-part apprehension is compatible with the existence of the whole. The whole is further established by support and traction; and it cannot be explained away by likening it to a merely collective array such as an army or a forest.

madhava/jaimini-nyaya-mala/1.4.35.sanskrit_iast: vākyaikyam upasaṃhārād vispaṣṭaṃ tattu bādhyate / nānāguṇavidhau tasmād aṃśadvārāṃśisaṃstutiḥ //
madhava/jaimini-nyaya-mala/1.4.35.english_close: Sentence-unity might seem clear from the concluding passage, but that is overridden. Therefore, where many accessory-qualities would otherwise have to be enjoined, there is praise of the whole through a part.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/27.17-2.sanskrit_iast: divyo yady ekadeśaḥ syād ekadeśaś ca mānuṣaḥ / aśāśvataṃ śāśvataṃ ca bhavet tac ca na yujyate // antavān ekadeśaś ced ekadeśas tv anantavān / syād antavān anantaś ca lokas tac ca na yujyate // kathaṃ tāvad upādātur ekadeśo vinaṅkṣyate / na naṅkṣyate caikadeśa evaṃ caitan na yujyate //
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/27.17-2.english_close: If one part were divine and another human, the same thing would become both eternal and non-eternal, which is untenable. If one part were finite and another infinite, the world would be both finite and infinite, which is likewise untenable. Nor can one coherently say that only one part of the appropriator perishes while another does not.

ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/14.sanskrit_iast: citivahnir arohapade channo 'pi mātrayā meyendhanaṃ pluṣyati // citir eva viśvagrasanaśīlatvāt vahniḥ ... mātrayā aṃśena nīlapītādiprameyendhanaṃ pluṣyati svātmasātkaroti ... api tu aṃśena saṃskārātmanā utthāpayati //
ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/14.english_close: Even in the stage of descent, consciousness as fire, though covered, scorches the fuel of the object only to a limited extent. That is to say: consciousness, whose nature is to devour the universe, appropriates blue, yellow, and the rest only by a part; it does not swallow them in total identity, but raises them up residually as traces.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/pradesa-bhajya.sanskrit_iast: asaṃkhyeyāḥ pradeśā dharmādharmekajīvānām // ākāśasyānantāḥ // saṃkhyeyāsaṃkhyeyāś ca pudgalānām // ekapradeśādiṣu bhājyaḥ pudgalānām // asaṃkhyeyabhāgādiṣu jīvānām // pradeśasaṃhāravisarpābhyāṃ pradīpavat //
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/pradesa-bhajya.english_close: Dharma, adharma, and each individual soul have innumerable spatial units; space has infinitely many; matter has numerable and innumerable ones. Matter is divisible down to one spatial unit and beyond; souls into innumerable fractions. The contraction and expansion of spatial units is like that of a lamp.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.7.english_close: Perception, inference, and authoritative transmission are the means of valid cognition.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.9.english_close: Conceptual construction is that which follows verbal cognition while being empty of a corresponding object.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.17.english_close: Because of the mutual superimposition of word, object, and cognition there is confusion; by saṃyama on their differentiation there is knowledge of the sounds of all beings.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.17.sanskrit_iast: evam ete śabdārthapratyayā itaretarādhyāsāt saṃkīrṇā gaur iti śabdo gaur ity artho gaur iti jñānam. ya eṣāṃ pravibhāgajñaḥ sa sarvavit.
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.17.english_close: Thus word, object, and cognition are intermixed through mutual superimposition: the word 'cow', the object cow, and the cognition 'cow'. One who knows their differentiation is all-knowing in that respect.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.8.english_close: It is of two kinds, because it concerns seen and unseen objects.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/2.1.53.english_close: From word there is cognition of the object because of the efficacy of trustworthy instruction.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/33.sanskrit_iast: śrotragrāhyo guṇaḥ śabdaḥ / ākāśamātravṛttiḥ / sa dvividho dvanyātmako varṇātmakaś ceti / dvanyātmako bheryādau / varṇātmakaḥ saṃskṛtabhāṣādirūpaḥ
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/33.english_close: Sound is a quality grasped by the ear and present only in ākāśa. It is twofold, as non-articulate sound and articulate sound; the first is found in things like a drum, the second in forms such as Sanskrit speech.

abhinavagupta/tantrasara/21.3-4.sanskrit_iast: tatra saṃvinmātramaye viśvasmin saṃvidi ca vimarśātmikāyāṃ vimarśasya ca śabdanātmakatāyāṃ siddhāyāṃ sakalajaganniṣṭhavastunaḥ tadgatasya ca karmaphalasambandhavaicitryasya yat vimarśanaṃ tad eva śāstram iti parameśvarasvabhāvābhinna eva samastaḥ śāstrasaṃdarbho vastuta ekaphalaprāpakaḥ. kecit tu viralaviralāḥ samastāvacchedavandhyasvātantryānandaparamārthasaṃvinmayaparameśvarasvarūpāmarśanātmani śrītrikaśāstrakrame.
abhinavagupta/tantrasara/21.3-4.english_close: In a universe consisting only of consciousness, where consciousness is reflective awareness and that reflection is of the nature of word, the discernment of things and their karmic relations is itself scripture.
