Thus the entire body of scripture is in reality non-different from Parameśvara and leads to one telos, while the Trika path is the disclosure of Parameśvara as unrestricted blissful consciousness.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/35.sanskrit_iast: sāntaḥkaraṇā buddhiḥ sarvaṃ viṣayam avagāhate yasmāt / tasmāt trividhaṃ karaṇaṃ dvāri dvārāṇi śeṣāṇi //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/35.english_close: Because buddhi, together with the internal organ, apprehends every object, the instrument is threefold, while the rest are merely doors.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/37.sanskrit_iast: sarvaṃ pratyupabhogaṃ yasmāt puruṣasya sādhayati buddhiḥ / saiva ca viśinaṣṭi tataḥ pradhānapuruṣāntaraṃ sūkṣmam //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/37.english_close: Because buddhi accomplishes the whole range of experience for puruṣa, it is also that which discriminates the subtle difference between pradhāna and puruṣa.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/4.22.english_close: Because consciousness does not pass over, when it takes on that form there is awareness of one's own buddhi.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.47.sanskrit_iast: aśuddhyāvaraṇamalāpetasya prakāśātmano buddhisattvasya rajastamobhyām anabhibhūtaḥ svacchaḥ sthitipravāho vaiśāradyam
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.47.english_close: When the defiling coverings of impurity have fallen away, the clear and steady flow of the luminous buddhi-sattva, no longer overpowered by rajas and tamas, is called clarity.

gautama/nyaya-sutra/1.1.15.english_close: Buddhi, apprehension, and knowledge are not different in meaning.

gautama/nyaya-sutra/3.2.24.english_close: Because its non-eternality is apprehended, a cognition perishes through another cognition, like a sound.

gautama/nyaya-sutra/3.2.39.english_close: By elimination, and because the reasons previously stated hold, [cognition belongs there].

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/4.english_close: Color, taste, smell, touch, number, magnitude, distinctness, conjunction, disjunction, remoteness, proximity, heaviness, fluidity, viscosity, sound, cognition, pleasure, pain, desire, aversion, effort, merit, demerit, and disposition are the twenty-four qualities.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/34-35.sanskrit_iast: sarvavyavahārahetur buddhir jñānam / sā dvividhā smṛtiranubhavaś ca / saṃskāramātrajanyaṃ jñānaṃ smṛtiḥ / tadbhinnaṃ jñānam anubhavaḥ // sa dvividho yathārtho 'yathārthaś ca
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/34-35.english_close: Cognition is the cause of all practical dealings. It is twofold: memory and experience. Memory is cognition produced only from a saṃskāra; cognition other than that is experience. Experience in turn is either veridical or non-veridical.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: bhrāntitve cāvasāyasya na jaḍād viṣayasthitiḥ / tato 'jāḍye nijollekhaniṣṭhān nārthasthitis tataḥ // ... adhyavasāya eva bhrāntyā viṣayavyavasthāpako na tu svasaṃvit sa ca jaḍaḥ katham arthavyavasthāyā hetuḥ
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: If ascertainment is erroneous, an inert thing cannot establish the object; and if it is non-inert yet fixed only in its own self-disclosure, even then the object's status is not secured. Ascertainment itself, as error, cannot be what establishes the object, for being inert, how could it be the cause of the object's determination?

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.11-12.english_close: The first two are indirect; the others are direct.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.13-15.sanskrit_iast: matiḥ smṛtiḥ saṃjñā cintābhinibodha ity anarthāntaram / tad indriyānindriyanimittam / avagrahehāvāyadhāraṇāḥ
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.13-15.english_close: Mati is not different from memory, recognition, thought, and determinative apprehension. It is caused by sense and non-sense factors. Its stages are apprehension, inquiry, ascertainment, and retention.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.100.english_close: Inference is cognition of the correlated item for one who sees the relation of constraint.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.5.sanskrit_iast: atha tatpūrvakaṃ trividham anumānaṃ pūrvavat śeṣavat sāmānyatodṛṣṭaṃ ca
aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.5.english_close: Now inference, preceded by that, is threefold: from what goes before, from remainder, and from the generally observed.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/44.sanskrit_iast: anumitikaraṇam anumānam / parāmarśajanyaṃ jñānam anumitiḥ / vyāptiviśiṣṭapakṣadharmatājñānaṃ parāmarśaḥ / yathā vahnivyāpyadhūmavān ayaṃ parvata iti jñānaṃ parāmarśaḥ / taj janyaṃ parvato vahnimān iti jñānam anumitiḥ / yatra yatra dhūmas tatrāgnir iti sāhacaryaniyamo vyāptiḥ / vyāpyasya parvatādivṛttitvaṃ pakṣadharmatā //
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/44.english_close: Inference is the instrument of inferential cognition. Inferential cognition is knowledge produced from parāmarśa. Parāmarśa is cognition of pakṣadharmatā qualified by vyāpti. Thus 'this mountain has smoke pervaded by fire' is parāmarśa, and 'the mountain has fire' is the inferential cognition born from it.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/45.sanskrit_iast: anumānaṃ dvividhaṃ svārthaṃ parārthaṃ ca / tatra svārthaṃ svānumitihetuḥ / tathā hi svayam eva bhūyo darśanena yatra dhūmas tatra agnir iti mahānasādau vyāptiṃ gṛhītvā parvatasamīpaṃ gatas tadgate cāgnau sandihānaḥ parvate dhūmaṃ paśyan vyāptiṃ smarati yatra dhūmas tatrāgnir iti / tadantaraṃ vahnivyāpyadhūmavān ayaṃ parvata iti jñānam utpadyate / ayam eva liṅgaparāmarṣaṃ ity ucyate / tasmāt parvato vahnimān iti jñānam anumitir utpadyate / tad etat svārthānumānam / yat tu svayaṃ dhūmād agnim anumāya parapratipattyarthaṃ pañcāvayavavākyaṃ prayukte tat parārthānumānam //
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/45.english_close: Inference is twofold, for oneself and for another. For oneself, it produces one's own inferential cognition by recollected vyāpti and liṅga-parāmarśa; for another, it is the five-membered statement used after one has oneself inferred fire from smoke.

shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.2-anuma.sanskrit_iast: nanu sāmānyato dṛṣṭaṃ bhaviṣyati. pauruṣeyaṃ vacanaṃ vitatham upalabhya vacanasāmānyād vedavacanaṃ mithyety anumīyate. na, anyatvāt.
shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.2-anuma.english_close: One might say there is an inference from general observation: having found human speech false, one infers from the commonness of speech that Vedic speech too is false. No, because it is other.

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.67.english_close: That very relation, marked by positive and negative concomitance, is the inferential sign; otherwise it would be contradictory.

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.75.sanskrit_iast: svalakṣaṇe ca pratyakṣamavikalpatayā vinā / vikalpena na sāmānyagrahastasmistato 'numā //
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.75.english_close: Perception concerns the particular apart from conceptual construction; a universal is not grasped there by conceptual construction, therefore it is grasped by inference.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: anumānam anābhātapūrve naiveṣṭam indriyam / ābhātam eva bījāder ābhāsād dhetuvastunaḥ //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: Inference is not accepted with respect to a sense-faculty regarding what has not previously appeared; only the causal factor is manifested from the appearance of seed and the like.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: ābhāsaḥ punarābhāsad bāhyasyāsīt kathaṃ cana / arthasya naiva tenāsya siddhir nāpy anumānataḥ //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: How could a mere appearance establish an external object from another appearance? By that there is no establishment of such an object, nor from inference either.

anonymous/kaivalya-upanishad/24.sanskrit_iast: anena jñānam āpnoti saṃsārārṇavanāśanam | tasmād evaṃ viditvainaṃ kaivalyaṃ padam aśnute |
anonymous/kaivalya-upanishad/24.english_close: By this one obtains the knowledge that destroys the ocean of saṃsāra; thus, knowing him in this way, one attains the state of kaivalya.

sankara/vivekacudamani/124.sanskrit_iast: atha te saṃpravakṣyāmi svarūpaṃ param ātmanaḥ | yadvijñāya naro bandhān muktaḥ kaivalyam aśnute ||
sankara/vivekacudamani/124.english_close: I shall now declare the supreme Self's true nature, by knowing which a person, freed from bondage, attains kaivalya.

sankara/vivekacudamani/567.sanskrit_iast: evaṃ videhakaivalyaṃ sanmātratvamakhaṇḍitam | brahmabhāvaṃ prapadyaiṣa yatir nāvartate punaḥ ||
sankara/vivekacudamani/567.english_close: Thus the renunciant attains disembodied kaivalya, undivided pure being, the state of Brahman, and does not return again.

ramanuja/gita-bhasya/8.20-21.sanskrit_iast: atha kaivalyaṃ praptānām api punarāvṛttir na vidyata ity aha ... ayam eva ... prakṛtisaṃsargaviyuktasvasvarūpeṇāvasthita ātmety arthaḥ ... acitsaṃsargaviyuktaṃ svarūpeṇāvasthitaṃ muktasvarūpaṃ paramaṃ niyamanasthānam ity arthaḥ.
ramanuja/gita-bhasya/8.20-21.english_close: He now says that even for those who have attained kaivalya there is no return. This means the self abiding in its own nature, disjoined from contact with prakṛti; the liberated self, free from association with insentient matter, is the supreme station.

ramanuja/gita-bhasya/13-intro.sanskrit_iast: atiśayitāiśvaryāpekṣāṇām ātmakaivalyamātrāpekṣāṇāṃ ca bhaktiyogas tattadapekṣitasādhanam iti coktam.
ramanuja/gita-bhasya/13-intro.english_close: It has also been taught that bhakti-yoga is the means respectively sought by those who desire extraordinary lordship and by those who desire only self-kaivalya.

jayatirtha/nyaya-sudha/duhkhapyaya-kaiva.sanskrit_iast: tat duḥkhāpyayādirūpaṃ kaivalyam | īśānāt viṣṇoreva bhavennānyasmāt | sa hi tasyeṣye nānyaḥ |
jayatirtha/nyaya-sudha/duhkhapyaya-kaiva.english_close: That kaivalya, of the nature of the cessation of suffering and the like, comes from Viṣṇu alone, the Lord, and from no other.

rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.2..sanskrit_iast: na kiñcit sādhavo dhīrā bhaktā hy ekāntino mama | vāñchanty api mayā dattaṃ kaivalyam apunar-bhavam ||
rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.2..english_close: The steadfast saints, my exclusive devotees, desire nothing at all, even kaivalya free from rebirth though granted by me.

jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/brahma-kaival.english_close: But not brahma-kaivalya, because the seed characterized as bhakti is imperishable.

jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/11.20.34-tika.sanskrit_iast: vāñchanty api mayā dattaṃ kaivalyam apunar-bhavam ... apunar-bhavam ātyantika-kaivalyam ity eṣā.
jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/11.20.34-tika.english_close: He quotes 'even kaivalya free from rebirth, granted by me,' and glosses 'free from rebirth' as ultimate kaivalya.

vallabha/anu-bhasya/gater-athevatvam.sanskrit_iast: anyathā jñānenaiva kaivalyaṃ yamevaiṣa vṛṇute iti vākyayor virodhaḥ syāt.
vallabha/anu-bhasya/gater-athevatvam.english_close: Otherwise there would be a contradiction between the statements 'kaivalya is by knowledge alone' and 'he is attained by the one whom he chooses.'

isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/19.english_close: Kaivalya is neutrality, witnesshood, and the state of non-agency.

isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/68.english_close: It attains kaivalya in both senses: definitive and final.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/2.25.sanskrit_iast: tadabhāvāt saṃyogābhāvo hānaṃ taddṛśeḥ kaivalyam ... taddṛśeḥ kaivalyaṃ puruṣasyāmiśrībhāvaḥ punar asaṃyogo guṇair ity arthaḥ.
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/2.25.english_close: With its absence, the absence of conjunction is release; for the seer that is kaivalya. Kaivalya for the puruṣa means non-mixture, that is, non-contact with the guṇas.

sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika/166.sanskrit_iast: tasya ca pūrvottarakarmanirodhānutpādābhyāṃ vigalitabhogabhogāyatanasya kaivalyaṃ mokṣam ācakṣmahe, na prapañcavilayam.
sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika/166.english_close: For such a one, whose earlier and later karmas have ceased and whose means of enjoyment have fallen away, we call kaivalya mokṣa, not the dissolution of the world-process.

ksemaraja/siva-sutra-varttika/3.5.sanskrit_iast: nāḍīsaṃhārabhūtajayabhūtakaivalyabhūtapṛthaktvāni ... bhūtebhyaḥ kila kaivalyaṃ cittapratyāhṛtis tataḥ ... pṛthaktvaṃ tadanāliptasvacchasvātmaikarūpatā.
ksemaraja/siva-sutra-varttika/3.5.english_close: There are the retraction of the channels, conquest of the elements, elemental kaivalya, and separateness. Kaivalya from the elements is the withdrawal of consciousness from them; separateness is stainless self-identity.

abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/83.english_close: Freed at the very moment of knowledge, free from grief, one goes to kaivalya.

abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara-comm/83.sanskrit_iast: mokṣasya parāhantācamatkārasārasya kaivalyasya dhāma vyatiriktaṃ sthānaṃ na vidyata eva ... ātmajñānādeva kaivalyaṃ yāti ... cidānandaikaghanāṃ turyātītarūpāṃ kevalatāṃ yāti.
abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara-comm/83.english_close: For kaivalya, whose essence is the wonder of supreme I-consciousness, there is no separate place at all. By self-knowledge alone one attains kaivalya, that is, a compact mass of consciousness and bliss beyond even turiya.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/10.1-2.sanskrit_iast: mohakṣayāj jñānadarśanāvaraṇāntarāyakṣayāc ca kevalam. bandhahetvabhāvanirjarābhyāṃ kṛtsnakarmavipramokṣo mokṣaḥ.
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/10.1-2.english_close: Kevala arises from the destruction of delusion and of the obscuring and obstructing karmas. Mokṣa is total release from all karma through the absence of the causes of bondage and through nirjarā.

vasubandhu/trimsika/29-30.sanskrit_iast: āśrayasya parāvṛttirdvidhā dauṣṭhulyahānitaḥ. sa evānasravo dhātur acintyaḥ kuśalo dhruvaḥ. sukho vimuktikāyo 'sau dharmākhyo 'yaṃ mahāmuneḥ.
vasubandhu/trimsika/29-30.english_close: The basis undergoes a turning through the removal of two kinds of defect. That itself is the undefiled element, inconceivable, wholesome, stable, and blissful as the body of liberation.

isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/27.sanskrit_iast: ubhayātmakam atra manaḥ saṃkalpakam indriyaṃ ca sādharmyāt
isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/27.english_close: Here mind has a double character: it is the faculty of conceptual projection, and, because of common origin, it is also an indriya.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.35.sanskrit_iast: viṣayavatī vā pravṛttir utpannā manasaḥ sthitinibandhanī
patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.35.english_close: Or an arisen cognition having an object becomes a binding of the mind to steadiness.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.53.english_close: And there is fitness of the mind for concentrations.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/18.sanskrit_iast: sukhādyupalabdhisādhanam indriyaṃ manaḥ / tac ca pratyātmaniyatatvād anantaṃ paramāṇurūpaṃ nityaṃ ca //
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/18.english_close: Mind is the sense-instrument for apprehending pleasure and the rest. Because it is restricted to each self, it is innumerable, atomic in form, and eternal.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.16.english_close: The non-arising of cognitions simultaneously is the mark of mind.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/3.2.56-59.sanskrit_iast: jñānāyaugapadyāt ekam manaḥ ... yathoktahetutvāt ca aṇu
aksapada/nyaya-sutra/3.2.56-59.english_close: Because cognitions do not occur simultaneously, the mind is one; and because it has the previously stated marks, it is atomic.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/7.1.23.english_close: Because that [pervasiveness] is absent, the mind is atomic.

kumarila/sloka-varttika/3.14.sanskrit_iast: mānasam iti cet / na / bahirasvātantryāt / ... svatantre hi bahiriṣyamāṇe manasi sarvaḥ sarvadarśī syāt
kumarila/sloka-varttika/3.14.english_close: If it be said that this is mental: no, because the mind is not autonomous with respect to external objects. Were mind externally autonomous, everyone would see everything.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/3.1.sanskrit_iast: darśanaṃ śravaṇaṃ ghrāṇaṃ rasanaṃ sparśanaṃ manaḥ / indriyāṇi ṣaḍ eteṣāṃ draṣṭavyādīni gocaraḥ //
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/3.1.english_close: Seeing, hearing, smelling, tasting, touching, and mind: these six are the sense-faculties, and the visible and the rest are their objects.

nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/3.9.sanskrit_iast: vyākhyātaṃ śravaṇaṃ ghrāṇaṃ rasanaṃ sparśanaṃ manaḥ / darśanenaiva jānīyāc chrotṛśrotavyakādi ca //
nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/3.9.english_close: Hearing, smelling, tasting, touching, and mind have been explained; by the same method one should understand hearer and heard, and the rest.

abhinavagupta/ishvara-pratyabhijna-vimar.english_close: Saṅkalpa arises in relation to mind and an indistinct object.
Mind is the instrument of saṅkalpa and the like.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9-14.sanskrit_iast: matiśrutāvadhimanaḥparyayakevalāni jñānam / tatpramāṇe / ādye parokṣam / pratyakṣam anyat / matiḥ smṛtiḥ saṃjñā cintābhinibodha ity anarthāntaram / tad indriyānindriyanimittam
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9-14.english_close: Knowledge is of five kinds: mati, śruta, avadhi, manaḥparyaya, and kevala. The first two are indirect; the others are direct. Mati is also called memory, recognition, thought, and apprehension, and it arises from sensory and non-sensory causes.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.23-29.sanskrit_iast: ṛjuvipulamatī manaḥparyayaḥ / viśuddhyapratipātābhyāṃ tadviśeṣaḥ / viśuddhikṣetrasvāmiviṣayo 'vadhimanaḥparyayayoḥ / matiśrutayor nibandho dravyeṣv asravaparyāyeṣu / rūpiṣv avadheḥ / tadanantabhāge manaḥparyayasya / sarvadravyaparyāyeṣu kevalasya
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.23-29.english_close: Manaḥparyaya is of two kinds, straightforward and expansive. Its specification depends on purity and non-decline. Avadhi and manaḥparyaya differ by purity, field, possessor, and object, and manaḥparyaya reaches a further range beyond avadhi.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/2.11.english_close: They are with mind and without mind.

sankara/vivekacudamani/11-local.sanskrit_iast: cittasya śuddhaye karma na tu vastūpalabdhaye / vastusiddhir vicāreṇa na kiṃcit karmakoṭibhiḥ //
sankara/vivekacudamani/11-local.english_close: Action is for the purification of citta, not for the apprehension of Reality; the ascertainment of the Real comes by inquiry, not by millions of actions.

sankara/vivekacudamani/26-local.sanskrit_iast: sarvadā sthāpanaṃ buddheḥ śuddhe brahmaṇi sarvadā / tatsamādhānam ity uktaṃ na tu cittasya lālanam //
sankara/vivekacudamani/26-local.english_close: The constant fixing of the intellect in pure Brahman is called samādhāna, not the pampering of citta.

sankara/upadesa-sahasri/17.22-citte-adar.sanskrit_iast: citte hy ādarśavad yasmāc chuddhe vidyā prakāśate / yamair nityaiś ca yajñaiś ca tapobhis tasya śodhanam //
sankara/upadesa-sahasri/17.22-citte-adar.english_close: For knowledge shines in a purified citta as in a mirror; its purification is by restraints, constant sacrifices, and austerities.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.2-local.english_close: Yoga is the cessation of the modifications of citta.

vyasa-bhasya/yoga-bhasya/1.1-citta-bhuma.sanskrit_iast: sa ca sārvabhaumaś cittasya dharmaḥ. kṣiptaṃ mūḍhaṃ vikṣiptam ekāgraṃ niruddham iti cittabhūmayaḥ.
vyasa-bhasya/yoga-bhasya/1.1-citta-bhuma.english_close: That is a universal property of citta. Its levels are the distracted, the stupefied, the scattered, the one-pointed, and the restrained.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.11-12-local.sanskrit_iast: sarvārthataikāgratayoḥ kṣayodayau cittasya samādhipariṇāmaḥ // tataḥ punaḥ śāntoditau tulyapratyayau cittasyaikāgratāpariṇāmaḥ //
patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.11-12-local.english_close: The decline of all-directedness and the rise of one-pointedness is citta's transformation into samādhi. Thereafter, when the quiescent and arising cognitions are alike, that is citta's transformation into one-pointedness.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/4.14-ekacitta.english_close: As with the arrow-maker, the loss of samādhi does not occur for one whose citta is not dispersed.

ramanuja/gita-bhasya/6.11-12-yatacittend.sanskrit_iast: tatraikāgraṃ manaḥ kṛtvā yatacittendriyakriyaḥ / upaviśyāsane yuñjyād yogam ātmaviśuddhaye // ... sarvātmanopasaṃhṛtacittendriyakriyaḥ ātmaviśuddhaye bandhanivṛttaye yogaṃ yuñjyād atmāvalokanaṃ kurvīta //
ramanuja/gita-bhasya/6.11-12-yatacittend.english_close: Having made the mind one-pointed and restrained the activities of citta and the senses, one should practice yoga for the purification of the self. With all the activities of citta and the senses withdrawn, one should practice yoga for release from bondage and for seeing the self.

ramanuja/gita-bhasya/6.14-19-maccitto.sanskrit_iast: praśāntātmā vigatabhīḥ brahmacārivrate sthitaḥ / manaḥ saṃyamya maccitto yukta āsīta matparaḥ // ... yadā viniyataṃ cittam ātmany evāvatiṣṭhate ... yathā dīpo nivātastho neṅgate sopamā smṛtā / yogino yatacittasya yuñjato yogam ātmanaḥ //
ramanuja/gita-bhasya/6.14-19-maccitto.english_close: With the mind restrained, one should sit absorbed in me, with citta fixed on me and taking me as the highest. When citta is controlled and remains in the self, the yogin of restrained citta is like a lamp in a windless place.

jayatirtha/nyaya-sudha/cittavrtti-pravrt.sanskrit_iast: trividhā hi puṃsāṃ cittavṛttiḥ / anubhava icchā prayatnaś ca / tatra sādhanagocarāv icchāprayatnau pravṛttir iti ucyete /
jayatirtha/nyaya-sudha/cittavrtti-pravrt.english_close: For the movements of citta in persons are threefold: cognition, desire, and effort. Of these, desire and effort with a means as their object are called activity.

jayatirtha/nyaya-sudha/cittavrttinirodha.sanskrit_iast: tasya dhyānasyāṅgam eva na tu svatantram upāsanam / ... śāstrābhyāsasya dhyānāṅgatve 'pi svatantropāsanatvaṃ cāsti / brahmasākṣātkārahetutvāt / cittavṛttinirodhā(katvā)c ca /
jayatirtha/nyaya-sudha/cittavrttinirodha.english_close: That dhāraṇā and the rest are only auxiliaries of meditation, not an independent upāsanā. Yet scriptural rehearsal does have independent upāsanā-status as well, because it is a cause of Brahman-realization and of the cessation of the movements of citta.

jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/suddha-citta-.sanskrit_iast: yataḥ, viśuddha-sattvāṃśa-bhāvitāyāṃ citta-vṛttau yad brahma sphurati ... ucyate - bhavan-mate tāvat śuddha-citta-vṛttau para-brahma sphurati samyag eva sphurati |
jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/suddha-citta-.english_close: When Brahman shines in a mental modification shaped by a share of pure sattva, then, on your view, the supreme Brahman appears correctly in a purified citta-vṛtti.

jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/subhasraya-ci.sanskrit_iast: atra śrī-viṣṇu-purāṇam api sampravadate - śubhāśrayaḥ sa cittasya savargasya tathātmanaḥ iti [vip 6.7.76] |
jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/subhasraya-ci.english_close: The Viṣṇu-Purāṇa too declares: He is the auspicious support of citta, of all that belongs to it, and likewise of the self.

ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/5-citirev.sanskrit_iast: citir eva cetanapadād avarūḍhā cetyasaṃkocinī cittam ||
ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/5-citirev.english_close: Consciousness itself, descended to the level of the limited knower and contracting through objectivity, is citta.

vasugupta/spanda-karika/2.2-saharadhakac.sanskrit_iast: tatraiva sampralīyante śāntarūpā nirañjanāḥ / sahārādhakacittena tenaite śivadharmiṇaḥ //
vasugupta/spanda-karika/2.2-saharadhakac.english_close: They dissolve there itself, peaceful and stainless; together with the worshipper's citta they are of the nature of Śiva.

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.522-citte.sanskrit_iast: sakṛd vijātīyajātāv apy ekena paṭīyasā / cittenāhitavaiguṇyād ālāyān nānyasambhavaḥ //
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.522-citte.english_close: Even when a heterogeneous cognition arises once through a stronger cause, there is no other source than the ālaya, because a defect has been deposited in citta.

isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/9.sanskrit_iast: asadakaraṇād upādānagrahaṇāt sarvasambhavābhāvāt / śaktasya śakyakaraṇāt kāraṇabhāvāc ca sat kāryam //
isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/9.english_close: The effect is existent: because the non-existent is not produced; because a material cause is taken up; because not everything arises from everything; because the potent produces only the producible; and because of the nature of the cause.

isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/14-15.sanskrit_iast: avivekyādiḥ siddhas traiguṇyāt tadviparyayābhāvāt / kāraṇaguṇātmakatvāt kāryasyāvyaktam api siddham // bhedānāṃ parimāṇāt samanvayāc chaktitaḥ pravṛtteś ca / kāraṇakāryavibhāgād avibhāgād vaiśvarūpyasya //
isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/14-15.english_close: The unmanifest too is established because the effect is constituted by the qualities of the cause. From the limitedness of differentiations, from coherence, from operation through potency, from the division of cause and effect, and from the undividedness of the all-formed whole.

vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/4.34.sanskrit_iast: puruṣārthaśūnyānāṃ guṇānāṃ pratiprasavaḥ kaivalyaṃ svarūpapratiṣṭhā vā citiśaktir iti || ys_4.34 || kṛtabhogāpavargāṇāṃ puruṣārthaśūnyānāṃ yaḥ pratiprasavaḥ kāryakāraṇātmakānāṃ guṇānāṃ tat kaivalyaṃ, svarūpapratiṣṭhā punar buddhisattvānabhisaṃbandhāt puruṣasya citiśaktir eva kevalā, tasyāḥ sadā tathaivāvasthānaṃ kaivalyam iti.
vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/4.34.english_close: Kaivalya is the reabsorption of the guṇas, once they are empty of purpose for puruṣa. More precisely: kaivalya is the reabsorption of the guṇas, which are of the nature of cause and effect, once enjoyment and liberation for the puruṣa have been accomplished.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.7.sanskrit_iast: sad anityaṃ dravyavat kāryaṃ kāraṇaṃ sāmānyaviśeṣavad iti dravyaguṇakarmaṇām aviśeṣaḥ |
kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.7.english_close: Substance, quality, and motion are non-different in sharing being, non-eternity, substance-like status, effecthood and causehood, and the possession of universals and particulars.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/7.2.29.english_close: From the cognition 'it is here', that relation is samavāya in the case of cause and effect.

vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/5.1.37.sanskrit_iast: prayatnānantarīyakatvād anityaḥ śabda iti / yasya prayatnānantaram ātmalābhaḥ tat khalv abhūtvā bhavati, yathā ghaṭādikāryam; anityam iti ca bhūtvā na bhavatīty etad vijñāyate /
vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/5.1.37.english_close: One argues: sound is impermanent because it comes immediately after effort. Whatever acquires being after effort comes to be from prior non-being, like a pot and other effects; and 'impermanent' means that, having come to be, it later is not.

shabara/shabara-bhasya/7.3.31.sanskrit_iast: na vaidikam agnidravyaṃ śāstreṇotpāditam. tasya śāstreṇaiva kāryaṃ nirdiṣṭam, yadāhavanīye juhotītīy ārabhya. tasmin sarvārthe kalpyamāne nirdeśo 'narthakaḥ syāt.
shabara/shabara-bhasya/7.3.31.english_close: That fire-substance is not produced as a Vedic thing by scripture. Its task is specified only by scripture, beginning with 'one should pour into the āhavanīya'; if it were assigned to every purpose indiscriminately, that specification would be pointless.

shabara/shabara-bhasya/7.4.1.sanskrit_iast: ucyate, kim atra tṛtīyānirdiṣṭena yajinā kāryam. yadā svabhāvasiddhaḥ karmaṇāṃ phalaṃ prati guṇabhāvaḥ. ... tasmād yāgenāpūrvasādhanam atrocyate.
shabara/shabara-bhasya/7.4.1.english_close: One may ask: what is to be done here with the sacrifice-term not marked by the instrumental? Since the subordination of acts to the result is already established by their nature, what is taught here is that sacrifice brings about apūrva.

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/3.172.sanskrit_iast: ananyabhāk sa evārthas tasya vyāvṛttayo 'pare / tat kāryakāraṇaṃ coktaṃ tat svalakṣaṇam iṣyate //
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/3.172.english_close: That very thing which is non-shared is the object; its exclusions are others. That is what is spoken of as cause-and-effect, and that is accepted as the particular.

utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2.sanskrit_iast: jaḍasya tu na sā śaktiḥ sattā yad asataḥ sataḥ / kartṛkarmatvatattvaiva kāryakāraṇatā tataḥ // ipv: jaḍaṃ pradhānaparamāṇubījādi tu na śaktam asato nirmāṇe, kartṛtvam eva hi kāraṇatvaṃ karmataiva ca kāryatvaṃ na tv anyat //
utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2.english_close: The inert does not possess that power by which the existent could arise from the non-manifest; therefore cause-and-effect is precisely the reality of agent and patient. The commentary explains: causehood is nothing but agency, and effecthood nothing but patienthood.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/58.sanskrit_iast:
autsukyanivṛttyarthaṃ yathā kriyāsu pravartate lokaḥ /
puruṣasya vimokṣārthaṃ pravartate tadvad avyaktam //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/58.english_close: Just as people engage in actions to remove restlessness, so the unmanifest proceeds for the sake of the puruṣa's release.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.1.english_close: Tapas, self-study, and devotion to Īśvara are kriyā-yoga.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.6.sanskrit_iast: utkṣepaṇam avakṣepaṇam ākuñcanaṃ prasāraṇaṃ gamanam iti karmāṇi
kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.6.english_close: Upward movement, downward movement, contraction, expansion, and locomotion are the motions.

shabara/mimamsa-bhasya/1.1.2.sanskrit_iast: codaneti kriyāyāḥ pravartakaṃ vacanam āhuḥ. ācāryacoditaḥ karomīti hi dṛśyate.
shabara/mimamsa-bhasya/1.1.2.english_close: `Codanā`, they say, is speech that impels action; for one is indeed seen to say, "I act, having been enjoined by the teacher."

ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/9-vrtti.sanskrit_iast: kriyāśaktiḥ krameṇa bhede sarvakartṛtvasya kiṃcitkartṛtvāpteḥ karmendriyarūpasaṃkocagrahaṇapūrvaṃ atyantaparimitatāṃ prāptā śubhāśubhānuṣṭānamayaṃ kārmaṃ malam
ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/9-vrtti.english_close: Kriyā-śakti, by progressive contraction into difference, passes from universal agency to limited agency; having assumed the contraction marked by the organs of action, it reaches extreme limitation and becomes the karmic impurity made of auspicious and inauspicious performance.

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/3.46-47-apo.sanskrit_iast: vastugrahe 'manumānācca dharmasyaikasya niścaye / sarvagraho hyapohe tu nāyaṃ doṣaḥ prasajyate // tasmādapohaviṣayamiti liṅgaṃ prakīrtitam / anyathā dharmiṇaḥ siddhavasiddhaṃ kimataḥ param //
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/3.46-47-apo.english_close: When one property alone is determined, the whole object is not thereby grasped; but if the object is exclusion, this fault does not arise. Therefore the inferential sign is said to have exclusion as its object.

vacaspati/nyaya-varttika-tatparya-tika/2.sanskrit_iast: anyāpohajātyorlakṣaṇābhede nāsti vivāda ityarthaḥ ... dravyamiti hi vidhirūpamevaṃ sadityapi vidhirūpaṃ tacchabdau tayorvācakau tayoranyāpohārthatvaṃ vyāhatam ... tasmādanyāpohagopacarau śabdavikalpāviti ... atrocyate ... tasmājjātimatyo vyaktayo vikalpānāṃ śabdānāṃ ca gocaraḥ.
vacaspati/nyaya-varttika-tatparya-tika/2.english_close: If universals and anyāpoha had the same definition, there would be no dispute; but positive terms such as 'substance' and 'being' cannot have mere anyāpoha as their meaning. Therefore the objects of words and concepts are individuals possessed of universals.

sucaritamisra/mimamsa-slokavarttika-kasi.sanskrit_iast: sādṛśyarūpam apoharūpaṃ ca sāmānyaṃ parastān nirākariṣyata ity āha ... na cātyantāsata evāpiho ghaṭate ity āha ... yadi jñānābhāvamātram ajñānaṃ tat tarhy avasturūpaṃ katham apohakriyākarma bhaviṣyatīti.
sucaritamisra/mimamsa-slokavarttika-kasi.english_close: The accounts of universality as resemblance-form and as exclusion-form are to be refuted. Exclusion cannot operate upon what is absolutely nonexistent; if ignorance were mere absence, it could not serve as the object of an exclusion-act.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: na ced antaḥkṛtānantaviśvarūpo maheśvaraḥ / syād ekaś cidvapur jñānasmṛtyapohanaśaktimān //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: If there were not one Maheśvara within, whose body is consciousness and who possesses the powers of cognition, memory, and exclusion, worldly continuity would not stand.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: tadatatpratibhābhājā mātraivātadvyapohanāt / tanniścayanamukto hi vikalpo ghaṭa ity ayam // pramātṛtvenāham iti vimarśo 'nyavyapohanāt / vikalpa eva sa parapratiyogy avabhāsajaḥ //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: A vikalpa such as 'this is a pot' is a determination precisely through the exclusion of what is not that; and the aham-thought of oneself as knower too is a vikalpa produced through exclusion of the other.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.34.english_close: The standpoints are naigama, saṃgraha, vyavahāra, rju-sūtra, śabda, samabhirūḍha, and evambhūta.

sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.2.33-36.sanskrit_iast: sarvatra cemaṃ saptabhaṅgīnayaṃ nāma nyāyam avatārayanti / syād asti, syān nāsti, syād asti ca nāsti ca, syād avaktavyaḥ ... nahy ekasmin dharmiṇi yugapat sadasattvādi-viruddha-dharma-samāveśaḥ saṃbhavati śītoṣṇavat.
sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.2.33-36.english_close: They everywhere introduce the method called the sevenfold scheme: 'in some respect it is', 'in some respect it is not', and so on. But mutually opposed properties such as being and non-being cannot belong together in one substratum, just as cold and heat cannot.

ramanuja/shri-bhasya/2.2.33-36.sanskrit_iast: svaṃ ca vastu-jātaṃ sattvāsattva-nityatvānityatva-bhinnatvābhinnatvādibhir anekāntikam icchanti -- syād asti, syān nāsti, syād asti ca nāsti ca ... atrābhidhīyate -- naikasminn asaṃbhavād iti ... ekasmin vastu-ni bhastitva-nāstitvādibhir viruddhasya yugapad asaṃbhavāt.
ramanuja/shri-bhasya/2.2.33-36.english_close: They hold the whole class of things to be non-one-sided: 'in some respect it is, in some respect it is not', and so on. The reply is that this is impossible in one thing, since contradictory features such as being and non-being cannot be present there at once.

nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.2.3.sanskrit_iast: imañ ca saptabhaṅgī-nayaṃ sarvatrāvatārayanti -- syād asti syān nāsti syād avaktavyaḥ ... sarva-vastu-jātaṃ dravya-paryāyātmakam anekāntikam ... tedevam ucyate -- naikasmin iti / ekasya dravyasyāstitva-nāstitva-nityatvānityatvādibhir anekāntikatvaṃ na yuktam.
nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.2.3.english_close: They everywhere introduce the sevenfold scheme: 'in some respect it is', 'in some respect it is not', 'it is indescribable', and so on, taking all things to be many-sided as substance-and-mode. The answer is that one and the same thing cannot coherently be both existent and non-existent, permanent and impermanent.

vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.2.33-36.sanskrit_iast: vinasana-samayo nirākriyate ... syāc-chando'bhivacanaḥ ... saptaprakāro bhavati, tad ekasmin nyojayanti, tad virodhenāsaṃbhavād ayuktam ... tathā sati paryāyeṇāvirodha iti na vaktavyam, tathā sati vikārāpatteḥ, saṃkoca-vikāse 'pi vikārasya duṣpariharatvāt.
vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.2.33-36.english_close: The naked ascetic view is refuted: it frames a sevenfold predication and applies it to one thing, but that is improper because contradiction makes it impossible. Nor can appeal to modes remove the problem, since contraction and expansion would still entail modification.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/2.1.20.sanskrit_iast: kvacin nivṛttidarśanād anivṛttidarśanāc ca kvacid anekāntaḥ.
aksapada/nyaya-sutra/2.1.20.english_close: In some cases, because one sees both cessation and non-cessation, there is inconclusiveness (`anekānta`).

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/3.2.17.english_close: Because in some cases a cause of destruction is not observed and in others it is observed, the matter is inconclusive (`anekānta`).

vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/2.1.20.sanskrit_iast: so 'yaṃ viśeṣahetu-parigraham antareṇa dṛṣṭānta ekasmin pakṣe upādeyo na pratipakṣa ity anekāntaḥ.
vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/2.1.20.english_close: Without securing a differentiating reason, the example is fit to be taken up on one side and also on the opposing side; that is `anekānta`.

vacaspati/bhamati/alayavijnana-vasana.locus: Bhāmati on BSB 2.2.30-31, critique of ālaya-vijñāna as vāsanā-ādhāra
vacaspati/bhamati/alayavijnana-vasana.sanskrit_iast: na ca kṣaṇikam ālayavijñānaṃ vāsanādhāro bhavitum arhati / dvayor yugapadutpadyamānayoḥ savyadakṣiṇaśṛṅgavad ādhārādheyabhāvābhāvāt / prāgutpannasya cādheyotpādasamaye 'sataḥ kṣaṇikatvavyāghāta iti.
vacaspati/bhamati/alayavijnana-vasana.english_close: A momentary ālaya-vijñāna cannot serve as the substrate of latent impressions. Two simultaneously arising items cannot stand in a support-supported relation, like right and left horns; and if one arose earlier, it would not exist at the moment the other arose, which would contradict momentariness.

yamuna/atma-siddhi/alayavijnana-pratisan.sanskrit_iast: nanu vijñānaṃ dvividhaṃ pravṛttivijñānam ālayavijñānaṃ ceti / tatrādyaṃ nīlapītādijñānam / antyaṃ cāhamākāram ātmarūpam / kṣaṇikatve 'py ubhayavidhavijñānasyālayavijñānadhārānuvṛttyā pratyabhijñopapadyata ity atrāha ... kṣaṇikakramikavijñānasamudāyalakṣaṇaṃ santānaṃ samudāyyanatiriktaṃ cet pratyabhijñānānupapattir eva.
yamuna/atma-siddhi/alayavijnana-pratisan.english_close: An opponent says: cognition is twofold, active cognition and ālaya-vijñāna; the former is blue, yellow, and the like, while the latter has the form of the self, and recognition is explained by the continuing stream of ālaya-vijñāna despite momentariness. Yāmuna replies that if the stream is nothing over and above the serial aggregate of momentary cognitions, recognition is impossible.

vallabha/anu-bhasya/alayavijnana-kshanik.locus: Aṇu-Bhāṣya on BSB 2.2.31, critique of ālaya-vijñāna
vallabha/anu-bhasya/alayavijnana-kshanik.sanskrit_iast: vāsanāyā ādhāro 'pi nāsti / ālayavijñānasya kṣaṇikatvāt / vṛttivijñānavat / evaṃ sautrāntika-vijñānavādī ca pratyuktaḥ.
vallabha/anu-bhasya/alayavijnana-kshanik.english_close: There is no substrate even for latent impressions, because ālaya-vijñāna is momentary, just like operative cognition. Thus the Sautrāntika and the Vijñānavādin are answered.

vacaspati/nyayavarttikatatparyatika/prat.sanskrit_iast: na ca pratyayānāṃ bhedāgrahāt pratisaṃdhānam iti sāṃpratam, pravṛttivijñānānāṃ rūpādiviṣayāṇāṃ bhedena pṛthagjanair api pratiyamānatvāt, ālayavijñānasya ca pravṛttivijñānātiriktasyānupalabdheḥ ... tasmāt smṛtyā saha pūrvāparapratyayayoḥ ekaviṣayatvalakṣaṇasya pratisandhānasyānekakartṛkatvād vyatireko nirūpādhir ekakartṛkatvaṃ sādhayati / sa caikaḥ kartā śarīrendriyabuddhibhyo bhinna ātmeti siddham.
vacaspati/nyayavarttikatatparyatika/prat.english_close: Recognition cannot be explained by non-apprehension of differences among cognitions, because ordinary people do apprehend the difference among operative cognitions with objects such as form, and no ālaya-vijñāna distinct from them is perceived. Therefore recognition, characterized by the single-object relation of prior and later cognitions together with memory, proves a single agent, and that one agent is the self distinct from body, senses, and intellect.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.39-no-ala.sanskrit_iast: yasmāt svarūpeṇa na tam niruddhaṃ ciraṃ niruddhād api karmaṇo 'taḥ / kvacid vinaivālayam asya śakteḥ phalaṃ samutpadyata ity avaihi //
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.39-no-ala.english_close: Since, in its own nature, that karmic potency is not destroyed, its fruit sometimes arises from karma long after the act, even without an ālaya; understand it in that way.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.43-44-ney.sanskrit_iast: evaṃ hi gambhīratarān padārthān na vetti yas taṃ prati deśaneyam / asty ālayaḥ pudgala eva cāsti skandhā ime vā khalu kevalāś ca // ahaṃ mamety eva yathā dideśa satkāyadṛṣṭer vigame 'pi buddhaḥ / tathā 'svabhāvān api sarvabhāvān astīti neyārthatayā dideśa //
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.43-44-ney.english_close: To one who does not understand these deeper matters, the teaching is: there is ālaya, there is a person, or again these skandhas alone. Just as the Buddha taught 'I' and 'mine' even after the abandonment of the personality-view, so too he taught all things as existing in a merely interpretable sense.

abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/30-31.sanskrit_iast: etat tad andhakāraṃ yad bhāveṣu prakāśamānatayā / ātmānatirikteṣv api bhavaty anātmābhimāno 'yam // timirād api timiram idaṃ gaṅdasyopari mahān ayaṃ sphoṭaḥ / yad anātmany api dehaprāṇadāv ātmamānitvam //
abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/30-31.english_close: This is the darkness: that, within manifest entities, there arises the conceit of the non-self even in what is other than the self. Worse than darkness is this great rupture: that even with respect to body and breath, which are non-self, there is the conceit that they are the self.

abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/39.sanskrit_iast: yad anātmany api tadrūpāvabhāsanaṃ tat purā nirākṛtya / ātmany anātmarūpāṃ bhrāntiṃ vidalayati paramātmā //
abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/39.english_close: Having first rejected the manifestation of that form in the non-self, the supreme Self shatters the error by which the form of non-self had been projected into the self.

gaudapada/mandukya-karika/4.22.sanskrit_iast: svato vā parato vāpi na kiṃcid vastu jāyate / sad asat sadasad vāpi na kiṃcid vastu jāyate
gaudapada/mandukya-karika/4.22.english_close: No thing whatsoever is born from itself or from another; no thing whatsoever is born as being, non-being, or both being and non-being.

sankara/mandukya-karika-bhasya/4.84.sanskrit_iast: asti nāstītyādyāś catasro yāsāṃ koṭīnāṃ grahair grahaṇair upalabdhiniścayaiḥ sadā sarvadāvṛta ācchāditas teṣāmeva prāvādukānāṃ yaḥ sa bhagavān ābhir astināstītyādikoṭibhiś catasṛbhir apy aspṛṣṭaḥ
sankara/mandukya-karika-bhasya/4.84.english_close: The four corners beginning with 'it is' and 'it is not' cover the disputants, but the Blessed One is untouched even by those four corners beginning with being and non-being.

ramanuja/vedartha-sangraha/46.sanskrit_iast: anirvacanīyapratyanīkaṃ nirvacanīyaṃ tac ca sad vāsad vā dvirūpaṃ vā koṭyantaraṃ na vidyate
ramanuja/vedartha-sangraha/46.english_close: The counterpositive of the indescribable must be describable, and that must be either being, non-being, or dual in form; there is no further corner.

madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.236-237.sanskrit_iast: āha kṣapaṇako viśvaṃ sadasad dvayamadvayam / dvayādvayamatatsarvaṃ saptabhaṅgisadātanam // naitat padārtha ekasmin yuktaṃ dṛṣṭivirodhataḥ
madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.236-237.english_close: The Jaina says the world is being, non-being, both, non-both, and so on in seven modes; this is not fitting in one entity, because the views conflict.

jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/43.sanskrit_iast: tata evābheda-śāstrāṇy ubhayoś cid-rūpatve jīva-samūhasya durghaṭa-ghaṭanā-paṭīyasyā svābhāvika-tad-acintya-śaktyā svabhāvata eva tad-raśmi-paramāṇu-guṇa-sthānīyatvāt tad-vyatirekeṇāvyatirekeṇa ca virodhaṃ parihṛtya
jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/43.english_close: Hence the non-difference texts are to be construed because both are of the nature of consciousness, and because the multitude of selves, by the Lord's natural inconceivable power, stands to him like rays or atoms, removing the contradiction of difference and non-difference.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika-commentary/9.sanskrit_iast: asti nāstīti varākā jīvakāḥ | naivāsti na ca nāsti | eṣa bauddhānāṃ pakṣaḥ ... asadakaraṇād upādānagrahaṇāt sarvasambhavābhāvāt / śaktasya śakyakaraṇāt kāraṇabhāvāc ca sat kāryam
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika-commentary/9.english_close: The Jainas say 'it is and it is not'; the Buddhists say 'it neither is nor is not'; against them it is said that the effect is real, because the unreal cannot be produced, because an appropriate material cause is taken, because not everything arises from everything, because the capable produces only the producible, and because of causal continuity.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.30.sanskrit_iast: saṃśaya ubhayakoṭispṛg vijñānaṃ syād idam evaṃ naivaṃ syād iti
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.30.english_close: Doubt is a cognition touching two corners: 'it may be thus, or it may not be thus.'

gautama/nyaya-sutra/1.1.23.sanskrit_iast: samānānekadharmopapatteḥ vipratipatteḥ upalabdhyanupalabdhyavyavasthātaś ca viśeṣāpekṣaḥ vimarśaḥ saṃśayaḥ
gautama/nyaya-sutra/1.1.23.english_close: Doubt is reflective consideration requiring a differentiating mark, arising from the possibility of common and distinct properties, conflicting views, and uncertainty in perception and non-perception.

gautama/nyaya-sutra/1.1.32-39.english_close: The members of argument are thesis, reason, example, application, and conclusion.

gautama/nyaya-sutra/4.1.37-40.sanskrit_iast: sarvam abhāvo bhāveṣv itaretarābhāvasiddheḥ / na, svabhāvasiddher bhāvānām / na svabhāvasiddhiḥ, āpekṣikatvāt / vyāhatatvādayuktam
gautama/nyaya-sutra/4.1.37-40.english_close: 'All is absence, because among entities reciprocal absence is established.' 'No, because entities are established by their own nature.' 'No own nature is established, because it is relational.' 'That is untenable because it is self-defeating.'

gautama/nyaya-sutra/4.1.48.english_close: It is not non-being, not being, and not both being and non-being, because being and non-being are mutually different in kind.

sabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.5.sanskrit_iast: yac ca nāma jñānam utpannaṃ na viparyeti, na tac chakyate vaktuṃ naitad evam iti ... evaṃ vadato viruddham idam avagamyata asti nāsti ceti
sabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.5.english_close: A cognition that has arisen and does not deviate cannot be denied as 'it is not so'; from someone who speaks that way there follows the contradiction 'it is and it is not.'

nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/23-primary.sanskrit_iast: nirmitako nirmitakaṃ māyāpuruṣa svamāyayā sṛṣṭam / pratiṣedhayati yadvat pratiṣedho yaṃ tathaiva syāt ... ṣaṭkoṭiko vāda uktaḥ sa evaṃ pratisiddhaḥ
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/23-primary.english_close: Just as a magically produced person might negate another magical construction, so too this negation could proceed in that way; the charge that this yields a six-cornered doctrine is thereby rejected.

abhinavagupta/isvara-pratyabhijna-vivrti.sanskrit_iast: iha ka īśvare kīdṛśe kīdṛśena pramāṇena asti iti jñānalakṣaṇāṃ siddhim, nāstīti jñānalakṣaṇaṃ vā niṣedhaṃ kuryāt
abhinavagupta/isvara-pratyabhijna-vivrti.english_close: Who, and by what proof, could establish of Īśvara 'he is' or negate 'he is not'?

isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/skmv_20.sanskrit_iast: tathā sattvādayo guṇāḥ kartāras taiḥ saṃyuktaḥ puruṣo 'pi akartāpi kartā bhavati | kartṛsaṃsargāt karteva | paraṃ paramārthatayā akartā puruṣaḥ ||
isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/skmv_20.english_close: Thus the guṇas beginning with sattva are the agents. The puruṣa, though in fact a non-agent, appears as an agent through conjunction with them. But in the ultimate sense the puruṣa is non-agent.

vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/3.13.sanskrit_iast: paramārthatas tv eka eva pariṇāmaḥ | dharmisvarūpamātro hi dharmo dharmivikriyaivaiṣā dharmadvārā prapañcyata iti |
vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/3.13.english_close: In the ultimate sense there is only one transformation. The dharma is nothing but the very nature of the substrate; this modification of the substrate is unfolded by way of the dharmas.

vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/3.55.sanskrit_iast: paramārthatas tu jñānād adarśanaṃ nivartate tasmin nivṛtte na santy uttare kleśāḥ | kleśābhāvāt karmavipākābhāvaḥ ... tatpuruṣasya kaivalyaṃ, tadā puruṣaḥ svarūpamātrajyotir amalaḥ kevalī bhavati |
vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/3.55.english_close: In the ultimate sense, through knowledge non-seeing ceases; when that has ceased, no further kleśas remain. With the kleśas absent, karmic fruition is absent; that is the puruṣa's kaivalya, and then the puruṣa abides as pure light of its own nature alone.

sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika/satya-dvaya.locus: Sucaritamiśra, Ślokavārttika commentary, satya-dvaya refutation (file lines 2969-2981)
sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika/satya-dvaya.sanskrit_iast: yaḥ khalv ayaṃ satyabhedo bauddhair āśritaḥ saṃvṛtisatyaṃ paramārthasatyam iti, nāyam upapadyate | saṃvṛteḥ satyatvābhāvāt ... yad tu tāvad vyavahārāvisaṃvādi tad asty eva ... tasmād yadi vyavahārāṅgatayā bāhyam asti, tat tarhi paramārthata evāsti | tad eva satyam ity ucyate |
sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika/satya-dvaya.english_close: This division adopted by the Buddhists as conventional truth and ultimate truth does not hold, because saṃvṛti lacks truth-status. What is non-belying in practice does exist; therefore, if an external object exists as a factor in practice, it exists in the ultimate sense itself, and that alone is called true.

ksemaraja/shiva-sutra-vimarshini/1.1.10-.sanskrit_iast: evaṃ śivoktayā nītyā jaṅgamasthāvarātmanaḥ || caitanyam eva viśvasya svarūpaṃ pāramārthikam ||
ksemaraja/shiva-sutra-vimarshini/1.1.10-.english_close: Thus, according to the method taught by Śiva, consciousness alone is the ultimate nature of the whole world, moving and unmoving.

ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/mahesvara.sanskrit_iast: asyā api prakāśaikasadbhāvāpāditāśeṣaviśvacamatkāramayaḥ śrīmānmaheśvara eva paramārthaḥ | na hi pāramārthikaprakāśāveśaṃ vinā kasyāpi prakāśamānatā ghaṭate |
ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/mahesvara.english_close: The glorious Maheśvara alone is the ultimate reality, as the entire wonder of the universe is grounded in the one being of light. Without immersion in ultimate light, nothing whatsoever can appear.

madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/184-186-prat.locus: Siddhānta-Bindu, discussion of pramāṇa-janya antaḥkaraṇa-vṛtti and bādhita-pratyakṣa
madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/184-186-prat.sanskrit_iast: tathā ca pramāṇajanyāntaḥkaraṇavṛttyabhāvasahakṛtam ajñānaṃ sati bhāty api vastuni nāsti na bhāti iti pratītijananasamartham āvaraṇam ity ucyate / vṛttau jātāyāṃ tv avacchedakābhāvād vidyamānam apy avidyamānasamam eveti na svakāryasamartham ajñānaṃ tenābhibhūtam ity ucyate / ... pratyakṣādīnāṃ tu bādhitaviṣayatayā bhramatve 'pi vyavahārasāmarthyena prāmāṇyābhimānāt
madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/184-186-prat.english_close: Ignorance functions as a covering so long as the pramāṇa-generated mental modification has not arisen; when that vṛtti arises, the covering is overpowered. Yet perception and the other pramāṇas, though ultimately erroneous because their objects are sublated, are still treated as authoritative on account of their practical efficacy.

mandana/brahma-siddhi/mbs_1.4.sanskrit_iast: āhur vidhātṛ pratyakṣaṃ na niṣeddhṛ vipaścitaḥ / naikatva āgamas tena pratyakṣeṇa virudhyate //
mandana/brahma-siddhi/mbs_1.4.english_close: The wise say that perception is an establisher, not a negator. Therefore the scriptural teaching of oneness is not contradicted by that perception.

ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/RVas_30.sanskrit_iast: nirvikalpakapratyakṣe 'pi saviśeṣam eva vastu pratīyate / anyathā savikalpake so 'yam iti pūrvāvagataprakāraviśiṣṭapratyayānupapatteḥ
ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/RVas_30.english_close: Even in indeterminate perception, only a qualified thing is apprehended; otherwise the later determinate cognition, 'this is that', would be impossible.

ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/RVas_64.sanskrit_iast: ata eva sanmātragrāhi pratyakṣaṃ na bhedagrāhītyādivādā nirastāḥ / jātyādisaṃsthānasaṃsthitasyaiva vastunaḥ pratyakṣeṇa gṛhītatvāt
ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/RVas_64.english_close: For that very reason, doctrines claiming that perception grasps mere being and not difference are refuted, because perception apprehends a thing only as structured by genus and other determinations.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.89-pratyaksa-laks.sanskrit_iast: yat saṃbaddhaṃ sat tadākārollekhi vijñānaṃ tat pratyakṣaṃ
kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.89-pratyaksa-laks.english_close: Perception is cognition that, with respect to a presently connected object, marks out that very object's form.

isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/4-commentary.sanskrit_iast: dṛṣṭam iti pratyakṣaparyāyaḥ | akṣam indriyaṃ pratītya yad utpadyate jñānaṃ tat pratyakṣaṃ dṛṣṭam ucyate | ... prathamam idaṃ mukhyaṃ sakalapramāṇānām
isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/4-commentary.english_close: "Seen" is a synonym for perception. The cognition that arises by relying on a sense-organ is called perception, and it is the first and chief among all pramāṇas.

isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/6-commentary.sanskrit_iast: atra pradhānapuruṣāv atīndriyau tayoḥ sāmānyatodṛṣṭād anumānāt siddhiḥ ... vyaktaṃ tu pratyakṣeṇaiva sādhitam iti
isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/6-commentary.english_close: Pradhāna and puruṣa are suprasensuous and are established by inference, whereas the manifest is established precisely by perception.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.7-pramanani.english_close: Perception, inference, and authoritative testimony are the pramāṇas.

vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/1.7-praty.sanskrit_iast: indriyapraṇālikayā cittasya bāhyavastūparāgāt tadviṣayā sāmānyaviśeṣātmano 'rthasya viśeṣāvadhāraṇapradhānā vṛttiḥ pratyakṣaṃ pramāṇam
vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/1.7-praty.english_close: Perception is that pramāṇa-vṛtti in which, by the channel of the senses, the external object impresses itself upon mind, yielding a determinative awareness of the object.

vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/1.43-1.49.sanskrit_iast: sā ca nirvitarkā samāpattiḥ | tat paraṃ pratyakṣam ... na cāsya sūkṣmavyavahitaviprakṛṣṭasya vastuno lokapratyakṣeṇa grahaṇam asti
vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/1.43-1.49.english_close: That nirvitarka samāpatti is a higher perception, and its object, whether subtle, remote, or otherwise inaccessible, cannot be grasped by ordinary worldly perception.

aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.4-pratyaksa.sanskrit_iast: indriyārthasannikarṣotpannam jñānam avyapadeśyam avyabhicāri vyavasāyātmakam pratyakṣam
aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.4-pratyaksa.english_close: Perception is a determinate cognition, non-errant and non-verbal in origin, produced by contact between sense and object.

vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/1.1.4-prat.sanskrit_iast: indriyasyārthena sannikarṣād utpadyate yaj jñānaṃ tat pratyakṣam ... yat pratyakṣajñānasya viśiṣṭakāraṇaṃ tad ucyate
vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/1.1.4-prat.english_close: That cognition which arises from the sense's contact with its object is perception, and the sūtra states precisely the distinctive cause of perceptual cognition.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.4-pratyaksa.sanskrit_iast: satsaṃprayoge puruṣasyendriyāṇāṃ buddhijanma tatpratyakṣam animittaṃ vidyamānopalambhanatvāt
jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.4-pratyaksa.english_close: Perception is the arising of cognition when a present object stands in contact with a person's senses, and it is non-inferential because it is the apprehension of what is presently there.

shabara/shabara-bhasya/1.1.4-pratyaksa.sanskrit_iast: pratyakṣaṃ tāvad animittam ... satīndriyārthasaṃbandhe yā puruṣasya buddhir jāyate, tat pratyakṣam ... yat pratyakṣam, na tad vyabhicarati, yad vyabhicarati, na tat pratyakṣam
shabara/shabara-bhasya/1.1.4-pratyaksa.english_close: Perception is non-inferential because it is the cognition that arises when sense and present object are in contact; what genuinely counts as perception does not deviate, and what deviates is not perception.

nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/5-pratyaksa.sanskrit_iast: tan nāsti pratyakṣaṃ bhāvā yenopalabhyante ... pratyakṣam api hi pramāṇaṃ svabhāvāntargatatvāt śūnyaṃ | yo 'pi sarvabhāvān upalabhate so 'pi śūnyaḥ
nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/5-pratyaksa.english_close: There is no perception by which entities are apprehended as self-grounded, because perception itself, being included within what is examined, is empty, and so too is the perceiver.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6-pratyaksa.locus: Madhyamakāvatāra, first bhūmi prose on direct seeing of pudgala-nairātmya
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6-pratyaksa.sanskrit_iast: tadā pudgalanairātmyaṃ pratyakṣaṃ dṛṣṭvā iyaṃ satkāyadṛṣṭi-saṃśayaśīlavrataparamārthateti saṃyojanatrayebhyo 'pi sannivartate
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6-pratyaksa.english_close: Then, having directly seen the selflessness of the person, one turns away from the three fetters beginning with identity-view.

vasubandhu/vimshatika/16-pratyaksa-buddh.sanskrit_iast: pratyakṣabuddhiḥ svapnādau yathā vināpy artheneti pūrvam eva jñāpitaṃ ... na so 'rtho dṛśyate tasya pratyakṣatvaṃ kathaṃ mataṃ
vasubandhu/vimshatika/16-pratyaksa-buddh.english_close: A so-called perceptual cognition, as in dream, can arise even without an external object; if that object is not actually seen, how can its perceived status be maintained?

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.75-svalak.sanskrit_iast: svalakṣaṇe ca pratyakṣam avikalpatayā vinā / vikalpena na sāmānyagrahas tasmis tato 'numā
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.75-svalak.english_close: Perception concerns the particular and is inseparable from non-conceptuality; universal apprehension belongs to conceptual construction and hence to inference, not to perception.

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.123-kalpa.sanskrit_iast: pratyakṣaṃ kalpanāpoḍhaṃ pratyakṣeṇaiva sidhyati / pratyātmavedyaḥ sarveṣāṃ vikalpo nāmasaṃśrayaḥ
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.123-kalpa.english_close: Perception is free from conceptual construction and is established only by perception itself, whereas conceptual construction is tied to names and is introspectively available to everyone.

utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.sanskrit_iast: vartamānāvabhāsānāṃ bhāvānām avabhāsanam / antaḥsthitavatām eva ghaṭate bahir ātmanā // ... pratyakṣe 'pi yāvad arthānāṃ bhedenāvabhāsaḥ pramātrantarlīnānām eva satāṃ yuktaḥ
utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.english_close: The manifestation of apparently external objects occurs only as of things abiding within the self; even in perception their differentiated appearance is intelligible only as internal to the knower.

abhinavagupta/isvara-pratyabhijna-vivrti.sanskrit_iast: pratyakṣe 'pi yāvad arthānāṃ bhedenāvabhāsaḥ pramātrantarlīnānām eva satāṃ yuktaḥ
abhinavagupta/isvara-pratyabhijna-vivrti.english_close: Even in perception, the differentiated appearing objects are intelligible only as realities lying within the perceiving subject.

abhinavagupta/isvara-pratyabhijna-vivrti.sanskrit_iast: na kārakavyāpāro bhagavati, nāpi jñāpakavyāpāro 'yam, api tu mohāpasāraṇamātrametat ... ghaṭo 'yam agragaḥ pratyakṣatvāt ... ghaṭo na jñāpyate pratyakṣeṇaiva prakāśamānatvāt ... kevalaṃ mohamātram apasāryate
abhinavagupta/isvara-pratyabhijna-vivrti.english_close: The work of a pramāṇa is neither to produce nor newly disclose the object; in the case of perception, the pot is already shining forth, and only delusion is removed.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9-1.12-praty.sanskrit_iast: matiśrutāvadhimanaḥparyayakevalāni jñānam / tatpramāṇe / ādye parokṣam / pratyakṣam anyat
umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9-1.12-praty.english_close: Knowledge is fivefold: mati, śruta, avadhi, manaḥparyāya, and kevala; these are the pramāṇas. The first two are indirect, and the rest are direct.

madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.108.sanskrit_iast:
abhinne 'pi viśeṣo 'yaṃ balādāpatati hy ataḥ /
viśeṣatadvatoś caiva svanirvāhakatā bhavet //
madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.108.english_close: Even where there is non-difference, this viśeṣa necessarily presents itself; and there must be self-sustaining determinacy both for the viśeṣa and for that which possesses it.

madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.109.sanskrit_iast:
bhedahīne tv aparyāyaśabdāntaraniyāmakaḥ /
viśeṣo nāma kathitaḥ so 'sti vastuṣv aśeṣataḥ //
madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.109.english_close: Where difference is absent, that which regulates the use of alternative words is called viśeṣa; it exists throughout things without remainder.

madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.110.sanskrit_iast:
viśeṣās te 'py anantāś ca parasparaviśeṣiṇaḥ /
svanirvāhakatāyuktāḥ santi vastuṣv aśeṣataḥ //
madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.110.english_close: Those viśeṣas too are infinite and mutually differentiating; endowed with self-sustaining determinacy, they exist throughout things without remainder.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/38.sanskrit_iast:
tanmātrāṇy aviśeṣās tebhyo bhūtāni pañca pañcabhyaḥ /
ete smṛtā viśeṣāḥ śāntā ghorāś ca mūḍhāś ca //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/38.english_close: The tanmātras are the non-particulars; from those arise the five gross elements, each from its corresponding source. These are remembered as the particulars, peaceful, fierce, and deluded.

isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/41.sanskrit_iast:
citraṃ yathāśrayamṛte sthāṇvādibhyo vinā yathā chāyā /
tadvad vināviśeṣais tiṣṭhati na nirāśrayaṃ liṅgam //
isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/41.english_close: Just as a picture cannot stand without a support, and a shadow cannot stand without post and the like, so the subtle body does not stand without the particulars and cannot stand without a substrate.

patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.19.english_close: The stages of the guṇas are the particular, the non-particular, the mere mark, and the unmarked.

kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.2.3.english_close: Universal and particular are relative to cognition.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/7.english_close: The viśeṣas residing in eternal substances are indeed infinite.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/8.1.1.english_close: Now, then, the definition of viśeṣa.

jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/8.1.2.english_close: That whose indication is fixed by connection with the relevant object, like a denomination.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast:
viśeṣo 'rthāvabhāsasya sattāyāṃ na punaḥ kva cit /
vikalpeṣu bhaved bhāvibhavadbhūtārthagāmiṣu //
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: The distinction of an object's appearance does not lie in being itself anywhere; it occurs in conceptual constructions that move among future, present, and past objects.

umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.34-35.english_close: Without distinguishing being and non-being, apprehension becomes random, like that of a madman. The standpoints are naigama, saṅgraha, vyavahāra, ṛju-sūtra, śabda, samabhirūḍha, and evambhūta.

sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.2.22-vaina.locus: Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.22, refutation of Buddhist nirodha and momentary causal series
sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.2.22-vaina.sanskrit_iast: caturvidhān hetūn pratītya cittacaittā utpadyanta itīyaṃ pratijñā hīyeta ... nahi bhāvānāṃ niranvayo nirupākhyo vināśaḥ saṃbhavati, sarvāsv apy avasthāsu pratyabhijñānabalenānvayyavicchedadarśanāt.
sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.2.22-vaina.english_close: The thesis that mind and mental factors arise in dependence on four kinds of causes would be lost. For entities cannot undergo a discontinuous and indescribable destruction, since in every state continuity is seen through the force of recognition.

sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.2.25-anusm.locus: Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.25, argument from memory against Buddhist momentariness
sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.2.25-anusm.sanskrit_iast: anusmṛteḥ ... sā copalabdhyekakartṛkā satī saṃbhavati ... kathaṃ hy aham ado 'drākṣam idaṃ paśyāmīti ca pūrvottaradarśiny ekasminn asati pratyayaḥ syāt ... tasmād apyanupapanno vaināśikasamayaḥ.
sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.2.25-anusm.english_close: Because of recollection: memory is possible only when perception and memory belong to one agent. Otherwise how could the cognition arise, 'I saw that, and I now see this'? Therefore the doctrine of the destructionist is untenable.

madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.172-175-bauddha-.locus: Anuvyākhyāna 2.2.172-175, statement of the Buddhist position
madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.172-175-bauddha-.sanskrit_iast: vaibhāṣikāś ca sautrāntāḥ svarasakṣaṇikaṃ jagat / aṇūnāṃ samudāyaṃ ca kālakarmanimittataḥ // utpattikāle yuktānām ātmānaṃ ca kṣaṇasthitim / nityaṃ santānam eteṣāṃ pañcaskandhātmanā sthitam // mokṣaṃ sa samudāyo hi naikasmād eva yujyate.
madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.172-175-bauddha-.english_close: The Vaibhāṣikas and Sautrāntikas hold the world to be intrinsically momentary, and the aggregate of atoms to arise through time and karma. They take a continuum of five-skandha form to persist, and even liberation is explained in terms of that aggregate.

madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.184-pratyabhijna.locus: Anuvyākhyāna 2.2.184, appeal to recognition and stability
madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.184-pratyabhijna.sanskrit_iast: dṛśyate pratyabhijñātaḥ sthiratvaṃ sarvavastuṣu / phalādīnāṃ viśeṣeṇa sarvatrāpy anumīyate //
madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.184-pratyabhijna.english_close: Stability is seen in all things through recognition, and it is also inferred everywhere from the distinctness of effects and the like.

madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.230-sunyavada.sanskrit_iast: yac chūnyavādinaḥ śūnyaṃ tadeva brahma māyinaḥ / na hi lakṣaṇabhedo 'sti nirviśeṣatvatas tayoḥ //
madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.230-sunyavada.english_close: What the śūnyavādins call emptiness is precisely what the māyāvādins call Brahman, for there is no difference in definition between the two, since both are without differentiation.

kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.34-1.44-kṣaṇikatv.locus: Sāṃkhya-Sūtra 1.34-1.44, rejection of momentariness, mind-only, and emptiness
kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.34-1.44-kṣaṇikatv.sanskrit_iast: sthirakāryāsiddheḥ kṣaṇikatvam / na pratyabhijñābādhāt / na vijñānamātraṃ bāhyapratīteḥ / tadabhāve tadabhāvāt śūnyaṃ tarhi / śūnyaṃ tattvaṃ, bhāvo vinaśyati, vastudharmatvāt vināśasya.
kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.34-1.44-kṣaṇikatv.english_close: Momentariness is untenable because a stable effect would not be possible; no, because recognition blocks it. Reality is not mere cognition, because external objects are apprehended. If that were absent, one would have emptiness; but destruction is only a property of a thing, not the whole of reality.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.32-kṣaṇika-citta-nir.locus: Yoga-Bhāṣya on Yoga-Sūtra 1.32, refutation of momentary cognition
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.32-kṣaṇika-citta-nir.sanskrit_iast: yasya tu pratyarthaniyataṃ pratyayamātraṃ kṣaṇikaṃ ca cittaṃ tasya sarvam eva cittam ekāgraṃ nāsty eva vikṣiptam ... anyapratyayopacitasya ca karmāśayasyānyaḥ pratyaya upabhoktā bhavet ... yad aham adrākṣaṃ tat spṛśāmi yac cāsprākṣaṃ tat paśyāmīty aham iti pratyayaḥ ... tasmād ekam anekārtham avasthitaṃ ca cittam.
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.32-kṣaṇika-citta-nir.english_close: If citta were nothing but momentary cognitions fixed to their objects, all citta would already be one-pointed and none distracted; the continuity of karmic deposit and its enjoyer would also collapse. The judgment 'What I saw, that I now touch; what I touched, that I now see' therefore shows that one enduring citta stands across many objects.

gautama/nyaya-sutra/3.2.10-17-kṣaṇabhaṅg.sanskrit_iast: sphaṭike 'py aparāparotpatteḥ kṣaṇikatvād vyaktīnām ahetuḥ ... niyamahetvabhāvād yathādarśanam abhyanujñā ... nānutpattivināśakāraṇopalabdheḥ.
gautama/nyaya-sutra/3.2.10-17-kṣaṇabhaṅg.english_close: The claim is examined that, because distinct manifestations arise successively even in crystal, particulars are momentary. Nyāya replies that without a determinate reason such a thesis is not established, and that no cause of sheer origination-and-destruction is observed.

vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/3.2.10-kṣaṇikatv.sanskrit_iast: sphaṭike 'pi anyā vyaktaya utpadyante anyā nirudhyanta iti / katham? kṣaṇikatvād vyaktīnām / kathaṃ punar gamyate kṣaṇikā vyaktaya iti? upacayāpacayaprabandhadarśanāc charīrādiṣu.
vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/3.2.10-kṣaṇikatv.english_close: The opponent says that in a crystal too some manifestations arise and others cease, because particulars are momentary. But how is their momentariness known? Only from observing ongoing increase and decrease in things like bodies.

isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/29-commentar.sanskrit_iast: asmin prāṇe yat spandanakarma tat katamena kṛtam ity atrocyate --- trayodaśavidhena karaṇena kṛtam ... tadvat prāṇe yat spandanakarma tat sarveṣām indriyāṇāṃ sāmānyam
isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/29-commentar.english_close: The motion called spanda in the vital breath is produced by the thirteenfold organ-complex; thus that spanda is common to all the senses.

vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/2.3.sanskrit_iast: te spandamānā guṇādhikāraṃ draḍhayanti, pariṇāmam avasthāpayanti, kāryakāraṇasrota unnamayanti, parasparānugrahatantrībhūtvā karmavipākaṃ cābhinirharantīti
vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/2.3.english_close: Those afflictions, when active, strengthen the rule of the guṇas, establish transformation, raise the stream of cause and effect, and bring forth karmic fruition.

vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/2.15.sanskrit_iast: sukhasādhanāni ca prārthayamānaḥ kāyena vācā manasā ca parispandate tataḥ param anugṛhṇāty upahanti ceti parānugrahapīḍābhyāṃ dharmādharmāv upacinoti
vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/2.15.english_close: Seeking the means of pleasure, one stirs with body, speech, and mind; then one benefits or harms others, and so accumulates dharma and adharma.

vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/2.1.34.sanskrit_iast: gṛhyante tu kumbho 'yaṃ śyāma eko mahān saṃyuktaḥ spandate asti mṛnmayaś ceti
vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/2.1.34.english_close: One apprehends: 'this pot is dark, one, large, conjoined, moving, existing, and made of clay.'

udayana/nyaya-kusumanjali/3.348.sanskrit_iast: tatra prathamam api spando 'spanda iti dvayameva / etacca yathāsaṅkhyaṃ karma guṇa iti vyapadiśyate
udayana/nyaya-kusumanjali/3.348.english_close: Here first too there are only two: spanda and aspanda; these are designated, respectively, motion and quality.

vasugupta/spanda-karika/1.1.sanskrit_iast: yasyonmeṣanimeṣābhyāṃ jagataḥ pralayodayau | taṃ śakticakravibhavaprabhavaṃ śaṅkaraṃ stumaḥ ||
vasugupta/spanda-karika/1.1.english_close: We praise Śaṅkara, source of the splendour of the circle of powers, whose opening and closing are the dissolution and arising of the world.

abhinavagupta/tantraloka/4.183.sanskrit_iast: bhāvagrahādiparyantabhāvī sāmānyasaṃjñakaḥ / spandaḥ sa kathyate śāstre svātmanyucchalanātmakaḥ //
abhinavagupta/tantraloka/4.183.english_close: That process, extending from the apprehension of objects onward and named the general principle, is called spanda in the śāstra, being the Self's own surging forth.

ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/1.english_close: Consciousness, autonomous, is the cause of the accomplishment of the universe.

ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/2.english_close: By her own will she unfolds the universe upon her own surface.

ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/4.english_close: The conscious subject, whose nature is a contraction of consciousness, is also a contracted form of the universe.

sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/1.1.27.sanskrit_iast: ubhayasminn api saptamyante pañcamyante copadeśe na pratyabhijñānaṃ virudhyate ... tasmād asti pūrvanirdiṣṭasya brahmaṇa iha pratyabhijñānam
sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/1.1.27.english_close: Recognition is not contradicted by the two differently inflected teachings; therefore there is here a re-cognition of the Brahman previously indicated.

sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/1.3.13.sanskrit_iast: sa eva ceha parapuruṣaśabdābhyām abhidhyātavyaḥ pratyabhijñāyate
sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/1.3.13.english_close: That very one is here recognized again as the object to be contemplated by the words 'supreme person'.

ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/25.sanskrit_iast: so 'yaṃ devadatta ity atrāpi lakṣaṇāgandho na vidyate ... ekasya bhūtavartamānakriyādvayasaṃbandho na viruddhaḥ
ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/25.english_close: Even in 'this is that Devadatta' there is not even a trace of secondary implication, for one entity can stand in both a past and a present relation without contradiction.

ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/63.sanskrit_iast: so 'yam iti buddhiḥ prakārāikyād ... ayam ca jātyādiprakāro vastuno bheda ity ucyate
ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/63.english_close: The cognition 'this is that' arises from identity of the mode; that modal factor is what is called the difference of the thing.

madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.184.sanskrit_iast: dṛśyate pratyabhijñātaḥ sthiratvaṃ sarvavastuṣu / phalādīnāṃ viśeṣeṇa sarvatrāpy anumīyate
madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.184.english_close: The stability recognized in all things is seen, and from particular cases such as fruits it is inferred everywhere.

madhva/anuvyakhyana/3.2.82.sanskrit_iast: mānatvaṃ pratyabhijñāyā api sarvānubhūtigam / atītavartamānatvadharmiṇī sā ca dṛśyate
madhva/anuvyakhyana/3.2.82.english_close: Pratyabhijñā too has pramāṇa-status, for it ranges over all experience and is seen to concern a subject qualified by past and present.

vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.14.sanskrit_iast: vastupratyabhijñānāc ca sthito 'nvayī dharmī yo dharmānyathātvam abhyupagataḥ pratyabhijñāyate tasmān nedaṃ dharmamātraṃ niranvayam iti
vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.14.english_close: Because of recognition of the thing, an enduring connected substrate is recognized even when change of properties is admitted; therefore this is not a mere property-stream without an underlying continuant.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha-comm/recognit.sanskrit_iast: 'yo 'ham adrākṣaṃ so 'ham idānīṃ spṛśāmi' iti pratītyā ... tayor aikyam avagamyate
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha-comm/recognit.english_close: From the cognition 'I who saw, I now touch,' the unity of those two perceptual substrates is understood.

annambhatta/tarka-sangraha-comm/recognit.sanskrit_iast: 'so 'yaṃ devadattaḥ' iti pratyabhijñāyāṃ ... pratyabhijñā tu saṃskārasahakṛtacakṣurādijanyā
annambhatta/tarka-sangraha-comm/recognit.english_close: In the recognition 'this is that Devadatta,' pratyabhijñā is produced by the senses with the assistance of saṃskāra.

shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.20.english_close: People do recognize it: 'this is that very one.'

madhava/jaimini-nyaya-mala/2.4.3-4.sanskrit_iast: rūpādipratyabhijñānād abhinnaṃ karma gamyate ... ananyathāsiddharūpapratyabhijñānāc chākhābhede 'pi karma na bhidyate
madhava/jaimini-nyaya-mala/2.4.3-4.english_close: From recognition of the same form and other features, the ritual act is understood to be non-different; thus even with difference of recension the act does not differ when its form is otherwise unaccountable.

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.118.sanskrit_iast: viśeṣapratyabhijñānaṃ na pratikṣaṇabhedataḥ / na vā viśeṣaviṣayaṃ dṛṣṭasāmyena tadgrahāt
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.118.english_close: Recognition of a particular does not hold if there is difference at every moment, nor is it really of a particular, since it is grasped through observed similarity.

dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.505-506.sanskrit_iast: tasmān na pratyabhijñānād varṇādyekatvaniścayaḥ / pūrvānubhūtasmaraṇāt taddharmāropaṇād vinā / sa evāyam iti jñānaṃ nāsti tacchakṣaje kutaḥ
dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.505-506.english_close: Therefore recognition does not establish the unity of a sound or the like; apart from memory of what was previously experienced and superimposition of its properties, no cognition 'this is that very one' arises, so how could it be perceptual?

jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/line-3532.sanskrit_iast: asmat pratyabhijñayā bhedanirāsād ity āhuḥ ... he bhagavan paraṃ kevalam ātmatattvaṃ brahmasvarūpaṃ tvāṃ vidāma vidmaḥ pratyabhijānīmaḥ
jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/line-3532.english_close: They say that by our recognition the difference is removed: 'O Lord, we know you, we recognize you as the supreme and sole ātma-tattva, the very form of Brahman.'

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: samastasaṃpatsamavāptihetuṃ tatpratyabhijñām upapādayāmi
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: I establish that recognition which is the cause of attaining all prosperity.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..sanskrit_iast: satyaṃ kiṃ tu smṛtijñānaṃ pūrvānubhavasaṃskṛteḥ / jātam apy ātmaniṣṭhaṃ tan nādyānubhavavedakam // dṛksvābhāsaiva nānyena vedyā rūpadṛśeva dṛk
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1..english_close: Memory-cognition, though arising from the impression of prior experience, remains fixed in the self and does not disclose the prior experience as such; awareness is self-manifest and not known by another awareness.

utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4..sanskrit_iast: tad atra nidadhat padaṃ bhuvanakartṛtām ātmano vibhāvya śivatāmayīm aniśam āviśan siddhyati ... lokasyaiṣa tathānavekṣitaguṇaḥ svātmāpi viśveśvaro naivālaṃ nijavaibhavāya tad iyaṃ tatpratyabhijñoditā
utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4..english_close: Placing one's step here, realizing the world's creatorship as one's own Śiva-nature, and entering that constantly, one attains siddhi; this recognition has been taught because the Lord of the universe, though one's very self, is not enough for one's own glory so long as his qualities are not recognized.

vacaspati/nyaya-varttika-tatparya-tika/1.sanskrit_iast: syād virodho yadi svalakṣaṇamevārthaḥ, na tvetadasti / upapādayiṣyati hi paramārthasantaṃ jātyādimantamarthaṃ sthemabhājamabhilāpasaṃsargayogyam / tena tajjanitaṃ jñānamarthasāmarthyajaṃ cābhilāpasaṃsargayogyapratibhāsaṃ ceti na virodhaḥ /
vacaspati/nyaya-varttika-tatparya-tika/1.english_close: There would be a contradiction if the object were only svalakṣaṇa, but that is not so. For he will establish that the real object possesses universals and the rest, is stable, and is fit for association with verbal expression. Therefore cognition produced by it can be both generated by the object's efficacy and possessed of an appearance fit for language; there is no contradiction.

sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika-tika/84.sanskrit_iast: yathānyaiḥ kalpanāpoḍham abhrāntaṃ pratyakṣam iti pratyakṣalakṣaṇam uktvā tasya svalakṣaṇaviṣayatvaṃ darśitam ... asmākaṃ tu sambaddhavartamānaviṣayaṃ pratyakṣaṃ, na sāmānyaviśeṣayor anyatarad api vyāvartyam iti kiṃ viṣayanirdeśeneti
sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika-tika/84.english_close: Others define perception as free from conceptual construction and non-erroneous, and then show it to have svalakṣaṇa as its object. But for us, perception has a connected present object; it excludes neither universal nor particular, so what need is there for a separate specification of its object?

sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika-tika/parart.sanskrit_iast: yadi tu svalakṣaṇaviṣayatvāt pratyakṣasya tasya cāśabdagocaratvān na pratyakṣaṃ parārtham ity ucyate, tad ayuktam ... yo hi pratyakṣaviruddham arthaṃ pratijānīte nāgnir uṣṇa iti, sa vacanena taṃ pratyakṣaviṣayam arthaṃ pratipādya nirākriyate
sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika-tika/parart.english_close: If it is said that perception is not for others because, having svalakṣaṇa as its object, it is not within the range of words, that is not right. For one who asserts something contrary to perception, such as 'fire is not hot', is refuted by a statement that presents that very perceptual object.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.34.sanskrit_iast: svalakṣaṇaṁ ced bhavati pratītya tasyāpavāde sati bhāvanāśāt / syāc chūnyatā bhāvavināśahetur yuktaṁ na caitan na tato 'sti bhāvaḥ //
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.34.english_close: If a svalakṣaṇa were to exist dependently, then on its refutation, because entities would perish, emptiness would become the cause of the destruction of entities. That is not reasonable, and therefore no such entity is established.

candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.201.sanskrit_iast: śūnyatā sarvadharmāṇāṁ yā teṣāṁ tair viyuktatā / rūpaṇāder abhāvo yaḥ sā svalakṣaṇaśūnyatā //
candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.201.english_close: That emptiness of all dharmas which is their being devoid of those marks, namely the absence of form-characteristic and the like, is svalakṣaṇa-śūnyatā.

dharmakirti/nyaya-bindu/1.12-17.sanskrit_iast: tasya viṣayaḥ svalakṣaṇam. yasya arthasya saṃnidhāna-asaṃnidhānābhyāṃ jñāna-pratibhāsa-bhedas tat svalakṣaṇam. tad eva paramārthasat. arthakriyāsāmarthya-lakṣaṇatvād vastunaḥ. anyat sāmānyalakṣaṇam. so anumānasya viṣayaḥ.
dharmakirti/nyaya-bindu/1.12-17.english_close: Its object is the svalakṣaṇa. That entity whose presence and absence make the appearance of cognition differ is the svalakṣaṇa. That alone is ultimately real, because a thing is marked by capacity for causal efficacy. What is other is the sāmānyalakṣaṇa. That is the object of inference.

abhinavagupta/ishvara-pratyabhijna-vimar.sanskrit_iast: yathāruci yathārthitvaṃ yathāvyutpatti bhidyate / ābhāso 'py artha ekasminn anusaṃdhānasādhite // ... nanvevaṃ cet kathamekaṃ svalakṣaṇam ? ucyate ---- teṣāṃ pṛthak bhāsamānānāmapi ābhāsānāṃ yo vimarśaḥ anuprāṇitabhūtaḥ ... tadā tadekaṃ svalakṣaṇam
